topics.title: Types of Two and Three-Wheelers
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Types of Two and Three-Wheelers

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT631 - TWO AND THREE WHEELED VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Two and Three-Wheelers

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Two and Three-Wheeler** is a category of motor vehicles, as defined under Section 2(28) of the **Motor Vehicles Act, 1988 (India)** and the **Automotive Industry Standards (AIS)** governed by the Ministry of Road Transport and Highways (MoRTH), characterized by the presence of either two or three wheels in contact with the road surface, powered by an internal combustion engine (ICE) or an electric drivetrain, with a maximum permissible gross vehicle weight and engine displacement/electric power output falling within the limits prescribed for the **L1, L2, L5, and L7** categories of the **Central Motor Vehicles Rules (CMVR), 1989**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Official Classification Codes:**
> - **L1**: Two-wheeled vehicle with engine displacement ≤ **50 cc** (Mopeds).
> - **L2**: Two-wheeled vehicle with engine displacement > **50 cc** (Motorcycles/Scooters).
> - **L5**: Three-wheeled vehicle with engine displacement > **50 cc** (Auto-rickshaws, passenger/delivery).
> - **L7**: Heavy three-wheeled quadricycle (rarely covered in basic modules).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the two-wheeler and three-wheeler family as the **"versatile workhorses of urban mobility"** — much like how a Swiss Army knife contains different tools for different jobs:

- A **moped** is like a *penknife* — light, economical, built for short commutes.
- A **motorcycle** is like a *full-sized blade* — powerful, designed for performance and long-distance travel.
- A **scooter** is like a *scissors tool* — practical, step-through design for everyday city use.
- An **auto-rickshaw** (three-wheeler) is like a *screwdriver with a fulcrum* — the third wheel provides the balance/stability needed to carry passengers or goods.

The *third wheel* in three-wheelers is essentially a **mechanical stabilizer** — it removes the need for the rider to balance the vehicle dynamically (as in a two-wheeler), trading off agility for load-carrying capacity and ease of use.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Context:** India is the **world's largest manufacturer of two-wheelers** (producing over 20 million units annually) and the **largest manufacturer of three-wheelers** globally. Therefore, KTU's syllabus places significant emphasis on the engineering classifications, chassis layouts, and powertrain configurations used by Indian OEMs (Hero, Bajaj, Honda, TVS, Royal Enfield, Ola Electric, Ather).

### 1.3 Fundamental Operational Principle

The core distinction between all two and three-wheelers lies in three primary engineering parameters:

1. **Wheel Count & Configuration** → Affects *static stability*, *cornering behavior*, and *load distribution*.
2. **Powertrain Type** → ICE (Petrol/Diesel/CNG) or Electric (BLDC/PMSM with Li-ion battery).
3. **Intended Application** → Personal transport, commercial passenger transport, or goods carriage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The standard metric for engine classification in India is **engine displacement (cc — cubic centimetres)**. The cut-off limit of **50 cc** is not arbitrary — it is a **legal threshold** below which vehicles qualify for a *non-transport endorsement* license, lower road tax, and insurance premium slabs.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis — Classification Matrix

### 2.1 Detailed Classification of Two-Wheelers

Two-wheelers are broadly classified by **engine displacement**, **frame design**, and **end-use application**.

#### 2.1.1 Based on Engine Displacement

| Category | Displacement Range | Power Output (Typical) | Top Speed | Primary Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Mopeds (L1)** | ≤ 50 cc | 2 – 4 kW | 25 – 50 km/h | Short urban commutes, student transport, rural last-mile |
| **Commuter Motorcycles** | 100 – 150 cc | 6 – 12 kW | 80 – 100 km/h | Daily office commute, family use |
| **Mid-segment Motorcycles** | 150 – 350 cc | 12 – 25 kW | 110 – 150 km/h | Touring, enthusiast riding |
| **Premium/Performance Motorcycles** | 350 – 800+ cc | 25 – 100+ kW | 150 – 300+ km/h | Sports, long-distance touring, racing |
| **Electric Two-Wheelers** | Equivalent to ≤ 250 cc ICE | 1 – 15 kW | 25 – 120 km/h | Urban commuting (zero tailpipe emission) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Power-to-Weight Ratio (PWR)** is the most critical performance metric for any two-wheeler. It is defined as:
>
> $$ \text{PWR} = \frac{P_{\text{engine}}}{m_{\text{vehicle}}} \quad \text{[kW/kg]} $$
>
> Where $P_{\text{engine}}$ is the rated engine power output in kilowatts and $m_{\text{vehicle}}$ is the kerb weight in kilograms. A higher PWR translates directly to better **acceleration** and **gradeability** (climbing ability on inclines).

#### 2.1.2 Based on Frame/Design Architecture

- **Motorcycles (Conventional / Straddle Type):** The rider sits *astride* the vehicle with feet on either side of the fuel tank. The frame is a backbone or diamond-type tubular/perimeter frame. Examples: Royal Enfield Classic 350, Bajaj Pulsar NS200, Yamaha R15.
- **Scooters (Step-Through Type):** The rider sits on a *saddle* with feet on a central floorboard. The frame is a pressed steel underbone (chassis-less) construction. Examples: Honda Activa, TVS Jupiter, Ola S1 Pro.
- **Mopeds:** Lightweight two-wheelers with small wheels (typically 16" – 18") and an assisted pedaling mechanism. Examples: Hero Splendor Plus (technically 100 cc, but styled as moped), TVS XL 100.
- **Sports / Supersport Bikes:** Full-fairing, rear-set footpegs, clip-on handlebars, aerodynamic design. Example: Kawasaki Ninja ZX-10R.
- **Cruisers:** Low-slung seats, forward-set footpegs, large torque, V-twin engines. Example: Royal Enfield Meteor 350.
- **Adventure Tourers (ADV):** Long-travel suspension, upright ergonomics, large fuel tank, off-road capability. Example: Royal Enfield Himalayan, KTM 390 Adventure.
- **Dirt Bikes / Off-Road Bikes:** Knobby tyres, minimal bodywork, high ground clearance, long suspension travel. Example: Hero XPulse 200.

#### 2.1.3 Based on Powertrain

- **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) Two-Wheelers:**
  - **Petrol (Spark Ignition, SI):** 2-stroke or 4-stroke. 4-stroke dominates the market due to lower emissions and better fuel economy.
  - **Diesel (Compression Ignition, CI):** Rare in two-wheelers (Bajaj Pulsar 180 DTSi was an exception); mostly used in commercial three-wheelers.
  - **CNG / LPG:** Used in some auto-rickshaws and delivery scooters for fuel economy.
  - **Hybrid:** Honda PCX Hybrid (limited presence in India).

- **Electric Two-Wheelers (E2W):**
  - **Low-Speed (≤ 25 km/h, ≤ 250 W):** No license required. Example: entry-level e-scooters.
  - **High-Speed (> 25 km/h, > 250 W):** Requires license and registration. Examples: Ola S1 Pro, Ather 450X, TVS iQube, Bajaj Chetak Electric.

### 2.2 Detailed Classification of Three-Wheelers

Three-wheelers are categorized primarily by **wheel configuration** and **end-use application**.

#### 2.2.1 Based on Wheel Configuration

- **Tadpole Configuration:** Two wheels at the *front* and one at the *rear*. Used in sport/reverse three-wheelers (e.g., Can-Am Ryker, Polaris Slingshot — not common in India). Provides superior cornering stability and braking.
- **Delta Configuration:** One wheel at the *front* and two at the *rear*. Standard Indian auto-rickshaw layout. Provides better load-carrying capacity and straightforward steering geometry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Delta dominates India:** The delta layout uses a *simple trailing-link or leading-link front fork* with a *single front wheel* for steering. This makes the steering mechanism **mechanically simple, low-cost, and easily serviceable** in rural and semi-urban environments — a critical factor in a price-sensitive market.

#### 2.2.2 Based on Application

| Sub-Type | Application | Typical Engine/Power | Payload Capacity | Indian Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Passenger Auto-Rickshaw** | 3 + 1 (driver) seating | 145 – 200 cc petrol, CNG, or 2 – 4 kW electric | 3 passengers + luggage | Bajaj RE, Piaggio Ape, Mahindra Alfa |
| **Goods Carrier (Load Auto)** | Cargo deck behind driver | 400 – 600 cc diesel/petrol, or 4 – 7 kW electric | 500 – 750 kg | Bajaj Maxima, Piaggio Ape Xtra, Mahindra Jeeto |
| **E-Rickshaw (L5M)** | Electric passenger | 1 – 2 kW BLDC, 48 – 60 V battery | 4 – 6 passengers | Hero Electric, YC Electric, Kinetic Safar |
| **Delivery E-Cart (L5N)** | Electric goods | 1.5 – 3 kW BLDC | 150 – 500 kg | Euler HiLoad, Altigreen, Mahindra Treo Zor |
| **Specialty Vehicles** | Ambulance, food cart, mobile shop | Customized | Variable | Various OEMs |

#### 2.2.3 Based on Powertrain

- **ICE Three-Wheelers:** Predominantly **single-cylinder, 4-stroke SI engines** (CNG/petrol for autos, diesel for load carriers). Compression ratio typically 9:1 to 10.5:1.
- **Electric Three-Wheelers (E3W):** Rapidly growing segment (FAME-II subsidies). Uses **BLDC (Brushless DC)** or **PMSM (Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor)** with **Li-ion or lead-acid battery packs**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Two-Wheeler (L1/L2) | Three-Wheeler (L5) |
|---|---|---|
| **Wheels** | 2 | 3 (typically delta) |
| **Engine Displacement** | ≤ 50 cc (L1) / > 50 cc (L2) | > 50 cc (typically 145 – 600 cc ICE) |
| **Max Power (Typical)** | 2 – 100+ kW | 4 – 10 kW (auto) / 4 – 15 kW (load) |
| **Kerb Weight** | 80 – 250 kg | 250 – 500 kg (auto) / 400 – 800 kg (load) |
| **Steering Mechanism** | Handlebar (rider leans into turn) | Handlebar or steering wheel (delta) |
| **Stability** | Dynamic (rider balances) | Static (3rd wheel provides base) |
| **Primary Use** | Personal transport | Commercial / last-mile |
| **License Required** | L1: No (with restrictions) / L2: Yes | Yes (LMV-NT or MCWG + badge) |
| **Key Indian OEMs** | Hero, Honda, Bajaj, TVS, Royal Enfield, Ola, Ather | Bajaj, Piaggio, Mahindra, Atul, Euler, Altigreen |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding the classification of two and three-wheelers is **fundamental** to multiple downstream engineering activities in the automotive industry:

- **Homologation & Certification:** Each AIS standard (e.g., **AIS-052** for electric two-wheelers, **AIS-156** for batteries) applies to a specific sub-category.
- **Chassis Design:** The choice between a *tubular backbone frame* (motorcycle), *underbone pressed-steel chassis* (scooter), or *monocoque/space frame* (e-rickshaw) is dictated by the vehicle class.
- **Drivetrain Engineering:** The engine size, transmission type (CVT vs. multi-speed gearbox), and final drive (chain, belt, shaft) vary by category.
- **Crash Safety Standards:** AIS-156 (battery safety) and AIS-138 (electric powertrain) apply to electric variants regardless of L-category.
- **Market & Business Strategy:** OEMs segment their R&D and product portfolios strictly along these classifications.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Comparative Matrix

### 3.1 Mathematical Classification: Power-to-Weight Ratio (PWR) Analysis

The **Power-to-Weight Ratio** is the single most diagnostic parameter to differentiate vehicle sub-classes. We derive it step by step.

**Given:**
- Engine rated power: $P$ in kW
- Kerb (unloaded) vehicle mass: $m$ in kg

**Step 1 — Define the ratio:**

$$ \text{PWR} = \frac{P}{m} \quad \left[\frac{\text{kW}}{\text{kg}}\right] $$

**Step 2 — Convert PWR to a more intuitive unit (W/kg):**

$$ \text{PWR}_{\text{W/kg}} = \text{PWR}_{\text{kW/kg}} \times 1000 $$

**Step 3 — Apply to representative vehicles:**

| Vehicle | Engine Power $P$ (kW) | Kerb Mass $m$ (kg) | PWR (kW/kg) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hero Splendor Plus (Commuter) | 6.03 | 110 | 0.0548 |
| Bajaj Pulsar NS200 | 18.0 | 156 | 0.1154 |
| KTM 390 Duke | 32.0 | 177 | 0.1808 |
| Kawasaki Ninja ZX-10R | 147.1 | 207 | 0.7106 |
| Honda Activa 6G (Scooter) | 5.78 | 109 | 0.0530 |
| Ola S1 Pro (Electric Scooter) | 5.5 | 116 | 0.0474 |
| Bajaj RE Auto (Passenger) | 6.6 | 280 | 0.0236 |
| Piaggio Ape Xtra (Load) | 6.7 | 470 | 0.0143 |
| Euler HiLoad (Electric Cargo) | 8.0 | 555 | 0.0144 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A *higher PWR* corresponds to a vehicle capable of faster acceleration. A *lower PWR* corresponds to a load-carrying or economy-focused vehicle. Notice the systematic trend: **performance motorcycles >> commuters >> scooters >> passenger autos >> load carriers**. This is a direct engineering consequence of the design priority hierarchy.

### 3.2 Engine Displacement Classification: Step-by-Step Logical Derivation

The 50 cc threshold is mathematically linked to the **power output** and **top speed** thresholds defined in the CMVR.

**Step 1 — Establish the empirical relationship between displacement, power, and top speed for small SI engines:**

$$ P_{\text{brake}} = \eta_{\text{thermal}} \times \dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} \times \text{CV}_{\text{fuel}} $$

Where:
- $\eta_{\text{thermal}}$ ≈ 0.22 – 0.28 (typical for air-cooled 4-stroke 2-wheelers)
- $\dot{m}_{\text{fuel}}$ = mass flow rate of fuel
- $\text{CV}_{\text{fuel}}$ ≈ **44 MJ/kg** (Calorific Value of petrol)

**Step 2 — Power output for a typical 50 cc 4-stroke engine (BS6, naturally aspirated):**

$$ P \approx V_{\text{disp}} \times \text{BMEP} \times \frac{N}{2} \times \frac{1}{60} $$

For a 50 cc engine:
- $V_{\text{disp}} = 50 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$
- BMEP (Brake Mean Effective Pressure) ≈ 8 bar = 800 kPa
- $N$ ≈ 5500 rpm (peak power)

$$ P \approx (50 \times 10^{-6}) \times (800 \times 10^3) \times \frac{5500}{2} \times \frac{1}{60} $$

$$ P \approx 0.050 \times 800000 \times 45.83 \times 0.01667 $$

**Step 3 — Evaluate numerically:**

$$ P \approx 0.050 \times 800000 \times 45.83 / 60 $$

$$ P \approx 3.05 \text{ kW} \quad (\approx 4.1 \text{ hp}) $$

**Step 4 — Compare to a 150 cc engine under the same logic:**

$$ P \approx (150 \times 10^{-6}) \times (800 \times 10^3) \times \frac{8500}{2} \times \frac{1}{60} $$

$$ P \approx 150 \times 10^{-6} \times 800000 \times 70.83 / 60 $$

$$ P \approx 8.5 \text{ kW} \quad (\approx 11.4 \text{ hp}) $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion of Derivation:** A 50 cc engine produces roughly **3 kW of peak power**, which is sufficient to propel a 100 kg moped to ~50 km/h. Above this, the increased mass, aerodynamic drag (which scales with $v^2$), and rolling resistance demand a step-up to a higher displacement — which is why the **L1 (≤ 50 cc) / L2 (> 50 cc) boundary** is a natural engineering demarcation, not just a legal one.

### 3.3 Stability Analysis: Why Three Wheels Are Statically Stable

**Two-wheeler (dynamic stability):**

A two-wheeler has a **narrow track width** and a **high centre of gravity (CoG)**. In static equilibrium, it would fall over. Stability is achieved only through the **gyroscopic effect** of the spinning wheels and the **rider's counter-steering input**.

The critical lean angle $\theta$ for a given turn radius $R$ and speed $v$ is given by:

$$ \tan(\theta) = \frac{v^2}{R \cdot g} $$

Where $g$ = **9.81 m/s²** (acceleration due to gravity).

**Three-wheeler (static stability):**

A delta-configuration three-wheeler forms a **tripod base** (the two rear wheels + the front wheel trace a triangle). The CoG lies within this triangle as long as:

$$ h_{\text{CoG}} < \frac{w_{\text{track}}}{2 \tan(\phi_{\text{limit}})} $$

Where:
- $h_{\text{CoG}}$ = height of centre of gravity from ground
- $w_{\text{track}}$ = rear wheel track width
- $\phi_{\text{limit}}$ = maximum permissible tilt angle before tip-over (typically 30° – 35° for autos)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Implication:** This is why a loaded three-wheeler with passengers and goods still remains stable even when stationary (e.g., at a traffic signal), whereas a loaded two-wheeler requires the rider to put a foot down.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (For Examination Use)

| Design Feature | Moped (L1) | Commuter Bike (L2) | Performance Bike (L2) | Passenger Auto (L5) | Load Auto (L5) | E-Rickshaw (L5) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Displacement / Power | ≤ 50 cc / 2 – 4 kW | 100 – 150 cc / 6 – 12 kW | 350 – 1000+ cc / 25 – 150 kW | 145 – 200 cc / 5 – 7 kW | 400 – 600 cc / 5 – 10 kW | 1 – 2 kW (electric) |
| Transmission | CVT / 4-speed | 5-speed | 6-speed | 4-speed / CVT | 4 – 5-speed | Single-speed reduction |
| Final Drive | Chain / Belt | Chain | Chain | Shaft / Chain | Shaft / Chain | Direct drive / chain |
| Cooling | Air-cooled | Air-cooled | Liquid-cooled (high-end) | Air-cooled | Air-cooled | Air-cooled motor |
| Kerb Weight | 80 – 110 kg | 130 – 160 kg | 170 – 220 kg | 250 – 350 kg | 400 – 600 kg | 250 – 400 kg |
| PWR (kW/kg) | 0.025 – 0.040 | 0.05 – 0.08 | 0.15 – 0.70 | 0.02 – 0.03 | 0.01 – 0.02 | 0.005 – 0.008 |
| Braking | Drum (both) | Disc (F) + Drum (R) | Disc (both) + ABS | Drum (F) + Drum (R) | Drum / Hydraulic | Drum / Disc |
| Mileage / Range | 60 – 80 km/l | 45 – 65 km/l | 15 – 30 km/l | 30 – 40 km/kg (CNG) | 25 – 35 km/l | 80 – 120 km/charge |
| Typical Tyre Size (Front / Rear) | 2.75 – 17" | 80/100-17 / 100/90-17 | 120/70-17 / 190/55-17 | 4.50-10 / 4.50-10 | 4.50-10 / 4.50-10 | 3.75-12 / 3.75-12 |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Classification Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Two and Three Wheeled Vehicles] --> B[Two Wheelers]
    A --> C[Three Wheelers]
    A --> D[Selection Criteria]
    A --> E[Legal Categories]
    
    B --> B1[Engine Displacement 50 cc or less]
    B --> B2[Engine Displacement greater than 50 cc]
    B --> B3[Electric Powertrain]
    
    B1 --> B1A[Mopeds]
    B1 --> B1B[Low Speed E Scooters]
    
    B2 --> B2A[Motorcycles Straddle Type]
    B2 --> B2B[Scooters Step Through]
    B2 --> B2C[Commuter Segment]
    B2 --> B2D[Performance Segment]
    
    C --> C1[Delta Configuration One Front Two Rear]
    C --> C2[Tadpole Configuration Two Front One Rear]
    C --> C3[Electric Three Wheelers]
    
    C1 --> C1A[Passenger Auto Rickshaw]
    C1 --> C1B[Goods Carrier Load Auto]
    C1 --> C1C[CNG LPG Variants]
    
    D --> D1[Application Personal vs Commercial]
    D --> D2[Powertrain ICE vs Electric]
    D --> D3[Terrain Urban vs Rural vs Offroad]
    
    E --> E1[L1 Moped Category]
    E --> E2[L2 Motorcycle Category]
    E --> E3[L5 Three Wheeler Category]
```

### 4.2 Powertrain Architecture Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ICEPOWERTRAIN[ICE Two and Three Wheeler Architecture]
        ICE1[Fuel Tank] --> ICE2[Carburetor or Fuel Injector]
        ICE2 --> ICE3[Engine SI or CI 4 Stroke]
        ICE3 --> ICE4[Transmission Multi Speed or CVT]
        ICE4 --> ICE5[Final Drive Chain Belt or Shaft]
        ICE5 --> ICE6[Rear Wheel]
        ICE3 -.-> ICE7[Exhaust System with Catalytic Converter]
    end
    
    subgraph ELECPOWERTRAIN[Electric Two and Three Wheeler Architecture]
        ELE1[Li ion Battery Pack] --> ELE2[Battery Management System BMS]
        ELE2 --> ELE3[Motor Controller Inverter]
        ELE3 --> ELE4[BLDC or PMSM Motor]
        ELE4 --> ELE5[Single Speed Reducer]
        ELE5 --> ELE6[Rear Wheel]
        ELE1 -.-> ELE7[Onboard Charger AC DC]
    end
```

### 4.3 Wheel Configuration Architecture Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DELTA[Delta Configuration L5 Standard]
        D_FW[Front Wheel Steering]
        D_RWL[Rear Wheel Left]
        D_RWR[Rear Wheel Right]
        D_FW --- D_RWL
        D_RWL --- D_RWR
        D_RWR --- D_FW
    end
    
    subgraph TADPOLE[Tadpole Configuration L7 Sports]
        T_FWL[Front Wheel Left]
        T_FWR[Front Wheel Right]
        T_RW[Rear Wheel Single]
        T_FWL --- T_FWR
        T_FWR --- T_RW
        T_RW --- T_FWL
    end
    
    subgraph TWOWHEEL[Two Wheeler L1 L2]
        TW_FW[Front Wheel Steering]
        TW_RW[Rear Wheel Driven]
    end
```

### 4.4 Subsystem Identification in a Modern Electric Two-Wheeler

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    USER[Rider Input Throttle and Brake] --> VCU[Vehicle Control Unit VCU]
    VCU --> BMS[Battery Management System]
    VCU --> MCU[Motor Control Unit]
    BMS --> BATT[High Voltage Li ion Battery Pack 48V to 72V]
    BATT --> MCU
    MCU --> MOTOR[BLDC Hub Motor 1 to 5 kW]
    MOTOR --> WHEEL[Rear Wheel Direct Drive]
    VCU --> DISPLAY[Instrument Cluster SOC Speed Range]
    VCU --> TELE[Telematics and IoT Module]
    BMS --> THERMAL[Battery Thermal Management]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The block diagrams above use the **Mermaid flowchart** syntax to map the high-level functional flow of the vehicle. The relationships (arrows) represent *signal/electrical/power flow* between modules — not physical positioning. This is the standard architectural representation used in KTU-style design and analysis questions.

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
*Classify two-wheelers based on engine displacement as per CMVR, India. Mention the specific power and license requirements for each sub-category.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Two-wheelers are classified by the **Central Motor Vehicles Rules (CMVR), 1989** into two sub-categories based on engine displacement:

1. **L1 Category — Mopeds:** Engine displacement ≤ **50 cc**. Maximum power output restricted to **4 kW** (for electric equivalents). Top speed typically **≤ 45 km/h**. A *non-transport* driving license is sufficient, and vehicles with ≤ 250 W rated power and ≤ 25 km/h top speed do not require registration.
2. **L2 Category — Motorcycles/Scooters:** Engine displacement > **50 cc**, with no upper limit prescribed by the L2 classification (though commercial motorcycles rarely exceed 800 cc in India). Power output ranges from 5 kW to over 100 kW. A valid **MCWG (Motorcycle With Gear)** license is mandatory.

*Distribution: 1 mark for L1, 1 mark for L2, 1 mark for license/power comparison.*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**
*Explain the difference between 'delta' and 'tadpole' configurations in three-wheeled vehicles. Which one is dominant in the Indian market and why?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Delta Configuration:** One wheel at the *front* and two wheels at the *rear*, forming a triangle with the front apex. The single front wheel handles steering, while the rear axle carries the load.
- **Tadpole Configuration:** Two wheels at the *front* and one at the *rear*. Used in high-performance recreational vehicles (e.g., Can-Am Ryker) but **not** in mass-market Indian vehicles.

**Dominance in India:** The **delta configuration** is overwhelmingly dominant in India (used in all auto-rickshaws and goods carriers) because:
1. The **mechanical simplicity** of single-front-wheel steering keeps the bill of materials low.
2. The **rear axle** can be designed as a simple live axle with leaf springs — easy to manufacture, repair, and replace in rural areas.
3. The **cost-effectiveness** aligns with the price-sensitive Indian market.

*Distribution: 1 mark for delta, 1 mark for tadpole, 1 mark for Indian dominance reasoning.*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice) (1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [Compulsory Choice]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the major types of two-wheelers manufactured in India. Compare the frame design and ergonomics of a *straddle-type motorcycle* and a *step-through scooter*.

**(b) [7 Marks)** A commuter motorcycle has the following specifications: Engine displacement = 150 cc, Rated power = 11.5 kW @ 8500 rpm, Kerb weight = 142 kg. Calculate (i) the power-to-weight ratio, (ii) the brake mean effective pressure (BMEP) at peak power, and (iii) the energy delivered per stroke at peak torque (assume peak torque = 12.5 N·m @ 6000 rpm, 4-stroke engine).

---

**Model Answer — Question A:**

**(a) [7 Marks] Major Types of Two-Wheelers in India:**

The Indian two-wheeler market comprises the following major types:

1. **Mopeds** (≤ 50 cc): Entry-level, lightweight. Example: TVS XL 100, Hero Pleasure.
2. **Standard/Commuter Motorcycles** (100 – 150 cc): Workhorses of Indian roads. Example: Hero Splendor Plus, Bajaj Platina.
3. **Sport-Commuter Motorcycles** (150 – 250 cc): Styling + performance. Example: Bajaj Pulsar NS200, Yamaha FZ-X.
4. **Scooters** (110 – 160 cc): Step-through, CVT transmission. Example: Honda Activa 6G, TVS Jupiter.
5. **Cruiser Motorcycles** (350 – 650 cc): Low seat, relaxed ergonomics. Example: Royal Enfield Classic 350, Meteor 350.
6. **Adventure Tourers** (200 – 450 cc): Off-road capable. Example: Royal Enfield Himalayan, KTM 390 Adventure.
7. **Electric Two-Wheelers**: Battery-powered, BLDC/PMSM motor. Example: Ola S1 Pro, Ather 450X, TVS iQube.

**Frame Design Comparison:**

| Parameter | Straddle-Type Motorcycle | Step-Through Scooter |
|---|---|---|
| **Frame Type** | Diamond / Tubular Backbone / Perimeter | Pressed Steel Underbone |
| **Riding Position** | Rider sits *astride* the tank | Rider sits on a *saddle*, feet on floorboard |
| **Foot Position** | On footpegs (rear-set or mid-set) | On a flat central floorboard |
| **Fuel Tank** | Mounted between knees (high CoG contribution) | Under-seat storage replaces tank |
| **Engine Position** | Centrally mounted, part of stressed member | Hanging from the frame on a swingarm pivot |
| **Ground Clearance** | Higher (170 – 220 mm) | Lower (140 – 170 mm) |
| **Primary Use** | Long-distance, performance, varied terrain | Urban commuting, short errands |

*Distribution: 2 marks for listing types, 3 marks for frame comparison, 1 mark for ergonomic comparison, 1 mark for diagram/sketch description.*

**(b) [7 Marks] Numerical Problem Solution:**

**Given:**
- $V_{\text{disp}}$ = 150 cc = $150 \times 10^{-6}$ m³
- $P_{\text{peak}}$ = 11.5 kW @ $N_p$ = 8500 rpm
- $m$ = 142 kg
- $T_{\text{peak}}$ = 12.5 N·m @ $N_t$ = 6000 rpm
- 4-stroke engine

**(i) Power-to-Weight Ratio:**

$$ \text{PWR} = \frac{P_{\text{peak}}}{m} = \frac{11.5}{142} $$

$$ \text{PWR} = 0.081 \text{ kW/kg} \quad \text{or} \quad 81 \text{ W/kg} $$

**(ii) Brake Mean Effective Pressure (BMEP):**

The relationship between power, BMEP, displacement, and speed for a 4-stroke engine is:

$$ P = \frac{\text{BMEP} \times V_{\text{disp}} \times N}{2 \times 60} $$

Rearranging for BMEP:

$$ \text{BMEP} = \frac{2 \times 60 \times P}{V_{\text{disp}} \times N} $$

Substituting values:

$$ \text{BMEP} = \frac{120 \times 11.5 \times 10^3}{150 \times 10^{-6} \times 8500} $$

$$ \text{BMEP} = \frac{1{,}380{,}000}{1.275} $$

$$ \text{BMEP} = 1{,}082{,}353 \text{ Pa} \approx 10.82 \text{ bar} $$

**(iii) Energy Delivered Per Stroke at Peak Torque:**

Energy per power stroke = Work done per power stroke = Torque × Angular displacement per stroke.

For a 4-stroke engine, one power stroke occurs every 2 crank revolutions (720°).

Work per power stroke:

$$ W = T \times 4\pi \quad [\text{since one power stroke} = 2 \text{ revolutions} = 4\pi \text{ radians}] $$

$$ W = 12.5 \times 4\pi $$

$$ W = 50\pi \approx 157.08 \text{ J} $$

Alternative derivation using crank angle:

$$ W = T \times \theta = 12.5 \times (4\pi) = 157.08 \text{ J} $$

**Final Tabulated Results:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| PWR | 0.081 kW/kg (81 W/kg) |
| BMEP | 10.82 bar |
| Energy per power stroke | 157.08 J |

*Distribution: 2 marks for (i), 3 marks for (ii) [with 1 mark for the formula], 2 marks for (iii).*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify three-wheelers used in India based on (i) wheel configuration, (ii) application, and (iii) powertrain. Discuss why the delta configuration is dominant, with reference to stability, cost, and manufacturability.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare a passenger auto-rickshaw (delta, 145 cc petrol, kerb weight 280 kg, 5.5 kW power) and an electric rickshaw (1.5 kW BLDC motor, kerb weight 320 kg, 60 V 100 Ah battery). Compute: (i) PWR of both, (ii) total energy stored in the e-rickshaw battery, and (iii) the theoretical range of the e-rickshaw if the average energy consumption is 50 Wh/km.

---

**Model Answer — Question B:**

**(a) [7 Marks] Classification of Three-Wheelers in India:**

**(i) Wheel Configuration:**
- **Delta:** One front + two rear wheels (dominant in India).
- **Tadpole:** Two front + one rear wheel (rare; sports vehicles only).

**(ii) Application:**
- **Passenger Auto-Rickshaw:** 3+1 seating, public transport.
- **Goods Carrier (Load Auto):** Cargo deck, last-mile logistics.
- **E-Rickshaw (L5M):** Electric passenger vehicle.
- **E-Cart (L5N):** Electric goods carrier.
- **Specialty Vehicles:** Ambulance, food truck, mobile workshop.

**(iii) Powertrain:**
- **ICE (Petrol/CNG/LPG):** 4-stroke SI, single cylinder, 145 – 600 cc.
- **ICE (Diesel):** 4-stroke CI, single cylinder, 400 – 600 cc (load carriers).
- **Electric:** BLDC or PMSM motor, 1 – 15 kW, with Li-ion or lead-acid battery.

**Why Delta Dominates:**

1. **Stability:** The two rear wheels provide a *wide track width* (typically 1.0 – 1.2 m), giving a low **tip-over angle** and inherent static stability even under load.
2. **Cost:** A single steering knuckle at the front is mechanically far simpler (and cheaper) than two front-wheel steering assemblies. The rear axle is a *simple beam axle* with leaf springs.
3. **Manufacturability:** Indian MSMEs and large OEMs can produce delta three-wheelers with **minimal automation**, using conventional fabrication (welding, sheet metal forming). This is a critical factor in Tier-2 and Tier-3 cities.
4. **Serviceability:** Spares are widely available; the simpler layout means even roadside mechanics can service the drivetrain.

*Distribution: 2 marks for (i)+(ii), 2 marks for (iii), 3 marks for the dominance discussion.*

**(b) [7 Marks] Numerical Comparison:**

**Given:**

*Passenger Auto (ICE):*
- $P_{\text{ICE}}$ = 5.5 kW
- $m_{\text{ICE}}$ = 280 kg

*E-Rickshaw (Electric):*
- $P_{\text{elec}}$ = 1.5 kW
- $m_{\text{elec}}$ = 320 kg
- $V_{\text{batt}}$ = 60 V
- $C_{\text{batt}}$ = 100 Ah
- Consumption = 50 Wh/km

**(i) Power-to-Weight Ratios:**

For the ICE auto:

$$ \text{PWR}_{\text{ICE}} = \frac{5.5}{280} = 0.0196 \text{ kW/kg} \approx 0.020 \text{ kW/kg} $$

For the E-rickshaw:

$$ \text{PWR}_{\text{elec}} = \frac{1.5}{320} = 0.00469 \text{ kW/kg} \approx 0.0047 \text{ kW/kg} $$

**Interpretation:** The ICE auto has a PWR ~4× higher than the E-rickshaw, indicating better acceleration and grade-climbing ability, but at the cost of tailpipe emissions.

**(ii) Energy Stored in the E-Rickshaw Battery:**

$$ E_{\text{batt}} = V_{\text{batt}} \times C_{\text{batt}} $$

$$ E_{\text{batt}} = 60 \text{ V} \times 100 \text{ Ah} = 6000 \text{ Wh} = 6.0 \text{ kWh} $$

**(iii) Theoretical Range of E-Rickshaw:**

$$ \text{Range} = \frac{E_{\text{batt}}}{\text{Consumption}} = \frac{6000 \text{ Wh}}{50 \text{ Wh/km}} $$

$$ \text{Range} = 120 \text{ km} $$

**Final Tabulated Results:**

| Parameter | ICE Auto | E-Rickshaw |
|---|---|---|
| PWR (kW/kg) | 0.020 | 0.0047 |
| Battery Energy | N/A | 6.0 kWh |
| Theoretical Range | N/A | 120 km |

*Distribution: 2 marks for (i), 2 marks for (ii), 2 marks for (iii), 1 mark for interpretation/comparison.*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (and Where You Lose Marks):**
>
> 1. **Confusing L1 with L2 / L5 with L7:** The L-category classification is a *legal* definition under CMVR. Mixing them up costs **at least 1 mark per sub-question**.
> 2. **Forgetting the 50 cc threshold:** Always state the *boundary value* (≤ 50 cc vs. > 50 cc) explicitly. Vague answers like "small engine bikes" fetch zero marks.
> 3. **Mixing up Delta and Tadpole:** Students often swap the wheel positions. The **single-wheel side is the front** in delta; the **two-wheel side is the front** in tadpole. Drawing it wrong in a diagram costs 2 marks.
> 4. **Unit Mistakes in PWR:** Writing "kW/kg" as "kg/kW" is a *dimensional* error and costs marks. Always cross-check units.
> 5. **Skipping the "Why" in Design Questions:** When asked "why delta dominates in India," merely *stating* the fact without justifying with **stability/cost/manufacturability** is a partial answer. KTU 2024 marking scheme allocates 50% weightage to justification.
> 6. **Missing the 4-stroke factor of 2 in BMEP:** The formula $P = (\text{BMEP} \times V \times N) / (2 \times 60)$ has the factor of 2 because one power stroke per **two** revolutions in a 4-stroke. Forgetting it gives BMEP = 21.6 bar (wrong) instead of 10.8 bar (correct).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — High-Density Summary:**

- ✅ Two and three-wheelers are classified under **L1, L2, L5 (and L7)** categories of CMVR.
- ✅ The **50 cc threshold** is a *legal and engineering* boundary — below = moped, above = motorcycle.
- ✅ **Two-wheeler types:** Moped, Commuter, Performance, Scooter, Cruiser, Adventure, Sport, Electric.
- ✅ **Three-wheeler types:** Passenger auto, Goods carrier, E-rickshaw, E-cargo, Specialty.
- ✅ **Delta (1F + 2R)** is the dominant Indian three-wheeler layout due to low cost, high stability, and simple manufacturing.
- ✅ **Tadpole (2F + 1R)** is reserved for high-performance recreational three-wheelers.
- ✅ **Power-to-Weight Ratio (PWR)** = $P / m$ [kW/kg] is the key performance metric.
- ✅ **BMEP formula** for 4-stroke: $P = (\text{BMEP} \times V_{\text{disp}} \times N) / 120$ — *remember the factor of 2*.
- ✅ Two-wheelers are **dynamically stable** (rider balances); three-wheelers are **statically stable** (tripod base).
- ✅ Indian two-wheeler market is **ICE-dominated but rapidly electrifying** (Ola, Ather, TVS, Bajaj Chetak).
- ✅ Three-wheeler electrification is the **fastest-growing segment** in Indian EV adoption (FAME-II subsidies).
- ✅ **Engine power for 50 cc 4-stroke** ≈ 3 kW (peak); for 150 cc ≈ 8 – 12 kW (peak).
- ✅ For 4-stroke engines, **one power stroke per 2 crank revolutions** (4π radians).
- ✅ Lean angle for a turning two-wheeler: $\tan(\theta) = v^2 / (R \cdot g)$ where $g$ = **9.81 m/s²**.
- ✅ Battery energy: $E = V \times Q$ [Wh]; Range = Energy / Consumption [km].

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** When asked to "list and explain types," always **group them** (e.g., by displacement, by design, by powertrain) and give **at least one Indian example per type**. Examiners reward structured, example-rich answers — disorganized lists lose marks even if the content is correct.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Frame and Chassis Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT631 - TWO AND THREE WHEELED VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Frame and Chassis Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of two and three-wheeled vehicles, the **chassis** is the complete structural framework upon which all major automotive components (engine, suspension, wheels, body, and rider/passenger load) are mounted. The **frame** is the load-bearing skeleton — a network of structural members (tubes, pressed panels, or cast sections) — that transmits all static and dynamic loads from the vehicle mass and road input to the suspension and wheels.

For motorcycles and scooters, the chassis is essentially synonymous with the frame, while for three-wheelers (auto-rickshaws, load carriers) the chassis typically includes a floor pan, side members, and cross members.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** The frame must be lightweight yet possess sufficient **stiffness** (to maintain wheel alignment and handling geometry) and **strength** (to resist failure under impact and fatigue loads). It must also accommodate the engine, fuel tank, battery, and provide ergonomically positioned mounting points for the rider.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the frame of a motorcycle as the **human skeleton**. Just as your skeleton:
- Holds your organs (engine, tank, battery) in place,
- Provides attachment points for muscles and joints (suspension, steering, swingarm),
- Maintains your shape so you can stand and move correctly,

…the motorcycle frame does the same for the vehicle. If the skeleton is weak, the body collapses. If it is too heavy, the body cannot move efficiently. The frame is a **carefully engineered balance** between rigidity, strength, and lightness.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industry Metric:** Modern motorcycle frames target a weight of **6–12 kg** for a 150–250 cc class bike, while still delivering torsional rigidity in the range of **2000–6000 N·m/deg**. Three-wheeler chassis weigh significantly more (35–80 kg) due to load-carrying demands.

### 1.3 Classification of Two-Wheeler Frames

| Frame Type | Construction | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Tubular Frame** | Welded steel tubes | Most motorcycles (Royal Enfield, KTM) |
| **Pressed Steel (Dual Cradle)** | Sheet metal stampings welded | Entry-level commuters (Hero Splendor) |
| **Trellis Frame** | Welded steel tube lattice | Sport bikes (Ducati, KTM Duke) |
| **Perimeter Frame** | Large-diameter main tubes forming a perimeter | Supersport bikes (Yamaha R15) |
| **Spine / Backbone Frame** | Single large rectangular tube running under the tank | Scooters (Honda Activa) |
| **Diamond Frame** | Twin down-tubes forming a closed loop | Most modern street bikes |
| **Space Frame** | 3D lattice of small tubes | Prototypes, premium sports |

> [!NOTE]
> **Three-Wheeler Chassis:** Auto-rickshaws typically use a **ladder-type chassis** with two long side members and several cross members, similar to a scaled-down car chassis, mounted on a three-wheel arrangement (one front + two rear, or two front + one rear).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Requirements of a Two/Three-Wheeler Frame

The frame must satisfy a non-negotiable set of engineering demands. Each requirement translates directly into design parameters.

1. **Static Load Carrying** — must support sprung weight (vehicle mass excluding wheels) plus rider and payload without yielding.
2. **Dynamic Load Absorption** — must absorb road shocks, braking forces, and cornering loads without transmitting harmful vibrations.
3. **Torsional Rigidity** — must resist twisting about the longitudinal axis; critical for maintaining wheel alignment during cornering.
4. **Lateral Rigidity** — must resist sideways bending; affects handling stability.
5. **Longitudinal Rigidity** — resists bending along the length; affects acceleration/braking response.
6. **Crashworthiness** — must deform progressively to absorb impact energy during collision.
7. **Anti-Vibration** — natural frequencies must be tuned away from engine firing frequency (typically 25–250 Hz range).
8. **Ergonomic Packaging** — mounting points for engine, swingarm, steering head, footpegs, seat, tank must align with rider triangle.

### 2.2 Frame Design Parameters (Why Each Matters)

- **Wheelbase (L)** — Distance between front and rear wheel contact patches. Affects stability, maneuverability, and weight distribution. Typical: 1200–1450 mm for motorcycles.
- **Steering Head Angle (Caster / Rake, φ)** — Angle of the steering axis from vertical. Typical: 22°–28°. Smaller angle → more stable but heavier steering; larger angle → quicker steering.
- **Trail (t)** — Horizontal distance from steering axis intersection with ground to front contact patch. Typical: 80–120 mm.
- **Ground Clearance** — Minimum vertical distance between lowest frame point and road. Typical: 150–200 mm.
- **Lean Angle (Max)** — Maximum banking angle achievable without ground scraping. Determined by frame, footpeg, and exhaust positioning. Sport bikes: 50°–57°.
- **Seat Height** — Distance from ground to seat top. Affected by frame height.

### 2.3 Loads Acting on the Frame

A motorcycle frame sees **five principal load cases**:

1. **Vertical Bending** — due to gravity load of sprung mass.
2. **Torsion** — uneven road inputs (pothole on one side) cause twisting.
3. **Braking Torsion** — front brake torque reaction transferred through steering head.
4. **Lateral Bending** — cornering forces at tire contact patches.
5. **Drive Reaction** — chain drive thrust during acceleration creates a longitudinal pull.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are **high-yield** for KTU ESE questions. Master these — they appear almost every semester in some form.

| # | Formula | Description / Variable Definitions |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{M \cdot c}{I} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | Bending stress; $M$ = bending moment (N·m), $c$ = distance from neutral axis (m), $I$ = second moment of area (m⁴), $Z$ = section modulus (m³) |
| 2 | $\tau = \dfrac{T \cdot r}{J}$ | Torsional shear stress in circular shaft; $T$ = torque (N·m), $r$ = outer radius (m), $J$ = polar moment of inertia (m⁴) |
| 3 | $\sigma_v = \sqrt{\sigma_b^{2} + 3\tau^{2}}$ | Von Mises equivalent stress for combined bending and torsion |
| 4 | $J = \dfrac{\pi}{32}\left(D^{4} - d^{4}\right)$ | Polar moment of inertia for hollow circular tube; $D$ = outer diameter, $d$ = inner diameter |
| 5 | $Z = \dfrac{\pi}{32}\dfrac{D^{4} - d^{4}}{D}$ | Section modulus for hollow circular tube |
| 6 | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{L_e^{2}}$ | Euler's critical buckling load; $E$ = Young's modulus, $L_e$ = effective length |
| 7 | $\theta = \dfrac{T \cdot L}{G \cdot J}$ | Angle of twist (radians); $G$ = shear modulus, $L$ = length |
| 8 | $K_t = \dfrac{\sigma_{max}}{\sigma_{nominal}}$ | Stress concentration factor (dimensionless) |
| 9 | $\tan\phi = \dfrac{t}{r_{w}}$ | Steering head geometry; $\phi$ = rake, $t$ = trail, $r_w$ = wheel radius |
| 10 | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | Natural frequency of frame vibration; $k$ = stiffness, $m$ = mass |

### 2.5 Material Selection for Frames

| Material | Young's Modulus E (GPa) | Density (kg/m³) | Specific Stiffness (E/ρ) | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mild Steel | 200 | 7850 | 25.5 | Standard commuter frames |
| Chrome-Moly Steel (CrMo, 4130) | 205 | 7850 | 26.1 | Racing/motocross frames |
| Aluminum Alloy (6061-T6) | 69 | 2700 | 25.6 | Lightweight sport bikes |
| Aluminum Alloy (7017-T6) | 70 | 2850 | 24.6 | Premium frames |
| Carbon Fiber Composite | 70–150 (longitudinal) | 1600 | 43–94 | High-end sport/racing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Insight:** Even though aluminum has only **1/3 the stiffness of steel**, its **specific stiffness** is comparable. This is why aluminum frames can match steel frames in stiffness while being **40–50% lighter** — a critical mass saving that improves power-to-weight ratio.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Frame and chassis design principles are applied in:
- **OEM product development** (Honda, Yamaha, TVS, Bajaj)
- **Motorsport engineering** (MotoGP, Isle of Man TT — where stiffness tuning per corner is critical)
- **Electric two-wheeler design** (battery placement stresses frame design heavily)
- **Three-wheeler commercial vehicles** (Piaggio Ape, Bajaj RE — load optimization essential)
- **Defense and off-road vehicles** (Royal Enfield Himalayan, KTM Adventure — impact resistance key)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Bending Stress in a Frame Tube

Consider a down-tube of a motorcycle frame loaded as a **simply supported beam** between the steering head and the swingarm pivot. The rider and vehicle mass acts as a distributed load, with engine mass as a concentrated load.

**Step 1: Identify the bending moment at the critical section.**

Let the down-tube carry a combined vertical load $W$ (rider + vehicle weight share) acting at its midpoint over a span $L$.

$$M_{max} = \frac{W \cdot L}{4}$$

**Step 2: Calculate section modulus for a hollow circular tube.**

For a tube with outer diameter $D$ and wall thickness $t$, the inner diameter is $d = D - 2t$.

$$Z = \frac{I}{c} = \frac{1}{c} \cdot \frac{\pi}{64}\left(D^{4} - d^{4}\right) = \frac{\pi}{32} \cdot \frac{D^{4} - d^{4}}{D}$$

**Step 3: Apply the flexure formula.**

$$\sigma_b = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{\dfrac{W L}{4}}{\dfrac{\pi}{32}\dfrac{D^{4} - d^{4}}{D}}$$

After simplifying:

$$\sigma_b = \frac{8 W L D}{\pi \left(D^{4} - d^{4}\right)}$$

**Step 4: Equate to allowable stress and solve for D.**

Setting $\sigma_b = \sigma_{allow}$ (typically 60–70% of yield strength for safety), and given $d = D - 2t$:

$$\sigma_{allow} = \frac{8 W L D}{\pi \left[D^{4} - (D - 2t)^{4}\right]}$$

This implicit equation is solved iteratively for $D$ given $t$, $W$, and $L$.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A motorcycle frame down-tube is modeled as a simply supported beam with effective span $L = 0.5$ m. The vertical load it carries is $W = 1500$ N. Using a hollow steel tube with outer diameter $D = 30$ mm and wall thickness $t = 2$ mm, calculate the maximum bending stress. Take $E = 200$ GPa.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate inner diameter.**

$$d = D - 2t = 30 - 2(2) = 26 \text{ mm} = 0.026 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Maximum bending moment at mid-span.**

$$M_{max} = \frac{W L}{4} = \frac{1500 \times 0.5}{4} = 187.5 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 3: Polar moment of inertia.**

$$J = \frac{\pi}{32}\left(D^{4} - d^{4}\right)$$

Computing each term (in m⁴):

$$D^{4} = (0.030)^{4} = 8.10 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^{4}$$

$$d^{4} = (0.026)^{4} = 4.57 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^{4}$$

$$J = \frac{\pi}{32}\left(8.10 \times 10^{-7} - 4.57 \times 10^{-7}\right) = \frac{\pi}{32} \times 3.53 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$J = 3.466 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^{4}$$

**Step 4: Section modulus.**

$$Z = \frac{J}{D/2} = \frac{3.466 \times 10^{-8}}{0.015} = 2.311 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{3}$$

**Step 5: Maximum bending stress.**

$$\sigma_b = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{187.5}{2.311 \times 10^{-6}} = 81.13 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 6: Safety check against yield.**

For mild steel, $\sigma_y \approx 250$ MPa. Factor of Safety (FoS):

$$\text{FoS} = \frac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_b} = \frac{250}{81.13} \approx 3.08$$

This is acceptable (FoS > 2.5 for static loads, > 1.5 for dynamic).

### 3.3 Derivation: Torsional Rigidity of the Frame

The torsional rigidity of a motorcycle frame is defined as:

$$K_{torsion} = \frac{T}{\theta}$$

where $T$ is the applied torque about the longitudinal axis (e.g., from cornering asymmetry), and $\theta$ is the angle of twist.

For a closed-section tubular frame (e.g., trellis), using the angle of twist formula:

$$\theta = \frac{T \cdot L}{G \cdot J}$$

Therefore:

$$K_{torsion} = \frac{G \cdot J}{L}$$

**Implication:** Doubling the tube diameter $D$ increases $J$ by a factor of 16 (since $J \propto D^{4}$), dramatically increasing rigidity with minimal weight gain — this is why designers prefer larger-diameter thin-walled tubes.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Design Choices and Their Consequences

| Design Choice | Effect on Stiffness | Effect on Weight | Effect on Cost | Effect on Handling |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Larger diameter tube (D ↑) | ↑↑ (∝ D⁴) | ↑ (∝ D²) | ↑ | More stable, less agile |
| Thicker wall (t ↑) | ↑ (linear) | ↑ | ↑ | Marginal change |
| Switch steel → aluminum | ↓ (E/3) | ↓↓ (–40%) | ↑ | Marginal change |
| Trellis vs. Spine | ↑ (multiple load paths) | ↑ | ↑ (more welds) | Sharper feedback |
| Lower frame height | ↓ (shorter lever arm) | ↓ | ↓ | Lower CG → better cornering |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Two-Wheeler Frame Design Process

The following diagram illustrates the engineering workflow for designing a motorcycle frame, mapping inputs, analysis stages, and outputs as required in a typical OEM development cycle.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vehicle Design Brief] --> B[Define Targets: Wheelbase, Mass, Power]
    B --> C[Conceptual Layout: Rider Triangle, Wheelbase, CG]
    C --> D[Select Frame Type: Trellis / Spine / Perimeter]
    D --> E[Set Mounting Points: Steering Head, Engine, Swingarm, Suspension]
    E --> F[Apply Load Cases: Static, Dynamic, Braking, Cornering]
    F --> G[Build FE Model: Mesh, BCs, Material Properties]
    G --> H[Static Analysis: Bending, Torsion, Combined Stress]
    H --> I[Modal Analysis: Natural Frequencies, Mode Shapes]
    I --> J{Validate vs. Targets?}
    J -- No --> K[Modify Geometry / Wall Thickness]
    K --> G
    J -- Yes --> L[Prototype Manufacturing]
    L --> M[Rig Testing: Stiffness, Fatigue]
    M --> N[Production Release]
```

### 4.2 Functional Component Map: Motorcycle Frame Major Nodes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Steering Head] --> B[Top Tube]
    A --> C[Down Tube]
    B --> D[Rear Subframe]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Swingarm Pivot]
    C --> F[Engine Mount Front]
    F --> G[Engine Mount Rear]
    G --> E
    D --> H[Seat Mount]
    H --> I[Pillion Footpeg Mount]
    C --> J[Rider Footpeg Mount]
    E --> K[Rear Suspension Linkage]
    A --> L[Front Fork Clamp]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this:** Each block represents a major frame mounting node or member junction. The arrows denote load transfer paths. For example, braking force enters at the **Front Fork Clamp** → transfers up the **Steering Head** → distributes through the **Down Tube** to the engine mounts and the **Swingarm Pivot**.

### 4.3 Frame Type Comparison Schematic (Functional View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Frame Selection] --> B{Load Path Strategy}
    B -- Single central member --> C[Spine / Backbone Frame]
    B -- Twin side rails --> D[Diamond / Dual Cradle]
    B -- Multiple triangulated tubes --> E[Trellis Frame]
    B -- Large perimeter loop --> F[Perimeter Frame]
    C --> G[Used in: Scooters, Commuters]
    D --> H[Used in: Entry Commuters]
    E --> I[Used in: Sport / Naked Bikes]
    F --> J[Used in: SuperSport, Race]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For three-wheelers**, the analogous decision tree begins with axle configuration: **1F+2R** (single front, like Bajaj RE) → typically uses a tubular backbone with a rigid rear axle; **2F+1R** (two front, like Piaggio Ape) → uses a pressed-steel ladder chassis similar to small cars.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
*List any three types of motorcycle frames and briefly state one merit of each.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Trellis Frame:** Made by welding small-diameter tubes in a triangulated pattern. *Merit:* High strength-to-weight ratio due to multiple load paths distributing stress efficiently.
2. **Pressed Steel (Dual Cradle) Frame:** Uses stamped sheet-metal sections welded together. *Merit:* Low manufacturing cost; suitable for mass-produced commuter motorcycles.
3. **Perimeter Frame:** Large-diameter tubes form a closed perimeter loop around the engine. *Merit:* Excellent torsional rigidity for high-power sport applications.
4. **Spine / Backbone Frame:** A single large rectangular tube runs centrally under the fuel tank. *Merit:* Allows easy engine removal for servicing; common in scooters.

*(Any three with merits — 1 mark each. Full marks for clarity and correctness.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*Define "trail" in a two-wheeler and explain its effect on handling stability.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Trail is the horizontal distance on the ground between the point where the steering axis (extended) meets the ground and the actual front tire contact patch.

$$t = r_w \cdot \tan\phi \quad \text{(approx.)}$$

where $r_w$ is the front wheel radius and $\phi$ is the steering head angle (rake).

**Effect on Handling:**
- **Positive trail** (typical 80–120 mm) creates a self-centering torque when the front wheel is deflected, giving **directional stability** at speed.
- **Excessive trail** makes steering heavy and slow to respond (sluggish turn-in).
- **Insufficient trail** causes instability, wobble, and poor high-speed tracking.

*(Definition: 1 mark; formula: 1 mark; effect explanation: 1 mark.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Question A (14 Marks):**
**(a)** With neat sketches, classify the major types of frames used in two-wheelers. Discuss the merits and demerits of **trellis** and **spine (backbone)** frames. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A motorcycle down-tube carries a vertical static load of 1800 N at its midpoint over an effective span of 0.45 m. The tube is hollow steel with outer diameter 32 mm and wall thickness 2.5 mm. Calculate:
(i) Maximum bending moment, (ii) Maximum bending stress, and (iii) Factor of safety, given yield strength $\sigma_y = 280$ MPa. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

Two-wheeler frames are classified as:

1. **Tubular Frame** – Welded steel tubes; versatile, good strength.
2. **Pressed Steel / Dual Cradle Frame** – Stamped sheet metal; economical.
3. **Trellis Frame** – Lattice of small triangulated tubes; high rigidity.
4. **Perimeter Frame** – Large tubes forming a perimeter; used in racing.
5. **Spine / Backbone Frame** – Single central rectangular tube; scooter-style.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Trellis Frame | Spine (Backbone) Frame |
|---|---|---|
| Construction | Multiple small tubes, welded in triangulated pattern | Single large rectangular tube |
| Rigidity | Very high (multiple load paths) | Moderate (single load path) |
| Weight | Moderate | Light |
| Cost | High (more welds) | Low |
| Repair | Difficult | Easy |
| Application | Sport, naked bikes | Scooters, commuters |

*(Sketch: 2 marks; classification: 2 marks; comparison: 3 marks.)*

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $W = 1800$ N, $L = 0.45$ m, $D = 0.032$ m, $t = 0.0025$ m, $\sigma_y = 280$ MPa.

**Step (i): Maximum bending moment.**

$$M_{max} = \frac{W L}{4} = \frac{1800 \times 0.45}{4} = 202.5 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step (ii): Maximum bending stress.**

Inner diameter:

$$d = D - 2t = 0.032 - 2(0.0025) = 0.027 \text{ m}$$

Second moment of area:

$$I = \frac{\pi}{64}\left(D^{4} - d^{4}\right)$$

$$D^{4} = (0.032)^{4} = 1.0486 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^4$$

$$d^{4} = (0.027)^{4} = 5.3144 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^4$$

$$I = \frac{\pi}{64}\left(1.0486 \times 10^{-6} - 5.3144 \times 10^{-7}\right) = 2.540 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^4$$

Section modulus:

$$Z = \frac{I}{D/2} = \frac{2.540 \times 10^{-8}}{0.016} = 1.5875 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$$

Maximum bending stress:

$$\sigma_b = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{202.5}{1.5875 \times 10^{-6}} = 127.6 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step (iii): Factor of safety.**

$$\text{FoS} = \frac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_b} = \frac{280}{127.6} = 2.19$$

Since FoS > 2, the design is acceptable for static loading. ✓

*(Each step: 2 marks + 1 mark for FoS interpretation.)*

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**
**(a)** Explain the various **loads acting on a motorcycle frame** during typical operation. Discuss how each load case influences frame design. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A hollow aluminum frame member has outer diameter 40 mm, inner diameter 34 mm. It experiences a torque of 250 N·m over a length of 0.8 m. Calculate the angle of twist. Take $G = 26$ GPa for aluminum. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Principal Loads on a Motorcycle Frame:**

1. **Vertical (Bending) Load:** Self-weight + rider + payload + dynamic vertical acceleration (bumps, jumps). *Influence:* Determines section modulus required for bending stress; affects fatigue life.

2. **Torsional Load:** Caused by unequal vertical forces at front and rear wheels (cornering, one-wheel bump). *Influence:* Drives the choice of closed cross-section tubes and trellis geometry to enhance torsional rigidity.

3. **Braking Load:** Front brake torque reaction transferred to the steering head. *Influence:* Steering head region must be reinforced; fork clamp design is critical.

4. **Lateral (Cornering) Load:** Centripetal force at tire contact patches. *Influence:* Determines lateral bending stress; affects swingarm pivot and engine mount design.

5. **Drive (Acceleration) Reaction:** Chain or shaft drive thrust. *Influence:* Creates longitudinal bending; rear frame section must resist pull from chain tension.

6. **Crash / Impact Load:** Sudden deceleration during collision. *Influence:* Frame must have progressive deformation zones for energy absorption.

*(Each load + influence: ~1 mark each, totaling 6 marks; concluding design impact statement: 1 mark.)*

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $D = 0.040$ m, $d = 0.034$ m, $T = 250$ N·m, $L = 0.8$ m, $G = 26 \times 10^9$ Pa.

**Step 1: Polar moment of inertia.**

$$J = \frac{\pi}{32}\left(D^{4} - d^{4}\right)$$

$$D^{4} = (0.040)^{4} = 2.56 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^4$$

$$d^{4} = (0.034)^{4} = 1.336 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^4$$

$$J = \frac{\pi}{32}\left(2.56 \times 10^{-6} - 1.336 \times 10^{-6}\right) = \frac{\pi}{32}\left(1.224 \times 10^{-6}\right)$$

$$J = 1.201 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 2: Angle of twist.**

$$\theta = \frac{T \cdot L}{G \cdot J} = \frac{250 \times 0.8}{26 \times 10^{9} \times 1.201 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$\theta = \frac{200}{3122.6} = 0.0641 \text{ rad}$$

Converting to degrees:

$$\theta = 0.0641 \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 3.67^\circ$$

*(Formula: 2 marks; J calculation: 2 marks; θ calculation: 2 marks; units & conversion: 1 mark.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors** — Students frequently forget to convert mm to m when computing $I$ and $Z$. Always work in SI units from the start.
> 2. **Confusing $I$ and $J$** — $I$ is the second moment of area (for bending); $J$ is the polar second moment (for torsion). For circular sections, $J = 2I$.
> 3. **Using $D$ instead of $D/2$** in section modulus — remember $Z = I/c$, where $c$ is the **distance from the neutral axis to the outer fiber**.
> 4. **Omitting the FoS interpretation** — KTU examiners specifically award 1 mark for stating whether the design is "safe" or "unsafe" based on the calculated FoS.
> 5. **Forgetting the safety factor requirement** — Static design FoS should be ≥ 2.5; dynamic/fatigue design FoS ≥ 1.5 (per ARAI/ISO standards).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Frame** = load-bearing structure; **Chassis** = complete framework including mounting points and sub-systems.
- ✅ Five major frame types: **Tubular, Pressed Steel, Trellis, Perimeter, Spine/Backbone**.
- ✅ Three-wheeler chassis: typically **ladder-type** for 2F+1R; **backbone** for 1F+2R.
- ✅ Bending stress formula: $\sigma_b = M/Z$; Section modulus for hollow tube: $Z = \pi(D^4 - d^4)/(32D)$.
- ✅ Polar moment for hollow circular tube: $J = \pi(D^4 - d^4)/32$.
- ✅ Von Mises stress: $\sigma_v = \sqrt{\sigma_b^2 + 3\tau^2}$ — used when bending and torsion act together.
- ✅ **Torsional rigidity** of frame ≈ $GJ/L$; increases **16×** when diameter is doubled.
- ✅ **Wheelbase, rake, trail, ground clearance, lean angle** are the key geometric design parameters.
- ✅ Materials: steel (cheap, strong), aluminum (light, similar specific stiffness), CrMo (stronger steel), composites (lightest, expensive).
- ✅ Five load cases: vertical bending, torsion, braking, lateral cornering, drive reaction — must all be checked.
- ✅ Euler buckling: $P_{cr} = \pi^2 E I / L_e^2$ — relevant for slender frame members under compression.
- ✅ Stress concentration factor $K_t$ — must be considered at welded joints and tube intersections.
- ✅ Typical FoS: **≥ 2.5** (static), **≥ 1.5** (dynamic/fatigue); **3–4** for safety-critical frame members.
- ✅ Natural frequency $f_n = (1/2\pi)\sqrt{k/m}$ — must be tuned away from engine firing frequency to avoid resonance.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Suspension Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT631 - TWO AND THREE WHEELED VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Suspension Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of two and three-wheeled vehicles, a **Suspension System** is a mechanical assembly of springs, dampers (shock absorbers), and linkages that connects the wheel assembly to the main frame/chassis. Its primary functions are:

1. To isolate the **sprung mass** (frame + rider + engine) from road irregularities.
2. To maintain tire contact with the road surface for effective traction, steering, and braking.
3. To support the static weight of the vehicle.
4. To absorb and dissipate the kinetic energy of road shocks as heat.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In two-wheelers, the suspension is typically a *de-coupled* system — the front and rear systems are independent and operate on separate kinematic principles. This is a high-frequency exam topic.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Mountain Climber's Boots**
> Imagine walking barefoot on a rocky trail — every pebble jars your spine. Now imagine wearing a cushioned sports shoe. The shoe's foam *compresses* (spring action) and the air inside *slowly leaks out* when compressed (damper action). The suspension on a motorcycle works exactly the same way: the **spring** stores the bump energy temporarily, and the **damper** slowly releases it as heat, preventing an oscillatory bounce.

- **Sprung Mass** → the mass supported by the springs (frame, engine, rider). Measured in **kg**.
- **Unsprung Mass** → mass not supported by springs (wheel, tire, brake, part of fork). Measured in **kg**.
- **Spring Rate (K)** → force required to compress the spring by unit distance. Measured in **N/m**.
- **Damping Coefficient (C)** → resistance force per unit velocity. Measured in **N·s/m**.

### 1.3 Why Suspension is Critical for 2W/3W

Unlike a four-wheeler, a two-wheeler must satisfy **two simultaneous dynamic requirements**: comfort *and* stability during cornering. A poorly damped suspension causes:
- **Brake dive** (front diving under braking)
- **Acceleration squat** (rear sinking under acceleration)
- **Wobble / Tank-slapper** (oscillatory instability of the front fork)
- **Loss of tire contact** → reduced grip → accidents

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Types of Suspension in Two and Three Wheelers

**A. Front Suspension Types**

| Type | Description | Application |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| **Telescopic Fork (USD/Cartridge type)** | Hydraulic damper concentric with coil spring; sliding stanchions inside slider tubes. | Almost all modern motorcycles |
| **Trailing Link (BMW Telelever-like)** | Wheel hub on a trailing arm; separates steering from suspension duty. | Concept / niche 2W |
| **Leading Link** | Wheel hub on a leading arm pivoted ahead of the wheel. | Older bicycles, mopeds |
| **Spring-Loaded Leading Link** | Used in Indian scooters (e.g., Bajaj Chetak, TVS Jupiter). | Indian gearless scooters |

**B. Rear Suspension Types**

| Type | Description |
|------|-------------|
| **Swingarm + Monoshock** | A single shock absorber mounted centrally, often progressive. |
| **Swingarm + Twin Shock (Dual)** | Two separate shock absorbers on either side — classic design. |
| **Swingarm + Unit Swing (with engine as stressed member)** | Engine itself acts as a structural member of the swingarm. |
| **Scooter-type Engine Mounted Trailing Arm** | Engine pivots to provide rear suspension travel. |

### 2.2 Operational Logic — The Spring–Damper Pair

- **Step 1 (Spring):** When a bump strikes the wheel, the spring compresses, storing energy as **Elastic Potential Energy (E = ½ Kx²)**.
- **Step 2 (Damper):** The hydraulic damper converts this kinetic energy into heat by forcing oil through calibrated orifices. Without the damper, the system would oscillate indefinitely.
- **Step 3 (Recovery):** The spring extends, pushing the wheel back to its rest position with the damper controlling the rebound velocity.
- **Why Both?** A spring *alone* gives bouncy ride; a damper *alone* gives a harsh ride. The **parallel combination** gives comfort *and* control.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Concept — "Critical Damping"**
> The damping coefficient **C** is tuned to be slightly *less* than critical damping (ζ ≈ 0.25 to 0.4 for 2W). This allows the suspension to settle within 1–1.5 oscillations after a bump.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $K = \dfrac{F}{x}$ | Spring rate (Hooke's Law) | N/m |
| 2 | $E_{spring} = \tfrac{1}{2} K x^{2}$ | Energy stored in a compressed spring | Joules (J) |
| 3 | $\omega_{n} = \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{m}}$ | Natural angular frequency of undamped system | rad/s |
| 4 | $f_{n} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{K}{m}}$ | Natural frequency in Hz | Hz |
| 5 | $\zeta = \dfrac{C}{2\sqrt{K m}}$ | Damping ratio (dimensionless) | — |
| 6 | $C_{c} = 2\sqrt{K m}$ | Critical damping coefficient | N·s/m |
| 7 | $C = \zeta \cdot C_{c} = 2\zeta\sqrt{K m}$ | Actual damping coefficient | N·s/m |
| 8 | $\omega_{d} = \omega_{n}\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}$ | Damped natural frequency | rad/s |
| 9 | $F_{damper} = C \cdot v$ | Damper force | N |
| 10 | $K_{eq,\ parallel} = K_{1} + K_{2}$ | Springs in parallel | N/m |
| 11 | $K_{eq,\ series} = \dfrac{K_{1} K_{2}}{K_{1} + K_{2}}$ | Springs in series | N/m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pipe Symbol Substitution Note:** In the formulas above, every absolute value or division bar is rendered using LaTeX (`\vert`, `\mid`, `\dfrac`) so the markdown table syntax remains intact. Do **not** use the literal `|` character inside table cells — it breaks KTU's PDF parser.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The same spring-damper model is used in:
- **Automotive suspensions** (cars, trucks, buses)
- **Aircraft landing gear** (with oleo-pneumatic dampers)
- **Building seismic isolators** (base-isolation against earthquakes)
- **Robotics and prosthetics** (active suspension control)
- **Spacecraft payload launch isolation** (NASA's vibration table design)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Natural Frequency of a Spring–Mass System

**Given:** A mass *m* attached to a spring of stiffness *K* and a damper of coefficient *C*, oscillating vertically.

**Free Body Diagram Equations (textual description):**
- The mass experiences three vertical forces: weight (*mg*, ignored in oscillation), spring restoring force (*−Kx*), and damper viscous force (*−C(dx/dt)*).

**Step-by-step derivation:**

Newton's second law applied to the mass:
$$m\ddot{x} = -Kx - C\dot{x}$$

Rearranging to standard form:
$$m\ddot{x} + C\dot{x} + Kx = 0$$

Divide the entire equation by *m*:
$$\ddot{x} + \dfrac{C}{m}\dot{x} + \dfrac{K}{m}x = 0$$

Compare with the standard second-order ODE:
$$\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_{n}\dot{x} + \omega_{n}^{2}x = 0$$

By term-matching:
$$\omega_{n}^{2} = \dfrac{K}{m} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \omega_{n} = \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{m}}$$

$$2\zeta\omega_{n} = \dfrac{C}{m} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \zeta = \dfrac{C}{2\sqrt{K m}}$$

Converting angular frequency to Hz:
$$f_{n} = \dfrac{\omega_{n}}{2\pi} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{K}{m}}$$

> **Physical interpretation:** A *stiffer* spring (higher *K*) raises natural frequency → bumpier ride. A *heavier* vehicle (higher *m*) lowers natural frequency → smoother ride.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Critical Damping Coefficient

**Definition:** Critical damping is the *minimum* damping that prevents any oscillation. At critical damping, ζ = 1.

**Derivation:**
$$\zeta = 1 \quad\Rightarrow\quad 1 = \dfrac{C_{c}}{2\sqrt{K m}}$$

Solving for $C_{c}$:
$$C_{c} = 2\sqrt{K m}$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):**

> *A 180 kg motorcycle + rider (sprung mass) is supported by a front fork with effective spring rate **40000 N/m**. Calculate: (a) natural frequency, (b) critical damping coefficient, (c) damping coefficient for ζ = 0.3.*

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Natural Frequency:**
$$f_{n} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{K}{m}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{40000}{180}}$$

$$f_{n} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{222.22} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}(14.907) = 2.373 \text{ Hz}$$

**Part (b) — Critical Damping:**
$$C_{c} = 2\sqrt{K m} = 2\sqrt{40000 \times 180} = 2\sqrt{7.2 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$C_{c} = 2 \times 2683.28 = 5366.56 \text{ N·s/m}$$

**Part (c) — Damping Coefficient at ζ = 0.3:**
$$C = \zeta \cdot C_{c} = 0.3 \times 5366.56 = 1610.0 \text{ N·s/m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Matrix:** For a 180 kg motorcycle, ideal ride frequency lies in the range **1.5 – 2.5 Hz**. Lower than 1 Hz → motion sickness. Higher than 3 Hz → harsh ride.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Suspension Stiffness in Parallel (e.g., Twin Shock Rear)

For a motorcycle with two identical rear shock absorbers, each with spring rate $K_1$:

$$K_{eq} = K_{1} + K_{2} = 2K_{1} \quad (\text{since } K_{1} = K_{2})$$

**Practical implication:** A twin-shock rear suspension is *twice as stiff* as a single shock of the same spring rate. Hence, twin shocks use *softer* individual springs (around 0.5× the rate of a monoshock) to achieve the same overall stiffness.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Anti-Dive Geometry Effect

**Anti-dive percentage** is defined as the fraction of braking load transferred *through the front suspension* rather than through the link geometry:

$$\%\text{Anti-Dive} = \dfrac{\text{Dive moment resisted by geometry}}{\text{Total braking dive moment}} \times 100$$

For a typical telescopic fork with rake angle $\alpha$ and trail *t*:
$$\text{Anti-dive ratio} \approx \dfrac{t \cdot \cos\alpha}{h} \times 100\%$$

where *h* is the height of the center of gravity. A typical motorcycle achieves **30% – 50% anti-dive** to balance stability and feel.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Telescopic Front Fork

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef input fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,color:#0d47a1
    classDef comp fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#bf360c
    classDef output fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#1b5e20

    ROAD[Road Irregularity / Bump]:::input
    TIRE[Tire and Wheel Hub - Unsprung Mass]:::comp
    SLIDER[Lower Slider Tube - Outer Body]:::comp
    SPRING[Coil Spring - Energy Storage]:::comp
    DAMPER[Hydraulic Damper - Oil Orifice]:::comp
    STANCH[Upper Stanchion Tube - Connects to Triple Tree]:::comp
    TRIPLE[Triple Tree / Yoke - Connects to Frame]:::comp
    FRAME[Main Frame - Sprung Mass]:::output
    RIDER[Rider - Sprung Mass]:::output

    ROAD --> TIRE
    TIRE --> SLIDER
    SLIDER --> SPRING
    SLIDER --> DAMPER
    SPRING --> STANCH
    DAMPER --> STANCH
    STANCH --> TRIPLE
    TRIPLE --> FRAME
    FRAME --> RIDER

    subgraph FORK_ASSEMBLY[Telescopic Fork Assembly]
        SLIDER
        SPRING
        DAMPER
        STANCH
    end
```

> **How to read this diagram:** The bump travels upward from the road through the *unsprung mass* (tire/wheel) into the fork. Inside the fork, the spring stores the energy and the damper dissipates it. The cleaned, damped force is then transmitted to the *sprung mass* (frame + rider).

### 4.2 Free Body Diagram — Vertical Forces on a Compressed Spring-Damper

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef force fill:#ffebee,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#7f0000
    classDef body fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,color:#01579b

    M[Mass m moving downward]:::body
    K1[Spring Force Fs = Kx upward]:::force
    C1[Damping Force Fd = C v upward]:::force
    W1[Weight mg downward]:::force

    M -- exerts --> K1
    M -- exerts --> C1
    M -- exerts --> W1
```

**Equation of motion (vertical):**
$$m\ddot{x} = mg - Kx - C\dot{x}$$

At static equilibrium, $\ddot{x} = 0$ and $\dot{x} = 0$, hence static deflection:
$$x_{static} = \dfrac{mg}{K}$$

This is the *ride-height drop* of the motorcycle due to its own weight.

### 4.3 Schematic of Rear Swingarm Suspension (Monoshock)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef frame fill:#e8eaf6,stroke:#283593,color:#1a237e
    classDef link fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#ff8f00,color:#e65100
    classDef wheel fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,color:#4a148c

    FRAME2[Main Frame]:::frame
    PIVOT[Pivot Point at Frame]:::frame
    SWING[Swingarm - Aluminum Alloy]:::link
    SHOCK[Monoshock with Progressive Spring]:::link
    DAMP2[Hydraulic Damper Unit]:::link
    HUB[Rear Wheel Hub]:::wheel
    TIRE2[Rear Tire]:::wheel

    FRAME2 --> PIVOT
    PIVOT --> SWING
    SWING --> SHOCK
    SHOCK --> DAMP2
    DAMP2 --> FRAME2
    SWING --> HUB
    HUB --> TIRE2
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define "sprung mass" and "unsprung mass" with reference to a motorcycle. Why is minimizing unsprung mass important?**

**Model Answer:**

- **Sprung Mass:** The portion of the vehicle's weight that is supported by the suspension springs. It includes the frame, engine, fuel tank, and rider.
- **Unsprung Mass:** The portion NOT supported by the springs — i.e., the wheel, tire, brake assembly, and lower portion of the fork/swingarm.

**Importance of minimizing unsprung mass:**
1. It directly affects the tire's ability to maintain contact with the road.
2. Lower unsprung mass → faster response to road bumps → better grip.
3. High unsprung mass causes the wheel to "skip" over bumps, reducing traction and worsening handling.
4. Typical target: unsprung mass should be less than **10% of sprung mass** for optimal performance.

*(3 marks: 1 mark each for definitions + 1 mark for the importance list)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] What is a damping ratio? State its significance in two-wheeler suspension design.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Damping Ratio (ζ):** It is the ratio of actual damping coefficient *C* to the critical damping coefficient $C_c$:
$$\zeta = \dfrac{C}{C_{c}} = \dfrac{C}{2\sqrt{K m}}$$

- **Significance:**
  1. **ζ = 0:** No damping — system oscillates forever.
  2. **ζ = 1:** Critical damping — fastest return without overshoot.
  3. **ζ < 1:** Underdamped — oscillations decay over time.
  4. **ζ > 1:** Overdamped — slow return, no oscillation.

- For motorcycles, ζ is typically chosen between **0.25 and 0.4** (slightly underdamped) to allow quick settling while filtering road noise.

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 2 marks significance)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **Telescopic Front Fork Suspension** used in modern motorcycles. List its advantages over leading-link suspension. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A motorcycle of sprung mass **220 kg** is supported on a rear monoshock of effective spring rate **60000 N/m**. Compute:
1. Static deflection of the spring.
2. Natural frequency of vibration.
3. Critical damping coefficient.
4. Damping coefficient for a damping ratio of 0.3. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**(a) Telescopic Fork — Construction and Working:**

A telescopic fork consists of two parallel tube assemblies mounted on the front wheel axle at the bottom and to the triple-tree (yoke) at the top.

**Main Components:**
1. **Stanchion tube (inner, upper):** Chrome-plated steel tube of ~30–41 mm diameter.
2. **Slider tube (outer, lower):** Seals the damper oil and houses the damping mechanism.
3. **Coil spring:** Progressive or linear wound around the damper rod.
4. **Damper rod + piston:** Moves through oil-filled chamber; oil passes through orifices to provide damping.
5. **Triple clamp (Top yoke and Bottom yoke):** Connects the fork to the steering head.
6. **Axle clamp:** At the slider bottom, holds the front wheel.

**Working Principle:**
- When the wheel hits a bump, the slider moves *upward* relative to the stanchion.
- The spring compresses, storing energy.
- The piston inside the damper rod forces oil through a *rebound orifice* (during compression) and a *compression orifice* (during extension).
- The viscous resistance of oil flow dissipates energy as heat.
- The fork then returns smoothly to its original position.

**Advantages over Leading-Link:**
1. **Better rigidity** → improved handling and cornering precision.
2. **Lower unsprung mass** → superior bump absorption.
3. **Simpler construction** → easier maintenance.
4. **Better aerodynamics** (streamlined tubes).
5. **Allows larger brake disc** mounting near the wheel axle.

*(7 marks: 2 for diagram explanation, 3 for working, 2 for advantages)*

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $m = 220$ kg, $K = 60000$ N/m, $\zeta = 0.3$.

**1. Static Deflection:**
$$x_{static} = \dfrac{mg}{K} = \dfrac{220 \times 9.81}{60000}$$

$$x_{static} = \dfrac{2158.2}{60000} = 0.03597 \text{ m} \approx 36 \text{ mm}$$

**2. Natural Frequency:**
$$\omega_{n} = \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{m}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{60000}{220}} = \sqrt{272.73} = 16.51 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$f_{n} = \dfrac{\omega_{n}}{2\pi} = \dfrac{16.51}{2\pi} = 2.628 \text{ Hz}$$

**3. Critical Damping Coefficient:**
$$C_{c} = 2\sqrt{K m} = 2\sqrt{60000 \times 220} = 2\sqrt{13{,}200{,}000}$$

$$C_{c} = 2 \times 3633.18 = 7266.36 \text{ N·s/m}$$

**4. Damping Coefficient at ζ = 0.3:**
$$C = \zeta \cdot C_{c} = 0.3 \times 7266.36 = 2180 \text{ N·s/m}$$

*(7 marks: 1+2+2+2 marks distributed as above)*

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**(a)** Differentiate between **leading-link, trailing-link, and telescopic front suspensions** in two-wheelers. State one application of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **anti-dive geometry** in a motorcycle. How does it affect braking performance? A motorcycle has a rake angle of 27° and trail of 95 mm. If the CG height is 540 mm, estimate the percentage anti-dive. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**(a) Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Leading Link | Trailing Link | Telescopic |
|-----------|--------------|---------------|------------|
| Pivot Location | Ahead of wheel | Behind wheel | In-line with wheel |
| Wheel Path | Moves rearward on bump | Moves forward on bump | Moves vertically only |
| Steering Effect | Steers the wheel (anti-dive built-in) | Steers the wheel | No steering effect |
| Unsprung Mass | Moderate | Moderate | Lowest |
| Rigidity | Low | Moderate | Highest |
| Maintenance | Moderate | Moderate | Easy |
| Application | Mopeds (Luna, Mobylette) | Concept bikes, BMW Telelever | All modern motorcycles |

*(7 marks: 5 for comparison + 1 for applications + 1 for neatness)*

**(b) Anti-Dive Geometry:**

**Concept:** Anti-dive geometry refers to the design of the front suspension linkages such that during braking, a portion of the braking torque creates a moment that *opposes* the natural tendency of the front fork to compress (i.e., dive). This is achieved through the geometric arrangement of the fork, axle, and frame.

**How it works:**
- When brakes are applied, weight transfers to the front, causing the fork to compress.
- In telescopic forks, the caliper-mounted fork slider moves upward.
