- The geometry is designed so that the brake torque reaction at the axle creates a moment about the steering axis that lifts the front, partially resisting dive.

**Effects on Braking:**
1. Reduces excessive front-end dive → more stable braking.
2. Maintains the rider's posture — less forward pitching.
3. Improves fork alignment and feedback.
4. Trade-off: Too much anti-dive can cause "fork-jacking" (the front lifts too much under hard braking, reducing grip).

**Numerical Estimation:**

Given: Rake angle $\alpha = 27°$, Trail $t = 95$ mm $= 0.095$ m, CG height $h = 540$ mm $= 0.54$ m.

$$\%\text{Anti-Dive} \approx \dfrac{t \cdot \cos\alpha}{h} \times 100$$

$$\%\text{Anti-Dive} = \dfrac{0.095 \times \cos(27°)}{0.54} \times 100$$

$$\%\text{Anti-Dive} = \dfrac{0.095 \times 0.8910}{0.54} \times 100 = \dfrac{0.08465}{0.54} \times 100$$

$$\%\text{Anti-Dive} \approx 15.68\%$$

> **Result:** The motorcycle achieves approximately **15.7% anti-dive**, which is on the lower side. Most sport motorcycles target 25–40% for optimal feel.

*(7 marks: 2 for concept, 2 for effects, 3 for numerical)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students often confuse the *rake angle* with the *steering head angle*. Rake is measured from the vertical; head angle is from the horizontal. They sum to 90°.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** In the natural frequency formula, students sometimes write $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{m}{K}}$ — this is **inverted**. Always remember: **K on top, m on bottom**.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Forgetting to convert mm to m in numerical problems (95 mm vs 0.095 m). This single error can cost **2–3 marks** in a 7-mark problem.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Confusing "anti-dive" with "anti-squat". Anti-dive is for the front under *braking*; anti-squat is for the rear under *acceleration*.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Suspension = Spring (energy storage) + Damper (energy dissipation).**
- [x] **Sprung mass** = frame + rider + engine. **Unsprung mass** = wheel + tire + brake + lower fork/slider.
- [x] **Telescopic fork** is the dominant front suspension in modern 2W/3W.
- [x] **Rear suspension** types: Swingarm + Monoshock (most common), Twin shock (older bikes), Trailing arm (scooters).
- [x] **Spring rate formula:** $K = F/x$. Energy stored: $E = \tfrac{1}{2} K x^{2}$.
- [x] **Natural frequency:** $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{K/m}$. Ideal range: **1.5 – 2.5 Hz** for 2W.
- [x] **Damping ratio:** $\zeta = C / (2\sqrt{K m})$. For 2W: $\zeta = 0.25$ to $0.4$.
- [x] **Critical damping:** $C_c = 2\sqrt{K m}$.
- [x] **Static deflection** = $mg / K$ — this is the *ride-height drop*.
- [x] **Anti-dive geometry** reduces front fork compression under braking; 25–40% is typical.
- [x] **Springs in parallel** (e.g., dual rear shocks) *add* stiffness: $K_{eq} = K_1 + K_2$.
- [x] **Springs in series** *reduce* effective stiffness: $K_{eq} = (K_1 K_2) / (K_1 + K_2)$.
- [x] **Lower unsprung mass** → better tire contact → improved safety.
- [x] **Unit conversions** are critical: mm → m, kg → N (multiply by 9.81), etc.
- [x] **Exam tip:** For any 14-mark question, always draw a labelled diagram — it fetches 2–3 easy marks even if the rest is incomplete.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steering Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT631 - TWO AND THREE WHEELED VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Steering Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Steering System (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A steering system in a two/three-wheeled vehicle is a **mechanical assembly** that converts the rider's input torque at the handlebar (or steering wheel in three-wheelers) into a precisely controlled **angular displacement** of the front wheel(s), enabling directional control, lateral stability, and self-centering behaviour.

In a **motorcycle (two-wheeler)**, steering is achieved via a **handlebar–fork–steering head** arrangement pivoting about an inclined axis known as the **steering axis (rake axis)**. In **three-wheeled vehicles (3WV)**, steering follows either the **tadpole** (two-front-one-rear) or **delta** (one-front-two-rear) layout, employing either motorcycle-style handlebar steering or automotive-style **rack-and-pinion / Ackermann steering**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🛵 Real-World Analogy — "The Shopping Trolley Wheel"**
>
> Imagine pushing a **shopping trolley** whose front wheels are mounted on a **tilted, vertical pin**. When you push forward, the wheels naturally trail behind the pin's contact point and **self-centre** when you let go. Now imagine adding a **handlebar on top of that pin** so you can twist it left or right. That is essentially what a motorcycle steering system is — a tilted pivot that converts rotational handlebar motion into wheel turn, while *gravity and geometry* keep the bike stable when you release the bar.

For a **three-wheeled tadpole vehicle**, think of it as a **"car minus one wheel"** — it uses a familiar automotive steering rack, but the geometry must compensate for the missing fourth wheel to avoid tire scrub.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Parameters (Standard KTU Metrics)

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rake (Caster) Angle | $\alpha$ | 24° – 32° | degrees |
| Steering Head Angle | $\phi$ | 60° – 66° | degrees |
| Trail (Mechanical) | $t$ | 70 – 130 | mm |
| Wheelbase | $L$ | 1200 – 1600 | mm |
| Steering Ratio (3WV) | $SR$ | 14:1 – 18:1 | — |
| Steering Damper Damping Coeff. | $C_d$ | 5 – 25 | N·m·s/rad |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Trail Matters:**
> Trail is the **horizontal distance** between the tyre contact patch and the point where the steering axis intersects the ground. A **larger trail** → greater high-speed stability but heavier steering effort. A **smaller trail** → lighter steering but reduced straight-line stability (wobble prone).

---

## 1.4 Sub-Classifications of Steering Systems in 2W & 3WV

1. **Handlebar Steering (Motorcycle / Scooter)** — Single tilted steering axis.
2. **Hub-centre Steering (HCS)** — Steering axis passes through wheel hub (e.g., Bimota Tesi, Yamaha GTS1000).
3. **Telescopic Fork Steering** — Most common; fork tubes slide in yokes.
4. **Rake-and-Trail Steering Geometry** — Defines stability behaviour.
5. **Tadpole Configuration Steering** — Two steered front wheels using Ackermann linkage.
6. **Delta Configuration Steering** — Single front wheel (motorcycle-style) + 2 rear wheels.
7. **Rack-and-Pinion Steering (3WV Automotive-type)** — Used in enclosed three-wheelers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations of Two-Wheeler Steering Geometry

### 2.1.1 Rake Angle (α)
The **rake angle** (also called *caster angle* in motorcycle terminology) is the inclination of the steering axis from the **vertical**, measured in the plane of the wheel.

$$\alpha = 90^\circ - \phi$$

where $\phi$ is the **steering head angle** (angle between steering axis and horizontal ground plane).

### 2.1.2 Trail (t)
Trail is the **moment arm** that produces the self-centring torque. It is calculated as:

$$t = \frac{r}{\sin\phi} - r_f \cos\phi$$

Where:
- $r$ = radius of front tyre (m)
- $r_f$ = offset of fork axis from wheel centre (typically small, often ignored)
- $\phi$ = steering head angle (rad)

For simplification when $r_f \approx 0$:

$$t \approx \frac{r}{\sin\phi}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning of Trail:**
> Trail acts like a **caster wheel on a shopping trolley**. When the wheel is displaced from straight-ahead, the contact patch falls behind the steering pivot, creating a **restoring moment** that pulls the wheel back to centre.

### 2.1.3 Self-Centring Torque (Stability Moment)
The gravitational + gyroscopic restoring moment about the steering axis:

$$M_{sc} = W_f \cdot t \cdot \sin\delta$$

Where:
- $W_f$ = vertical load on front wheel (N)
- $t$ = trail (m)
- $\delta$ = steer angle (rad)

---

## 2.2 Hub-Centre Steering (HCS) Theory

In **Hub-Centre Steering**, the steering axis passes directly through the wheel hub. Result:

$$t \approx 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad M_{sc} \to 0$$

Stability is then provided by a **mechanical steering-damper** + the **weight transfer effect**.

**Counter-steer torque (HCS):**

$$M_{cs} = I_{wheel} \cdot \dot{\omega} - M_{gyro}$$

---

## 2.3 Three-Wheeled Vehicle Steering Geometry

### 2.3.1 Tadpole Layout (Two Steered Front Wheels)
Uses **Ackermann steering geometry** to avoid tire scrub during cornering.

**Ackermann Condition:**

$$\cot\delta_o - \cot\delta_i = \frac{w}{L}$$

Where:
- $\delta_o$ = steer angle of outer wheel
- $\delta_i$ = steer angle of inner wheel
- $w$ = track width (m)
- $L$ = wheelbase (m)

**Percentage Ackermann Error:**

$$\%A_{err} = \frac{(\cot\delta_i - \cot\delta_o) - \frac{w}{L}}{\frac{w}{L}} \times 100$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Standard:** For low-speed 3WV, **100 % Ackermann** is preferred. For high-speed, **60–80 % Ackermann** is used to balance scrub vs. transient response.

### 2.3.2 Delta Layout (Single Front Steered Wheel)
Behaves like a motorcycle in steering, with a **steering axis tilt** identical to motorcycle geometry. However, lateral stability is **compromised** under hard cornering because the rear track resists the roll couple differently.

**Effective Stability Factor (Delta 3WV):**

$$K_{stab} = \frac{W_r \cdot t_{r} - W_f \cdot t_f}{g \cdot h_{cg}}$$

If $K_{stab} > 0$ → **stable**, else prone to roll-over.

---

## 2.4 Steering Damper Dynamics

The **steering damper** is a hydraulic/pot-damped device in parallel with the steering linkage that suppresses wobble/shimmy.

**Equation of Motion for Steering Oscillation:**

$$I_{eq} \ddot{\delta} + C_d \dot{\delta} + K_t \delta = T_{rider}(t) + T_{road}(t)$$

Where:
- $I_{eq}$ = equivalent moment of inertia of steering assembly (kg·m²)
- $C_d$ = damping coefficient (N·m·s/rad)
- $K_t$ = self-aligning stiffness (N·m/rad) ≈ $W_f \cdot t$
- $T_{rider}$ = rider input torque
- $T_{road}$ = road-induced disturbance torque

**Damping Ratio:**

$$\zeta = \frac{C_d}{2\sqrt{K_t \cdot I_{eq}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Rule of Thumb:** $\zeta \approx 0.3 - 0.5$ gives a good balance — enough damping to kill wobble, light enough to allow the rider to feel the road.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\alpha = 90^\circ - \phi$ | Rake angle from steering head angle | degrees |
| 2 | $t = \dfrac{r}{\sin\phi}$ | Mechanical trail | m |
| 3 | $M_{sc} = W_f \cdot t \cdot \sin\delta$ | Self-centring torque | N·m |
| 4 | $\cot\delta_o - \cot\delta_i = \dfrac{w}{L}$ | Ackermann condition | rad |
| 5 | $M_{cs} = I_{wheel} \cdot \dot{\omega} - M_{gyro}$ | Counter-steer torque (HCS) | N·m |
| 6 | $I_{eq}\ddot{\delta} + C_d\dot{\delta} + K_t\delta = T_{net}$ | Steering EOM | N·m |
| 7 | $\zeta = \dfrac{C_d}{2\sqrt{K_t I_{eq}}}$ | Damping ratio | — |
| 8 | $K_{stab} = \dfrac{W_r t_r - W_f t_f}{g h_{cg}}$ | Delta 3WV stability index | — |
| 9 | $\delta_{min} = \arcsin\!\left(\dfrac{R_{min} - \dfrac{w}{2}}{R_{min}}\right)$ | Min. steer angle for turning | rad |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** These equations directly feed into CAD tools like **Adams Motorcycle**, **IPG Moto**, and **BikeSim** for virtual prototyping of 2W/3WV steering.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Trail from Geometric Construction

### Given
- Front wheel radius $r = 0.30$ m
- Steering head angle $\phi = 63^\circ$
- Fork offset $r_f = 0.025$ m

### To Find
Mechanical trail $t$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

The point $A$ is the **wheel ground contact patch**. The point $B$ is where the **steering axis intersects the ground**. The line $AB$ projected along the wheel's longitudinal axis is the trail.

From the **triangle formed by the steering axis, wheel centre, and contact patch**:

$$\sin\phi = \frac{r}{AB}$$

$$AB = \frac{r}{\sin\phi}$$

The horizontal component (along the ground) gives the trail:

$$t = AB \cdot \cos\phi - r_f$$

$$t = \frac{r \cos\phi}{\sin\phi} - r_f = \frac{r}{\tan\phi} - r_f$$

### Substitute values:

$$t = \frac{0.30}{\tan(63^\circ)} - 0.025$$

Compute $\tan(63^\circ)$:

$$\tan(63^\circ) \approx 1.9626$$

$$t = \frac{0.30}{1.9626} - 0.025 = 0.1529 - 0.025$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 0.128 \text{ m } = 128 \text{ mm}}$$

This falls within the typical 70–130 mm band ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Self-Centring Torque and Rider Effort

### Step 1 — Vertical Front Wheel Load

For a stationary motorcycle with rider mass $m_r$ and bike mass $m_b$:

$$W_f = \frac{m_b \cdot g \cdot L_{cg,r} + m_r \cdot g \cdot L_{cg,rider}}{L}$$

Where:
- $L_{cg,r}$ = distance of CG from rear axle
- $L_{cg,rider}$ = distance of CG from rear axle (with rider)

### Step 2 — Self-Centring Moment at Small Steer Angle

$$M_{sc} = W_f \cdot t \cdot \sin\delta$$

For small $\delta$, $\sin\delta \approx \delta$ (rad):

$$M_{sc} \approx W_f \cdot t \cdot \delta$$

### Step 3 — Required Rider Torque

The rider must overcome $M_{sc}$ plus friction $M_f$ and damping $C_d \dot{\delta}$:

$$T_{rider} = M_{sc} + M_f + C_d \dot{\delta}$$

### Worked Example
Given:
- $W_f = 1100$ N
- $t = 0.10$ m
- $\delta = 15^\circ = 0.262$ rad
- $C_d = 15$ N·m·s/rad
- $\dot{\delta} = 0.5$ rad/s
- $M_f = 5$ N·m

Compute:

$$M_{sc} = 1100 \times 0.10 \times \sin(15^\circ) = 1100 \times 0.10 \times 0.2588$$

$$M_{sc} \approx 28.47 \text{ N·m}$$

Damping torque:

$$M_d = 15 \times 0.5 = 7.5 \text{ N·m}$$

Total:

$$T_{rider} = 28.47 + 7.5 + 5 = 40.97 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T_{rider} \approx 41 \text{ N·m}}$$

This is realistic for a 200–250 kg motorcycle at parking speeds.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Ackermann Steer Angles for a Tadpole 3WV

### Given
- Track width $w = 1.40$ m
- Wheelbase $L = 2.00$ m
- Inner wheel steer angle $\delta_i = 20^\circ$

### To Find
Outer wheel steer angle $\delta_o$ and percentage Ackermann.

### Step-by-Step

Ackermann equation:

$$\cot\delta_o = \cot\delta_i - \frac{w}{L}$$

Compute $\cot(20^\circ)$:

$$\cot(20^\circ) = \frac{1}{\tan(20^\circ)} = \frac{1}{0.3640} = 2.7475$$

Compute $w/L$:

$$\frac{w}{L} = \frac{1.40}{2.00} = 0.70$$

Therefore:

$$\cot\delta_o = 2.7475 - 0.70 = 2.0475$$

$$\delta_o = \arccot(2.0475) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{1}{2.0475}\right)$$

$$\delta_o = \arctan(0.4884) = 26.04^\circ$$

### Percentage Ackermann Error Check
**% Ackermann:**

$$\%A = \frac{\cot\delta_i - \cot\delta_o}{w/L} \times 100$$

$$\%A = \frac{2.7475 - 2.0475}{0.70} \times 100 = \frac{0.70}{0.70} \times 100 = 100\%$$

**Result:** This 3WV uses **100 % Ackermann** — ideal for low-speed manoeuvring with zero tyre scrub. ✓

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Damping Ratio for Steering Wobble Suppression

### Given
- $K_t = 50$ N·m/rad (self-aligning stiffness)
- $I_{eq} = 0.5$ kg·m²
- Damper: linear viscous, $C_d = 5$ N·m·s/rad

### Compute Natural Frequency:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{K_t}{I_{eq}}} = \sqrt{\frac{50}{0.5}} = \sqrt{100} = 10 \text{ rad/s}$$

### Compute Damping Ratio:

$$\zeta = \frac{C_d}{2\sqrt{K_t \cdot I_{eq}}} = \frac{5}{2\sqrt{50 \times 0.5}} = \frac{5}{2\sqrt{25}} = \frac{5}{10} = 0.5$$

$$\boxed{\zeta = 0.5 \rightarrow \text{Optimal damping (sub-critical, fast decay)}}$$

This matches KTU-recommended $0.3 < \zeta < 0.5$ for production motorcycles.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Steering Architectures

| Property | Handlebar (Std.) | Hub-Centre (HCS) | Tadpole 3WV | Delta 3WV |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Trail (mm) | 70–130 | ~0 | N/A (rack) | 50–90 |
| Self-centring | Mechanical (trail) | Damper only | Mechanical + steering gear | Mechanical |
| Cornering Stability | High | Medium-High | Very High | Medium |
| High-Speed Wobble Risk | Medium | Low | Low | Medium |
| Steering Effort | Medium | Low | Low (power optional) | Medium |
| Manufacturing Cost | Low | Very High | High | Medium |
| Maintenance | Low | Medium | Medium | Low |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Motorcycle Steering Geometry — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rider Input Torque] --> B[Handlebar Assembly]
    B --> C[Top Triple Clamp Yoke]
    C --> D[Steering Stem Shaft]
    D --> E[Steering Head Bearing Set]
    E --> F[Bottom Triple Clamp Yoke]
    F --> G[Telescopic Fork Tubes]
    G --> H[Front Wheel Axle]
    H --> I[Front Tyre Contact Patch]
    J[Gravity Load Wf] --> I
    I --> K[Mechanical Trail t]
    K --> L[Self-Centring Moment Msc]
    L --> E
    M[Road Disturbance] --> I
    N[Steering Damper] --> E
    O[Frame Pivot Point] --> E
```

**Caption:** Functional flow of rider input → handlebar → triple clamps → bearings → forks → wheel. Trail generates self-centring moment that returns the system to neutral.

---

## 4.2 Tadpole Three-Wheeler Ackermann Steering — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Driver Input Steering Wheel] --> B[Steering Column]
    B --> C[Steering Rack / Pinion Gearbox]
    C --> D[Left Tie Rod]
    C --> E[Right Tie Rod]
    D --> F[Left Knuckle Arm]
    E --> G[Right Knuckle Arm]
    F --> H[Left Front Wheel delta_i]
    G --> I[Right Front Wheel delta_o]
    J[Track Width w] --> K[Ackermann Linkage Geometry]
    K --> H
    K --> I
    L[Wheelbase L] --> K
    M[Compliance Bushings] --> H
    M --> I
```

**Caption:** The pinion moves the rack laterally; tie rods convert this into different steer angles on each side per Ackermann condition to prevent tire scrub.

---

## 4.3 Steering Damper Integration — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Front Wheel Shimmy Disturbance] --> B[Forks]
    B --> C[Steering Axis Pivot]
    C --> D[Torque to Steering Axis]
    D --> E[Steering Damper Hydraulic Piston]
    E --> F[Damping Force Cd times delta_dot]
    F --> G[Reduced Angular Velocity]
    G --> H[Stable Steering Response]
    I[Rider Input Torque] --> C
    J[Trail-Generated Restoring Moment] --> C
```

---

## 4.4 Hub-Centre Steering (HCS) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Handlebar Input] --> B[Steering Lever Arm]
    B --> C[Upper Steering Link]
    C --> D[Central Pivot Bearing]
    D --> E[Lower Steering Link]
    E --> F[Wheel Hub Knuckle]
    F --> G[Front Wheel]
    H[Steering Damper Inline] --> D
    I[No Mechanical Trail] --> J[Damper-Only Stability]
    J --> G
    K[Suspension Link] --> F
    L[Suspension Link] --> G
```

**Caption:** In HCS, the steering axis passes through the hub → trail ≈ 0 → stability is achieved through damper + suspension geometry. Examples: Yamaha GTS1000, Bimota Tesi.

---

## 4.5 Comparison Chart of 2W vs 3WV Steering Types

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Steering Systems for 2W and 3WV] --> A[Two-Wheelers]
    S --> B[Three-Wheelers]
    A --> A1[Standard Handlebar with Telescopic Fork]
    A --> A2[Hub-Centre Steering HCS]
    A --> A3[Bottom-Link Steering]
    B --> B1[Tadpole two front steered wheels]
    B --> B2[Delta one front steered wheel]
    B1 --> C1[Ackermann Geometry]
    B1 --> C2[Rack and Pinion]
    B2 --> D1[Motorcycle-Style Tilted Axis]
    B2 --> D2[Single Steering Head]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "Trail" as applied to motorcycle steering geometry. Mention its typical range.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Trail is the **horizontal distance** along the ground between the point where the **steering axis intersects the ground** and the **tyre contact patch** of the front wheel.

- **Function:** Produces self-centring (restoring) torque to keep the motorcycle in straight-line motion.
- **Typical Range:** **70 mm – 130 mm** for standard motorcycles.
- **Larger trail** → better stability, heavier steering; **Smaller trail** → lighter steering, prone to wobble.

**(Award: 1 mark definition, 1 mark function, 1 mark range & trade-off.)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is Ackermann steering condition? Why is it important in three-wheeled vehicles?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Ackermann condition states that during a turn, the **inner and outer wheel steer angles must differ** such that both wheels rotate about a **common instantaneous centre**.

$$\cot\delta_o - \cot\delta_i = \frac{w}{L}$$

- **Importance:** Prevents **tyre scrub** during cornering, reduces tyre wear, improves manoeuvrability.
- Critical in **tadpole 3WV** with two steered front wheels.

**(Award: 1 mark formula, 1 mark explanation, 1 mark importance.)**

---

## 📘 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> - ❌ **Don't confuse Rake angle ($\alpha$) with Steering Head Angle ($\phi$).** They are complementary: $\alpha = 90^\circ - \phi$.
> - ❌ **Don't forget units** — convert degrees to radians when using small-angle approximations.
> - ❌ **Don't mix up Ackermann with Anti-Ackermann** — Anti-Ackermann is used in racing for better transient response.
> - ❌ **Always state assumptions** (rigid frame, dry road, no rider lean) in derivations to score the **assumptions mark**.
> - ✅ **Draw a free-body diagram** of the front wheel to score the diagram mark (often 2 marks).

---

### 📝 QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q. (a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **steering geometry of a motorcycle**. Define rake angle, steering head angle, and trail. Derive the relationship between trail and steering head angle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A motorcycle has front wheel radius **300 mm**, steering head angle **65°**, and fork offset **20 mm**. Calculate:
- (i) Mechanical trail
- (ii) Self-centring torque when the rider turns the handlebar by **20°** under a front-wheel vertical load of **1200 N**.

**(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

#### Part (a) — Steering Geometry Explanation (7 marks)

**Step 1: Components (2 marks)**

The motorcycle steering system consists of:
1. **Handlebar** — receives rider input torque.
2. **Top yoke (triple clamp)** — clamps the fork tubes; carries handlebar and steering stem.
3. **Steering stem (riser shaft)** — rotates inside the frame head.
4. **Steering head bearings** (tapered roller or angular contact) — provide smooth rotation.
5. **Bottom yoke** — clamps the upper end of fork tubes.
6. **Telescopic fork tubes** — carry the front wheel and provide suspension.
7. **Front axle** — holds the wheel between fork sliders.

**Step 2: Geometry Definitions (3 marks)**

- **Rake angle ($\alpha$):** Angle between steering axis and vertical. Typically **24°–32°**.
- **Steering head angle ($\phi$):** Angle between steering axis and horizontal ground plane. Typically **60°–66°**.
- **Trail ($t$):** Horizontal distance from steering axis–ground intersection to tyre contact patch.

**Step 3: Derivation (2 marks)**

From the triangle formed by the wheel centre $O$, contact patch $A$, and steering-axis-ground intersection $B$:

$$\sin\phi = \frac{r}{OB} \quad \Rightarrow \quad OB = \frac{r}{\sin\phi}$$

Horizontal projection (along ground) gives:

$$t = OB \cos\phi - r_f = \frac{r \cos\phi}{\sin\phi} - r_f = \frac{r}{\tan\phi} - r_f$$

Hence the relationship: **As $\phi$ increases (raked-out bike), trail decreases.** ✓

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $r = 0.300$ m
- $\phi = 65^\circ$
- $r_f = 0.020$ m
- $\delta = 20^\circ = 0.3491$ rad
- $W_f = 1200$ N

**Step 1: Compute trail**

$$\tan(65^\circ) = 2.1445$$

$$t = \frac{0.300}{2.1445} - 0.020 = 0.1399 - 0.020 = 0.1199 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 120 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 2: Compute self-centring torque**

$$M_{sc} = W_f \cdot t \cdot \sin\delta = 1200 \times 0.1199 \times \sin(20^\circ)$$

$$\sin(20^\circ) = 0.3420$$

$$M_{sc} = 1200 \times 0.1199 \times 0.3420$$

$$M_{sc} = 49.21 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\boxed{M_{sc} \approx 49.2 \text{ N·m}}$$

---

### 📝 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the **Ackermann steering geometry** used in tadpole-type three-wheeled vehicles. Discuss the difference between **100 % Ackermann** and **Anti-Ackermann** geometry, and state where each is preferred. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A tadpole three-wheeler has a **track width of 1.50 m** and a **wheelbase of 2.20 m**. If the inner front wheel is steered at **22°**, calculate:
- (i) The required outer wheel steer angle for 100 % Ackermann condition.
- (ii) The percentage Ackermann error if the outer wheel is actually steered at **27°**.

**(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

#### Part (a) — Ackermann Theory (7 marks)

**Step 1: Ackermann Condition (2 marks)**

For a vehicle turning about a common instantaneous centre, the inner wheel must turn through a larger angle than the outer wheel. The geometric condition:

$$\cot\delta_o - \cot\delta_i = \frac{w}{L}$$

**Step 2: 100 % Ackermann (2 marks)**

- Both wheels rotate about a **single common instantaneous centre** → **zero tyre slip/scrub**.
- Preferred in: **low-speed urban 3WV**, delivery vehicles, autorickshaws (when 3-wheeled).
- Trade-off: at high speeds, 100 % Ackermann creates **understeer tendency**.

**Step 3: Anti-Ackermann (2 marks)**

$$\cot\delta_i - \cot\delta_o = \frac{w}{L}$$

- Outer wheel turns more than inner — used in **motorsport and racing 3WV** for:
  - Better initial turn-in response.
  - Increased lateral grip transfer to outer wheel under cornering load.
- Trade-off: **high tyre scrub** at low speed, more tyre wear.

**Step 4: Compromise Geometry (1 mark)**

Most production 3WV use **60–80 % Ackermann** — a balance between scrub and turn-in feel.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $w = 1.50$ m
- $L = 2.20$ m
- $\delta_i = 22^\circ$
- Actual $\delta_o^{actual} = 27^\circ$

**Step 1: Compute $\cot(22^\circ)$**

$$\cot(22^\circ) = \frac{1}{\tan(22^\circ)} = \frac{1}{0.4040} = 2.4751$$

**Step 2: Compute w/L**

$$\frac{w}{L} = \frac{1.50}{2.20} = 0.6818$$

**Step 3: Compute ideal $\delta_o$ for 100 % Ackermann**

$$\cot\delta_o^{ideal} = \cot\delta_i - \frac{w}{L} = 2.4751 - 0.6818 = 1.7933$$

$$\delta_o^{ideal} = \arccot(1.7933) = \arctan(0.5576) = 29.14^\circ$$

$$\boxed{\delta_o^{ideal} \approx 29.14^\circ}$$

**Step 4: Compute % Ackermann error**

$$\%A_{err} = \frac{\cot\delta_i - \cot\delta_o^{actual}}{\frac{w}{L}} \times 100$$

$$\cot(27^\circ) = \frac{1}{\tan(27^\circ)} = \frac{1}{0.5095} = 1.9627$$

$$\%A_{err} = \frac{2.4751 - 1.9627}{0.6818} \times 100 = \frac{0.5124}{0.6818} \times 100 = 75.16\%$$

$$\boxed{\%A_{err} \approx 75.2\% \text{ (below 100\%, so under-Ackermann / neutral)}$$

**Conclusion:** The 3WV uses approximately **75 % Ackermann**, which is within the typical high-speed preference range (60–80 %), and balances scrub vs. cornering response. ✓

---

## 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Steering Systems (Module 1)**

- ⭐ **Steering Axis Tilt** — All motorcycle steering axes are tilted, NOT vertical. This tilt = steering head angle $\phi$.
- ⭐ **Rake + Trail are the heart of motorcycle steering** — they define stability AND effort.
- ⭐ **Larger Trail** → High-speed stability but heavy steering effort at parking.
- ⭐ **Smaller Trail** → Light steering but wobble-prone (death wobble at high speed).
- ⭐ **Hub-Centre Steering** has trail ≈ 0 → relies on damper for stability.
- ⭐ **Ackermann condition** governs **3WV tadpole** geometry to avoid tyre scrub.
- ⭐ **100 % Ackermann** = zero scrub, ideal for **low-speed** vehicles.
- ⭐ **Anti-Ackermann** = used in **racing** for sharper turn-in.
- ⭐ **Steering Damper** = critical for wobble suppression; $\zeta \approx 0.3$–$0.5$ is optimal.
- ⭐ **Delta 3WV** uses motorcycle-style steering → stability index $K_{stab}$ must be positive.
- ⭐ **KTU Formulae to memorize:**
  - $t = \dfrac{r}{\tan\phi} - r_f$
  - $M_{sc} = W_f \cdot t \cdot \sin\delta$
  - $\cot\delta_o - \cot\delta_i = \dfrac{w}{L}$
  - $\zeta = \dfrac{C_d}{2\sqrt{K_t I_{eq}}}$
- ⭐ **Always state assumptions** in derivations (rigid frame, small angle, no rider lean).
- ⭐ **Common mistakes:** Confusing rake vs. head angle; forgetting to convert degrees to radians; using 100 % Ackermann in high-speed contexts.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Powertrain and Engine Technology: Types of Engines (Two-Stroke and Four-Stroke)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT631 - TWO AND THREE WHEELED VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Powertrain and Engine Technology: Types of Engines (Two-Stroke and Four-Stroke)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Powertrain:** A powertrain is the complete system of components that generate, transmit, and deliver mechanical power from the prime mover (engine) to the driving wheels of a vehicle. In two and three-wheeled vehicles, the powertrain is typically simplified and consists of the engine, clutch (or centrifugal automatic clutch), transmission (gearbox), final drive (chain or belt), and the driving wheel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE):** A heat engine in which the combustion of a fuel (typically petrol, diesel, or alternate fuels) occurs with an oxidizer (air) in a confined combustion chamber, which is an integral part of the working fluid flow circuit. The combustion products directly drive the rotating output shaft through a piston-crank mechanism.

**Classification of Engines Used in Two and Three-Wheeled Vehicles:**

| Criterion | Type | Common Usage in 2W/3W |
|---|---|---|
| **Cycle of Operation** | Two-Stroke Engine | Mopeds, entry-level motorcycles, scooters (older) |
| **Cycle of Operation** | Four-Stroke Engine | Modern motorcycles, scooters, three-wheelers (auto-rickshaws) |
| **Fuel Used** | Petrol / Gasoline | Almost universal in India for 2W/3W |
| **Fuel Used** | Diesel | Commercial three-wheelers, cargo auto-rickshaws |
| **Cooling** | Air-Cooled | Most 2W; entry 3W |
| **Cooling** | Liquid-Cooled | Premium motorcycles, KTM Duke, Royal Enfield 650 |
| **Ignition** | Spark Ignition (SI) | All petrol 2W/3W |
| **Ignition** | Compression Ignition (CI) | Diesel three-wheelers |

## 1.2 The Two-Stroke Engine — Concept & Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Two-Stroke Engine (Definition per KTU 2024 Syllabus):** A reciprocating internal combustion engine in which the complete working cycle of suction, compression, expansion (power), and exhaust is completed in **two piston strokes** (one up-stroke and one down-stroke) corresponding to **one full revolution (360°) of the crankshaft**.

**Intuitive Analogy (The Bicycle Pump / Heartbeat):**
Imagine you are using a bicycle pump. You push the handle down (compression), and air rushes out of the tire. Now, you pull the handle back up, and fresh air is drawn in. **In a single complete up-down motion (one revolution), the pump performs two distinct functions.** This is exactly how a two-stroke engine works — **one revolution = one power stroke**. Think of it as the heart of a small animal that beats twice per cycle, completing the entire job in half the time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Characteristic Feature:** A two-stroke engine delivers **one power stroke per revolution**, making it capable of producing theoretically double the power of a four-stroke engine of the same swept volume and speed.

## 1.3 The Four-Stroke Engine — Concept & Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Four-Stroke Engine (Definition per KTU 2024 Syllabus):** A reciprocating internal combustion engine in which the complete working cycle is accomplished in **four piston strokes** (two up-strokes and two down-strokes) corresponding to **two full revolutions (720°) of the crankshaft**. The four distinct strokes are: **Intake, Compression, Power (Expansion), and Exhaust.**

**Intuitive Analogy (The Four-Cylinder Pumping Action):**
Imagine drawing water from a deep well using a bucket on a rope. To fill the bucket, you have to (1) **lower** it down (intake), (2) **lift** it up filled (compression-like action), (3) **pour** the water out (power/useful work), and (4) **send** the empty bucket back down (exhaust). This **four-step process requires two full cycles of the rope** — exactly mirroring the four-stroke engine's logic. **One power stroke is delivered every two revolutions.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 emphasizes understanding the **port-timing diagram**, the role of the **crankcase** in 2-stroke engines, and the **valve-train mechanism** in 4-stroke engines. Both are mandatory in Part B questions.

## 1.4 Why These Engines Dominate the 2W/3W Segment

- **Two-Stroke Advantages:** High power-to-weight ratio, mechanical simplicity, low manufacturing cost, lighter crank assembly, and excellent low-end torque — making it ideal for mopeds and small scooters.
- **Four-Stroke Advantages:** Higher fuel efficiency, lower exhaust emissions, better durability, less frequent refueling — making it the modern standard for motorcycles and three-wheelers under the **BS-VI emission norms** of India (post-2020).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Four-Stroke Spark Ignition (SI) Engine

The four-stroke petrol engine operates on the **Otto Cycle** and completes its thermodynamic processes in four discrete piston strokes:

### Step 1: Intake (Suction) Stroke — 180° Crank Rotation
- The **intake valve opens**; the **exhaust valve remains closed**.
- The piston moves from **Top Dead Center (TDC)** to **Bottom Dead Center (BDC)**, creating a partial vacuum in the cylinder.
- A mixture of petrol vapour and air (or pure air in direct-injection systems) is drawn in through the intake manifold.
- **Crankshaft rotation: 0° → 180°.**

### Step 2: Compression Stroke — Next 180° Crank Rotation
- Both valves are **closed**.
- The piston moves from BDC back to TDC, compressing the charge to a typical **compression ratio of 9:1 to 12:1**.
- The temperature of the charge rises due to adiabatic compression.
- **Crankshaft rotation: 180° → 360°.**

### Step 3: Power (Expansion/Combustion) Stroke — Next 180° Crank Rotation
- Just before TDC, the **spark plug** fires, igniting the compressed mixture.
- Combustion causes a rapid pressure rise (3 MPa to 5 MPa typical), forcing the piston from TDC to BDC.
- This is the **only useful work-producing stroke**.
- Both valves remain closed.
- **Crankshaft rotation: 360° → 540°.**

### Step 4: Exhaust Stroke — Final 180° Crank Rotation
- The **exhaust valve opens**; the intake valve remains closed.
- The piston moves from BDC to TDC, pushing the burnt gases out of the cylinder.
- The cycle repeats.
- **Crankshaft rotation: 540° → 720°.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "4-Stroke" = 2 Revolutions?** Because one power stroke occurs only every 720° of crank rotation, the four-stroke engine is inherently less power-dense than the two-stroke but offers **superior fuel economy and lower unburned hydrocarbon emissions.**

---

## 2.2 Working Principle — Two-Stroke SI Engine

The two-stroke engine achieves all four processes in **one revolution (360°)** by using the **crankcase** as a charging pump and **ports** (instead of poppet valves) for gas exchange.

### Phase 1: Compression + Crankcase Charging (Piston Moving UP, 0° → 180°)
- Piston moves from BDC to TDC.
- The **transfer port and exhaust port are closed** by the piston skirt.
- The fuel-air mixture in the **crankcase** is compressed (typical pressure 0.15 – 0.3 bar above atmospheric).
- Above the piston, the previously drawn charge is being **compressed** in the cylinder.

### Phase 2: Combustion + Power + Scavenging (Piston Moving DOWN, 180° → 360°)
- At TDC, the spark plug fires → combustion occurs → high pressure pushes the piston down.
- As the piston descends, the **exhaust port is uncovered first** (around 25°–30° before BDC) → burnt gases escape.
- Shortly after, the **transfer port is uncovered** → fresh charge from the crankcase rushes into the cylinder, **scavenging** out the residual exhaust gases.
- As the piston moves down, it uncovers the **intake port** (or the reed valve opens) → fresh mixture re-enters the crankcase.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind the Two-Stroke:** By using the crankcase as a pump and ports instead of valves, the engine eliminates the complex valve-train mechanism. However, this comes at the cost of **short-circuiting losses** — some fresh charge escapes directly out of the exhaust port, reducing fuel efficiency and increasing emissions (HC + CO).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examinable in KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination) and Module Internal Tests. Memorize SI units carefully.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Swept Volume (Vs)** | $V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 \cdot L$ | D = Bore, L = Stroke length | m³ |
| 2 | **Clearance Volume (Vc)** | $V_c = \frac{V_s}{(r-1)}$ | r = Compression Ratio | m³ |
| 3 | **Total Cylinder Volume (V1)** | $V_1 = V_s + V_c$ | — | m³ |
| 4 | **Compression Ratio (r)** | $r = \frac{V_1}{V_c} = 1 + \frac{V_s}{V_c}$ | — | Dimensionless |
| 5 | **Brake Power (BP)** | $BP = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60{,}000}$ | N = rpm, T = Torque in N·m | kW |
| 6 | **Indicated Power (IP)** | $IP = \frac{P_m \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot K}{60}$ | Pm = mean effective pressure, A = πD²/4, K = 1 (2S) or 1/2 (4S) | kW |
| 7 | **Friction Power (FP)** | $FP = IP - BP$ | — | kW |
| 8 | **Mechanical Efficiency** | $\eta_{mech} = \frac{BP}{IP}$ | — | % |
| 9 | **Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC)** | $BSFC = \frac{m_f \cdot 3600}{BP \cdot t}$ | mf = fuel mass (kg), t = time (s) | kg/kWh |
| 10 | **Brake Thermal Efficiency** | $\eta_{bth} = \frac{BP \times 3600}{m_f \times CV}$ | CV = Calorific Value (kJ/kg) | % |
| 11 | **Air Standard Efficiency (Otto)** | $\eta_{otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}$ | γ = Cp/Cv ≈ 1.4 for air | % |
| 12 | **Air Standard Efficiency (Diesel)** | $\eta_{diesel} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \frac{\rho^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)}$ | ρ = cut-off ratio | % |
| 13 | **Volumetric Efficiency** | $\eta_{vol} = \frac{\text{Actual mass of air}}{\text{Theoretical mass at atm. density}}$ | — | % |
| 14 | **Mean Piston Speed** | $\bar{V}_p = \frac{2 \cdot L \cdot N}{60}$ | — | m/s |
| 15 | **Number of Power Strokes per Second** | For 4-stroke: $\frac{N}{120}$ ; For 2-stroke: $\frac{N}{60}$ | — | strokes/s |

> [!WARNING]
> **Frequent Student Mistake:** The "K" factor in the IP formula is **K = 1 for two-stroke** (one power stroke per revolution) and **K = 1/2 for four-stroke** (one power stroke per two revolutions). Forgetting this divisor is the most common cause of wrong answers in KTU papers.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Engine Type | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Hero Splendor / Honda Activa | 4-Stroke SI | Fuel economy, BS-VI compliance, low maintenance |
| TVS XL 100 (Moped) | 4-Stroke SI | Light, durable, low cost |
| Older Bajaj Chetak / Vespa | 2-Stroke SI | Lightweight, simple, high torque |
| Bajaj RE Auto (Three-wheeler) | 4-Stroke SI / Diesel | Load-carrying, durability, mileage |
| Royal Enfield Classic 350 | 4-Stroke SI | Low-end torque, refinement |
| KTM Duke 390 | 4-Stroke SI (Liquid-Cooled) | High specific output, premium performance |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Brake Power (BP) From First Principles

> **Problem Setup:** A 2-wheeler's four-stroke petrol engine runs at **3000 rpm** and develops a brake torque of **80 N·m**. Compute the brake power in kW.

### Step 1: Understand the Physical Meaning of Torque
Torque (T) is the tangential force (F, in Newtons) applied at a radius (R, in meters) from the axis of rotation. The work done by this force during one full revolution is:

$$W = F \times \text{distance per revolution} = F \times 2\pi R = T \times 2\pi$$

where the relationship $T = F \times R$ has been substituted. This represents the work done in **one complete revolution of the crankshaft**.

### Step 2: Determine Work Done per Minute
If the crankshaft rotates at **N revolutions per minute (rpm)**, then the work done per minute is:

$$W_{per\, min} = N \times T \times 2\pi \quad [Joules/min]$$

### Step 3: Convert to Work Done per Second (Watts)
Power is defined as work done per unit time. Dividing by 60 seconds per minute:

$$W_{per\, sec} = \frac{N \times T \times 2\pi}{60} \quad [Joules/sec \equiv Watts]$$

### Step 4: Convert Watts to Kilowatts
Since $1 \, kW = 1000 \, W$:

$$BP = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60{,}000} \quad [kW]$$

### Step 5: Substitute the Numerical Values
Given: $N = 3000 \, rpm$ and $T = 80 \, N \cdot m$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
BP &= \frac{2 \times \pi \times 3000 \times 80}{60{,}000} \\
&= \frac{2 \times 3.1416 \times 3000 \times 80}{60{,}000} \\
&= \frac{1{,}508{,}000}{60{,}000} \quad \text{(approximated for clarity)} \\
&= 25.13 \quad [kW]
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Result
$$\boxed{BP \approx 25.13 \, kW \quad \text{or} \quad \approx 33.7 \, hp}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Indicated Power (IP) Using Mean Effective Pressure

> **Problem Setup:** A single-cylinder **two-stroke** engine has bore **70 mm**, stroke **60 mm**, runs at **5000 rpm**, and has a mean effective pressure of **8 bar**. Calculate IP.

### Step 1: Express the Cylinder Cross-Sectional Area (A)
The cylinder is circular, so its area is:

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.070)^2 = 3.848 \times 10^{-3} \quad [m^2]$$

### Step 2: Compute the Swept Volume (Vs)

$$V_s = A \times L = 3.848 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.060 = 2.309 \times 10^{-4} \quad [m^3]$$

### Step 3: Understand Mean Effective Pressure (Pm)
The **mean effective pressure** is a hypothetical constant pressure that, if it acted on the piston throughout the entire power stroke, would produce the same work as the actual varying pressure during the cycle. Its units are **N/m² (Pa)** or **bar** (where 1 bar = 10⁵ Pa).

Given: $P_m = 8 \, bar = 8 \times 10^5 \, N/m^2$.

### Step 4: Work Done per Power Stroke
For one power stroke, the work done is:

$$W = P_m \times A \times L = P_m \times V_s \quad [Joules]$$

### Step 5: Account for the Number of Power Strokes per Second
The factor K is critical:
- **Two-stroke engine:** 1 power stroke per revolution → K = 1 → Power strokes/sec = N/60
- **Four-stroke engine:** 1 power stroke per 2 revolutions → K = 1/2 → Power strokes/sec = N/120

### Step 6: Final IP Formula

$$IP = \frac{P_m \cdot V_s \cdot N \cdot K}{60} = \frac{P_m \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot K}{60} \quad [W]$$

### Step 7: Substitute Values (Two-Stroke, So K = 1)

$$
\begin{aligned}
IP &= \frac{8 \times 10^5 \times 2.309 \times 10^{-4} \times 5000 \times 1}{60} \\
&= \frac{8 \times 10^5 \times 2.309 \times 10^{-4} \times 5000}{60} \\
&= \frac{923{,}600}{60} \\
&= 15{,}393.3 \quad [W] \\
&= 15.39 \quad [kW]
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Result
$$\boxed{IP \approx 15.39 \, kW}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Quick-Reference for Comparison Problems

| Parameter | Two-Stroke Engine | Four-Stroke Engine |
|---|---|---|
| Power strokes per revolution | **1** | **1/2** (one per two revolutions) |
| Crank revolutions per cycle | **1 (360°)** | **2 (720°)** |
| Valve mechanism | **Ports (no poppet valves)** | **Poppet valves (intake + exhaust)** |
| Crankcase role | **Acts as a charging pump** | **Just houses the crankshaft; oil sump** |
| Lubricating oil | **Mixed with fuel (petrol + 2T oil)** | **Separate sump; splash + pump circulation** |
| Weight (per kW) | **Lighter** | **Heavier** |
| Specific power output | **Higher** | **Lower** |
| Fuel efficiency | **Poorer (short-circuiting losses)** | **Better** |
| Emissions (HC, CO) | **Higher** | **Lower** |
| Thermal efficiency | **Lower (~20–25%)** | **Higher (~25–35%)** |
| Maintenance | **Frequent (oil change every 1000 km)** | **Less frequent (every 3000–5000 km)** |
| Noise / Vibration | **Higher** | **Lower** |
| Cooling | **Air-cooled (almost always)** | **Air or liquid-cooled** |
| Examples in 2W | **Yamaha RX100, Bajaj KB100 (legacy)** | **Hero Splendor, Honda CB Shine, RE Classic** |
| Examples in 3W | **Rare today** | **Bajaj RE, Piaggio Ape, Mahindra Alfa** |

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Air-Standard Otto Efficiency

> **Problem:** A 4-stroke SI engine operates on the ideal Otto cycle with compression ratio r = 9 and γ = 1.4. Calculate the air-standard efficiency.

### Step 1: Identify the Otto Cycle Processes
The ideal Otto cycle consists of:
- **Process 1→2:** Isentropic (adiabatic) compression
- **Process 2→3:** Constant-volume heat addition (combustion)
- **Process 3→4:** Isentropic expansion
- **Process 4→1:** Constant-volume heat rejection

### Step 2: Apply the Isentropic Relations
For process 1→2: $T_2 / T_1 = (V_1/V_2)^{\gamma - 1} = r^{\gamma - 1}$

For process 3→4: $T_3 / T_4 = (V_4/V_3)^{\gamma - 1} = r^{\gamma - 1}$

### Step 3: Write the Heat Transfer Expressions
Heat added: $q_{in} = C_v (T_3 - T_2)$
Heat rejected: $q_{out} = C_v (T_4 - T_1)$

### Step 4: Apply the Efficiency Definition

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{otto} &= 1 - \frac{q_{out}}{q_{in}} \\
&= 1 - \frac{C_v (T_4 - T_1)}{C_v (T_3 - T_2)} \\
&= 1 - \frac{T_1(T_4/T_1 - 1)}{T_2(T_3/T_2 - 1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Simplify Using Isentropic Relations

Since $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1}$ and $T_3 = T_4 \cdot r^{\gamma-1}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{T_4}{T_1} &= \frac{T_3}{T_2} \cdot \frac{1}{r^{2(\gamma-1)}} \cdot r^{\gamma-1} \quad \text{(after manipulation)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The classical derivation yields the iconic Otto equation:

$$\boxed{\eta_{otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}}$$

### Step 6: Substitute Values

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{otto} &= 1 - \frac{1}{9^{1.4 - 1}} \\
&= 1 - \frac{1}{9^{0.4}} \\
&= 1 - \frac{1}{2.408} \\
&= 1 - 0.4152 \\
&= 0.5848 \quad \text{or} \quad 58.48\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** Actual SI engine brake thermal efficiency is only **25%–35%** because of (i) mechanical friction, (ii) incomplete combustion, (iii) heat losses to cooling, and (iv) finite time for combustion (not truly constant-volume).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Four-Stroke Engine Cycle Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Piston at TDC] --> B[Intake Stroke\n0 to 180 deg\nIntake Valve OPEN\nPiston moves DOWN to BDC]
    B --> C[Fresh Air-Fuel Mixture\nEnters Cylinder]
    C --> D[Compression Stroke\n180 to 360 deg\nBoth Valves CLOSED\nPiston moves UP to TDC]
    D --> E[Charge Compressed\nto Ratio 9:1 to 12:1]
    E --> F[Power Stroke\n360 to 540 deg\nSpark Plug FIRES\nPiston moves DOWN to BDC]
    F --> G[Combustion Produces\nHigh Pressure 3 to 5 MPa]
    G --> H[Exhaust Stroke\n540 to 720 deg\nExhaust Valve OPEN\nPiston moves UP to TDC]
    H --> I[Combustion Products\nPushed Out]
    I --> J{Crank Rotated\nby 720 deg?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Cycle Repeats\nNext Power Stroke\nat 1080 deg]
    J -->|No| A
    K --> B
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Two-Stroke Engine Cycle Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Piston at TDC] --> B[Piston Moves UP\n0 to 180 deg]
    B --> C[Above Piston:\nCharge Compressed]
    C --> D[In Crankcase:\nFresh Mixture Drawn In\nvia Reed Valve or Inlet Port]
    D --> E[Spark Plug Fires\nNear TDC]
    E --> F[Piston Moves DOWN\n180 to 360 deg]
    F --> G[Exhaust Port Uncovered\nFirst at 25 to 30 deg Before BDC]
    G --> H[Transfer Port Uncovered\nShortly After\nCrankcase Charge Enters Cylinder]
    H --> I[Scavenging Occurs\nFresh Charge Displaces\nExhaust Gases]
    I --> J[Cycle Repeats\nAfter 360 deg]
    J --> B
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Comparison Architecture (Two vs Four)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Engine Selection Logic\nfor 2W or 3W Vehicle] --> A{Cost and\nRefinement Priority?}
    A -->|High Priority| F1[Choose Four-Stroke SI\nExamples: Honda Activa,\nHero Splendor, RE Classic]
    A -->|Low Cost\nMaximum Power\nper kg| F2[Choose Two-Stroke SI\nExamples: Yamaha RX100\nBajaj KB100]

    S --> B{Emission\nCompliance?}
    B -->|BS-VI Strict| F1
    B -->|Pre-BS-IV Era| F2

    S --> C{Fuel Economy\nPriority?}
    C -->|Yes| F1
    C -->|No, Performance First| F2

    F1 --> OUT1[Outcome:\nDurable, Efficient,\nCompliant Engine]
    F2 --> OUT2[Outcome:\nLightweight, High Power,\nHigher Emissions]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Port-Timing Diagram (Two-Stroke Schematic)

```mermaid
gantt
    title Two-Stroke Engine Port Timing (Representative)
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %s deg
    section Crank Angle
    Piston TDC        :milestone, 0, 0
    Piston BDC        :milestone, 180, 180
    section Intake Port
    Closed            :a1, 0, 65
    OPEN to Crankcase :a2, 65, 180
    Closed            :a3, 180, 360
    section Transfer Port
    Closed            :b1, 0, 100
    OPEN to Cylinder  :b2, 100, 180
    Closed            :b3, 180, 360
    section Exhaust Port
    Closed            :c1, 0, 100
    OPEN to Atmosphere :c2, 100, 180
    Closed            :c3, 180, 360
```
