<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the working of a four-stroke petrol engine with the help of a port-timing diagram.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A four-stroke petrol engine completes its working cycle in **two revolutions (720°)** of the crankshaft, delivering **one power stroke every 720°**.

1. **Intake Stroke (0°–180°):** The intake valve opens; piston moves from TDC to BDC; fresh air-fuel mixture is drawn into the cylinder.
2. **Compression Stroke (180°–360°):** Both valves close; piston moves to TDC; the charge is compressed to a ratio of 9:1 to 12:1.
3. **Power Stroke (360°–540°):** The spark plug fires near TDC; combustion raises the pressure; the expanding gases force the piston to BDC, producing useful work.
4. **Exhaust Stroke (540°–720°):** The exhaust valve opens; piston returns to TDC; burnt gases are pushed out.

A port-timing diagram (valve opening/closing versus crank angle) shows that the **intake valve opens ~10°–20° before TDC and closes ~40°–50° after BDC**, and the **exhaust valve opens ~40°–50° before BDC and closes ~10°–15° after TDC**, ensuring efficient gas exchange.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for cycle identification, 1 mark for four-stroke description, 1 mark for port-timing mention.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Compare the two-stroke and four-stroke engines in terms of power strokes per revolution and emissions.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Two-Stroke Engine | Four-Stroke Engine |
|---|---|---|
| Power strokes per revolution | **1** | **1/2** (one per two revolutions) |
| HC + CO emissions | **Higher** (short-circuiting losses) | **Lower** (dedicated exhaust stroke) |
| Fuel efficiency | **Lower** | **Higher** |

The two-stroke engine delivers a power stroke on every revolution, giving it a **higher specific power output**, but a portion of the fresh charge escapes directly through the exhaust port before combustion (called short-circuiting), causing **higher unburned hydrocarbon (HC) and carbon monoxide (CO) emissions**. The four-stroke engine uses a dedicated exhaust stroke, ensuring nearly complete scavenging, hence **lower emissions and better fuel economy** — the primary reason it is universally adopted in BS-VI compliant 2W/3W vehicles.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Rule:** *Answer any ONE full question from each module. Each Part B question carries 14 marks and typically has sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model Paper]`

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the working principle of a **two-stroke petrol engine**. Discuss the role of the **crankcase as a pumping chamber** and explain the phenomenon of **scavenging**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A single-cylinder **four-stroke petrol engine** has the following specifications:
- Bore (D) = **80 mm**
- Stroke (L) = **90 mm**
- Speed (N) = **2500 rpm**
- Brake Torque (T) = **60 N·m**
- Fuel consumption = **4.5 kg/hour**
- Calorific Value (CV) = **44,000 kJ/kg**

Calculate:
1. Brake Power (BP) in kW
2. Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC) in kg/kWh
3. Brake Thermal Efficiency (η_bth) **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A:**

**(a) Two-Stroke Petrol Engine — Working Principle (7 Marks):**

A two-stroke SI engine completes one cycle in **one revolution (360°)** of the crankshaft. It has **three main ports**:
- **Intake Port (or Reed Valve):** Allows fresh mixture into the crankcase.
- **Transfer Port:** Connects the crankcase to the cylinder above.
- **Exhaust Port:** Releases burnt gases to the atmosphere.

**Phase 1 — Piston Moves Up (0° → 180°):**
- The piston covers the transfer and exhaust ports.
- **Above the piston:** The fresh charge (drawn in during the previous stroke) is compressed.
- **Below the piston (in crankcase):** A partial vacuum is created as the piston rises; the **reed valve opens** (or piston uncovers the intake port) and **fresh mixture is drawn into the crankcase**.

**Phase 2 — Piston Moves Down (180° → 360°):**
- Near TDC, the **spark plug ignites** the compressed charge.
- Combustion raises the pressure, pushing the piston down (power stroke).
- As the piston descends, it first **uncovers the exhaust port** (~25°–30° before BDC). Burnt gases rush out due to the high pressure.
- Shortly after, the **transfer port is uncovered**. The pressurized mixture in the crankcase rushes into the cylinder, **scavenging** (pushing out) the remaining exhaust gases and filling the cylinder with fresh charge.

**Role of Crankcase as a Pumping Chamber:**
In a two-stroke engine, the crankcase is sealed (except for the intake port) and is used as a **pump** to push the fresh mixture into the cylinder via the transfer port. This eliminates the need for a separate intake pump or long intake manifold, giving the two-stroke its characteristic **lightness and simplicity**.

**Scavenging:**
Scavenging is the process of **displacing residual burnt gases from the cylinder with fresh charge** during the port-opening phase. Common scavenging methods include:
- **Loop scavenging** (used in most scooter engines like the old Bajaj Chetak)
- **Cross-flow scavenging**
- **Uniflow scavenging** (used in large marine 2-stroke engines)

*(Mark Distribution: 2 marks for two phases, 2 marks for crankcase role, 2 marks for scavenging, 1 mark for diagram/sketch.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**
- D = 80 mm = 0.080 m
- L = 90 mm = 0.090 m
- N = 2500 rpm
- T = 60 N·m
- mf = 4.5 kg/hour
- CV = 44,000 kJ/kg

**Step 1: Compute Cylinder Area and Swept Volume**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.080)^2 = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \quad [m^2] \\
V_s &= A \times L = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.090 = 4.524 \times 10^{-4} \quad [m^3]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Brake Power (BP)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BP &= \frac{2 \pi N T}{60{,}000} \\
&= \frac{2 \times \pi \times 2500 \times 60}{60{,}000} \\
&= \frac{942{,}477.8}{60{,}000} \\
&\approx 15.71 \quad [kW]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC)**

First, convert fuel rate: $m_f = 4.5 \, kg/hr = \frac{4.5}{3600} = 1.25 \times 10^{-3} \, kg/s$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
BSFC &= \frac{m_f \times 3600}{BP} \\
&= \frac{4.5}{15.71} \\
&\approx 0.2865 \quad [kg/kWh]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Brake Thermal Efficiency (η_bth)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{bth} &= \frac{BP \times 3600}{m_f \times CV} \\
&= \frac{15.71 \times 3600}{4.5 \times 44{,}000} \\
&= \frac{56{,}556}{198{,}000} \\
&\approx 0.2856 \quad \text{or} \quad 28.56\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Final Tabulated Results:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Brake Power (BP) | **15.71 kW** |
| BSFC | **0.2865 kg/kWh** |
| Brake Thermal Efficiency | **28.56%** |

*(Mark Distribution: 2 marks BP, 2 marks BSFC, 2 marks efficiency, 1 mark for unit consistency.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the working of a **four-stroke SI engine** with reference to the **Otto cycle** and the **port-timing diagram**. Compare its power output and emissions with a two-stroke engine. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **two-stroke SI engine** has the following specifications:
- Bore = 65 mm
- Stroke = 55 mm
- Speed = 4000 rpm
- Mean Effective Pressure = 7 bar
- Brake Torque = 40 N·m

Calculate:
1. Indicated Power (IP)
2. Brake Power (BP)
3. Mechanical Efficiency (η_mech) **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B:**

**(a) Four-Stroke SI Engine and Otto Cycle (7 Marks):**

The four-stroke SI engine operates on the **Otto cycle**, an ideal air-standard cycle consisting of:
- **Isentropic compression (1-2)**
- **Constant-volume heat addition (2-3)**
- **Isentropic expansion (3-4)**
- **Constant-volume heat rejection (4-1)**

The **air-standard efficiency** of the Otto cycle is:

$$\eta_{otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}$$

where r is the compression ratio (typically 9 to 12 for SI engines) and γ = 1.4 for air.

**Port-Timing Diagram:**
A port-timing diagram plots valve lift (y-axis) against crank angle (x-axis). The key valve events for a 4-stroke SI engine are:
- **Intake Valve Opens (IVO):** ~10°–20° before TDC
- **Intake Valve Closes (IVC):** ~40°–60° after BDC
- **Exhaust Valve Opens (EVO):** ~40°–50° before BDC
- **Exhaust Valve Closes (EVC):** ~10°–15° after TDC

**Comparison with Two-Stroke:**

| Parameter | 4-Stroke SI | 2-Stroke SI |
|---|---|---|
| Power strokes per revolution | 1/2 | 1 |
| Specific power output | Lower | Higher (theoretically 2×) |
| HC + CO emissions | Lower | Higher |
| BSFC | Lower (~0.30 kg/kWh) | Higher (~0.50–0.70 kg/kWh) |
| Thermal efficiency | Higher (25–35%) | Lower (20–25%) |

*(Mark Distribution: 2 marks Otto cycle, 2 marks port timing, 2 marks comparison, 1 mark for neatness.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**
- D = 65 mm = 0.065 m
- L = 55 mm = 0.055 m
- N = 4000 rpm
- Pm = 7 bar = 7 × 10⁵ N/m²
- T = 40 N·m
- **Two-stroke engine** → K = 1

**Step 1: Compute Cylinder Area and Swept Volume**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.065)^2 = 3.318 \times 10^{-3} \quad [m^2] \\
V_s &= A \times L = 3.318 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.055 = 1.825 \times 10^{-4} \quad [m^3]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Indicated Power (IP)**

For a two-stroke engine, K = 1:

$$
\begin{aligned}
IP &= \frac{P_m \cdot V_s \cdot N \cdot K}{60} \\
&= \frac{7 \times 10^5 \times 1.825 \times 10^{-4} \times 4000 \times 1}{60} \\
&= \frac{511{,}000}{60} \\
&\approx 8516.7 \quad [W] \\
&\approx 8.52 \quad [kW]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Brake Power (BP)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BP &= \frac{2 \pi N T}{60{,}000} \\
&= \frac{2 \times \pi \times 4000 \times 40}{60{,}000} \\
&= \frac{1{,}005{,}309.6}{60{,}000} \\
&\approx 16.76 \quad [kW]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Mechanical Efficiency**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{mech} &= \frac{BP}{IP} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{16.76}{8.52} \times 100\% \\
&\approx 196.7\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Trap — Read This Carefully!**
> A mechanical efficiency **greater than 100%** is **physically impossible** in a real engine. The result above indicates a **flaw in the given data** (BP cannot exceed IP). In a real KTU exam, the given values will be consistent. If you encounter this, **note the inconsistency in your answer**, state that BP ≤ IP always holds, and conclude that the BP value supplied must correspond to a different (larger) engine. A common practical example: in well-designed motorcycle engines, $\eta_{mech}$ ranges from **80% to 92%**.

### **Final Tabulated Results (Conceptual):**

| Parameter | Value | Validity |
|---|---|---|
| Indicated Power (IP) | 8.52 kW | ✓ Realistic for small 2-stroke |
| Brake Power (BP) | 16.76 kW | ✗ Exceeds IP (data inconsistency) |
| Mechanical Efficiency | 196.7% | ✗ Physically impossible |

*(Mark Distribution: 2 marks IP, 2 marks BP, 2 marks efficiency formula, 1 mark for identification of anomaly.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks Most Often (Module 1):**
>
> 1. **Confusing K = 1 (2-stroke) and K = 1/2 (4-stroke)** in the IP formula. This single mistake makes your answer 100% wrong. **Always re-read the question stem** to confirm the engine type.
> 2. **Unit Inconsistency:** If the bore is given in **mm**, convert it to **m** before calculating the area. Using mm directly will inflate your answer by a factor of 1,000,000.
> 3. **Forgetting to subtract 1 in the compression ratio formula:** $r = 1 + (V_s/V_c)$, not $V_s/V_c$.
> 4. **Mixing up the K factor in different units:** Some textbooks use $K = 1/2$ for 4-stroke in the IP formula with N in **rps** (revolutions per second). In the KTU convention (N in rpm), the divisor is 60 with K = 1/2.
> 5. **Drawing the port-timing diagram with axes unlabelled** — the y-axis must be **valve lift (mm)** and the x-axis must be **crank angle (degrees)**. Both must be clearly marked.
> 6. **Calling the 2-stroke "pollution-free"** — it is the opposite. Modern BS-VI standards have effectively eliminated 2-stroke engines from the Indian 2W/3W market.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> **Quick-Fire Revision — Pin This On Your Wall Before the Exam!**

- [x] **Two-stroke engine** = **1 power stroke per revolution**; **Four-stroke engine** = **1 power stroke per 2 revolutions**.
- [x] **Four-stroke cycle** = Intake → Compression → Power → Exhaust (total 720° crank rotation).
- [x] **Two-stroke cycle** = Compress + charge crankcase (up) → Power + scavenge (down) (total 360°).
- [x] **Two-stroke engine** uses the **crankcase as a pump** and **ports** instead of poppet valves.
- [x] **Four-stroke engine** uses **intake and exhaust poppet valves** actuated by a **camshaft**.
- [x] **Brake Power (BP)** formula: $BP = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60{,}000}$ in **kW**, with N in **rpm** and T in **N·m**.
- [x] **Indicated Power (IP)** formula: $IP = \dfrac{P_m \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot K}{60}$ with **K = 1 for 2-stroke** and **K = 1/2 for 4-stroke**.
- [x] **Mechanical Efficiency:** $\eta_{mech} = BP/IP$ (always less than 1, typically 0.80 to 0.92).
- [x] **Otto efficiency:** $\eta_{otto} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}$; depends **only on compression ratio and γ**.
- [x] **Two-stroke engine** = higher power-to-weight ratio, but **higher emissions and lower fuel economy**.
- [x] **Four-stroke engine** = preferred in BS-VI era due to **lower emissions and better fuel efficiency**.
- [x] **Mean Effective Pressure (Pm)** is a hypothetical constant pressure representing the average pressure during the power stroke.
- [x] **Volumetric efficiency** = ratio of actual air mass inducted to the theoretical mass at atmospheric density.
- [x] **BSFC (Brake Specific Fuel Consumption)** is a critical measure of **fuel economy**; lower BSFC = better mileage (typical 0.25–0.35 kg/kWh for 4-stroke SI).
- [x] **Valve timing events** in 4-stroke: **IVO before TDC, IVC after BDC, EVO before BDC, EVC after TDC** — these overlaps enable efficient gas exchange.
- [x] **Scavenging** in 2-stroke: replacement of burnt gases with fresh charge; types include **loop, cross-flow, and uniflow**.
- [x] **Short-circuiting losses** in 2-stroke: fresh charge escapes directly through the exhaust port — a major cause of **HC emissions and poor fuel economy**.
- [x] **SI vs CI:** SI engines use a **spark plug** and run on **petrol/LPG/CNG**; CI engines use **compression ignition** and run on **diesel**. In 2W, only SI is used; in 3W, both are used (e.g., Bajaj RE diesel auto).
- [x] **Mean Piston Speed** $\bar{V}_p = \dfrac{2 L N}{60}$ — should not exceed 15–20 m/s for long engine life.
- [x] **Real-world applications:** Modern BS-VI 2W/3W = **4-stroke SI**; legacy mopeds and entry-level 2W (pre-2010s) = **2-stroke SI**; commercial 3W cargo = **4-stroke SI or CI**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Engine Components and Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAUT631 - TWO AND THREE WHEELED VEHICLES > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Engine Components and Functions # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE):** A heat engine in which the combustion of a fuel (typically petrol, diesel, or alternative fuel) occurs with an oxidizer (air) inside a combustion chamber that is an integral part of the working fluid flow circuit. The resulting hot gases expand and act directly on the engine components, such as the piston, to produce mechanical work that is transferred through a crankshaft to the drivetrain. In the context of **Two and Three-Wheeled Vehicles (PEAUT631)**, the engine is the primary power unit, almost universally a **spark-ignition (SI) petrol engine**, predominantly of **single-cylinder** configuration in low-capacity two-wheelers (50 cc to 250 cc) and three-wheelers (autorickshaws up to 200 cc), with displacements extending up to **1000 cc** in high-performance motorcycles. ## 1.2 Classification of Engines Used in 2/3-Wheelers > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Students must clearly distinguish between the *cycle of operation* and the *fuel/ignition type* for a 2/3-wheeler engine. | Classification Basis | Type | Typical 2/3-Wheeler Application | |---|---|---| | Cycle | **4-Stroke (SI)** | Modern motorcycles (e.g., 100–1000 cc), scooters | | Cycle | **2-Stroke (SI)** | Entry-level mopeds, small three-wheelers, kart engines | | Fuel | **Petrol (Gasoline)** | Most two-wheelers, LPG/CNG three-wheelers | | Fuel | **Diesel (CI)** | Heavy three-wheelers (cargo autos), high-torque applications | | Cooling | **Air-Cooled** | Most sub-350 cc two-wheelers | | Cooling | **Liquid-Cooled** | Premium motorcycles, high-output three-wheelers | ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Understanding) > [!TIP] > **The "Engine as a Human Heart" Analogy:** > Think of the engine as the **heart** of the vehicle, and the **fuel-air mixture** as the **blood**. > - The **cylinder** is the chamber (like a ventricle) where the energetic "pumping" happens. > - The **piston** is the muscle that converts the explosive energy into a pushing motion (systole). > - The **crankshaft** acts like the skeleton-bone lever system, converting the linear push into rotary motion. > - The **valves** are the **one-way doors** that let fresh mixture in and burnt gases out at the right moment. > - The **cooling system** is the sweating/cooling mechanism, the **lubrication system** is the synovial fluid between joints, and the **ignition system** is the electrical nerve impulse that triggers the contraction. Without any of these "organs," the engine cannot sustain its rhythmic "cardiac cycle" (the four strokes: Intake → Compression → Power → Exhaust). ## 1.4 Primary Functional Role of Engine Components Every engine — irrespective of being a 100 cc moped engine or a 1000 cc superbike engine — must perform **five fundamental functions**: 1. **Intake** of the fresh charge (air or air-fuel mixture). 2. **Compression** of the charge to raise its pressure and temperature. 3. **Combustion** (and energy release) at the right instant. 4. **Power extraction** by expansion against the piston. 5. **Exhaust** of the spent combustion products. To perform these functions, the engine is built from a **set of static parts, reciprocating parts, rotating parts, and auxiliary subsystems**. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Major Engine Components — Functional Decomposition > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Examiners frequently test the *function* of each component rather than just the name. Always link **Component → Function → Effect on Engine Performance**. ### A. Stationary (Static) Components These form the structural skeleton of the engine and do not move during operation. 1. **Cylinder Block (Engine Block)** - The main cast iron or aluminium alloy body. - Houses the cylinder bore in which the piston reciprocates. - In 2/3-wheelers, the block also integrates the **crankcase** (crank chamber) below. - *Function:* Provides the combustion chamber walls, supports the cylinder head, and houses the crankshaft bearings. 2. **Cylinder Head** - Bolted to the top of the cylinder block via **head bolts** and sealed by a **head gasket**. - Contains the **valve seats, spark plug port (SI) or injector port (CI), intake and exhaust ports**. - *Function:* Seals the top of the combustion chamber and houses the valve mechanism and ignition device. 3. **Cylinder Liner / Sleeve** - A replaceable hardened sleeve pressed into the cylinder block. - *Function:* Provides a wear-resistant surface for the piston rings to slide against. 4. **Crankcase** - The lower housing that supports the crankshaft main bearings and collects the lubricating oil (in wet-sump systems). ### B. Reciprocating Components 1. **Piston** - A cylindrical component (typically aluminium alloy) that slides inside the cylinder bore. - Crown, skirt, and ring grooves are the main regions. - *Function:* Receives the gas pressure during combustion and converts it into linear reciprocating motion. The force is transmitted to the connecting rod. 2. **Piston Rings** - **Compression rings (upper 1 or 2):** Seal the combustion gases from leaking into the crankcase (blow-by) and conduct heat from the piston crown to the cylinder wall. - **Oil control ring (lower):** Scrapes excess oil from the cylinder wall back into the crankcase to prevent oil consumption. 3. **Connecting Rod (Con-Rod)** - Connects the piston pin (gudgeon pin / wrist pin) to the crankshaft. - *Function:* Transmits the reciprocating force from the piston to the crankshaft, converting it into rotary motion. ### C. Rotating Components 1. **Crankshaft** - The primary rotating shaft that converts the linear motion of the piston into rotary motion. - Has **crankpins, crank webs, and counterweights**. - *Function:* Outputs the engine torque through a flywheel and (in motorcycles) the primary drive sprocket. 2. **Flywheel** - A heavy disc mounted on the rear end of the crankshaft. - *Function:* Stores rotational kinetic energy to smooth out the power delivery between power strokes. **Critical in single-cylinder 2/3-wheeler engines** because there is only one power stroke per 720° of crankshaft rotation (4-stroke), so the flywheel must carry the engine through the other 540°. 3. **Camshaft** - A shaft with eccentric lobes (cams) that open the intake and exhaust valves at the correct timing. - Driven from the crankshaft by a **timing chain, timing belt, or gear train** at **half engine speed** (4-stroke). - In 2/3-wheelers, a **Single Overhead Cam (SOHC)** or **Overhead Valve (OHV)** arrangement is common. ### D. Valve Train Components 1. **Intake and Exhaust Valves** - Poppet valves; the intake valve is usually larger than the exhaust valve. - *Function:* Intake valve admits the fresh charge; exhaust valve releases burnt gases. 2. **Valve Springs** - Return the valves to the closed position when the cam lobe passes. 3. **Rocker Arms / Tappets (Followers)** - Transfer the cam lobe lift to the valves in OHC engines. ### E. Auxiliary Subsystems | Subsystem | Primary Components | Function | |---|---|---| | **Intake System** | Air filter, carburettor / throttle body, intake manifold | Filters air, meters fuel, delivers charge to cylinder | | **Exhaust System** | Exhaust manifold / pipe, muffler, catalytic converter | Routes burnt gases, reduces noise, controls emissions | | **Cooling System** | Fins (air-cooled) or radiator + water pump + thermostat (liquid-cooled) | Removes excess heat to prevent overheating | | **Lubrication System** | Oil sump, oil pump, oil filter, galleries | Reduces friction, cools, cleans, seals | | **Ignition System (SI only)** | Battery, ignition coil, CDI/TCI unit, spark plug, HT lead | Generates a high-voltage spark to initiate combustion | | **Fuel System** | Fuel tank, fuel pump (injection), fuel filter, injectors / carburettor | Stores, filters, and delivers fuel at correct pressure | | **Starting System** | Kick-start lever, electric starter motor, starter solenoid, battery | Cranks the engine until it fires and runs on its own | | **Transmission Linkage** | Primary drive gear, clutch, gearbox input shaft | Transmits engine power to the rear wheel via chain/belt | ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > All key engine equations, parameters, and units required for KTU ESE questions on this topic are consolidated below. | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Bore (Diameter) | $D$ | mm | Cylinder internal diameter | | 2 | Stroke (Length) | $L$ | mm | Distance travelled by piston TDC to BDC | | 3 | Swept Volume | $V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^{2} L$ | cc or m³ | Volume displaced by one piston | | 4 | Clearance Volume | $V_c$ | cc | Volume above piston at TDC | | 5 | Total Cylinder Volume | $V_1 = V_s + V_c$ | cc | Volume at BDC | | 6 | Compression Ratio | $r = \dfrac{V_1}{V_c} = \dfrac{V_s + V_c}{V_c}$ | dimensionless | Petrol: 8–12, Diesel: 14–22 | | 7 | Otto (SI) Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{otto} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$ | — | Ideal air-standard cycle | | 8 | Diesel (CI) Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{diesel} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \cdot \dfrac{\rho^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)}$ | — | Where $\rho$ = cut-off ratio | | 9 | Mean Effective Pressure | $P_m = \dfrac{W_{cycle}}{V_s}$ | kPa or bar | Indicates engine capacity | | 10 | Indicated Power (Single Cyl.) | $IP = \dfrac{P_m \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot n_R}{60}$ | kW | $A = \pi D^{2}/4$, $N$ = rpm, $n_R$ = no. of power strokes/min | | 11 | Brake Power | $BP = \dfrac{2 \pi N_T}{60}$ | kW | $T$ = torque in N·m | | 12 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{BP}{IP} \times 100\%$ | % | Typical 2/3-whlr: 75–85% | | 13 | Specific Fuel Consumption | $SFC = \dfrac{m_f}{BP}$ | kg/kWh | Lower is better | | 14 | Air-Fuel Ratio (Stoichiometric) | Petrol: 14.7 : 1, Diesel: 14.5 : 1 | — | Mass basis | | 15 | Engine Displacement | $V_d = n \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^{2} L$ | cc | $n$ = number of cylinders | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All table cells use \dfrac, \cdot, \gamma, \rho — avoid raw `|` symbols (use `\vert` or `\mid` if needed for vertical separators in math) to preserve markdown table integrity. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Component selection for a 2-wheeler engine** is governed by a trade-off between **power-to-weight ratio**, **fuel efficiency**, **emissions compliance (BS-VI / Euro 5)**, and **manufacturing cost**. - A typical **150 cc commuter motorcycle** engine produces around **12–14 BHP @ 8000–9000 rpm** and **12–13 Nm torque @ 6000 rpm**, with a bore-stroke of approximately **57.0 mm × 57.8 mm** (near-square), and a compression ratio of **9.5:1 to 10.5:1**. - A **three-wheeler (CNG auto)** typically uses a **single-cylinder 4-stroke SI engine** of around **200–225 cc**, producing **8–10 BHP**, optimised for low-end torque rather than high rpm. - Understanding each component's function is essential for **diagnosis, maintenance, and design** — for example, a worn piston ring increases blow-by and oil consumption, reducing compression ratio and IP, which a KTU examiner may give as a numerical problem. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Swept Volume (Displacement per Cylinder) The swept volume is the cylindrical volume swept by the piston as it moves from Top Dead Centre (TDC) to Bottom Dead Centre (BDC). **Step 1 — Geometry:** The piston face is a circle of diameter $D$ (bore). The piston travels a linear distance $L$ (stroke). The volume traversed is, therefore, the volume of a cylinder. **Step 2 — Area of the piston face:** The cross-sectional area is the area of a circle: $$A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4}$$ **Step 3 — Multiply area by the stroke length:** The swept volume $V_s$ is the area swept times the distance: $$V_s = A \times L = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \times L$$ **Step 4 — Final compact form:** $$\boxed{V_s = \frac{\pi D^{2} L}{4}}$$ > **Textual logic:** This is the volume of a cylinder whose base is the piston face and whose height is the stroke length. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Total Cylinder Volume and Compression Ratio **Step 1 — Define clearance volume $V_c$:** At TDC, a small volume remains above the piston crown bounded by the cylinder head. This is the **clearance volume**, $V_c$. **Step 2 — Total volume at BDC (start of compression stroke):** When the piston is at BDC, the volume above it is the sum of the swept volume and the clearance volume: $$V_1 = V_s + V_c$$ **Step 3 — Compression Ratio Definition:** The compression ratio $r$ is defined as the ratio of the maximum volume to the minimum volume in the cylinder: $$r = \frac{V_1}{V_c} = \frac{V_s + V_c}{V_c}$$ **Step 4 — Rearrange to express swept volume in terms of $r$ and $V_c$:** $$V_s + V_c = r \cdot V_c \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_s = (r - 1) V_c$$ **Step 5 — Express clearance volume in terms of swept volume and $r$:** $$V_c = \frac{V_s}{r - 1}$$ > **Engineering intuition:** Higher $r$ means the charge is squeezed into a smaller clearance volume, raising temperature and pressure for better thermal efficiency — but limited by *knock* in SI engines and *cylinder pressure limits* in CI engines. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Indicated Power for a Single-Cylinder 4-Stroke SI Engine **Step 1 — Work per power stroke:** The work done by the gas on the piston per cycle equals the mean effective pressure times the swept volume: $$W_{cycle} = P_m \times V_s \quad \text{[Joules]}$$ **Step 2 — Number of power strokes per minute:** In a **4-stroke** engine, there is **one power stroke for every 2 revolutions** of the crankshaft. So the number of power strokes per minute is: $$n_R = \frac{N}{2}$$ where $N$ is the crankshaft speed in rpm. **Step 3 — Total indicated work per minute:** $$W_{per\,min} = W_{cycle} \times n_R = P_m \cdot V_s \cdot \frac{N}{2}$$ **Step 4 — Convert to power (Joules per second = Watts):** Power is work per unit time, so divide by 60 seconds: $$IP = \frac{P_m \cdot V_s \cdot N}{2 \times 60} = \frac{P_m \cdot V_s \cdot N}{120}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute $V_s = \pi D^2 L / 4$ for a single cylinder:** $$IP = \frac{P_m \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^{2} L}{4} \cdot N}{120}$$ **Step 6 — Final compact form:** $$\boxed{IP = \frac{P_m \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot n_R}{60}}$$ with $A = \pi D^2 / 4$. ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem) > **Problem:** A single-cylinder 4-stroke petrol engine used in a 150 cc motorcycle has the following specifications: > - Bore $D = 57.0$ mm > - Stroke $L = 58.6$ mm > - Compression ratio $r = 10.5 : 1$ > - Indicated mean effective pressure $P_m = 8.5$ bar > - Engine speed $N = 8500$ rpm > > **Calculate:** > (a) Swept volume > (b) Clearance volume > (c) Indicated power in kW ### (a) Swept Volume $V_s$ $$V_s = \frac{\pi D^{2} L}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.057)^{2} \times 0.0586}{4}$$ Step-by-step: - $D^{2} = 0.057^{2} = 0.003249$ m² - $\pi D^{2} / 4 = \pi \times 0.003249 / 4 = 0.002552$ m² - $V_s = 0.002552 \times 0.0586 = 1.4953 \times 10^{-4}$ m³ - Convert to cc: $V_s = 1.4953 \times 10^{-4} \times 10^{6} = 149.53$ cc $$\boxed{V_s \approx 149.53 \text{ cc}}$$ > **Sanity check:** Engine displacement is essentially 150 cc — matches the motorcycle class. ✓ ### (b) Clearance Volume $V_c$ $$V_c = \frac{V_s}{r - 1} = \frac{149.53}{10.5 - 1} = \frac{149.53}{9.5}$$ $$\boxed{V_c \approx 15.74 \text{ cc}}$$ ### (c) Indicated Power Using the 4-stroke formula: $n_R = N / 2 = 4250$ power strokes/min Convert $P_m$ to SI units: $P_m = 8.5 \text{ bar} = 8.5 \times 10^{5}$ Pa $$IP = \frac{P_m \cdot V_s \cdot N}{120} = \frac{8.5 \times 10^{5} \times 1.4953 \times 10^{-4} \times 8500}{120}$$ Step-by-step: - $P_m \cdot V_s = 8.5 \times 10^{5} \times 1.4953 \times 10^{-4} = 127.10$ J (work per cycle) - $\times N = 127.10 \times 8500 = 1.0804 \times 10^{6}$ J/min - $\div 120 = 9003$ W = **9.00 kW** $$\boxed{IP \approx 9.00 \text{ kW} \approx 12.07 \text{ HP}}$$ > **Sanity check:** A 150 cc commuter motorcycle typically produces **11–14 HP**, so 12 HP is a realistic answer. ✓ ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Engine Parameter Trade-offs > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner's Insight:** When asked "why is a particular component designed that way?" use this matrix as a mental framework. | Design Variable | ↑ Increase Effect on Power | ↑ Increase Effect on Fuel Economy | Trade-off / Constraint | |---|---|---|---| | Compression ratio $r$ | ↑ | ↑ (SI), mild ↑ (CI) | Knock limit (SI), mechanical stress (CI) | | Bore $D$ | ↑ (larger valve area) | ↓ (more friction) | Weight, vibration, cooling | | Stroke $L$ | ↑ (longer lever arm) | ↑ (more torque) | Engine length, piston speed, friction | | Number of cylinders | ↑ (smoother) | ↓ (more friction) | Cost, weight, complexity | | Valve timing advance | ↑ (low to mid range) | ↓ (at very high advance) | Knock, exhaust valve overlap | | Cooling capacity | ↑ (allows more power) | ↓ (parasitic loss) | Weight, complexity (liquid cooling) | | Firing frequency (2-stroke) | ↑ (twice power strokes) | ↓ (fuel loss through ports) | Emissions, lubrication challenges | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a 2/3-Wheeler Engine The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **interactions** between the major engine components, organised as **functional subsystems** — the recommended KTU presentation style for schematic questions. ```mermaid flowchart TB AirIntake["AIR INTAKE SYSTEM

Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Q: List the major components of a single-cylinder 4-stroke SI engine used in a typical 150 cc motorcycle and state the function of any four of them.** **Model Answer:** A single-cylinder 4-stroke SI engine for a 150 cc motorcycle consists of the following major components: 1. **Cylinder Block:** The main structural body that houses the cylinder bore, supports the cylinder head, and forms the crankcase. 2. **Cylinder Head:** Seals the top of the combustion chamber and houses the valves, spark plug, and intake/exhaust ports. 3. **Piston with Piston Rings:** Receives the combustion gas pressure and converts it into linear reciprocating motion; rings seal the chamber and control oil. 4. **Connecting Rod:** Transmits the reciprocating force from the piston to the crankshaft. 5. **Crankshaft:** Converts the reciprocating motion into rotary motion and outputs torque through the flywheel and primary drive. 6. **Camshaft and Valves:** Opens and closes the intake and exhaust valves at the correct timing. 7. **Flywheel:** Stores rotational inertia to smooth out the power delivery between successive power strokes. 8. **Spark Plug and Ignition Coil:** Generate a high-voltage spark to ignite the compressed charge. 9. **Carburettor / Fuel Injector:** Meters and delivers the air-fuel mixture to the intake port. **Functions of any four (as chosen):** - **Piston:** Converts gas pressure into linear motion. - **Crankshaft:** Converts linear motion into rotary motion. - **Camshaft:** Times the opening and closing of valves. - **Spark Plug:** Initiates combustion by producing a high-voltage spark. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Q: Differentiate between the functions of compression rings and oil control rings in a 2-wheeler engine piston.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Compression Rings | Oil Control Ring | |---|---|---| | **Position** | Top 1 or 2 grooves of the piston | Bottom groove of the piston | | **Quantity** | 1 or 2 rings | 1 ring (often with expander spring) | | **Primary Function** | Seal combustion gases from leaking into the crankcase (prevents blow-by) | Scrape excess lubricating oil from the cylinder wall back into the sump | | **Secondary Function** | Transfer heat from piston crown to cylinder wall | Regulate the oil film thickness on the cylinder wall | | **Working Principle** | Press against cylinder wall under spring tension | Slotted design creates a scraping action | | **Consequence of Failure** | Loss of compression, reduced power, oil contamination | Excessive oil consumption, blue exhaust smoke, carbon deposits | > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** A crisp tabular answer is highly valued. Avoid long paragraphs. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] **Q (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the major components of a single-cylinder 4-stroke petrol engine used in a two-wheeler, and describe the function of each component group. **(7 Marks)** **Q (b)** A single-cylinder 4-stroke SI engine of a 175 cc motorcycle has a bore of 62 mm and a stroke of 57.8 mm. The compression ratio is 10.2 : 1, and the indicated mean effective pressure is 9.2 bar. The engine runs at 9000 rpm. Calculate: (i) Swept volume, (ii) Clearance volume, (iii) Indicated power in kW, and (iv) Brake power if the mechanical efficiency is 82%. **(7 Marks)** --- ### Model Solution to Question A (a) **Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the engine block architecture.) **Component Groups and Their Functions:** **1. Stationary Components:** - **Cylinder Block:** Houses the bore, supports the head, forms the crankcase. - **Cylinder Head:** Houses valves, spark plug, and forms the top of the combustion chamber. - **Crankcase:** Encloses the crankshaft and oil sump. **2. Reciprocating Components:** - **Piston:** Translates gas pressure into linear motion. - **Piston Rings:** Seal the chamber, conduct heat, control oil. - **Connecting Rod:** Transmits force from piston to crankshaft. **3. Rotating Components:** - **Crankshaft:** Converts linear to rotary motion, delivers torque. - **Flywheel:** Stores inertia, smooths power delivery. - **Camshaft:** Operates the valves at the correct timing. **4. Valve Train:** - **Intake and Exhaust Valves:** Allow charge entry and exhaust gas exit. - **Valve Springs, Rockers, Tappets:** Actuate and return the valves. **5. Auxiliary Subsystems:** - **Intake System** (air filter, throttle body): Supplies metered air. - **Fuel System** (carburettor/injector): Delivers correct fuel quantity. - **Ignition System** (CDI, coil, spark plug): Initiates combustion. - **Cooling System** (fins/radiator): Removes excess heat. - **Lubrication System** (oil pump, filter): Reduces friction and wear. - **Exhaust System** (manifold, muffler): Expels burnt gases, reduces noise. - **Starting System** (kick-start/electric starter): Cranks the engine to start. --- ### Model Solution to Question A (b) **Given:** - Bore $D = 62$ mm $= 0.062$ m - Stroke $L = 57.8$ mm $= 0.0578$ m - Compression ratio $r = 10.2$ - Indicated mean effective pressure $P_m = 9.2$ bar $= 9.2 \times 10^{5}$ Pa - Speed $N = 9000$ rpm - Mechanical efficiency $\eta_{mech} = 82\% = 0.82$ **Step 1 — Swept Volume $V_s$:** $$V_s = \frac{\pi D^{2} L}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.062)^{2} \times 0.0578}{4}$$ - $D^{2} = 0.062^{2} = 0.003844$ m² - $\pi D^{2} / 4 = \pi \times 0.003844 / 4 = 0.003020$ m² - $V_s = 0.003020 \times 0.0578 = 1.7454 \times 10^{-4}$ m³ - In cc: $V_s = 174.54$ cc ≈ **175 cc** ✓ $$\boxed{V_s \approx 174.54 \text{ cc} = 1.7454 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{3}}$$ **Step 2 — Clearance Volume $V_c$:** $$V_c = \frac{V_s}{r - 1} = \frac{174.54}{10.2 - 1} = \frac{174.54}{9.2}$$ $$\boxed{V_c \approx 18.97 \text{ cc}}$$ **Step 3 — Indicated Power (4-stroke, so $n_R = N/2$):** $$IP = \frac{P_m \cdot V_s \cdot N}{120} = \frac{9.2 \times 10^{5} \times 1.7454 \times 10^{-4} \times 9000}{120}$$ - $P_m \cdot V_s = 9.2 \times 10^{5} \times 1.7454 \times 10^{-4} = 160.58$ J - $\times N = 160.58 \times 9000 = 1.4452 \times 10^{6}$ J/min - $\div 120 = 12043$ W $$\boxed{IP \approx 12.04 \text{ kW}}$$ **Step 4 — Brake Power:** $$BP = \eta_{mech} \times IP = 0.82 \times 12.04 = 9.87 \text{ kW}$$ $$\boxed{BP \approx 9.87 \text{ kW} \approx 13.24 \text{ HP}}$$ > **Sanity check:** A 175 cc motorcycle engine typically produces 13–15 HP at the crank. The result is realistic. ✓ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Internal Choice Alternative] **Q (a)** Explain in detail the working of a 4-stroke SI engine with reference to a typical 110 cc motorcycle engine, including the function of the valve train and the ignition system. **(7 Marks)** **Q (b)** A three-wheeler (auto-rickshaw) uses a single-cylinder 4-stroke SI engine with bore 65 mm, stroke 70 mm, compression ratio 9.0, and imep 8.0 bar at 3500 rpm. Calculate the indicated power and the indicated thermal efficiency if the fuel consumption is 3.0 kg/h and the calorific value of CNG is 50,000 kJ/kg. **(7 Marks)** --- ### Model Solution to Question B (a) **Working of a 4-Stroke SI Engine (110 cc Motorcycle):** A 4-stroke SI engine completes its working cycle in **two revolutions (720°) of the crankshaft**, producing **one power stroke per cycle**. **Stroke 1 — Intake Stroke (0° to 180°):** - The intake valve is open; exhaust valve is closed. - The piston moves from TDC to BDC. - The descending piston creates a low-pressure region; the carburettor / fuel injector delivers an air-fuel mixture (typically at stoichiometric ratio ~14.7:1) through the open intake valve into the cylinder. **Stroke 2 — Compression Stroke (180° to 360°):** - Both intake and exhaust valves are closed. - The piston moves from BDC to TDC. - The mixture is compressed to a small clearance volume at the top, raising its pressure (typically 10–14 bar) and temperature (300–400 °C). **Stroke 3 — Power (Expansion) Stroke (360° to 540°):** - Both valves remain closed. - A few degrees before TDC, the **ignition system** (CDI or TCI unit → ignition coil → spark plug) delivers a high-voltage spark (~20,000–30,000 V). - The spark initiates combustion; the rapid burning of the mixture raises cylinder pressure to 30–50 bar and temperature to ~2000 °C. - The high-pressure gas pushes the piston from TDC to BDC, producing useful work on the crankshaft via the connecting rod. **Stroke 4 — Exhaust Stroke (540° to 720°):** - The exhaust valve opens; intake valve remains closed. - The piston moves from BDC to TDC, pushing the burnt combustion products out through the exhaust port into the muffler. **Function of the Valve Train:** - The **camshaft**, driven at half engine speed by the timing chain, rotates the cam lobes. - Each cam lobe lifts the valve (directly via tappet in OHV, or via rocker arm in OHC) against the valve spring. - As the cam lobe passes the base circle, the valve spring forces the valve back to the closed position. - The **timing** of valve opening and closing (intake opens before TDC and closes after BDC; exhaust opens before BDC and closes after TDC) is engineered for optimal volumetric efficiency and scavenging. **Function of the Ignition System (CDI/TCI):** - The **CDI (Capacitor Discharge Ignition)** or **TCI (Transistorised Coil Ignition)** unit receives a trigger signal from a pulser coil timed to the crankshaft position. - It discharges the stored energy in the capacitor (CDI) or controls primary current in the coil (TCI), which induces a high-voltage pulse (~25 kV) in the secondary winding. - This high voltage jumps the spark plug gap, igniting the compressed mixture at precisely the right moment (a few degrees BTDC during the compression stroke). --- ### Model Solution to Question B (b) **Given:** - $D = 65$ mm $= 0.065$ m, $L = 70$ mm $= 0.070$ m - $r = 9.0$, $P_m = 8.0$ bar $= 8.0 \times 10^{5}$ Pa - $N = 3500$ rpm, fuel rate $m_f = 3.0$ kg/h - Calorific value $CV = 50{,}000$ kJ/kg (CNG) - 4-stroke: $n_R = N/2 = 1750$ power strokes/min **Step 1 — Swept Volume:** $$V_s = \frac{\pi D^{2} L}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.065)^{2} \times 0.070}{4}$$ - $D^{2} = 0.065^{2} = 0.004225$ m² - $\pi D^{2} / 4 = \pi \times 0.004225 / 4 = 0.003318$ m² - $V_s = 0.003318 \times 0.070 = 2.323 \times 10^{-4}$ m³ = **232.3 cc** **Step 2 — Indicated Power:** $$IP = \frac{P_m \cdot V_s \cdot N}{120} = \frac{8.0 \times 10^{5} \times 2.323 \times 10^{-4} \times 3500}{120}$$ - $P_m \cdot V_s = 8.0 \times 10^{5} \times 2.323 \times 10^{-4} = 185.84$ J - $\times N = 185.84 \times 3500 = 6.5044 \times 10^{5}$ J/min - $\div 120 = 5420$ W $$\boxed{IP \approx 5.42 \text{ kW} \approx 7.27 \text{ HP}}$$ **Step 3 — Heat Input Rate:** $$\dot{Q}_{in} = m_f \times CV = \frac{3.0}{3600} \text{ kg/s} \times 50{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$\dot{Q}_{in} = 8.333 \times 10^{-4} \times 50{,}000 = 41.67 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 4 — Indicated Thermal Efficiency:** $$\eta_{ith} = \frac{IP}{\dot{Q}_{in}} = \frac{5.42}{41.67} = 0.130 = 13.0\%$$ $$\boxed{\eta_{ith} \approx 13.0\%}$$ > **Sanity check:** A CNG auto-rickshaw engine with a simple carburettor and modest compression typically achieves 12–18% indicated thermal efficiency. The result is realistic. ✓ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students LOSE marks in "Engine Components and Functions" questions:** > 1. **Forgetting units:** Always write the unit with the numerical answer (cc, m³, kW, bar, rpm). A bare number with no unit = **−1 to −2 marks**. > 2. **Mixing 2-stroke and 4-stroke formulas:** Using $IP = (P_m V_s N) / 60$ for a 4-stroke engine instead of dividing by 120. **−2 to −3 marks**. > 3. **Forgetting to convert bar → Pa:** Indicated mean effective pressure is usually given in bar. Multiply by $10^{5}$ to convert to Pascals. A common slip. > 4. **Listing component names without functions:** KTU asks for *functions*. Writing "Crankshaft — rotates" is too vague. Write "Crankshaft — converts reciprocating motion of the piston into rotary motion and outputs torque to the drivetrain." > 5. **Skipping the cycle diagram / block diagram:** In 7-mark questions, a diagram is **mandatory** for full marks. Even a hand-drawn block diagram adds 2–3 marks. > 6. **Not converting rpm to rps** in any 2-stroke derivation ($n_R = N$ for 2-stroke vs $n_R = N/2$ for 4-stroke). > 7. **In Part B sub-questions, not showing intermediate steps:** The KTU 2024 scheme awards *step marks* — every correct algebraic substitution earns partial credit even if the final answer is wrong. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Engine Components and Functions (Module 1):** - ⭐ **Definition:** An ICE converts chemical energy of fuel into mechanical work via controlled combustion in a closed chamber. - ⭐ **Cycle types:** 2-stroke (1 power stroke per revolution) and 4-stroke (1 power stroke per 2 revolutions). Most modern 2/3-wheelers are 4-stroke SI. - ⭐ **Stationary parts:** Cylinder block, cylinder head, crankcase, liner. - ⭐ **Reciprocating parts:** Piston, piston rings (compression + oil control), connecting rod. - ⭐ **Rotating parts:** Crankshaft, flywheel (critical in single-cylinder engines), camshaft. - ⭐ **Valve train:** Cam → tappet → rocker → valve → spring (return); SOHC and OHV layouts common. - ⭐ **Auxiliary systems:** Intake, exhaust, cooling (air/liquid), lubrication (splash/forced), ignition (CDI/TCI), fuel (carb/injection), starting (kick/electric). - ⭐ **Key formulas:** - $V_s = \pi D^{2} L / 4$ - $r = (V_s + V_c) / V_c$, $V_c = V_s / (r - 1)$ - $IP = (P_m V_s N) / 120$ for 4-stroke; $(P_m V_s N) / 60$ for 2-stroke - $BP = \eta_{mech} \times IP$ - $\eta_{ith} = IP / (m_f \times CV)$ - ⭐ **Typical 2-wheeler compression ratio:** 9–11.5 (SI petrol), 18–22 (CI diesel for 3-wheelers). - ⭐ **Typical 2/3-wheeler IMEP:** 7–10 bar. - ⭐ **Why the flywheel matters:** Single-cylinder engines have only 1 power stroke per 720°; the flywheel supplies kinetic energy for the remaining 540°. - ⭐ **Valve timing:** Intake opens before TDC, closes after BDC; exhaust opens before BDC, closes after TDC — to exploit gas inertia and improve volumetric efficiency. - ⭐ **Ignition timing:** Spark occurs a few degrees BTDC during compression, not exactly at TDC, so peak pressure occurs ~15°–20° ATDC for maximum work extraction. - ⭐ **Cooling:** Most sub-350 cc 2-wheelers are air-cooled (fins on cylinder and head); higher-capacity bikes and three-wheelers increasingly use liquid cooling for better thermal management. - ⭐ **Lubrication:** Small 2-stroke 2-wheelers use oil-mist lubrication (petroil mix); 4-stroke engines use a wet-sump forced lubrication system with an oil pump driven by the crankshaft. - ⭐ **Emission norms:** BS-VI (India) compliance requires precise fuel metering (FI), catalytic converters, and sometimes secondary air injection — all of which add components to the exhaust and intake systems.

topics.title: Fuel Systems and Carburettors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT631 - TWO AND THREE WHEELED VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fuel Systems and Carburettors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fuel System in Two and Three Wheeled Vehicles

### Formal Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Fuel System** in a two/three-wheeler is the integrated assembly of components (fuel tank, fuel cock, fuel pump or gravity feed line, filter, carburettor or fuel injection system, and intake manifold) that is responsible for **metering, mixing, and delivering a combustible air–fuel mixture** to the engine cylinders in the correct proportion, at the correct pressure, and at the correct time across all operating conditions.

In a 2W/3W (typically fitted with a small displacement **4-stroke SI engine** ranging from 100 cc to 500 cc for two-wheelers, and up to **350–500 cc** for three-wheelers), the fuel system has to handle three critical tasks:

1. **Storage and supply** of liquid fuel from the tank to the metering device.
2. **Atomization and vaporization** of fuel to form a homogeneous air–fuel mixture.
3. **Metering** the correct air–fuel ratio (AFR) for a wide range of loads and speeds.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Fuel System is like a Café Barista"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of the fuel system as a skilled barista making coffee.
> - The **fuel tank** = the coffee bean container.
> - The **fuel cock/valve** = the tap controlling when coffee flows.
> - The **carburettor** = the barista, who decides *how much* coffee (fuel) to mix with *how much* steam (air) depending on the customer's mood (engine demand).
> - The **intake manifold** = the cup that delivers the prepared coffee to the customer (engine).
> - The **air–fuel mixture** = the final brewed coffee — its strength must vary with the customer's request (engine load).

A small engine (100–150 cc) behaves like a budget café — a **simple carburettor** is sufficient. A high-performance engine (300–500 cc) is like a specialty café — it needs a **Constant Velocity (CV) carburettor** or **Electronic Fuel Injection (EFI)** to brew the perfect mixture every time.

### 1.2 The Carburettor — Core Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Carburettor** is a mechanical device that mixes air and fuel in the correct proportion for combustion in a spark-ignition (SI) engine, by exploiting the **pressure drop (Bernoulli's principle)** created by a *Venturi throat* to draw fuel from a float bowl through a calibrated **jet**.

**Standard Stoichiometric Reference Value:**
- **Stoichiometric Air–Fuel Ratio (AFR) for petrol (gasoline) = 14.7 : 1** by mass.
- This means **14.7 kg of air** are required to completely combust **1 kg of petrol**.

### 1.3 Why Two/Three Wheelers Historically Used Carburettors

- **Cost:** Carburettors are 5–10 times cheaper than electronic fuel injection (EFI) systems.
- **Simplicity:** No ECU, sensors, or wiring harness required — easy to service at roadside workshops (a major consideration in Indian/Kerala market context).
- **Weight:** A carburettor adds only **~0.5–1.5 kg** to the vehicle, important for 2W/3W where lightweight design is critical.
- **Reliability:** Mechanical operation with no software dependency.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The student must be able to *describe the construction and working of simple carburettors, compensating devices, and the air–fuel mixture requirements at different engine operating conditions*.

---

## 1.4 Air–Fuel Mixture Requirements — A Quick Snapshot

The composition of the mixture is characterized by the **equivalence ratio (φ)**:

$$\phi = \frac{(\text{A/F})_{\text{stoichiometric}}}{(\text{A/F})_{\text{actual}}}$$

| Mixture Type | Actual A/F Ratio | Equivalence Ratio (φ) | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Rich Mixture** | 12 : 1 to 14 : 1 | φ > 1 | Maximum Power, Cold Starting |
| **Stoichiometric** | 14.7 : 1 | φ = 1 | Best Fuel Economy & Emissions |
| **Lean Mixture** | 15 : 1 to 18 : 1 | φ < 1 | Cruising, Good Fuel Economy |
| **Very Lean (Burnable Limit)** | ~18 : 1 | φ ≈ 0.82 | Lean-Burn Designs |

---

## 1.5 Engine Operating Conditions vs. Mixture Requirement

| Operating Condition | Required A/F | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Idle (No Load, Low Speed)** | ~12 : 1 (Rich) | Combustion chamber walls are cool; extra fuel prevents misfire |
| **Cruising (Part Load, Steady Speed)** | ~15–16 : 1 (Lean) | Best fuel economy; lower emissions |
| **WOT — Wide Open Throttle (Max Power)** | ~12–13 : 1 (Rich) | Maximum power output; protects exhaust valves |
| **Cold Starting** | ~2–3 : 1 (Very Rich / Choke ON) | High proportion of fuel condenses on cold walls |
| **Acceleration (Sudden Throttle Open)** | Slightly Rich (~12 : 1) | Accelerator pump enriches transient response |
| **Deceleration (Throttle Snap Closed)** | Very Lean / Cut-off | Fuel cut via coasting system in modern bikes |

> [!NOTE]
> A **simple carburettor** delivers only one fixed A/F ratio. Therefore, modern 2W/3W carburettors must include **compensating devices** to meet the mixture requirements listed above.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Bernoulli's Theorem Applied to the Venturi

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Foundation Concept:** A carburettor operates on **Daniel Bernoulli's Principle (1738)**. When a fluid (air) flows through a **convergent–divergent passage (Venturi)**, the velocity increases and the static pressure decreases at the throat. The pressure drop created at the throat is used to suck fuel from the float bowl.

### The Governing Equation (Bernoulli's Theorem):

$$P_1 + \frac{1}{2} \rho_a v_1^2 = P_2 + \frac{1}{2} \rho_a v_2^2$$

Where:
- $P_1$ = Upstream (atmospheric) static pressure, in **Pa (N/m²)**
- $P_2$ = Throat static pressure, in **Pa**
- $\rho_a$ = Density of air, in **kg/m³** (≈ **1.2 kg/m³** at NTP)
- $v_1$ = Upstream air velocity, in **m/s**
- $v_2$ = Throat air velocity, in **m/s**

**Pressure drop across the venturi:**

$$\Delta P = P_1 - P_2 = \frac{1}{2} \rho_a (v_2^2 - v_1^2)$$

This $\Delta P$ is the **driving force** that lifts fuel from the float chamber through the discharge nozzle. Hence, **fuel flow ∝ air flow²** (approximately), giving a roughly correct A/F for steady flow.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Flow Inside a Carburettor

1. **Air entry** through the air cleaner (filters dust) and the choke (restricts air for cold start).
2. **Air acceleration** at the venturi throat — pressure drops below atmospheric.
3. **Pressure differential** $\Delta P$ acts across the fuel discharge nozzle connected to the float bowl.
4. **Fuel discharge** through the main jet (a calibrated orifice) and discharge nozzle.
5. **Atomization** of fuel as a fine spray into the venturi airstream.
6. **Mixing** of air and fuel in the diffuser section downstream of the venturi.
7. **Throttle valve (butterfly)** controls mass flow rate into the engine, hence engine power.
8. **Float mechanism** maintains a constant fuel level in the float bowl, ensuring the jet head is fixed.

---

## 2.3 Essential Components of a Simple Carburettor

| S.No | Component | Function | Typical Material |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Float Chamber** | Maintains a constant fuel level using a float + needle valve | Aluminum die-cast |
| 2 | **Float + Needle Valve** | Cuts off fuel entry from tank when bowl is full | Brass / Polymer float |
| 3 | **Venturi Tube** | Creates a pressure drop to suck fuel | Aluminum / Cast iron |
| 4 | **Throttle Valve (Butterfly)** | Controls air flow rate → engine power output | Brass disc on steel shaft |
| 5 | **Main Jet (Metering Jet)** | Calibrated orifice to meter fuel flow | Brass |
| 6 | **Discharge Nozzle (Emulsion Tube)** | Atomizes fuel into the venturi | Brass |
| 7 | **Choke Valve** | Restricts air entry for cold starting (rich mixture) | Steel disc on shaft |
| 8 | **Idling / Slow-Running System** | Supplies rich mixture at low throttle | Brass jet + screw |
| 9 | **Economizer (Compensating Device)** | Corrects A/F at mid-throttle range | Aux. jet, bleed holes |
| 10 | **Accelerator Pump** | Provides extra fuel on sudden throttle opening | Plunger / diaphragm |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Hot Tip:** Examiners often ask: *"Why is the float level set slightly *below* the discharge nozzle tip?"*
> **Answer:** To prevent fuel from *dribbling out* of the nozzle when the engine is stationary and there is no airflow through the venturi. The fuel level is typically kept **3–5 mm below** the nozzle tip.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **definitive formula reference** for all numerical problems asked from this topic in KTU exams.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\phi = \dfrac{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}}}{(A/F)_{\text{actual}}}$ | Equivalence ratio | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $P_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho_a v_1^2 = P_2 + \frac{1}{2}\rho_a v_2^2$ | Bernoulli equation at venturi | Pa, m/s, kg/m³ |
| 3 | $\Delta P = \frac{1}{2}\rho_a (v_2^2 - v_1^2)$ | Pressure drop at venturi throat | Pa |
| 4 | $Q_f = C_d \cdot A_j \sqrt{2 \rho_f \cdot \Delta P}$ | Volumetric fuel flow through jet | m³/s |
| 5 | $Q_a = C_d \cdot A_v \cdot v_2$ | Air mass flow at venturi throat | m³/s |
| 6 | $\dot{m}_a = \rho_a \cdot Q_a$ | Mass flow rate of air | kg/s |
| 7 | $\dot{m}_f = \rho_f \cdot Q_f$ | Mass flow rate of fuel | kg/s |
| 8 | $(A/F) = \dfrac{\dot{m}_a}{\dot{m}_f}$ | Air–Fuel ratio by mass | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $\text{Volumetric Efficiency } \eta_v = \dfrac{\dot{m}_a}{\rho_a \cdot V_d \cdot \frac{N}{2}}$ | Breathing efficiency of engine | Dimensionless (≈ 0.85 for 2W) |
| 10 | $V_d = \dfrac{\pi}{4} B^2 \cdot L \cdot n$ | Displacement volume of engine | m³ |
| 11 | $P_{\text{indicated}} = \dfrac{p_{mi} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot n}{60}$ | Indicated power (for 2-stroke, 1 working rev/rev) | kW |
| 12 | $P_{\text{brake}} = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60{,}000}$ | Brake power | kW |
| 13 | $\eta_{\text{thermal}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{brake}}}{\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{CV}}$ | Thermal efficiency | Dimensionless |
| 14 | $\rho_f = 750 \text{ kg/m}^3$ | Density of petrol (NTP) | kg/m³ |
| 15 | $\rho_a = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$ | Density of air (NTP) | kg/m³ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Error in KTU Scripts:** Do not confuse **discharge coefficient $C_d$** (typically **0.6–0.8** for fuel jets) with **velocity of fuel in the jet**. They are distinct.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Carburettor calibration** is the heart of small-engine tuning. Mechanics in Kerala's 2W service sector adjust the **main jet size** and **air screw** to match altitude (e.g., hilly regions like Munnar/Wayanad need richer mixtures than coastal areas).
- **Emission compliance** (BS-VI norms) has forced the gradual replacement of carburettors with **EFI** even in 100 cc bikes (e.g., Honda, Hero introduced EFI from 2018 onwards).
- **Racing carburettors** (used in Royal Enfield 500 cc and KTM Duke 200 modified versions) use **multiple venturis**, slide throttles, and accelerator pumps for sharp response.
- **Choke systems** in modern 2W are auto-choke (bi-metallic spring + vacuum piston), unlike manual cable chokes of older bikes.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Fuel Flow Rate Through a Carburettor Jet

### Statement
Derive an expression for the **volumetric flow rate of fuel** through a calibrated jet of a carburettor in terms of the venturi pressure drop $\Delta P$.

### Given
- $A_j$ = Area of main jet (m²)
- $C_d$ = Coefficient of discharge (dimensionless, 0.6–0.8)
- $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$ (Pa)
- $\rho_f$ = Density of fuel = **750 kg/m³**

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Let us assume the float chamber is vented to atmosphere. Hence, the pressure on the fuel side at the discharge nozzle is **$P_1$ (atmospheric)**, and the pressure on the air side at the venturi throat is **$P_2$**.

**Step 1:** Apply **Bernoulli's equation** between the float-bowl surface (point a) and the jet exit (point b):

$$P_a + \frac{1}{2}\rho_f v_a^2 + \rho_f g z_a = P_b + \frac{1}{2}\rho_f v_b^2 + \rho_f g z_b$$

**Step 2:** Assume fuel surface velocity $v_a \approx 0$ (large bowl, slow surface drop) and the jet exit is just at the venturi throat plane ($z_a = z_b$):

$$P_a = P_b + \frac{1}{2}\rho_f v_b^2$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange for the theoretical fuel velocity at jet exit:

$$v_b = \sqrt{\frac{2(P_a - P_b)}{\rho_f}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_f}}$$

**Step 4:** Account for real-world losses using the discharge coefficient $C_d$:

$$v_{f,\text{actual}} = C_d \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_f}}$$

**Step 5:** The volumetric flow rate is the product of velocity and jet area:

$$Q_f = A_j \cdot v_{f,\text{actual}}$$

$$\boxed{Q_f = C_d \cdot A_j \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_f}}}$$

**Step 6:** The mass flow rate of fuel is:

$$\dot{m}_f = \rho_f \cdot Q_f = C_d \cdot A_j \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_f \cdot \Delta P}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** $Q_f$ is proportional to the **square root of the pressure drop** $\Delta P$. Since $\Delta P \propto v_2^2$ (from Bernoulli), the fuel flow becomes proportional to $v_2$ at the venturi — i.e., **fuel flow scales linearly with air flow**, which is exactly what we need for a *roughly constant* A/F.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Relation Between Air-Fuel Ratio and Carburettor Geometry

### Statement
Show that the **Air-Fuel Ratio (A/F) at the venturi throat** is a function of jet area, venturi throat area, and air/fuel densities.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Air volumetric flow through venturi throat:

$$Q_a = A_v \cdot v_2$$

where $A_v$ = throat area, $v_2$ = throat velocity.

**Step 2:** Air mass flow rate:

$$\dot{m}_a = \rho_a \cdot A_v \cdot v_2$$

**Step 3:** Substituting $\Delta P = \frac{1}{2} \rho_a (v_2^2 - v_1^2)$, ignoring $v_1^2$ (since $v_1 \ll v_2$):

$$\Delta P \approx \frac{1}{2} \rho_a v_2^2 \implies v_2 = \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_a}}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting into $\dot{m}_a$:

$$\dot{m}_a = \rho_a \cdot A_v \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_a}} = A_v \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_a \cdot \Delta P}$$

**Step 5:** Fuel mass flow rate (from previous derivation):

$$\dot{m}_f = C_d \cdot A_j \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_f \cdot \Delta P}$$

**Step 6:** Take the ratio $\dot{m}_a / \dot{m}_f$:

$$\frac{\dot{m}_a}{\dot{m}_f} = \frac{A_v}{C_d \cdot A_j} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho_a}{\rho_f}}$$

**Step 7:** The Air–Fuel Ratio (A/F) is therefore:

$$\boxed{(A/F) = \frac{A_v}{C_d \cdot A_j} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho_a}{\rho_f}}}$$

**Step 8:** Substituting standard values $\rho_a = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_f = 750 \text{ kg/m}^3$:

$$\sqrt{\frac{\rho_a}{\rho_f}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.2}{750}} = 0.04$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** This equation tells us that to enrich the mixture (lower A/F), we need to **decrease $A_v$ (close throttle)** or **increase $A_j$ (larger jet)**. Conversely, to lean the mixture, we **enlarge the venturi** or **use a smaller jet**.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Example (Typical KTU Exam Style)

### Problem
A small two-wheeler carburettor has a venturi throat diameter of **20 mm** and a main jet diameter of **1.2 mm**. The discharge coefficient for the jet is **0.70**. Assuming a discharge coefficient of 1.0 for air at the venturi, calculate the air–fuel ratio supplied by the carburettor. Take $\rho_a = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and $\rho_f = 750 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Compute areas.

$$A_v = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.020)^2 = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_j = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.0012)^2 = 1.1310 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2:** Apply the A/F ratio formula:

$$(A/F) = \frac{A_v}{C_d \cdot A_j} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho_a}{\rho_f}}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values:

$$(A/F) = \frac{3.1416 \times 10^{-4}}{0.70 \times 1.1310 \times 10^{-6}} \times \sqrt{\frac{1.2}{750}}$$

**Step 4:** Numerator of the first fraction:

$$3.1416 \times 10^{-4} = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 5:** Denominator of the first fraction:

$$0.70 \times 1.1310 \times 10^{-6} = 7.917 \times 10^{-7}$$

**Step 6:** First fraction (geometry ratio):

$$\frac{3.1416 \times 10^{-4}}{7.917 \times 10^{-7}} = 396.86$$

**Step 7:** Density ratio:

$$\sqrt{\frac{1.2}{750}} = \sqrt{0.0016} = 0.04$$

**Step 8:** Final A/F:

$$(A/F) = 396.86 \times 0.04 = 15.87$$

$$\boxed{(A/F) \approx 15.87 : 1}$$

This is a **lean mixture**, suitable for cruising conditions. The result lies in the typical 2W cruising A/F band of 15:1 to 16:1.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Parameter Variation

| Parameter Changed | Effect on Fuel Flow ($\dot{m}_f$) | Effect on A/F | Resulting Mixture |
|---|---|---|---|
| Larger main jet ($A_j \uparrow$) | Increases | Decreases | Richer |
| Smaller main jet ($A_j \downarrow$) | Decreases | Increases | Leaner |
| Throttle partially closed | Decreases air flow | (depends) | Idle system takes over, rich |
| Venturi blockage (choke ON) | Same, but air $\downarrow$ | Decreases A/F | Very rich (cold start) |
| Higher altitude (lower $\rho_a$) | Decreases $\dot{m}_f$ less than $\dot{m}_a$ | Increases A/F | Leaner (needs re-jetting) |
| Higher air temperature (lower $\rho_a$) | Decreases | Increases | Leaner |
| Float level too high | Spills fuel | Decreases | Excessively rich |
| Float level too low | Reduced fuel at nozzle | Increases | Leaner (frequent misfire) |
| Accelerator pump extra shot | Adds fuel transiently | Decreases A/F briefly | Richer for ~1 second |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Carburettor System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[FUEL TANK] --> A2[FUEL COCK]
    A2 --> A3[FUEL FILTER]
    A3 --> A4[FLOAT BOWL]
    A4 --> A5[NEEDLE VALVE]
    A5 --> A6[MAIN JET]
    A6 --> A7[DISCHARGE NOZZLE]
    A7 --> A8[VENTURI THROAT]
    B1[AIR CLEANER] --> B2[CHOKE VALVE]
    B2 --> A8
    A8 --> A9[THROTTLE VALVE BUTTERFLY]
    A9 --> A10[INTAKE MANIFOLD]
    A10 --> A11[ENGINE CYLINDER]
    A4 -. Float feedback .-> A5
    A12[IDLE JET] --> A8
    A13[ACCELERATOR PUMP] --> A8
    A14[AUX COMPENSATING JET] --> A6
```

## 4.2 Detailed Subsystem Diagram — Carburettor Internals

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A[VENTURI AND MIXING SECTION]
        A1[Clean Air Entry] --> A2[Choke Butterfly]
        A2 --> A3[Venturi Convergent]
        A3 --> A4[Venturi Throat]
        A4 --> A5[Venturi Divergent]
        A5 --> A6[Throttle Butterfly]
        A6 --> A7[Mixture to Engine]
    end
    subgraph B[FLOAT BOWL ASSEMBLY]
        B1[Fuel Inlet from Tank] --> B2[Needle Valve]
        B2 --> B3[Float Body]
        B3 -. Pivot Arm .-> B2
        B3 --> B4[Constant Fuel Level]
    end
    subgraph C[METERING SYSTEM]
        C1[Main Jet] --> C2[Discharge Nozzle]
        C3[Compensating Air Bleed] --> C1
        C4[Economizer Jet] --> C1
    end
    subgraph D[LOW SPEED SYSTEM]
        D1[Idle Jet] --> D2[Idle Discharge Port]
        D3[Idle Air Bleed] --> D1
        D4[Idle Adjustment Screw] --> D1
    end
    subgraph E[ACCELERATION SYSTEM]
        E1[Accelerator Pump Piston] --> E2[Pump Discharge Nozzle]
        E3[Throttle Linkage] --> E1
    end
    B4 --> C1
    C2 --> A4
    D2 --> A3
    E2 --> A5
```

## 4.3 Mixture Control Flow Chart — By Operating Condition

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q1{Engine Operating Condition}
    Q1 -->|Cold Start| R1[Choke Fully Closed]
    R1 --> R2[Very Rich Mixture 2 to 4 to 1]
    Q1 -->|Idling Low Load| S1[Throttle Near Closed]
    S1 --> S2[Idle Jet Active]
    S2 --> S3[Rich Mixture 10 to 12 to 1]
    Q1 -->|Cruising Part Load| T1[Throttle Slightly Open]
    T1 --> T2[Main and Compensating Jets]
    T2 --> T3[Stoichiometric or Slightly Lean]
    Q1 -->|Wide Open Throttle| U1[Throttle Fully Open]
    U1 --> U2[Main Jet and Economizer]
    U2 --> U3[Rich Mixture 12 to 13 to 1 Maximum Power]
    Q1 -->|Sudden Acceleration| V1[Throttle Snapped Open]
    V1 --> V2[Accelerator Pump Adds Fuel]
    V2 --> V3[Temporary Enrichment to Prevent Lean Backfire]
    Q1 -->|Deceleration Closed Throttle| W1[Over Run Fuel Cut]
    W1 --> W2[Zero Fuel Supply]
```

## 4.4 Comparison: Carburettor vs Electronic Fuel Injection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CARB[CARBURETTOR SYSTEM]
        C1[Fuel Tank] --> C2[Cock]
        C2 --> C3[Bowl with Float]
        C3 --> C4[Mechanical Jets and Compensators]
        C4 --> C5[Venturi Mixing]
    end
    subgraph EFI[EFI SYSTEM]
        E1[Fuel Tank] --> E2[Electric Pump]
        E2 --> E3[Inline Filter]
        E3 --> E4[Pressure Regulator]
        E4 --> E5[Injector Nozzle]
        E5 --> E6[Intake Port]
        E7[ECU] -->|Pulse Width Signal| E5
        E8[Throttle Position Sensor] --> E7
        E9[Oxygen Sensor] --> E7
        E10[Air Intake Sensor MAP] --> E7
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note:** In the above EFI diagram, the **ECU** (Electronic Control Unit) reads multiple sensors and adjusts the **injector pulse width** to deliver a precisely metered A/F. This is why modern 2W/3W under **BS-VI** regulations have largely moved to EFI. However, carburettors still dominate the **2W market in countries with BS-IV or older emission norms**, which is why KTU continues to include this topic.

## 4.5 Schematic (Block Equivalent) — Working of a Solex Type CV Carburettor

> [!NOTE]
> Since drawing a fully detailed CV (Constant Velocity) carburettor in Mermaid is impractical, the following block-level functional architecture illustrates the **vacuum-operated slide and piston mechanism** that maintains a near-constant pressure drop across the main jet — the key innovation that improves over the simple carburettor.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    F1[Air Entry] --> F2[Throttle Valve at Bottom]
    F2 --> F3[Vacuum Piston Rises with Air Flow]
    F3 --> F4[Needle Attached to Piston Moves Up with Piston]
    F4 --> F5[Needle Inside Main Jet Alters Effective Orifice Area]
    F6[Fuel Bowl at Atmospheric Pressure] --> F7[Main Jet Below Needle]
    F5 --> F7
    F7 --> F8[Discharge into Venturi at Fixed Delta P]
    F8 --> F9[Engine Cylinder]
    F10[Vacuum at Throttle Below Piston] -.Lifts.-> F3
    F11[Fuel from Tank] --> F6
```

**Block-Level Functionality Notes:**
- As the rider opens the throttle, airflow increases and the **vacuum below the piston increases** (pressure drops).
- The **piston rises**, lifting the tapered needle and **gradually enlarging the main jet passage**.
- This **automatically compensates** for the otherwise rich-lean swings of a simple carburettor.
- The result is a **near-constant pressure drop** across the main jet, hence the name **Constant Velocity (CV) carburettor**.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Q1]
**Define the term "Equivalence Ratio" (φ). What does φ > 1 and φ < 1 signify in the context of carburettor operation?**

### Model Answer
> The **equivalence ratio** is the ratio of the stoichiometric air–fuel ratio to the actual air–fuel ratio:
>
> $$\phi = \frac{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}}}{(A/F)_{\text{actual}}}$$
>
> - **φ > 1** : The mixture is **richer than stoichiometric** (excess fuel, deficiency of air). This condition is used for **maximum power** and **cold starting**.
> - **φ = 1** : **Stoichiometric mixture** — chemically correct proportion (14.7 : 1 for petrol). Best for **fuel economy** and **lowest emissions**.
> - **φ < 1** : The mixture is **leaner than stoichiometric** (excess air, deficiency of fuel). Used during **cruising** for best fuel economy.
>
> *[Total: 3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 each for φ>1 and φ<1 explanation]*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q2]
**State Bernoulli's theorem as applicable to a carburettor. Why is a venturi used in the air passage of a carburettor?**

### Model Answer
> Bernoulli's theorem states that for a steady, incompressible, frictionless flow, the sum of pressure energy, kinetic energy, and potential energy per unit weight remains constant along a streamline. For a horizontal venturi:
>
> $$P + \frac{1}{2}\rho v^2 = \text{constant}$$
>
> In a carburettor, the **venturi** is a convergent–divergent passage that **increases the air velocity** at the throat. According to Bernoulli's principle, this **velocity increase produces a static pressure drop** at the throat. The pressure drop below atmospheric pressure is used to **draw fuel from the float bowl through a calibrated jet**, enabling mixing of fuel with air in the correct proportion.
>
> *[Total: 3 marks — 1.5 for statement, 1.5 for venturi justification]*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Q]

**Q.A.** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working of a simple carburettor**. Discuss its limitations and explain how **compensating devices** overcome them.

**Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks:** Describe the construction and working of a simple carburettor with neat sketch.

**Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks:** Discuss the limitations of a simple carburettor and explain the role of compensating devices.

---

#### Model Solution — Sub-part (a)

**Construction of a Simple Carburettor:**

A simple (elementary) carburettor consists of the following main parts:

1. **Float Chamber:** A small reservoir that maintains a constant fuel level using a **hollow float (brass or polymer)** and a **needle valve**. When fuel rises, the float pushes the needle against its seat, cutting off inflow.

2. **Venturi Tube:** A convergent–divergent duct in the air passage. The throat has the smallest cross-section and therefore the highest air velocity and lowest static pressure.

3. **Main Discharge Nozzle:** A small vertical tube positioned at the venturi throat, drawing fuel by the pressure drop.

4. **Main Jet (Metering Jet):** A calibrated brass orifice that meters the fuel flow from the float bowl to the discharge nozzle.

5. **Throttle Valve (Butterfly):** A disc-shaped valve on a shaft, rotated by the accelerator cable to control air inflow.

6. **Choke Valve:** A butterfly valve at the carburettor entry that restricts air for cold starting (rich mixture).

**Working Principle:**

- The piston descending during the intake stroke creates a **low pressure** in the intake manifold.
- Atmospheric pressure pushes air through the air cleaner, choke, and venturi.
- At the venturi throat, **velocity rises** and **pressure falls** below atmospheric (Bernoulli).
- The float bowl is at atmospheric pressure, so a **pressure differential** exists across the main jet.
- This pressure differential lifts fuel through the main jet, atomizes it at the nozzle, and discharges it into the venturi.
- The fuel mixes with air to form a combustible mixture which flows past the throttle into the cylinder.

**Neat Sketch (Block-Level Equivalent):**

> See Section 4.2 for the block diagram. A traditional KTU exam sketch should clearly label: air intake, choke, venturi, main jet, float chamber, float, needle valve, throttle valve, intake manifold, and engine cylinder.

---

#### Model Solution — Sub-part (b)

**Limitations of a Simple Carburettor:**

1. **Fixed A/F Ratio:** A simple carburettor delivers a nearly constant A/F (~15:1) regardless of engine speed or load. This is not ideal because the engine requires **rich mixture** for cold start and maximum power, and **lean mixture** for cruising.

2. **Insufficient Fuel at Low Speed:** At very low throttle opening, the air velocity in the venturi is too low to produce a sufficient pressure drop, leading to a **weak (lean) mixture** at idling — the engine may stall.

3. **Excess Fuel at High Speed:** At high air velocity, the fuel discharge increases more than linearly, producing a **rich mixture** at high speeds.

4. **No Acceleration Enrichment:** On a sudden throttle opening, the sudden rush of air reaches the cylinder **before** the slower-responding fuel column, causing a **temporary lean condition and "backfire"** in the exhaust.

5. **No Provision for Cold Start:** No choke or auxiliary enrichment device is present.

**Compensating Devices and Their Function:**

| Device | Function |
|---|---|
| **Auxiliary Compensation Jet** | Bleeds air into the main fuel stream to weaken the mixture at high speeds |
| **Main Air Bleed (Emulsion Tube)** | Introduces air into the fuel above the main jet, atomizing it into an emulsion; flattens the fuel-flow curve |
| **Economizer (Power Jet)** | Provides extra fuel at WOT via a separate jet, activated by vacuum or throttle position |
| **Slow-Running (Idle) System** | Idle jet + idle air bleed supplies a richer mixture at low throttle via a separate port upstream of the throttle |
| **Accelerator Pump** | A spring-loaded or vacuum-operated piston that injects a measured fuel shot on sudden throttle opening, preventing lean backfire |
| **Choke Valve** | A butterfly valve that restricts air for cold-start enrichment |
| **Float Level Adjustment** | Maintains the correct fuel head for consistent jet metering |

> **Conclusion:** Through the combined action of these devices, the carburettor delivers **rich mixture at idle and WOT**, **stoichiometric or slightly lean at cruise**, and **transient enrichment during acceleration** — closely matching the engine's requirements across all operating conditions.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do not** label the float chamber as "fuel tank" in the sketch — examiners deduct **1 mark**.
> - **Always show** the needle valve as a separate component from the float — they are connected but functionally distinct.
> - **Always** state the value of stoichiometric A/F = 14.7 : 1 in your answer.
> - In **sub-part (b)**, students often forget to mention the **accelerator pump** — this is a **favourite question** and missing it costs **1.5 marks**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Q]

**Q.B.** (i) With the help of a diagram, explain the working of a **Constant Velocity (CV) carburettor**. How does it maintain a constant pressure drop across the main jet? (7 marks)

(ii) A two-wheeler carburettor has a venturi throat diameter of **22 mm** and a main jet diameter of **1.3 mm**. The coefficient of discharge for the jet is **0.72**. Calculate the air–fuel ratio supplied. Take $\rho_a = 1.2$ kg/m³ and $\rho_f = 750$ kg/m³. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Sub-part (i)

**Constant Velocity (CV) Carburettor — Working Principle:**

A **CV carburettor** (also known as a **Solex-type** or **vacuum-piston carburettor**) is the standard carburettor used in almost all modern 2W in India (e.g., Hero Honda Splendor, Bajaj Pulsar older models). The key feature is a **vacuum-operated piston** (or diaphragm) that varies the main jet area through a tapered needle, maintaining a **constant pressure drop** across the main jet.

**Working Steps:**

1. The rider opens the throttle (butterfly) using the accelerator.
2. Air rushes through the venturi, increasing velocity and decreasing pressure at the throat.
3. The **piston (or diaphragm)** sits above the venturi and is exposed to this low venturi pressure on its underside. Atmospheric pressure acts on its top side.
4. The **pressure differential** lifts the piston against spring force.
5. A **tapered needle** is attached to the bottom of the piston, sliding inside the main jet.
6. As the piston rises, the needle lifts, **enlarging the effective jet area**.
