7. This **automatically compensates** for the larger fuel demand at higher air flow, maintaining a near-constant A/F.
8. The pressure drop across the main jet is **kept constant** because the piston's height is itself a function of the venturi depression — equilibrium is achieved dynamically.

**Advantages of CV Carburettor:**
- Near-constant A/F across all loads.
- Better fuel economy.
- Smooth throttle response.

> See **Section 4.5** for the block-level functional schematic.

---

#### Model Solution — Sub-part (ii)

**Given:**
- Venturi throat diameter, $D_v = 22$ mm $= 22 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- Main jet diameter, $D_j = 1.3$ mm $= 1.3 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- Coefficient of discharge for jet, $C_d = 0.72$
- $\rho_a = 1.2$ kg/m³
- $\rho_f = 750$ kg/m³

**Step 1:** Calculate venturi area $A_v$:

$$A_v = \frac{\pi}{4} D_v^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (22 \times 10^{-3})^2$$

$$A_v = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 484 \times 10^{-6} = 3.801 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2:** Calculate jet area $A_j$:

$$A_j = \frac{\pi}{4} D_j^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (1.3 \times 10^{-3})^2$$

$$A_j = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 1.69 \times 10^{-6} = 1.327 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3:** Apply the A/F ratio formula:

$$(A/F) = \frac{A_v}{C_d \cdot A_j} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho_a}{\rho_f}}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute values:

$$(A/F) = \frac{3.801 \times 10^{-4}}{0.72 \times 1.327 \times 10^{-6}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1.2}{750}}$$

**Step 5:** Denominator of first fraction:

$$0.72 \times 1.327 \times 10^{-6} = 9.554 \times 10^{-7}$$

**Step 6:** First fraction:

$$\frac{3.801 \times 10^{-4}}{9.554 \times 10^{-7}} = 397.84$$

**Step 7:** Density ratio:

$$\sqrt{\frac{1.2}{750}} = \sqrt{0.0016} = 0.04$$

**Step 8:** Final A/F:

$$(A/F) = 397.84 \times 0.04 = 15.91$$

$$\boxed{(A/F) \approx 15.91 : 1}$$

**Conclusion:** The A/F ratio of 15.91 : 1 is slightly **leaner than stoichiometric** and falls in the **cruising / part-load** range — ideal for a 2W cruising at 50–60 km/h.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Numerical):**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting to convert mm → m. The factor of $10^6$ error will result in A/F of $1.5 \times 10^7$ — marks zero. **Always square the conversion factor.**
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Using $C_d = 1$ for the jet (it should be ~0.7). This is a 30% error.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Mixing up $\sqrt{\rho_a/\rho_f}$ with $\sqrt{\rho_f/\rho_a}$. The ratio is **air density under the root, fuel density under the root** (because fuel flow depends on $\sqrt{\rho_f}$ in the denominator).
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Writing the final A/F without units (e.g., "15.91") — full marks require "**15.91 : 1**".

---

## 5.3 Additional Practice Questions (For Internal Assessment)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following questions are typical **internal exam** (Series Test 1 / 2) level and provide additional preparation.

### Practice 1 (5 marks)
**Explain with a sketch the function of an accelerator pump in a carburettor.** Why is it necessary?

### Practice 2 (5 marks)
**Compare a simple carburettor with a CV carburettor** in terms of construction, working, A/F consistency, and cost.

### Practice 3 (10 marks — full module question)
**A 150 cc 4-stroke SI engine runs at 4000 rpm. The carburettor has a venturi diameter of 25 mm and a main jet diameter of 1.5 mm. The density of air is 1.2 kg/m³, fuel is 750 kg/m³, $C_d$ for jet is 0.75. If the volumetric efficiency is 0.85, find (a) the air–fuel ratio, (b) the fuel flow rate in kg/h, and (c) the brake power if the brake thermal efficiency is 25% and CV of fuel is 44 MJ/kg.**

*(Model solution should apply the formula from Section 3.2 and integrate it with the engine displacement and rpm.)*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Bullet Points**

### Core Concepts
- ✅ Carburettor operates on **Bernoulli's principle** — pressure drop at venturi draws fuel.
- ✅ **Venturi** = convergent-divergent duct creating velocity increase and pressure decrease.
- ✅ **Float chamber** maintains a constant fuel level ≈ 3–5 mm below the nozzle tip.
- ✅ **Stoichiometric A/F for petrol = 14.7 : 1** by mass.
- ✅ **Equivalence ratio $\phi = (A/F)_{\text{stoich}}/(A/F)_{\text{actual}}$**.

### Mixture Requirements
- ✅ **Rich** at idle (10–12:1) and WOT (12–13:1).
- ✅ **Lean/Stoichiometric** at cruising (15–16:1).
- ✅ **Very rich** at cold start (choke ON).
- ✅ **Lean spike** prevented by accelerator pump during sudden throttle opening.

### Components
- ✅ Float + needle valve → constant fuel level.
- ✅ Float bowl vented to atmosphere.
- ✅ Main jet, compensating jet, economizer jet, idle jet.
- ✅ Accelerator pump, choke, slow-running system.

### Formulas to Memorize
- ✅ $Q_f = C_d A_j \sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho_f}$
- ✅ $(A/F) = (A_v / C_d A_j) \sqrt{\rho_a / \rho_f}$
- ✅ $\phi = (A/F)_{\text{stoich}} / (A/F)_{\text{actual}}$

### CV Carburettor Highlight
- ✅ **Vacuum piston + tapered needle** maintains **constant $\Delta P$** across the main jet.
- ✅ Result: **near-constant A/F** across load range.
- ✅ Standard on most 2W until 2018; now replaced by **EFI** in BS-VI vehicles.

### Common Pitfalls in KTU Exams
- ❌ Don't forget unit conversion ($10^{-3}$ for mm).
- ❌ Don't confuse $C_d$ for jet (~0.7) with $C_d$ for venturi (~1.0).
- ❌ Don't label the float chamber as fuel tank.
- ❌ Always mention 14.7 : 1 when stoichiometric ratio is asked.
- ❌ For Q.B. numerical, present the final answer with **units and box**.

> **End of Notes — Module 1: Fuel Systems and Carburettors**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Transmissions (Manual, Automatic, and CVT)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT631 - TWO AND THREE WHEELED VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Transmissions (Manual, Automatic, and CVT)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Transmission?

A **Transmission** in a two or three-wheeled vehicle is a speed/torque-modifying mechanism interposed between the engine crankshaft and the driving wheels (or final drive). Its fundamental purpose is to vary the **torque-speed characteristics** delivered to the rear wheel so the engine can operate within its narrow sweet-spot (the region of best BSFC — Brake Specific Fuel Consumption) across varying road and load conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Standard):** "A transmission is a power transmission system that transmits the power from the engine to the driving wheels by varying the torque and speed according to the vehicle operating conditions."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Bicycle Gear Analogy

Imagine you are cycling up a steep hill. On a single-speed cycle, you would either spin the pedals too fast on flat roads (wasting energy as heat) or simply *stall* going uphill. To handle both, the bicycle uses **gears** — small front chainrings for climbs, large ones for speed.

Now replace your legs with an **IC engine**, which has a *fixed* narrow RPM band of high efficiency (typically **2,500 – 5,000 rpm** for a small two-wheeler). A transmission acts like an "automatic gear-shifting helper" between the engine's *fixed-output shaft* and the *wheel*, which needs a variable torque/speed depending on slope, load, and acceleration. Just as you manually (or automatically) change bicycle gears, the transmission in your motorcycle or scooter does the same — but with engineered precision.

### 1.3 Classification of Transmissions in Two & Three Wheelers

Transmissions for 2W/3W are broadly classified into **three families**:

| Transmission Family | Operating Principle | Typical 2W/3W Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Manual Transmission (MT)** | Driver-actuated gear selection using a clutch and shift fork | Royal Enfield, KTM Duke, Yamaha R15, TVS Apache |
| **Automatic Transmission (AT)** | Hydraulic torque converter + planetary (epicyclic) gearsets, no manual clutch | Honda Activa (V-Matic), Honda Dio (roller-weight V-belt CVT is a sub-class) |
| **Continuously Variable Transmission (CVT)** | Variable-diameter pulleys provide infinitely variable ratio within a range | Most scooters: Honda Activa, Suzuki Access, TVS Jupiter, Vespa |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU:** Automatic Transmission in the strict engineering sense refers to the *hydraulic torque converter + planetary gearbox* system used in cars. In the 2W/3W industry, what is colloquially called "automatic" is almost always a **CVT**. You must understand both clearly for KTU questions.

### 1.4 Why Different Transmission Types for 2W vs 3W?

- **Two-Wheelers (Motorcycles):** Demand engagement, sporty feel, and efficiency → predominantly **Manual (synchromesh)**.
- **Scooters:** Urban stop-go traffic → simple twist-and-go operation → **CVT**.
- **Three-Wheelers (Auto-rickshaws):** Heavy load, low-speed torque, driver convenience → **CVT** (petrol CNG autos) or **planetary/constant-mesh AT** (battery e-rickshaws often use single-speed reduction).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Manual Transmission — Internal Architecture

A manual transmission uses a set of gears of varying sizes on parallel or countershafts. The driver engages them through a clutch pedal and shift fork. Three historical subtypes exist:

#### A. Sliding Mesh Gearbox (Oldest, Rare Today)
- Gears are physically slid along splined shafts to engage.
- Used in pre-1970s motorcycles (e.g., Royal Enfield Bullet up to 2008 in some markets).
- **Drawback:** Gear clash noise, requires double-declutching, no synchromesh.

#### B. Constant Mesh Gearbox
- All gears are permanently in mesh with their countershaft counterparts.
- Only the dog clutches (sliding sleeves) move to lock a particular ratio.
- **Advantages over Sliding Mesh:** Less wear, quieter, gears always rotating (oil splash lubricated).

#### C. Synchromesh Gearbox (Modern Standard)
- Adds a **synchromesh unit** (brass/bronze synchronizer ring with friction cone) to each sliding sleeve.
- The synchro ring equalizes speeds *before* dog teeth engage → **eliminating clash and allowing single-declutch shifting**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Modern KTM, Yamaha, Bajaj, and Royal Enfield motorcycles universally use **5-speed or 6-speed constant-mesh synchromesh** gearboxes. Always draw a layout diagram if asked in KTU Part B (14-mark).

### 2.2 Automatic Transmission (AT) — Hydraulic Torque Converter + Epicyclic Gearbox

In strict automobile terminology, an Automatic Transmission (AT) uses two core components:

#### 1. Torque Converter (Fluid Coupling + Clutch)
- Three elements: **Impeller (P)**, **Turbine (T)**, **Stator (S)**.
- Transfers torque via automatic transmission fluid (ATF).
- Provides a **torque multiplication factor** (typically **1.8 to 2.5×**) at stall, decaying to ~1.0× at lockup.

#### 2. Epicyclic (Planetary) Gearset
- Three elements: **Sun gear (S)**, **Planet gears (P)**, **Ring gear (R)**, mounted on a **Carrier (C)**.
- Provides multiple forward ratios by fixing/holding one element and driving another.
- **Why epicyclic?** Compact, coaxial, simultaneous engagement of multiple ratios → enables seamless shifts.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Reality Check:** Pure hydraulic ATs are rare in Indian 2W/3W. They are found in large maxi-scooters (Suzuki Burgman 650, BMW C650) and some three-wheeled cargo vehicles. The **Honda Activa-class "automatic"** is technically a CVT, not a true AT.

### 2.3 Continuously Variable Transmission (CVT)

CVT replaces discrete gears with a **stepless ratio mechanism**, typically using:

#### A. V-Belt / V-Chain CVT (Most Common in Scooters)
- Two variable-pitch pulleys: **Driving (engine side)** and **Driven (clutch side)**.
- Connected by a **V-belt** (Kevlar-reinforced rubber) or **V-chain** (modern, more durable).
- The driving pulley has a **sliding sheave** actuated by **roller weights** (centrifugal force) or electronically.

#### B. Toroidal CVT (Niche, Premium)
- Uses **two toroidal discs** and **rollers** instead of pulleys.
- Tilting the rollers changes the contact radius → stepless ratio.
- Used in some experimental and high-end vehicles (e.g., NSK, Extroid by Nissan).

#### C. Hydrostatic / Magnetic CVT (Theoretical / Niche)
- Uses fluid pumps or magnetic coupling — not used in production 2W/3W.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `|` inside table cells to prevent markdown-breaking.

| # | Formula / Symbol | Expression | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Gear Ratio (GR)** | $GR = \dfrac{N_{\text{in}}}{N_{\text{out}}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{out}}}{T_{\text{in}} \cdot \eta}$ | Ratio of input speed to output speed | dimensionless |
| 2 | **Output Torque** | $T_{\text{out}} = T_{\text{in}} \times GR \times \eta_{\text{tr}}$ | Torque available at wheel | N·m |
| 3 | **Output Speed** | $N_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{in}}}{GR}$ | Wheel rotational speed | rpm |
| 4 | **Torque Converter Slip** | $s = \dfrac{N_{\text{pump}} - N_{\text{turbine}}}{N_{\text{pump}}} \times 100$ | Slip in % | % |
| 5 | **Torque Multiplication (TM)** | $TM = \dfrac{T_{\text{turbine}}}{T_{\text{impeller}}} = f(s)$ | Function of slip; max at stall (~2.0–2.5) | dimensionless |
| 6 | **CVT Effective Ratio** | $GR_{\text{CVT}}(t) = \dfrac{R_{\text{driving}}(t)}{R_{\text{driven}}(t)}$ | Time-varying ratio | dimensionless |
| 7 | **Epicyclic Speed Eqn** | $N_{\text{sun}} S + N_{\text{ring}} R = N_{\text{carrier}} (S + R)$ | Willis Equation (S = sun teeth, R = ring teeth) | rpm |
| 8 | **Overall Driveline Ratio** | $GR_{\text{overall}} = GR_{\text{gearbox}} \times GR_{\text{final\ drive}}$ | Combined transmission + final drive | dimensionless |

Where:
- $\eta_{\text{tr}}$ = Transmission efficiency (**0.92 – 0.97** for helical gears, **0.95 – 0.98** for epicyclic).
- $N_{\text{pump}}$, $N_{\text{turbine}}$ = rotational speeds of impeller and turbine.
- $R_{\text{driving}}(t)$, $R_{\text{driven}}(t)$ = effective pitch radii of CVT pulleys (vary with time).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fuel Economy Tuning:** CVTs keep the engine in its BSFC sweet spot → **+15 to +20%** fuel economy over equivalent MT in city cycle (ARAI certification basis).
- **Down-Sizing:** CVT enables smaller engines to deliver acceptable performance → lower emissions.
- **Driveability:** No torque interruption → smoother ride in heavy traffic (key selling point of Honda Activa, TVS Jupiter).
- **Performance:** MT synchromesh allows direct mechanical engagement → **0% power loss** at engaged gear → preferred for sport motorcycles (KTM RC, Yamaha R15).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Gear Ratio from Rotational Kinematics

Consider two meshing gears — pinion (driver) with $N_p$ teeth and gear (driven) with $N_g$ teeth, both having the same module $m$.

**Step 1:** The linear pitch-line velocity must be identical at the point of contact (no slip):

$$v_{\text{pinion}} = v_{\text{gear}}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $v = \pi \cdot d \cdot N$ where $d$ is pitch diameter and $N$ is rotational speed in rev/sec:

$$\pi \cdot d_p \cdot N_p = \pi \cdot d_g \cdot N_g$$

**Step 3:** Since $d = m \cdot (\text{number of teeth})$ (module times teeth count):

$$\pi \cdot m \cdot N_p \cdot N_p = \pi \cdot m \cdot N_g \cdot N_g$$

**Step 4:** Cancel common terms ($\pi$ and $m$):

$$N_p \cdot N_p^{\text{teeth}} = N_g \cdot N_g^{\text{teeth}}$$

(Using superscript for tooth count to disambiguate from rotational speed variable.)

**Step 5:** Define Gear Ratio:

$$GR = \frac{N_p^{\text{teeth}}}{N_g^{\text{teeth}}} = \frac{N_g}{N_p}$$

**Step 6:** Torque relation — Power in = Power out × efficiency:

$$T_p \cdot \omega_p \cdot \eta = T_g \cdot \omega_g$$

**Step 7:** Solve for output torque:

$$T_g = T_p \cdot \frac{\omega_p}{\omega_g} \cdot \eta = T_p \cdot GR \cdot \eta$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{T_{\text{out}} = T_{\text{in}} \cdot GR \cdot \eta_{\text{tr}} \quad \text{and} \quad N_{\text{out}} = \frac{N_{\text{in}}}{GR}}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Epicyclic Gearbox Speed (Willis Equation)

In an epicyclic (planetary) gearset with **Sun (S)**, **Ring (R)**, and **Carrier (C)** holding the planet gears.

**Step 1:** Imagine "fixing" the carrier and rotating the system backwards by $N_C$. The sun and ring now appear to rotate at:

$$N_S^{\text{rel}} = N_S - N_C$$
$$N_R^{\text{rel}} = N_R - N_C$$

**Step 2:** With carrier fixed, it behaves as a *simple* gear train. The fundamental relation between sun and ring (with carrier fixed) is:

$$\frac{N_S^{\text{rel}}}{N_R^{\text{rel}}} = -\frac{N_R^{\text{teeth}}}{N_S^{\text{teeth}}}$$

The negative sign indicates **opposite rotation directions**.

**Step 3:** Let $N_S^{\text{teeth}} = S$ and $N_R^{\text{teeth}} = R$. Substituting:

$$\frac{N_S - N_C}{N_R - N_C} = -\frac{R}{S}$$

**Step 4:** Cross-multiply:

$$S(N_S - N_C) = -R(N_R - N_C)$$

**Step 5:** Rearrange:

$$S \cdot N_S - S \cdot N_C = -R \cdot N_R + R \cdot N_C$$

**Step 6:** Group $N_C$ terms:

$$S \cdot N_S + R \cdot N_R = N_C (S + R)$$

**Final Result (Willis Equation):**

$$\boxed{N_C = \frac{S \cdot N_S + R \cdot N_R}{S + R}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — CVT Effective Ratio Variation

For a V-belt CVT, the relationship between pulley pitch radii and CVT ratio is:

**Step 1:** Total belt length is conserved (closed loop):

$$L_{\text{belt}} = 2 \cdot d_c + \pi(R_{\text{driving}} + R_{\text{driven}})$$

Where $d_c$ is center distance (fixed). Thus:

$$R_{\text{driving}} + R_{\text{driven}} = \text{constant} = L_0$$

**Step 2:** Define instantaneous CVT ratio as:

$$GR_{\text{CVT}}(t) = \frac{R_{\text{driving}}(t)}{R_{\text{driven}}(t)}$$

**Step 3:** From the conservation relation:

$$R_{\text{driven}}(t) = L_0 - R_{\text{driving}}(t)$$

**Step 4:** Substitute back:

$$GR_{\text{CVT}}(t) = \frac{R_{\text{driving}}(t)}{L_0 - R_{\text{driving}}(t)}$$

**Step 5:** Define extremes — Low Ratio (start-up): $R_{\text{driving}} \to R_{\text{min}}$ → $GR \to R_{\text{min}} / (L_0 - R_{\text{min}})$ (small, gives high torque).

High Ratio (cruise): $R_{\text{driving}} \to R_{\text{max}}$ → $GR \to R_{\text{max}} / (L_0 - R_{\text{max}})$ (large, gives high speed).

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{GR_{\text{CVT}}(t) = \frac{R_{\text{driving}}(t)}{L_0 - R_{\text{driving}}(t)}, \quad R_{\text{driving}} \in [R_{\min}, R_{\max}]}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Exam Favourite)

| Parameter | Manual (Synchromesh) | Automatic (Torque Converter + Epicyclic) | CVT (V-Belt) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Ratio Steps** | Discrete (4, 5, or 6) | Discrete (3 to 6 via epicyclic) | Infinite (stepless) |
| **Efficiency** | 96 – 98% (very high) | 85 – 92% (torque converter loss) | 80 – 90% (belt friction) |
| **Driver Skill** | High (clutch + shift) | Low (P-R-N-D only) | Lowest (twist-and-go) |
| **Fuel Economy** | Good (manual best) | Moderate | Best in stop-go |
| **Sport Feel** | Excellent (direct link) | Limited | Poor (rubber-band feel) |
| **2W Examples** | KTM Duke, RE Classic | BMW C650, Suzuki Burgman 650 | Honda Activa, TVS Jupiter |
| **3W Examples** | Bajaj Maxima (cargo) | Bajaj RE (old), Piaggio Ape | TVS King, Piaggio Ape (auto) |
| **Cost** | Low | High | Moderate |
| **Maintenance** | Low (long life) | High (ATF + filter) | Moderate (belt change 24,000 km) |
| **Weight** | Lightest | Heaviest | Moderate |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram A — Manual Synchromesh Gearbox Layout (Countershaft Type)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    engine[Engine Crankshaft] --> clutch[Multi-plate Clutch]
    clutch --> mainshaftDriven[Mainshaft Driven Gear]
    mainshaftDriven --> countershaft[Countershaft Cluster]
    countershaft --> mainshaftOut[Mainshaft Output Gears 1 to 5]
    mainshaftOut --> synchromesh[Synchromesh Unit with Sliding Sleeves]
    synchromesh --> outputShaft[Output Shaft to Final Drive]
    shiftFork[Shift Fork and Drum] --> synchromesh
    riderFoot[Rider Foot Lever] --> shiftFork
```

### 4.2 Diagram B — Torque Converter + Epicyclic AT (Three-Speed Concept)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    engine[Engine] --> pump[Impeller Pump]
    pump -->|ATF fluid jet| turbine[Turbine]
    turbine --> stator[Stator on One-way Clutch]
    stator -->|returns fluid| pump
    turbine --> inputShaft[Input Shaft to Planetary Set]
    inputShaft --> sunGear[Sun Gear]
    sunGear --> planets[Planet Gears on Carrier]
    planets --> ringGear[Ring Gear]
    ringGear --> outputShaft[Output Shaft]
    bandBrake[Band Brake / Multi-plate Clutch] --> ringGear
    oneWayClutch[One-way Clutch] --> carrier
```

### 4.3 Diagram C — V-Belt CVT Functional Flow (Scooter Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    engine[Engine] --> drivingPulley[Driving Pulley with Sliding Sheave]
    rollerWeights[Centrifugal Roller Weights] -->|throws sheaves apart| drivingPulley
    drivingPulley --> vbelt[V Belt or V Chain]
    vbelt --> drivenPulley[Driven Pulley with Sliding Sheave]
    drivenPulley --> drivenPulleyShaft[Driven Pulley Shaft]
    drivenPulleyShaft --> centrifugalClutch[Centrifugal Automatic Clutch]
    centrifugalClutch --> finalDrive[Final Drive Gearbox]
    finalDrive --> rearWheel[Rear Wheel]
    drivenPulleyShaft -->|also via spring| drivenPulley
```

### 4.4 Diagram D — Block Architecture of CVT Ratio Control

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    throttlePos[Throttle Position Sensor TPS] --> ecu[ECU or Electronic Controller]
    rpmSensor[Engine RPM Sensor] --> ecu
    vssSensor[Vehicle Speed Sensor VSS] --> ecu
    ecu --> actuatorStepper[Stepper Motor or Linear Actuator]
    actuatorStepper --> drivenPulleySheave[Driven Pulley Sliding Sheave]
    drivingPulleySheave[Driving Pulley Sliding Sheave via Roller Weights] -->|mechanical feedback| drivenPulleySheave
    drivenPulleySheave --> ratioVariation[Continuously Variable Ratio]
    ratioVariation --> drivenPulleyShaftOut[Output to Clutch and Final Drive]
```

### 4.5 Diagram E — Manual Gearbox Power Flow (Top-Down Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    e[Engine Output] --> a[Clutch]
    a --> b[Primary Reduction]
    b --> c[Constant Mesh Gear Cluster on Countershaft]
    c --> d[Synchromesh Engagement]
    d --> f[Selected Gear Ratio Applied]
    f --> g[Output Shaft]
    g --> h[Final Drive Chain or Shaft]
    h --> i[Rear Wheel]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Drawing Tip:** Even if Mermaid is not allowed in your answer sheet, sketch the **synchromesh unit**, the **3-element torque converter** (with P-T-S labeled), and the **two-pulley V-belt CVT** with arrows showing ratio variation. These are the 3 highest-mark-scoring diagrams for this module.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Differentiate between a sliding-mesh gearbox and a synchromesh gearbox.

**Model Answer:**
A *sliding-mesh gearbox* requires the gears themselves to be physically slid along a splined shaft to engage, causing gear clash and noise. A *synchromesh gearbox* keeps gears in constant mesh and uses a synchronizer ring to equalize speeds before engagement, enabling smooth and clash-free gear changes. Synchromesh is the modern standard in two-wheeler manual transmissions (e.g., KTM Duke, Royal Enfield).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
What is the function of a torque converter in an automatic transmission?

**Model Answer:**
A torque converter is a hydrodynamic device that transmits engine torque to the transmission using automatic transmission fluid (ATF). It performs three functions:
1. Multiplies engine torque at stall (up to **2.5×**) for launch.
2. Acts as a fluid coupling allowing engine to idle without stalling.
3. Smoothly transfers power without mechanical wear.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Choose Any ONE)

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of a synchromesh gearbox used in two-wheelers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare manual, automatic, and CVT transmissions for two and three-wheelers on the basis of efficiency, driver skill, cost, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Construction (4 marks):**
A modern motorcycle synchromesh gearbox consists of:
1. **Input Shaft (Mainshaft)** — receives power from clutch.
2. **Countershaft** — carries a cluster of gears permanently meshed with mainshaft gears.
3. **Gear pairs for each ratio** (typically 5 or 6) — providing different torque multiplication.
4. **Synchromesh units** — each containing a brass/bronze synchronizer ring with friction cone and dog clutch sleeve.
5. **Shift drum and forks** — operated by rider's foot lever; the drum rotates and slides appropriate fork.
6. **Output shaft** — transmits the selected ratio to the chain/shaft final drive.

**Working (3 marks):**
When the rider presses the shift lever:
- The **shift drum** rotates by a cam profile, moving the **shift fork** laterally.
- The fork slides the **synchronizer sleeve** toward the desired gear.
- The friction surface of the **synchronizer ring** contacts the dog teeth of the gear, equalizing rotational speeds.
- Once speeds match, the sleeve locks onto the dog teeth → torque is transmitted at the selected ratio.
- A **neutral position** exists where no gear is engaged.

**Key Features:** Constant mesh, no gear clash, single-declutch operation possible.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

| Parameter | Manual (Synchromesh) | Automatic (AT) | CVT |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Efficiency** | **95–98%** (highest) | **85–92%** | **80–90%** |
| **Driver Skill** | High (clutch + gear lever) | Low (P-R-N-D selector) | Lowest (twist-and-go) |
| **Cost (approx)** | ₹ 8,000–15,000 | ₹ 35,000–60,000 | ₹ 12,000–20,000 |
| **Maintenance** | Low; periodic oil change | High; ATF + filter change | Moderate; belt change at 24,000 km |
| **2W Application** | Sport bikes, commuter motorcycles (KTM, Yamaha, RE) | Maxi-scooters (BMW C650) | Most scooters (Activa, Jupiter) |
| **3W Application** | Cargo autos (Bajaj Maxima) | Piaggio Ape (older models) | Passenger autos (TVS King, Bajaj RE auto) |
| **Fuel Economy** | Best on highways | Moderate | Best in city stop-go |

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the working of a V-belt Continuously Variable Transmission (CVT) used in scooters. State the role of centrifugal roller weights. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the Willis equation for an epicyclic gearbox and use it to find the speed of the carrier when the sun gear is fixed and the ring gear rotates at 1000 rpm. Take number of teeth: Sun = 20, Ring = 60. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Components (3 marks):**
A V-belt CVT consists of:
1. **Driving Pulley** — on engine crankshaft, has a fixed and a sliding sheave.
2. **Driven Pulley** — on clutch/driven shaft, also has fixed and sliding sheaves.
3. **V-Belt** — Kevlar-reinforced rubber belt (or modern V-chain) running on V-grooves.
4. **Centrifugal Roller Weights** — located in the driving pulley sliding sheave.
5. **Spring-loaded Driven Sheave** — uses a torsional spring to maintain tension.
6. **Final Drive Gearbox** — fixed ratio to the rear wheel.

**Working (3 marks):**
- At **low RPM (idle/start)**: roller weights are retracted → driving pulley has small effective radius → driven pulley has large radius → **low gear ratio** (high torque, low speed) for take-off.
- As **RPM increases**: roller weights fly outward under centrifugal force → driving sheave slides apart → effective radius of driving pulley **increases**.
- Simultaneously, the driven pulley sheave slides together → its radius **decreases** → ratio increases smoothly and continuously.
- At **high RPM (cruise)**: ratio is maximum → high speed, low torque for cruising.

**Role of Centrifugal Roller Weights (1 mark):**
They convert engine RPM into mechanical radial force that automatically varies the pulley ratio, eliminating the need for any electronic or hydraulic actuator in budget scooters. They provide a **mechanical feedback** linking engine speed to transmission ratio.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Derivation of Willis Equation (5 marks):**

Step 1 — Consider an epicyclic gearset with Sun (S), Ring (R), and Carrier (C).

Step 2 — Imagine "freezing" the carrier by subtracting its speed from all elements:

$$N_S^{\text{rel}} = N_S - N_C \quad ; \quad N_R^{\text{rel}} = N_R - N_C$$

Step 3 — With carrier frozen, the system behaves as a simple gear pair:

$$\frac{N_S - N_C}{N_R - N_C} = -\frac{N_R^{\text{teeth}}}{N_S^{\text{teeth}}}$$

Step 4 — Substituting $N_R^{\text{teeth}} = R$ and $N_S^{\text{teeth}} = S$:

$$\frac{N_S - N_C}{N_R - N_C} = -\frac{R}{S}$$

Step 5 — Cross-multiplying and rearranging:

$$S(N_S - N_C) = -R(N_R - N_C)$$

$$S \cdot N_S + R \cdot N_R = N_C (S + R)$$

**Willis Equation:**

$$\boxed{N_C = \frac{S \cdot N_S + R \cdot N_R}{S + R}}$$

---

**Numerical Problem (2 marks):**

**Given:**
- $N_S = 0$ (sun gear fixed)
- $N_R = 1000$ rpm (ring gear rotates at 1000 rpm)
- $S = 20$ teeth, $R = 60$ teeth

**Substituting into Willis Equation:**

$$N_C = \frac{(20)(0) + (60)(1000)}{20 + 60}$$

$$N_C = \frac{0 + 60000}{80}$$

$$N_C = \frac{60000}{80}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{N_C = 750 \text{ rpm}}$$

The carrier rotates at 750 rpm in the same direction as the ring gear.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign convention errors in Willis equation:** Forgetting the negative sign in the relative-velocity step. If the ring gear rotates in the same direction as the carrier, the answer's sign must match — do not lose **1 mark** on this.
> 2. **Confusing AT and CVT:** In KTU exams, if the question says "automatic", do not jump straight to CVT unless the question specifies a scooter. Cars and maxi-scooters use true AT (torque converter). Read the wording carefully.
> 3. **Missing unit on final answers:** $N_C = 750$ without "rpm" loses **0.5 mark** in strict KTU valuation.
> 4. **In CVT diagrams, forgetting to label the spring on the driven pulley** → loses **1 mark** as it is critical to operation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Transmission Definition:** A device to vary torque-speed ratio between engine and wheels.
- [ ] **Three Types:** Manual (synchromesh), Automatic (torque converter + epicyclic), CVT (V-belt / chain).
- [ ] **Manual (MT):** 5/6-speed synchromesh; highest efficiency (**96–98%**); used in sport/commuter motorcycles.
- [ ] **Sliding Mesh vs Synchromesh:** Sliding = old, gear clash; Synchromesh = modern, clash-free via brass synchronizer ring.
- [ ] **Automatic (AT):** Torque converter multiplies torque up to **2.5×** at stall; epicyclic gearbox provides discrete forward ratios.
- [ ] **Torque Converter Slip (s):** $s = \frac{N_P - N_T}{N_P} \times 100$ %.
- [ ] **Epicyclic Elements:** Sun (S), Planet (P), Ring (R), Carrier (C) — four components.
- [ ] **Willis Equation:** $S \cdot N_S + R \cdot N_R = N_C (S + R)$.
- [ ] **CVT Pulleys:** Driving + Driven, connected by V-belt or V-chain.
- [ ] **Centrifugal Roller Weights:** Mechanically convert RPM into radial force → automatic ratio change in budget scooters.
- [ ] **Honda Activa = CVT**, NOT a true automatic — this distinction is a KTU favorite.
- [ ] **V-Chain CVT (modern)** is more durable than V-belt — used in Honda Activa 6G, Suzuki Access 125.
- [ ] **3W Application:** Passenger autos (TVS King, Bajaj RE) → CVT; Cargo autos (Bajaj Maxima) → manual.
- [ ] **Typical Gear Ratio Range for 2W MT:** 1st ≈ **3.0–3.5:1**, 5th/6th ≈ **0.8–1.0:1** (overdrive).
- [ ] **Epicyclic uses:** Compact, coaxial, multi-ratio simultaneous engagement → preferred for AT.
- [ ] **Lock-up Clutch** in modern torque converters eliminates slip at cruising speed → improves efficiency.
- [ ] **BSFC Sweet-Spot:** CVT keeps engine at ~**2,500–3,000 rpm** regardless of vehicle speed → best fuel economy.

---

### 5.4 High-Yield Mnemonic for KTU Viva

> **"M-A-C"** → **M**anual, **A**utomatic, **C**VT — the **3 Transmission Families**.
>
> For Manual, remember: **"Sliding-Constant-Synchro"** (the 3 evolutionary stages).
> For Automatic, remember: **"P-T-S"** (Pump-Turbine-Stator) of the torque converter.
> For CVT, remember: **"Two-Pulleys-and-a-Belt"** with centrifugal rollers on the driving side.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** In any 14-mark Part B question on this topic, always include:
> 1. A **labeled diagram** (4–5 marks weight).
> 2. A **derivation with steps** (3–4 marks).
> 3. A **comparison table** (3 marks).
> 4. A **real-world example** (1 mark for application knowledge).
> Following this structure guarantees full marks under KTU 2024 Scheme valuation norms.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Clutch Systems and Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT631 - TWO AND THREE WHEELED VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Clutch Systems and Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clutch (KTU 2024 Scheme Definition):** A clutch is a mechanical friction device interposed between the engine crankshaft and the transmission input shaft of a two-wheeled or three-wheeled vehicle. Its primary function is to transmit torque from the prime-mover (engine) to the drivetrain while allowing smooth engagement, controlled disengagement, and protection of the driveline from torsional shock during start-up, gear shifts, and idle conditions.

In the specific context of **PEAUT631 – Two and Three Wheeled Vehicles**, clutches are predominantly **dry or wet multi-plate units**, **centrifugal automatic clutches**, or **shoe-type centrifugal clutches**, depending on whether the vehicle is a geared motorcycle (150cc–1500cc), a scooter (CVT-based), or a three-wheeler (auto-rickshaw / cargo loader).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 2):** The emphasis is placed on (a) clutch requirement, (b) types — multi-plate wet, multi-plate dry, centrifugal, and automatic/semi-automatic, (c) clutch plate design (friction material, number of plates, spring force), and (d) operating mechanisms — cable, hydraulic, and automatic.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Think of a clutch the same way you think of **two hands rubbing together**:

- Imagine your **left hand** is rotating at a constant high speed (this is the **engine crankshaft** turning at, say, **5000 RPM**).
- Imagine your **right hand** is connected to a heavy wheel (this is the **rear wheel** of your motorcycle, which is stationary).
- If you slam your left hand onto the right hand, the sudden jerk will break something — this is exactly what happens if the engine is directly bolted to the wheel. The vehicle would stall, the chain would snap, or the gearbox would shatter.
- A **clutch** is the controllable "rubbing interface" between these two hands. It lets you *gradually* increase friction so the spinning engine slowly drags the wheel up to speed without shock.
- When you **squeeze the left-hand lever on the handlebar**, you are essentially telling the clutch to "let go" of the engine so you can change gears. When you **release the lever**, the clutch "bites" and power flows to the rear wheel.

This analogy directly translates to why every geared two-wheeler uses a **left-hand clutch lever** — it is the rider's mechanical interface to control torque transmission.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics in Two/Three-Wheeler Clutches

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Metrics (Bold values for emphasis):**
> - **Coefficient of friction (μ) of organic friction material:** **0.25 to 0.45**
> - **Coefficient of friction (μ) of sintered (cermet) material:** **0.35 to 0.55**
> - **Maximum clutch slip duration (engagement time):** **0.4 to 1.2 seconds**
> - **Typical number of friction plates in a wet multi-plate clutch:** **5 to 8 plates**
> - **Maximum clutch-plate temperature during continuous slipping:** **180 °C to 260 °C**
> - **Operating fluid for wet clutches (engine oil grade):** **SAE 10W-30 to 20W-50 JASO MA / MA2**
> - **Clamping spring load per plate:** **150 N to 600 N** (depending on engine torque)

## 1.4 Why a Clutch is Mandatory in 2W / 3W Vehicles

A clutch is **not optional** in a geared two/three-wheeler because of three fundamental mechanical demands:

| S.No | Demand | Consequence Without Clutch |
|------|--------|----------------------------|
| 1 | Engine cannot be started under load | Engine would stall at every ignition |
| 2 | Gearbox requires interrupted power for shifting | Dog clutches would grind and fail |
| 3 | Vehicle must crawl/idle in traffic without stalling | Rider cannot balance at 0 km/h |
| 4 | Protect driveline from torsional shock | Chain breakage, shaft failure, sprocket wear |
| 5 | Enable smooth launch from standstill | Wheel hop and rider fall |

> [!NOTE]
> Only **scooters with CVT (Continuous Variable Transmission)** and **electric two-wheelers** can omit a manual friction clutch, because their torque coupling is achieved via a **centrifugal clutch** or an **electronic torque-vectoring controller**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Architecture of a 2W/3W Clutch System

The clutch system on a geared motorcycle is a **friction torque-transfer device** with the following functional architecture (read in the order of torque flow):

1. **Engine Crank / Primary Drive Gear** — the rotating input source (driver).
2. **Clutch Basket (Outer Drum)** — a toothed aluminium or steel drum bolted to the primary gear; rotates with the engine.
3. **Friction Plates (Driving Plates)** — steel plates with external teeth that engage the basket slots; rotate with the engine.
4. **Clutch Hub (Centre Splined Hub)** — the inner member; its splines engage the gearbox input shaft.
5. **Driven Plates (Plain Plates with Friction Linings)** — fibre/cork/sintered discs with internal teeth on the hub; *receive* torque via friction.
6. **Pressure Plate + Clamp Spring(s)** — a coil spring or diaphragm spring that clamps the driven plates against the driving plates using axial force **W**.
7. **Actuation Mechanism** — left-hand lever → cable or hydraulic pushrod → release mechanism → spring decompression.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **basket rotates with the engine**, the **hub rotates with the gearbox**, and the **friction between the plates transmits torque**. When the rider squeezes the lever, the spring force is released, plates separate, and torque flow stops.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Design Choices

- **Why multi-plate (not single-plate as in cars)?**
  Multi-plate design is used because a 2W/3W engine has a **small crankcase envelope**. By stacking multiple plates, the same torque capacity is achieved within a smaller *outer diameter*. The torque transmitted scales **linearly with the number of contact surfaces**, while the *outer diameter* stays compact.

- **Why wet clutches dominate modern 2W?**
  Immersing the plates in **engine oil** continuously cools them and carries away wear debris. This allows **5–8 plates in a stack** without thermal failure, and the engagement is smoother because oil film damping reduces judder.

- **Why centrifugal automatic clutch in scooters?**
  Scooters use a **CVT + centrifugal clutch** so that a learner rider needs **no clutch lever**. Engine RPM directly governs engagement — at idle the shoes retract (no drive); above a threshold RPM (typically **2500–3000 RPM**) the centrifugal weights fly outward, closing the shoes on the drum and engaging drive.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in 80% of clutch numericals in KTU ESE.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Variables | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-----------|-------|
| 1 | $T = n \cdot \mu \cdot W \cdot R_{eff}$ | Torque transmitted by multi-plate clutch | $T$=torque, $n$=number of contact surfaces, $\mu$=coeff. of friction, $W$=axial clamp force, $R_{eff}$=effective radius | $N \cdot m$ |
| 2 | $R_{eff} = \dfrac{2(R_o^3 - R_i^3)}{3(R_o^2 - R_i^2)}$ | Effective radius (Uniform Pressure) | $R_o$=outer radius, $R_i$=inner radius of friction lining | m |
| 3 | $R_{eff} = \dfrac{R_o + R_i}{2}$ | Effective radius (Uniform Wear) | $R_o$, $R_i$ | m |
| 4 | $p_{max} \cdot R_i = p_{min} \cdot R_o = C$ | Uniform wear condition (pressure is inversely proportional to radius) | $p$=contact pressure, $C$=constant | Pa |
| 5 | $p_{max} = \dfrac{2W}{\pi \cdot n \cdot (R_o^2 - R_i^2)}$ | Max pressure (uniform pressure theory) | $W$, $n$, $R_o$, $R_i$ | Pa |
| 6 | $W_{spring} = \dfrac{T}{n \cdot \mu \cdot R_{eff}}$ | Required spring clamp force (design check) | All known | N |
| 7 | $E = \dfrac{T^2 \cdot t}{2 \cdot I_{eq} \cdot \omega_i^2}$ | Energy dissipated during slip-engagement | $T$=torque, $t$=slip time, $I_{eq}$=equivalent inertia, $\omega_i$=initial angular velocity | J |
| 8 | $T_{centrifugal} = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot (r + e)$ | Centrifugal shoe-tip force | $m$=shoe mass, $\omega$=angular speed, $r$=pivot radius, $e$=shoe eccentricity | N |
| 9 | $\mu_{eff} = \mu_{static} - k \cdot v_{slip}$ | Effective friction (declines with slip speed — Tizard-Cameron effect) | $\mu_{static}$, $k$=constant, $v_{slip}$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | $Q = \dfrac{E}{A \cdot \Delta T \cdot t}$ | Heat dissipation rate check (for wet clutches) | $E$=energy, $A$=area, $\Delta T$=allowable rise, $t$=time | W |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** In Part B numericals, the question will often state "uniform pressure" or "uniform wear". Do **not** mix up $R_{eff}$ — using the **wrong formula costs full marks** because the rest of the calculation cascades from this one choice.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Motorcycle clutch design** balances three competing constraints: **torque capacity**, **package space**, and **heat dissipation**. A sports bike clutch must transmit **>120 N·m** from a 1000cc engine within a clutch housing of only ~120 mm outer diameter.
- **Three-wheeler (auto-rickshaw) clutches** are typically **dry single-plate** units because the engine (often a 175–435 cc single-cylinder diesel) produces very low peak torque and the chassis has a flat floor to accommodate a larger diameter.
- **Wet multi-plate clutches** are standard on premium motorcycles (Royal Enfield, KTM, Honda CBR) because the **oil cooling** prevents fade during stop-and-go city traffic.
- The **centrifugal automatic clutch** dominates scooters (Honda Activa, TVS Jupiter, Suzuki Access) because it eliminates the need for a left-hand lever — making the vehicle **"gearless"** to the rider.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Effective Radius for Uniform Pressure Theory

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumption (Uniform Pressure):** The contact pressure **p** between the friction surfaces is **constant** over the entire annular contact area (from $R_i$ to $R_o$). This is the assumption for a *new* clutch with rigid plates.

**Step 1 — Differential annular ring element.**
Consider a small ring of radius $r$ and width $dr$ on the friction plate. The area of this ring is:

$$dA = 2\pi r \, dr$$

**Step 2 — Differential normal force on the ring.**
The differential normal force on this ring is the pressure times the area:

$$dW = p \cdot dA = 2\pi p \cdot r \, dr$$

**Step 3 — Differential frictional force.**
The differential tangential (frictional) force is $\mu$ times $dW$:

$$dF = \mu \cdot dW = 2\pi \mu p \cdot r \, dr$$

**Step 4 — Differential torque about the axis.**
Each ring contributes a torque equal to force × radius:

$$dT = r \cdot dF = 2\pi \mu p \cdot r^2 \, dr$$

**Step 5 — Integrate from $R_i$ to $R_o$.**

$$T = \int_{R_i}^{R_o} 2\pi \mu p \cdot r^2 \, dr = 2\pi \mu p \int_{R_i}^{R_o} r^2 \, dr$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the integral.**

$$T = 2\pi \mu p \left[ \frac{r^3}{3} \right]_{R_i}^{R_o} = \frac{2\pi \mu p}{3} (R_o^3 - R_i^3)$$

**Step 7 — Express the total axial force $W$.**
Integrate $dW = 2\pi p r \, dr$ from $R_i$ to $R_o$:

$$W = 2\pi p \int_{R_i}^{R_o} r \, dr = 2\pi p \cdot \frac{R_o^2 - R_i^2}{2} = \pi p (R_o^2 - R_i^2)$$

**Step 8 — Solve for $p$ in terms of $W$.**

$$p = \frac{W}{\pi (R_o^2 - R_i^2)}$$

**Step 9 — Substitute back into the torque equation.**

$$T = \frac{2\pi \mu}{3} (R_o^3 - R_i^3) \cdot \frac{W}{\pi (R_o^2 - R_i^2)}$$

**Step 10 — Simplify and define the Effective Radius.**

$$\boxed{\,T = \mu \cdot W \cdot \underbrace{\left[\frac{2(R_o^3 - R_i^3)}{3(R_o^2 - R_i^2)}\right]}_{R_{eff}}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The bracketed expression is the **effective radius** under the uniform pressure assumption. The full single-surface torque equation $T = \mu W R_{eff}$ holds for every friction surface, and for a multi-plate clutch with $n$ contact surfaces, the **total transmitted torque** is **$n$ times** this value.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Effective Radius for Uniform Wear Theory

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumption (Uniform Wear):** The *wear rate* (and therefore $\mu p v$) is constant over the surface. Since the *sliding velocity* $v = \omega r$ scales linearly with $r$, this implies **$p \cdot r = $ constant = $C$** (this is the standard uniform-wear boundary condition for friction clutches and brakes).

**Step 1 — Apply the uniform wear condition.**
Since $p \cdot r = C$, we have:

$$p = \frac{C}{r}$$

**Step 2 — Differential normal force.**

$$dW = p \cdot dA = \frac{C}{r} \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = 2\pi C \, dr$$

**Step 3 — Total axial clamping force $W$.**

$$W = \int_{R_i}^{R_o} 2\pi C \, dr = 2\pi C (R_o - R_i)$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the wear constant $C$.**

$$C = \frac{W}{2\pi (R_o - R_i)}$$

**Step 5 — Differential frictional force on a ring.**

$$dF = \mu \cdot dW = \mu \cdot 2\pi C \, dr = 2\pi \mu C \, dr$$

**Step 6 — Differential torque.**

$$dT = r \cdot dF = 2\pi \mu C \cdot r \, dr$$

**Step 7 — Integrate.**

$$T = \int_{R_i}^{R_o} 2\pi \mu C \cdot r \, dr = 2\pi \mu C \cdot \frac{R_o^2 - R_i^2}{2} = \pi \mu C (R_o^2 - R_i^2)$$

**Step 8 — Substitute $C$.**

$$T = \pi \mu \cdot \frac{W}{2\pi (R_o - R_i)} \cdot (R_o^2 - R_i^2)$$

**Step 9 — Simplify $(R_o^2 - R_i^2) = (R_o - R_i)(R_o + R_i)$.**

$$T = \frac{\mu W (R_o - R_i)(R_o + R_i)}{2 (R_o - R_i)} = \mu W \cdot \frac{R_o + R_i}{2}$$

**Step 10 — Final boxed form.**

$$\boxed{\,T = \mu \cdot W \cdot \underbrace{\left[\frac{R_o + R_i}{2}\right]}_{R_{eff}}\,\,(\text{Uniform Wear})\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this is the *correct* design assumption (KTU Note):** In a *new* uniform-pressure design, the inner radius wears faster because $p$ is the same but sliding distance per revolution is smaller at $R_i$. Once the clutch runs in, the pressure *redistributes* so that wear becomes uniform. Hence **uniform wear** is the *steady-state design condition* and gives a *more conservative (lower)* torque capacity.

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — KTU Board Pattern

> [!NOTE]
> **Question Type:** A **multi-plate wet clutch** on a 250cc motorcycle has **6 driving plates and 5 driven plates**, inner radius **$R_i = 45$ mm**, outer radius **$R_o = 75$ mm**, coefficient of friction **$\mu = 0.30$**, and axial spring clamp force **$W = 1200$ N**. Calculate the torque capacity assuming (a) uniform pressure and (b) uniform wear. State which is more conservative.

**Solution — Part (a): Uniform Pressure**

**Step 1 — Number of contact surfaces.**
With 6 driving and 5 driven plates, there are **5 inner friction surfaces and 5 outer friction surfaces** = **$n = 10$ friction surfaces**.

**Step 2 — Calculate $R_{eff}$ under uniform pressure.**

$$R_{eff,UP} = \frac{2(R_o^3 - R_i^3)}{3(R_o^2 - R_i^2)}$$

$$R_o^3 = (0.075)^3 = 4.21875 \times 10^{-4}\ m^3$$

$$R_i^3 = (0.045)^3 = 9.1125 \times 10^{-5}\ m^3$$

$$R_o^3 - R_i^3 = 3.3075 \times 10^{-4}\ m^3$$

$$R_o^2 = (0.075)^2 = 5.625 \times 10^{-3}\ m^2$$

$$R_i^2 = (0.045)^2 = 2.025 \times 10^{-3}\ m^2$$

$$R_o^2 - R_i^2 = 3.600 \times 10^{-3}\ m^2$$

$$R_{eff,UP} = \frac{2 \times 3.3075 \times 10^{-4}}{3 \times 3.600 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{6.615 \times 10^{-4}}{1.080 \times 10^{-2}} = 6.125 \times 10^{-2}\ m$$

**Step 3 — Torque.**

$$T_{UP} = n \cdot \mu \cdot W \cdot R_{eff,UP} = 10 \times 0.30 \times 1200 \times 0.06125$$

$$\boxed{T_{UP} = 220.5\ N \cdot m}$$

**Solution — Part (b): Uniform Wear**

**Step 1 — Calculate $R_{eff}$ under uniform wear.**

$$R_{eff,UW} = \frac{R_o + R_i}{2} = \frac{0.075 + 0.045}{2} = 0.060\ m$$

**Step 2 — Torque.**

$$T_{UW} = 10 \times 0.30 \times 1200 \times 0.060$$

$$\boxed{T_{UW} = 216.0\ N \cdot m}$$

**Step 3 — Comparison.**

$$T_{UP} = 220.5\ N \cdot m > T_{UW} = 216.0\ N \cdot m$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The **uniform wear assumption yields a lower torque capacity** and is therefore the **more conservative design basis**. Real clutches in service converge toward uniform wear after the running-in phase. This is the value used for the *rated* torque capacity of the clutch.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Clutch Types for 2W/3W

| Property | Multi-Plate Wet | Multi-Plate Dry | Centrifugal Automatic | Shoe-Type Centrifugal |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Lubrication** | Engine oil bath | Air-cooled (no oil) | Air-cooled | Air-cooled |
| **No. of plates** | 5–8 | 3–5 | N/A (shoes) | 3 shoes typically |
| **Rider interface** | Left-hand lever | Left-hand lever | None (auto) | None (auto) |
| **Application** | Sports/commuter geared bikes | Off-road, vintage bikes | Scooters (CVT) | Mopeds, small 3W |
| **Heat capacity** | Very high (oil cooled) | Moderate | Moderate | Low |
| **Typical peak torque** | 80–250 N·m | 50–120 N·m | 15–40 N·m | 5–25 N·m |
| **Engagement feel** | Smooth, progressive | Slightly abrupt | Automatic, RPM-driven | Automatic, RPM-driven |
| **Maintenance** | Oil change interval | Plate wear inspection | Shoe lining inspection | Shoe lining inspection |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a 2W/3W Clutch System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ENGINE CRANKSHAFT] --> B[PRIMARY DRIVE GEAR]
    B --> C[CLUTCH BASKET OUTER DRUM]
    C --> D[STEEL DRIVING PLATES TEETH IN BASKET]
    D --> E[FRICTION LINED DRIVEN PLATES]
    E --> F[CLUTCH HUB ON GEARBOX INPUT SHAFT]
    F --> G[GEARBOX PRIMARY SHAFT]
    G --> H[TRANSMISSION GEARS]
    H --> I[FINAL DRIVE CHAIN OR SHAFT]
    I --> J[REAR WHEEL]
    K[RIDER LEFT HAND LEVER] --> L[CABLE OR HYDRAULIC PUSHPULL]
    L --> M[RELEASE MECHANISM]
    M --> N[PRESSURE PLATE LIFTS]
    N --> D
    O[COIL OR DIAPHRAGM SPRING] --> P[CLAMPS FRICTION STACK]
    P --> D
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **How to read this Mermaid diagram:** The bold capital-letter blocks represent physical components. Follow the **outer flow** A → B → C → … → J to see the *torque transmission path* when the clutch is engaged. Follow the **inner branch** K → L → M → N to see the *rider's disengagement command* flowing backward into the plate stack. The block **O → P** is the spring that *actively* presses the stack together when the rider is not pulling the lever.

## 4.2 State Diagram — Engagement vs Disengagement

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> DISENGAGED : Rider squeezes lever
    DISENGAGED --> SLIPPING : Lever partially released
    SLIPPING --> ENGAGED : Lever fully released
    ENGAGED --> SLIPPING : Throttle blip during downshift
    SLIPPING --> DISENGAGED : Lever fully squeezed
    ENGAGED --> [*] : Engine off
    DISENGAGED --> [*] : Engine off

    note right of DISENGAGED : Plates separated, zero torque transfer
    note right of SLIPPING : Plates slipping, energy dissipated as heat
    note right of ENGAGED : Plates locked together, full torque transfer
```

> [!NOTE]
> **State Diagram Insight:** The `SLIPPING` state is the only state in which the clutch plates move relative to each other and *generate heat*. Designers must size the clutch for the **thermal load** during slipping (e.g., during a hill start) — this is exactly why wet multi-plate clutches have an oil bath for cooling.

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Centrifugal Automatic Clutch (CVT Scooter)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ENGINE CRANK] --> B[PRIMARY PULLEY OF CVT]
    B --> C[SECONDARY PULLEY OF CVT]
    C --> D[COUNTERSHAFT]
    D --> E[CLUTCH OUTER DRUM]
    E --> F[CENTRIFUGAL SHOES 3 PIECE]
    F --> G[CLUTCH INNER DRUM]
    G --> H[FINAL DRIVE BELT OR CHAIN]
    H --> I[REAR WHEEL]
    J[ENGINE RPM SENSOR] --> K[FLYWEIGHT CENTRIFUGE OUTWARD FORCE]
    K --> F
    L[RETURN SPRING PER SHOE] --> F
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Visualization Tip:** In a centrifugal clutch, there is **no cable and no rider lever**. Engagement is purely a function of **centrifugal force versus spring return force**. The shoes fly outward when $\omega^2 > $ threshold and engage the drum. Below idle threshold RPM, the spring retracts them, and the vehicle freewheels.

## 4.4 Cross-Sectional Schematic Description of a Wet Multi-Plate Clutch

While a true cross-sectional drawing cannot be rendered in pure Mermaid syntax, the following **layered block stack** accurately represents the axial arrangement of a wet multi-plate clutch from the rear (pressure plate side) to the front (clutch basket side):

```
[AXIAL LAYOUT — view looking along crankshaft axis, from rear to front]

LAYER 1  |  PRESSURE PLATE (bolted to basket)
LAYER 2  |  DRIVING STEEL PLATE   ─┐
LAYER 3  |  DRIVEN FRICTION PLATE   │  ← FRICTION PAIR 1
LAYER 4  |  DRIVING STEEL PLATE   ─┤
LAYER 5  |  DRIVEN FRICTION PLATE   │  ← FRICTION PAIR 2
LAYER 6  |  DRIVING STEEL PLATE   ─┤
LAYER 7  |  DRIVEN FRICTION PLATE   │  ← FRICTION PAIR 3
LAYER 8  |  DRIVING STEEL PLATE   ─┤
LAYER 9  |  DRIVEN FRICTION PLATE   │  ← FRICTION PAIR 4
LAYER 10 |  DRIVING STEEL PLATE   ─┤
LAYER 11 |  DRIVEN FRICTION PLATE   │  ← FRICTION PAIR 5
LAYER 12 |  DRIVING STEEL PLATE   ─┘
LAYER 13 |  HUB (splined to gearbox input)
LAYER 14 |  CLUTCH SPRINGS (5 or 6 coil springs around the hub)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the stack:** Steel plates are *driving* (their outer teeth engage the basket). Friction-lined plates are *driven* (their inner teeth engage the hub). The basket and steel plates rotate as one rigid body; the hub and friction plates rotate together; friction between the alternating surfaces transmits torque. The coil springs in **LAYER 14** are preloaded to push **LAYER 1** leftward, clamping the entire stack.
