# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Two compulsory questions from the module, each carrying **3 marks**. Answers should be precise (50–80 words). Diagrams earn an extra 0.5 mark per KTU valuation key.

### Question A1 — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

> **Q: List the four primary functions of a clutch in a two-wheeled vehicle.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Permit smooth engagement** of engine torque to the drivetrain from rest, preventing jerky launch and wheel hop.
2. **Allow interruption of power flow** during gear shifting, so that the dog clutches inside the gearbox can engage without grinding.
3. **Enable engine idling** without driving the rear wheel, allowing the rider to wait at signals with the engine running.
4. **Protect the driveline** from torsional shocks caused by sudden throttle changes or back-load from road irregularities, by allowing controlled slip.

*(4 well-spaced points × 0.75 mark = 3 marks.)*

---

### Question A2 — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

> **Q: Compare wet multi-plate and dry single-plate clutches as used in two-wheelers. State two advantages of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Type | Two Advantages |
|------|----------------|
| **Wet Multi-Plate** | (i) Excellent heat dissipation via engine-oil cooling allows repeated slipping in city traffic without fade. (ii) Compact outer diameter — multiple plates stacked axially permit high torque capacity within a small crankcase envelope. |
| **Dry Single-Plate** | (i) Lower cost and simpler construction; no oil-seal leakage concerns. (ii) Higher coefficient of friction in dry condition (typically μ ≈ 0.4) since no hydrodynamic oil film reduces grip. |

*(2 marks for the table, 1 mark for the contextual conclusion.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** In ESE (End Semester Exam), Part B carries 14 marks. Module-internal choice is **mandatory**. Below are **Question A** and **Question B** as alternative choices. Attempt *either* Question A *or* Question B.

---

### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]

> **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **multi-plate wet clutch** used in a geared motorcycle.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A multi-plate wet clutch has **6 driving and 5 driven plates**. The inner and outer radii of the friction surface are **50 mm and 80 mm** respectively. The axial spring load is **1500 N** and the coefficient of friction is **0.32**. Calculate the torque transmitted assuming **uniform pressure**. State the engine RPM at which this clutch can transmit **15 kW** of power.

---

#### Solution to Question A (a):

**Construction (3 marks):**

A multi-plate wet clutch consists of the following parts:

1. **Clutch basket (outer drum)** — Aluminium or steel, toothed on its inner circumference. Bolted to the engine's primary drive gear and rotates at engine speed.
2. **Driving plates (steel plates)** — Plain steel plates with **external teeth** on their circumference that slide into the basket slots. They rotate with the engine.
3. **Driven plates (friction plates)** — Steel core plates riveted with friction material (cork, paper-based, or sintered) on both faces. They have **internal teeth** that mesh with the splines on the clutch hub.
4. **Clutch hub** — Internally splined to the gearbox input shaft. It rotates with the driven plates.
5. **Pressure plate and springs** — A set of **5 or 6 coil springs** (or one diaphragm spring) located behind the pressure plate. They exert the axial clamping force $W$.
6. **Release mechanism** — A central pushrod operated by the rider via cable or hydraulic line.

**Working (3 marks):**

- **Engaged position (lever released):** The springs push the pressure plate leftward, clamping the friction stack tightly. Friction between steel and friction-lined plates locks them together, transmitting full engine torque to the gearbox.
- **Slipping position (lever partially released):** The springs are partially decompressed; the stack slips and dissipates kinetic energy as heat in the engine oil. This is the launch phase.
- **Disengaged position (lever fully squeezed):** The release mechanism lifts the pressure plate, the stack is free, and no torque passes to the gearbox. The rider now shifts gear.

**Role of oil bath (1 mark):** The entire assembly sits in engine oil (typically JASO MA / MA2 grade), which carries away heat, removes wear debris, and damps vibration.

---

#### Solution to Question A (b):

**Given:**
- Driving plates: 6, Driven plates: 5 → Number of contact surfaces $n = 10$
- $R_i = 0.050\ m$, $R_o = 0.080\ m$
- $W = 1500\ N$, $\mu = 0.32$
- Assumption: **Uniform Pressure**
- Power $P = 15\ kW = 15{,}000\ W$

**Step 1 — Compute $R_{eff}$ under uniform pressure.**

$$R_{eff} = \frac{2(R_o^3 - R_i^3)}{3(R_o^2 - R_i^2)}$$

$$R_o^3 = (0.080)^3 = 5.12 \times 10^{-4}\ m^3$$

$$R_i^3 = (0.050)^3 = 1.25 \times 10^{-4}\ m^3$$

$$R_o^3 - R_i^3 = 3.87 \times 10^{-4}\ m^3$$

$$R_o^2 = 6.40 \times 10^{-3}\ m^2, \quad R_i^2 = 2.50 \times 10^{-3}\ m^2$$

$$R_o^2 - R_i^2 = 3.90 \times 10^{-3}\ m^2$$

$$R_{eff} = \frac{2 \times 3.87 \times 10^{-4}}{3 \times 3.90 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{7.74 \times 10^{-4}}{1.17 \times 10^{-2}} = 6.615 \times 10^{-2}\ m$$

**Step 2 — Torque transmitted.**

$$T = n \cdot \mu \cdot W \cdot R_{eff} = 10 \times 0.32 \times 1500 \times 0.06615$$

$$\boxed{T = 317.5\ N \cdot m}$$

**Step 3 — Engine RPM for 15 kW.**

$$P = \frac{2\pi N T}{60}$$

$$N = \frac{P \times 60}{2\pi T} = \frac{15{,}000 \times 60}{2\pi \times 317.5} = \frac{900{,}000}{1995.1}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 451\ RPM}$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** At just 451 RPM the clutch can already transmit 15 kW, which is well below the engine's idle RPM — confirming that this clutch is properly sized for the 250–400cc motorcycle application.

---

### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **centrifugal automatic clutch** used in two-wheelers. Describe how the engagement speed is determined and the role of the return spring.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A centrifugal clutch on a scooter has **three shoes**, each of mass **0.25 kg**, with the **centre of mass at radius 75 mm from the pivot** when fully retracted. The pivot is at radius **40 mm** and the **friction lining inner radius is 95 mm**. The **coefficient of friction between shoe and drum is 0.35**, and the **return spring exerts a radial force of 30 N at each shoe** at rest. Find the **engine RPM at which engagement begins**, assuming engagement occurs when the centrifugal force just overcomes the spring force at the retracted position.

---

#### Solution to Question B (a):

**Construction (2 marks):**

A centrifugal automatic clutch consists of:

1. **Driving drum** — Rotates with the engine crankshaft. Inner surface is a smooth cylindrical friction surface.
2. **Three or four friction shoes** — Mounted on a **stationary backing plate** via pivots. The shoes carry friction linings (usually Ferodo or sintered bronze) on their outer curved face.
3. **Flyweights / Shoe masses** — Each shoe has a mass offset from the pivot so that rotation generates centrifugal force.
4. **Return springs** — One spring per shoe, pulling the shoe inward toward the pivot, opposing centrifugal force.
5. **Driven member (input to CVT or gearbox)** — Coupled to the friction surface of the drum via the shoes.

**Working (3 marks):**

- At **engine idle**, the centrifugal force on the shoes is too small to overcome the return springs. The shoes stay retracted, no contact with the drum, no torque transmitted. The vehicle freewheels.
- As engine RPM rises, $\omega^2$ increases linearly, so the **outward centrifugal force on each shoe** grows. At a critical engagement RPM (typically 2500–3000 RPM), the centrifugal force balances the spring force, and the shoes just kiss the drum.
- Above the engagement RPM, the shoes press harder against the drum, friction rises, and torque is transmitted to the CVT/rear wheel. The scooter accelerates.
- As the vehicle speeds up and engine RPM drops toward idle, the springs retract the shoes, disengaging the clutch automatically — no rider action needed.

**Engagement speed determination (1 mark):**

Set the centrifugal force at the shoe's centre of mass equal to the spring preload force:

$$m \omega_e^2 r = F_{spring}$$

$$\omega_e = \sqrt{\frac{F_{spring}}{m \cdot r}}$$

**Role of the return spring (1 mark):**

The return spring ensures **automatic disengagement** when the engine RPM falls below the engagement threshold — typically at idle. It also sets the **engagement RPM threshold** and **modulates the rate at which torque ramps up** with RPM, controlling how aggressively the scooter launches.

---

#### Solution to Question B (b):

**Given:**
- Number of shoes: $n_s = 3$
- Mass per shoe: $m = 0.25\ kg$
- Centre-of-mass radius (retracted): $r = 0.075\ m$
- Pivot radius: $0.040\ m$ (not needed for engagement-RPM calculation)
- Friction lining inner radius: $0.095\ m$ (used in part (a) for torque, not for engagement RPM)
- $\mu = 0.35$ (used for torque capacity later, not for engagement RPM)
- Spring force per shoe: $F_{spring} = 30\ N$

**Step 1 — Write the centrifugal force equation.**

For each shoe, the centrifugal force acting outward at the centre of mass is:

$$F_c = m \omega_e^2 r$$

where $\omega_e$ is the angular velocity at which engagement begins.

**Step 2 — Apply the engagement condition.**
At engagement, the centrifugal force equals the spring preload force:

$$m \omega_e^2 r = F_{spring}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\omega_e$.**

$$\omega_e^2 = \frac{F_{spring}}{m \cdot r} = \frac{30}{0.25 \times 0.075} = \frac{30}{0.01875} = 1600\ rad^2/s^2$$

$$\omega_e = \sqrt{1600} = 40\ rad/s$$

**Step 4 — Convert to RPM.**

$$N_e = \omega_e \cdot \frac{60}{2\pi} = 40 \times \frac{60}{2\pi} = \frac{2400}{6.2832}$$

$$\boxed{N_e \approx 382\ RPM}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Engagement starts at a relatively low RPM (382 RPM), which is *below* typical scooter idle (≈ 1300–1500 RPM). This means the clutch is *always* engaged during normal running — and the actual *useful torque transmission* depends on shoe-drum normal force growing with $\omega^2$ above this threshold. In practice, designers set the spring force so that full engagement torque is reached around **2500–2800 RPM** — the threshold above is only the *first contact* RPM.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Clutch Numericals (Valuation Key Insights):**
>
> 1. **Number of contact surfaces $n$:** With $N_d$ driving plates and $N_{dr}$ driven plates, the *number of contact surfaces is* **$n = N_d + N_{dr} - 1$**, not $n = N_d + N_{dr}$. Many students use $n = N_d + N_{dr}$ and over-estimate torque by **10–20%**. Verify the *alternating* pattern in the stack diagram.
>
> 2. **Uniform pressure vs. uniform wear $R_{eff}$:** Switching the formulas gives an **8–12% torque difference**. KTU valuation keys *expect* the formula stated in the question — re-read the question carefully.
>
> 3. **Units of radius:** Always convert **mm to m** before substituting into the SI torque equation. A common slip-up is plugging mm directly into $T = \mu W R_{eff}$ and getting torque in $N \cdot mm$ instead of $N \cdot m$.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the factor $n$:** Students often compute torque for *one* friction surface and stop, missing the factor of 5 or 10 from the multi-plate stack. This is a guaranteed **2–3 mark loss** on a 7-mark sub-question.
>
> 5. **Drawing the Mermaid/state diagram in the exam:** KTU now awards an **extra 1 mark** for *correctly labelled block diagrams* in clutch questions. Always draw a labelled schematic even if not explicitly asked.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print This Before Walking Into the Exam Hall):**

- ☐ A clutch **transmits torque via friction** between driving and driven members; it is **not** a positive-engagement dog clutch.
- ☐ **Multi-plate wet clutch** is the standard on modern geared motorcycles; uses **5–8 friction surfaces**, immersed in **JASO MA/MA2 engine oil**.
- ☐ **Centrifugal automatic clutch** dominates scooters (CVT applications) and small mopeds; engagement is **purely RPM-driven**, no rider lever.
- ☐ **Three-wheeler clutches** are usually **dry single-plate** because the engine torque is low and chassis space is generous.
- ☐ **Master equation:** $T = n \cdot \mu \cdot W \cdot R_{eff}$
  - Uniform pressure: $R_{eff} = \dfrac{2(R_o^3 - R_i^3)}{3(R_o^2 - R_i^2)}$
  - Uniform wear: $R_{eff} = \dfrac{R_o + R_i}{2}$
- ☐ **Number of contact surfaces:** $n = N_d + N_{dr} - 1$
- ☐ **Uniform wear is the design/conservative assumption** for steady-state torque rating.
- ☐ **Spring clamp force** is calculated from the desired torque: $W = \dfrac{T}{n \cdot \mu \cdot R_{eff}}$
- ☐ **Left-hand lever** controls disengagement; right-hand throttle controls engine torque output — never mix them up.
- ☐ **JASO MA / MA2 oil** is mandatory for wet clutches — using **JASO MB** (energy-conserving car oil) will cause clutch slippage.
- ☐ **Engagement RPM of a centrifugal clutch** is set by $m \omega^2 r = F_{spring}$; solve for $\omega$ and convert to RPM.
- ☐ **Heat dissipation** during slipping is the limiting factor in city-traffic clutch design — wet clutches outperform dry ones here.
- ☐ In **drum-brake-style shoe clutches**, the engagement torque depends on $\mu$, shoe-drum contact angle, and the radial shoe force — distinct from disc-clutch equations.
- ☐ **Always convert mm → m** before plugging into SI formulas.
- ☐ In Part A (3 marks), be ready to **list, compare, or sketch** — KTU rarely asks derivations at this level.
- ☐ In Part B (14 marks), **state your assumption** (uniform pressure OR uniform wear) at the top of the solution — examiners check for this first.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gearbox and Gear Ratios
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT631 - TWO AND THREE WHEELED VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Gearbox and Gear Ratios

# MODULE 2: GEARBOX AND GEAR RATIOS

## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard):**
> A **gearbox** (also called a transmission gearbox) in a two or three-wheeled vehicle is a mechanical assembly of gear trains enclosed in a single housing, mounted between the engine crankshaft and the final drive (chain/shaft). Its primary function is to **vary the torque-speed ratio** delivered to the rear wheel by selecting different gear ratios, thereby enabling the vehicle to start from rest, climb gradients, cruise efficiently, and achieve maximum speed within the narrow operating band of an internal combustion (IC) engine.

Mathematically, the **overall gear ratio** $i_g$ for any selected gear is defined as the ratio of the rotational speed of the driving member (engine crankshaft / input shaft) to the rotational speed of the driven member (output / countershaft / rear wheel):

$$i_g = \frac{N_{in}}{N_{out}} = \frac{T_{out}}{T_{in}} \cdot \eta_g$$

where:
- $N_{in}$ = rotational speed of the input shaft (rpm)
- $N_{out}$ = rotational speed of the output shaft (rpm)
- $T_{in}$ = input torque on the countershaft (N·m)
- $T_{out}$ = output torque on the rear wheel (N·m)
- $\eta_g$ = gear mesh efficiency (typically **0.92 – 0.97** for spur gears, **0.95 – 0.98** for helical gears)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Bicycle on a Hill:**
> Imagine you are riding a bicycle. When starting from rest on a flat road, you pedal easily in a *low gear* (small front chainring, large rear sprocket) — each pedal rotation moves the wheel only a little, but you can climb a hill. Once you gather speed on level ground, you shift to a *medium gear*, and at cruising speed on a downhill, you shift to a *high gear* (big front chainring, small rear sprocket) where each pedal stroke whips the wheel around multiple times at low pedalling force.
> A motorcycle gearbox does **exactly the same job** — it converts the engine's fixed narrow power band into the wide range of torque × speed combinations the rear wheel needs. Without it, the bike would either stall at a traffic light or "redline" (over-rev) at 30 km/h.

### 1.3 Why a Gearbox is Essential in Two/Three Wheelers

| S.No | Engine Limitation | Gearbox Solution |
|------|------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | IC engines develop peak torque only in a narrow rpm band (typically **2000–6000 rpm** for small two-wheeler engines) | Multi-ratio gearbox keeps engine within peak torque/power zone |
| 2 | Engine cannot start a heavily loaded vehicle from rest at low rpm | 1st gear provides very high reduction (ratio 3:1 to 4:1) for starting torque |
| 3 | Wide speed range required (0 to 120+ km/h) | 4, 5, or 6-speed gearbox maps engine curve to vehicle demand |
| 4 | Frequent stop-and-go city traffic | Smooth gear shifts enable rider to keep engine in optimal zone |
| 5 | Climbing gradients on inclines | Lower gears multiply torque at the wheel for gradient climbing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In modern geared scooters and mopeds (e.g., Honda Activa, TVS Jupiter), a **CVT (Continuously Variable Transmission)** has largely replaced the stepped manual gearbox. However, the underlying principle of *gear ratio variation* remains — CVT uses a variable-pitch pulley instead of discrete gears.

### 1.4 Classification of Gearboxes Used in Two and Three Wheelers

```
                    GEARBOX
                       │
        ┌──────────────┼──────────────┐
        │              │              │
   MANUAL (Stepped)   CVT         AUTOMATIC
        │           (Belt/Pulley)  (Torque
        │                           Converter)
   ┌────┴─────┐
   │          │
Sliding    Constant
 Mesh       Mesh
   │          │
   │       Synchromesh
   │       (rare in 2W)
   ▼
4/5/6-speed
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Note (Module 2):** The syllabus specifically focuses on **manual stepped gearboxes** — sliding mesh, constant mesh, and synchromesh — and the corresponding **gear ratio design principles** for two and three-wheeled vehicles.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Operational Principle of a Stepped Gearbox

A stepped gearbox operates on the principle of **gear ratio selection**. The engine delivers power to a **main shaft (input shaft)** which is coupled to the engine output (crankshaft via clutch). The main shaft drives a **countershaft (layshaft)** through a fixed primary reduction. The countershaft carries pinions (driven gears) which are in constant mesh with corresponding gears on the main shaft. By engaging different gears using a **dog clutch** or **synchromesh**, the rider varies the effective ratio transmitted to the output (and hence to the rear wheel via chain/shaft).

### 2.2 Key Components and Their Functions

| Component | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| **Main Shaft (Input Shaft)** | Receives power from clutch, carries sliding dog clutches |
| **Countershaft (Layshaft)** | Holds fixed pinions in constant mesh with main-shaft gears |
| **Primary Drive Pinion & Gear** | Provides fixed reduction from engine to gearbox input |
| **Gear Selector Fork** | Engages desired gear by sliding the dog clutch |
| **Detent Mechanism** | Spring-loaded ball locks gear in engaged position |
| **Output Shaft** | Delivers selected gear ratio to chain sprocket / universal joint |

### 2.3 Types of Manual Gearboxes in Two/Three Wheelers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three architectural types are examinable in KTU Module 2:**

#### (a) Sliding Mesh Gearbox
- Gears physically slide on splined shafts to engage.
- **Disadvantage:** Produces loud clashing noise during shifting; gear teeth must be in motion to mesh.
- **Used in:** Older entry-level motorcycles (e.g., old Bajaj Boxer, Yamaha RX100).

#### (b) Constant Mesh Gearbox
- All gears are always in mesh (permanently engaged); only **dog clutches** slide to transmit power.
- **Advantage:** Quiet operation, smoother shifts, longer gear life.
- **Used in:** Almost every modern geared motorcycle (Royal Enfield, KTM Duke, Bajaj Pulsar, Honda CB Shine).

#### (c) Synchromesh Gearbox
- Uses **synchronizer rings (brass cones)** to equalize speeds of input and output gears before engagement.
- **Used in:** Cars predominantly; rare in motorcycles because the gear ratios are spaced wider and the cost/complexity are not justified.

### 2.4 Derivation of the Number of Speeds

For a typical 5-speed gearbox:

$$N_{speeds} = N_{forward} + N_{reverse} + N_{neutral}$$

Most two-wheelers have **$N_{forward} = 4, 5,$ or $6$** forward gears, **$N_{reverse} = 0$**, and **$N_{neutral} = 1$**.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Overall gear ratio | $i_{overall} = i_{primary} \times i_{gearbox} \times i_{final}$ | dimensionless | Used to find wheel torque |
| 2 | Wheel torque | $T_w = T_{engine} \times i_{overall} \times \eta_{total}$ | N·m | $\eta_{total}$ includes gearbox, chain, bearing losses |
| 3 | Wheel speed (rpm) | $N_w = \dfrac{N_{engine}}{i_{overall}}$ | rpm | Direct inverse of overall ratio |
| 4 | Vehicle speed | $V = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D_w \cdot N_w}{60}$ | m/s | $D_w$ = wheel diameter |
| 5 | Vehicle speed in km/h | $V_{km/h} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D_w \cdot N_w}{1000} \times 60 = 0.1885 \cdot D_w \cdot N_w$ | km/h | $D_w$ in meters, $N_w$ in rpm |
| 6 | Gear reduction ratio | $i = \dfrac{Z_{driven}}{Z_{driver}} = \dfrac{N_{driver}}{N_{driven}}$ | dimensionless | $Z$ = number of teeth |
| 7 | Torque on driven gear | $T_2 = T_1 \times i \times \eta$ | N·m | $\eta$ = mesh efficiency |
| 8 | Gear mesh efficiency | $\eta_{gear} = 1 - f \cdot \pi \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{Z_1} + \dfrac{1}{Z_2}}\right)$ | — | $f$ = coefficient of friction ($\approx$ 0.05 for spur, 0.04 for helical) |
| 9 | Power transmitted | $P = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ | W | $N$ in rpm, $T$ in N·m |
| 10 | Gradeability | $\tan(\alpha) \geq \dfrac{i_{overall} \cdot \eta \cdot T_{max}/r_w - \mu_r \cdot W}{W - i_{overall} \cdot \eta \cdot T_{max} \cdot h/(r_w^2)}$ | — | Used in 1st gear selection |
| 11 | Step ratio (progression) | $K = \dfrac{i_{n}}{i_{n+1}}$ | dimensionless | Geometric progression ratio ($K \approx 1.25$ to $1.45$) |
| 12 | Pitch line velocity | $v = \dfrac{\pi \cdot m \cdot n}{60000}$ | m/s | $m$ = module (mm), $n$ = rpm |

> [!WARNING]
> **Important Boundary Condition for KTU Problems:**
> The overall ratio must satisfy **two opposing constraints simultaneously**:
> - **Torque constraint:** $i_{overall,1st} \geq \dfrac{W \cdot r_w \cdot (f \cos\alpha + \sin\alpha)}{\eta \cdot T_{max}}$  (so the vehicle can start on a gradient of angle $\alpha$).
> - **Speed constraint:** $i_{overall,top} \leq \dfrac{\pi D_w N_{max}}{60 \cdot V_{max}}$ (so the engine does not exceed max safe rpm at top speed).

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In industry, the choice of gear ratios determines:
1. **Fuel efficiency curve** — wider ratio spread allows engine to operate near BSFC (Brake Specific Fuel Consumption) minimum on every gear.
2. **Rideability** — closely spaced ratios (small step ratio $K$) avoid engine "bogging" between shifts.
3. **Acceleration performance** — 0–60 km/h time is dominated by 1st and 2nd gear ratios.
4. **Top-speed ceiling** — limited by the smallest (top gear) overall ratio.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Overall Drive Ratio and Vehicle Speed

**Given:**
- Engine peak power $P = 9.0$ kW at $N_e = 8000$ rpm
- Primary reduction $i_p = 3.0$
- Gearbox 4th (top) gear ratio $i_g = 1.0$
- Final drive (chain sprocket) ratio $i_f = 2.8$
- Rear wheel diameter $D_w = 0.6$ m
- Overall efficiency $\eta_{total} = 0.85$

**Find:** Vehicle top speed $V_{max}$ in km/h.

**Step 1 — Compute overall ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_{overall} &= i_p \times i_g \times i_f \\
&= 3.0 \times 1.0 \times 2.8 \\
&= 8.4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Wheel speed in rpm:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_w &= \frac{N_e}{i_{overall}} \\
&= \frac{8000}{8.4} \\
&= 952.38 \; \text{rpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Linear wheel speed in m/s:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \frac{\pi \cdot D_w \cdot N_w}{60} \\
&= \frac{\pi \cdot 0.6 \cdot 952.38}{60} \\
&= 29.92 \; \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to km/h:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{km/h} &= V \times 3.6 \\
&= 29.92 \times 3.6 \\
&= 107.7 \; \text{km/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The factor of 3.6 converts m/s to km/h because $1 \, \text{m/s} = 3600/1000 \, \text{km/h} = 3.6 \, \text{km/h}$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Wheel Torque and Tractive Force

**Given:**
- Engine torque at peak power $T_e = P \cdot 60 / (2\pi N_e) = 9000 \times 60 / (2\pi \times 8000) = 10.74$ N·m
- Same ratios as above.

**Find:** Maximum tractive force at the wheel in 1st gear where $i_{g1} = 3.2$.

**Step 1 — Overall ratio in 1st gear:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_{overall,1} &= i_p \times i_{g1} \times i_f \\
&= 3.0 \times 3.2 \times 2.8 \\
&= 26.88
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Torque at the wheel:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_w &= T_e \times i_{overall,1} \times \eta_{total} \\
&= 10.74 \times 26.88 \times 0.85 \\
&= 245.4 \; \text{N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Tractive force:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_t &= \frac{T_w}{r_w} \\
&= \frac{245.4}{0.3} \\
&= 818 \; \text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The wheel radius $r_w = D_w/2 = 0.3$ m. Tractive force is the force the wheel applies to the road; it overcomes rolling resistance, aerodynamic drag, and gradient force.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Step Ratio for a 5-Speed Gearbox

For a **geometric progression**, the gear ratios follow:

$$
i_n = i_1 \cdot K^{-(n-1)}
$$

where $K$ is the constant step ratio.

**Given:** 1st gear ratio $i_1 = 3.0$, 5th (top) gear ratio $i_5 = 1.0$.

**Find:** Step ratio $K$ and intermediate ratios $i_2, i_3, i_4$.

**Step 1 — Solve for $K$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_5 &= i_1 \cdot K^{-(5-1)} \\
1.0 &= 3.0 \cdot K^{-4} \\
K^4 &= 3.0 \\
K &= 3.0^{1/4} \\
K &= 1.316
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Intermediate ratios:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_2 &= \frac{3.0}{1.316^1} = 2.279 \\
i_3 &= \frac{3.0}{1.316^2} = 1.732 \\
i_4 &= \frac{3.0}{1.316^3} = 1.316 \\
i_5 &= \frac{3.0}{1.316^4} = 1.000
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Closely spaced lower gears (1→2: 3.0→2.28) allow smooth take-off, while top gears (4→5: 1.32→1.0) are spaced closer because the vehicle is at cruising speed and only minor torque changes are needed.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Typical Gear Ratio Table (5-Speed Motorcycle)

| Gear | Ratio $i_g$ | Overall $i_{overall} = 3.0 \cdot i_g \cdot 2.8$ | Use Case | Engine Speed Range |
|------|------------|-----------------------------------------------|----------|-------------------|
| 1st  | **3.00** | **25.20** | Take-off, steep hills | 1500 – 4500 rpm |
| 2nd  | **2.28** | **19.15** | Acceleration, moderate climbs | 2000 – 5500 rpm |
| 3rd  | **1.73** | **14.53** | City riding 40–60 km/h | 2500 – 6500 rpm |
| 4th  | **1.32** | **11.09** | Highway cruising 60–80 km/h | 3000 – 7500 rpm |
| 5th  | **1.00** | **8.40**  | Top speed, fuel economy cruise | 3500 – 8500 rpm |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATS

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Motorcycle Transmission

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENG[Engine Crankshaft] -->|Torque Te| CLU[Clutch Assembly]
    CLU -->|Engaged/Disengaged| PRI[Primary Reduction Gear Pair]
    PRI -->|Ratio ip = 3.0| MS[Main Shaft Input]
    MS --> CM[Constant Mesh Pair Countershaft]
    CM --> CS[Countershaft Layshaft Pinions]
    CS --> DOG1{Dog Clutch Selector}
    DOG1 -->|1st gear| OUT1[Output Gear 1 Ratio 3.0]
    DOG1 -->|2nd gear| OUT2[Output Gear 2 Ratio 2.28]
    DOG1 -->|3rd gear| OUT3[Output Gear 3 Ratio 1.73]
    DOG1 -->|4th gear| OUT4[Output Gear 4 Ratio 1.32]
    DOG1 -->|5th gear| OUT5[Output Gear 5 Ratio 1.00]
    OUT1 --> OS[Output Shaft]
    OUT2 --> OS
    OUT3 --> OS
    OUT4 --> OS
    OUT5 --> OS
    OS --> FD[Final Drive Chain Sprocket Ratio if = 2.8]
    FD --> RW[Rear Wheel]
    RW -->|Tr Force| GRD[Road Surface]
```

### 4.2 Gear-Shift Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([Rider Inputs Throttle and Shifts]) --> IDLE{Engine Running?}
    IDLE -->|No| STALL[Engine Off]
    IDLE -->|Yes| NEUTRAL{Neutral Selected?}
    NEUTRAL -->|Yes| IDLE2[Disengaged Power Flow]
    NEUTRAL -->|No| GEAR1{Gear Position 1 2 3 4 5}
    GEAR1 -->|1st| G1[Highest Reduction 25.2 Take Off]
    GEAR1 -->|2nd| G2[Reduction 19.2 Climb Acceleration]
    GEAR1 -->|3rd| G3[Reduction 14.5 City Riding]
    GEAR1 -->|4th| G4[Reduction 11.1 Open Road]
    GEAR1 -->|5th| G5[Reduction 8.4 Top Speed Cruise]
    G1 --> TORQ[Wheel Torque Multiplied]
    G2 --> TORQ
    G3 --> TORQ
    G4 --> TORQ
    G5 --> TORQ
    TORQ --> FCT[Tractive Force at Tyre Contact Patch]
    FCT --> VEH[Vehicle Moves]
```

### 4.3 Constant Mesh Gearbox Internal Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Input_End
        A[Engine Sprocket / Primary Pinion]
    end
    subgraph Gearbox_Housing
        B[Primary Driven Gear on Main Shaft]
        C[Main Shaft with Sliding Dogs]
        D1[Countershaft Pinion 1st]
        D2[Countershaft Pinion 2nd]
        D3[Countershaft Pinion 3rd]
        D4[Countershaft Pinion 4th]
        D5[Countershaft Pinion 5th]
        E1[Output Gear 1st on Main Shaft]
        E2[Output Gear 2nd on Main Shaft]
        E3[Output Gear 3rd on Main Shaft]
        E4[Output Gear 4th on Main Shaft]
        E5[Output Gear 5th on Main Shaft]
        F[Sliding Dog Coupling]
        G[Output Sprocket]
    end
    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D1
    C --> D2
    C --> D3
    C --> D4
    C --> D5
    D1 --> E1
    D2 --> E2
    D3 --> E3
    D4 --> E4
    D5 --> E5
    F --> E1
    F --> E2
    F --> E3
    F --> E4
    F --> E5
    F --> G
```

### 4.4 Gear Ratio Selection Flow for KTU Problem-Solving

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Read Problem Statement] --> B{Identify Given: Te Ne Dw eta}
    B --> C[Compute Overall Ratio ioverall = ip x ig x if]
    C --> D{Required: Vmax or Tractive Force?}
    D -->|Vmax| E[Nw = Ne / ioverall]
    E --> F[V = pi x Dw x Nw / 60]
    F --> G[V kmh = V x 3.6]
    D -->|Force| H[Tw = Te x ioverall x eta]
    H --> I[Ft = Tw / rw]
    G --> J[Final Answer with Units]
    I --> J
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is the function of a gearbox in a two-wheeled vehicle? List any two types of gearboxes used in motorcycles.

**Model Answer:**

A gearbox in a two-wheeled vehicle is a mechanical assembly that **varies the torque-speed ratio** between the engine and the rear wheel. It enables the vehicle to:
1. Start from rest under high load by providing high torque multiplication in 1st gear.
2. Cruise at high speed with low engine rpm in top gear.

It also keeps the engine operating within its narrow **peak power/torque rpm band** for fuel efficiency and performance.

**Two types of gearboxes used in motorcycles:**
1. **Sliding Mesh Gearbox** — gears physically slide on splines to engage (older bikes, e.g., Yamaha RX100).
2. **Constant Mesh Gearbox** — gears always remain in mesh; power transmission is achieved by sliding dog clutches (modern bikes, e.g., Royal Enfield, KTM Duke).

> **Examiner's Note:** Students often forget to mention *why* the gearbox is needed beyond "changing speed" — explicitly linking it to engine's narrow power band earns the third mark.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** Define "overall gear ratio" of a motorcycle transmission and write the formula relating engine speed to wheel speed.

**Model Answer:**

The **overall gear ratio** $i_{overall}$ of a motorcycle transmission is the product of the primary reduction ratio, the selected gearbox ratio, and the final drive ratio.

**Formula:**

$$
i_{overall} = i_{primary} \times i_{gearbox} \times i_{final}
$$

The relation between engine speed and wheel speed is:

$$
N_w = \frac{N_e}{i_{overall}}
$$

where $N_e$ is the engine crankshaft speed in rpm and $N_w$ is the rear wheel speed in rpm. A higher overall ratio gives lower wheel speed but higher wheel torque, suited for 1st gear; a lower overall ratio gives higher wheel speed for top gear cruising.

---

### Part B — Full-Question Bank (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]

**(a)** A 5-speed motorcycle has the following gearbox ratios: $i_{g1} = 3.0$, $i_{g2} = 2.28$, $i_{g3} = 1.73$, $i_{g4} = 1.32$, $i_{g5} = 1.00$. The primary reduction ratio is 3.0 and the final drive (chain) ratio is 2.8. **(7 marks)**

(i) Calculate the overall ratio in 1st and 5th gears.
(ii) If the engine peak power is 9 kW at 8000 rpm, determine the top speed of the vehicle assuming a rear wheel diameter of 0.6 m. Take overall transmission efficiency as 0.85.

**(b)** Explain with a neat sketch the working of a **constant mesh gearbox** as used in modern geared motorcycles. Compare it with a **sliding mesh gearbox** on at least three parameters. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) (i) Overall Ratios:**

For 1st gear:

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_{overall,1} &= i_p \times i_{g1} \times i_f \\
&= 3.0 \times 3.0 \times 2.8 \\
&= 25.2
\end{aligned}
$$

For 5th gear:

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_{overall,5} &= 3.0 \times 1.00 \times 2.8 \\
&= 8.4
\end{aligned}
$$

**(a) (ii) Top Speed:**

Engine speed at peak power: $N_e = 8000$ rpm
Wheel speed in 5th gear:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_w &= \frac{N_e}{i_{overall,5}} = \frac{8000}{8.4} = 952.38 \; \text{rpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Wheel circumference: $\pi D_w = \pi \times 0.6 = 1.885$ m
Top speed in m/s:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \frac{\pi D_w N_w}{60} = \frac{1.885 \times 952.38}{60} = 29.92 \; \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to km/h:

$$
V_{max} = 29.92 \times 3.6 = \mathbf{107.7 \; km/h}
$$

(Note: The efficiency value is irrelevant for *speed* calculation — it only affects torque. Students who incorrectly multiply speed by $\eta$ will lose 2 marks.)

**(b) Constant Mesh Gearbox — Working:**

A constant mesh gearbox has all gear pairs on the main shaft and countershaft **permanently in mesh**. The countershaft pinions rotate freely on their shaft; they are not fixed. Power is transmitted only when a **dog clutch** slides along the main shaft to lock the desired gear to the main shaft.

**Working sequence:**
1. Engine torque enters via primary gear pair → main shaft.
2. Main shaft gears rotate freely with their mates on countershaft.
3. Rider presses shift lever → shift cam rotates → shift fork slides dog clutch.
4. Dog clutch teeth engage with internal teeth on the chosen output gear → torque flows to output shaft.
5. Two dogs (one for 1st/3rd/5th cluster, one for 2nd/4th) typically share the main shaft.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Sliding Mesh | Constant Mesh |
|-----------|--------------|---------------|
| Gear engagement | Gears physically slide into mesh | Dog clutches engage pre-meshed gears |
| Noise | Loud (gear clashing) | Quiet |
| Wear | High on gear teeth | Low (only dog teeth wear) |
| Modern usage | Obsolete in motorcycles | Standard in modern geared motorcycles |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]

**(a)** A geared scooter (e.g., 110 cc motorcycle) has the following specifications:
- Engine peak torque: $T_{max} = 9.5$ N·m at 6000 rpm
- Primary reduction: $i_p = 3.5$
- Gearbox 1st gear ratio: $i_{g1} = 3.2$
- Final drive ratio: $i_f = 2.93$
- Rear wheel radius: $r_w = 0.27$ m
- Total transmission efficiency: $\eta = 0.85$

**(7 marks)**

Calculate:
(i) The tractive force available at the rear wheel in 1st gear.
(ii) The grade (in percent) the vehicle can just climb, neglecting rolling resistance and assuming the vehicle weight is 105 kg.

**(b)** With a suitable block diagram, describe the **power flow path** from the engine to the rear wheel of a typical 5-speed motorcycle. Explain the role of the **clutch** and **primary reduction** in this layout. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) (i) Tractive Force in 1st Gear:**

Overall ratio in 1st gear:

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_{overall,1} &= 3.5 \times 3.2 \times 2.93 \\
&= 32.82
\end{aligned}
$$

Torque at the rear wheel:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_w &= T_{max} \times i_{overall,1} \times \eta \\
&= 9.5 \times 32.82 \times 0.85 \\
&= 265.0 \; \text{N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Tractive force:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_t &= \frac{T_w}{r_w} = \frac{265.0}{0.27} = \mathbf{981.5 \; \text{N}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(a) (ii) Grade Calculation:**

Vehicle weight: $W = m \cdot g = 105 \times 9.81 = 1030$ N.

On a slope of angle $\alpha$, the tractive force must overcome the gravity component along the slope:

$$
F_t = W \sin\alpha
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sin\alpha &= \frac{F_t}{W} = \frac{981.5}{1030} = 0.953 \\
\alpha &= \sin^{-1}(0.953) = 72.4^\circ
\end{aligned}
$$

Grade (percent):

$$
\text{Grade \%} = \tan\alpha \times 100 = \tan(72.4^\circ) \times 100 = 3.15 \times 100 = \mathbf{315\%}
$$

> **Engineering Comment:** This unusually high grade percentage (315% = slope of 72°) is the **theoretical maximum on level ground with no rolling resistance**. In practice, rolling resistance and tyre-road friction limit the climbable grade to about **30–40%** for typical two-wheelers. The question's "neglecting rolling resistance" clause is critical.

**(b) Power Flow Path (Block Diagram):**

```
Engine → Clutch → Primary Reduction (Gear Pair)
      → Main Shaft → Constant Mesh Countershaft
      → Sliding Dog Clutch (selected gear)
      → Output Shaft → Chain Sprocket (Final Drive)
      → Rear Wheel → Road
```

**Role of the Clutch:**
The clutch is a **friction plate assembly** between the engine crankshaft and the gearbox input. It allows the rider to **disengage engine power** from the gearbox so that the engine can idle while the rider shifts gears or stops at traffic signals, and to **gradually re-engage** power to avoid stalling the engine or jerking the vehicle during take-off.

**Role of the Primary Reduction:**
The primary reduction is a **fixed gear pair** (typically a pinion on the crankshaft meshing with a larger gear on the main shaft) that provides a constant reduction (commonly 2.5 to 4:1). It:
1. Reduces the high engine rpm to a more usable input rpm for the gearbox.
2. Increases the torque entering the gearbox.
3. Is housed inside the engine casing on most modern motorcycles (wet sump arrangement).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Units trap:** Students frequently forget to convert m/s to km/h (multiply by 3.6) or mm to m for wheel diameter. Always check unit consistency at the *last* step.
> 2. **Efficiency misuse:** Efficiency $\eta$ is used for *torque multiplication* (energy loss), NOT for *speed conversion*. Wheel speed depends purely on gear ratio and engine speed.
> 3. **Gear ratio confusion:** Primary reduction ratio $i_p$ is part of the *overall* ratio. Some students only multiply $i_g \times i_f$ and miss a factor of 2–4 — losing 3–4 marks.
> 4. **Grade calculation trap:** Grade (%) = $\tan\alpha \times 100$, NOT $\sin\alpha \times 100$. Confusing these leads to wrong answers.
> 5. **Step ratio vs progression:** Geometric progression is *not* linear; using arithmetic interpolation for missing gear ratios is a common error.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ A **gearbox** is needed because an IC engine has a narrow usable rpm range, while a vehicle needs a wide torque × speed range.
- ✅ **Three types** to remember: Sliding Mesh, Constant Mesh, Synchromesh. Constant mesh is the modern standard in motorcycles.
- ✅ **Overall ratio:** $i_{overall} = i_p \times i_g \times i_f$ — multiply all three stages.
- ✅ **Wheel speed:** $N_w = N_e / i_{overall}$ — *inverse* of overall ratio.
- ✅ **Wheel torque:** $T_w = T_e \times i_{overall} \times \eta$ — *direct* product times efficiency.
- ✅ **Tractive force:** $F_t = T_w / r_w$ — use wheel *radius*, not diameter.
- ✅ **Vehicle speed:** $V_{km/h} = 0.1885 \times D_w \times N_w$ — easy memorization formula.
- ✅ **Step ratio (Geometric Progression):** $i_n = i_1 \cdot K^{-(n-1)}$ where $K \approx 1.25$ to $1.45$.
- ✅ **Top speed** is governed by the *smallest* overall ratio (5th gear) and **engine redline rpm**.
- ✅ **Clutch** disengages engine from gearbox during shifts and gradual take-off.
- ✅ **Primary reduction** is fixed (built into engine casing) and provides the first torque multiplication.
- ✅ **Final drive** in 2-wheelers is usually a chain-sprocket ($i_f \approx 2.5$ to $3.5$); in 3-wheelers it may be a shaft-drive with bevel gears.
- ✅ **Efficiency range:** spur gears $\approx 0.92$–$0.97$, helical gears $\approx 0.95$–$0.98$ (per mesh pair).
- ✅ **Modern trend:** CVT in scooters (Honda Activa, TVS Jupiter) replaces stepped gearbox; uses variable-pitch pulleys with a V-belt.
- ✅ **KTU favourite 14-mark question pattern:** "Calculate overall ratio + vehicle speed/tractive force in a specific gear + sketch and explain constant mesh gearbox."

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Drive Systems: Chain, Belt, and Shaft Drives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT631 - TWO AND THREE WHEELED VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Drive Systems: Chain, Belt, and Shaft Drives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of two and three-wheeled vehicles, **Drive Systems** refer to the mechanical assemblies responsible for transmitting the rotational power (torque × angular velocity) developed by the engine's crankshaft to the driving wheel (rear wheel in two-wheelers, rear axle in three-wheelers). The transmitted motion must be converted in speed (reduction), direction (90° or 180°), and sometimes in the type of motion (rotational to rotational through different axes). The three principal secondary drive systems studied under KTU Module 2 are:

- **Chain Drives** – Positive (no-slip) drives using roller chains and sprockets. Dominant in geared motorcycles.
- **Belt Drives** – Friction drives using V-belts, flat belts, or toothed (timing) belts. Dominant in scooters employing CVT (Continuous Variable Transmission).
- **Shaft Drives** – Enclosed drives using a propeller shaft with universal/CV joints, terminating in a bevel or hypoid final-drive gear. Used in high-capacity motorcycles and most three-wheelers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 specifically classifies the secondary transmission into these three categories. The student must be able to **identify**, **compare**, and **derive performance parameters** of each system. Chain and belt drives are flexible drives (long center distances), whereas shaft drives are rigid enclosed drives.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Plain-English Intuition

Think of the engine as a person spinning a large steering wheel very fast but with limited pushing effort. The rear wheel is small and needs to spin slower but push harder. The **drive system is the "translator"** that sits between the engine and the wheel:

- **Chain Drive Analogy → A bicycle chain.** Two sprockets (a small one attached to the engine output and a large one attached to the rear wheel) are connected by a metal chain with interlocking teeth. The chain pulls the large sprocket exactly as fast as the small one, gear-by-gear. Because the rear sprocket has more teeth, it rotates slower but with more torque — perfect for hill climbing and acceleration.

- **Belt Drive Analogy → A long fan belt in a scooter.** Imagine gripping a rubber band between two pulleys. As the engine pulley turns, friction drags the belt, which in turn rotates the rear pulley. There is no "tooth" — just friction. In modern scooters, the V-belt rides on variable-diameter pulleys, allowing the gear ratio to change continuously (CVT) — the scooter automatically "chooses" the right gear without the rider touching a clutch lever.

- **Shaft Drive Analogy → The drive shaft under a car.** Instead of a chain or belt, a solid steel rod with flexible joints (like a wrist joint in your arm) carries the rotation. This is enclosed, clean, quiet, and needs almost no maintenance — but adds weight and cost.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Rule of Thumb:** *Flexible drives (chain & belt) are light, cheap, and efficient for short-to-medium distances. Shaft drives are heavy and expensive, but enclosed, durable, and preferred for high-torque, long-distance applications.*

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value / Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Coefficient of friction (V-belt on pulley, μ) | **0.20 – 0.40** | Determines grip and tension ratio |
| Coefficient of friction (chain roller, negligible) | µ ≈ 0 (positive drive) | Chain does not rely on friction |
| Standard chain pitch, *P* | **12.7 mm (1/2"), 15.875 mm (5/8"), 19.05 mm (3/4")** | Distance between consecutive pin centers |
| V-belt angle, *2α* | **32°, 36°, 38°, 40°** | Wedge angle of the trapezoidal cross-section |
| Density of chain material (steel) | **ρ_c = 7.85 × 10⁻⁶ kg/mm³** | Used to compute chain mass for centrifugal load |
| Density of belt (rubber) | **ρ_b ≈ 1.1 × 10⁻⁶ kg/mm³** | Used to compute belt mass for centrifugal tension |
| Universal joint maximum working angle | **8° – 15° (single Hooke's), 0°–45° (CV joint)** | Higher angles introduce velocity fluctuation |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 CHAIN DRIVE — Operational Breakdown

### A. Anatomy of a Roller Chain

A standard **bush roller chain** (ISO 606) consists of repeating link pairs:

- **Inner link (Pitch link):** Two inner plates press-fitted onto two **bushings**.
- **Outer link:** Two outer plates press-fitted onto two **pins** that pass through the bushings.
- **Roller:** A free-rotating cylinder mounted over each bushing; it engages the sprocket tooth and reduces wear.
- **Pitch, P:** Center-to-center distance between two consecutive pins = the distance between two consecutive sprocket teeth measured along the pitch circle.

### B. Geometric & Kinematic Relationships

Let:
- *N₁* = number of teeth on the smaller (driver) sprocket
- *N₂* = number of teeth on the larger (driven) sprocket
- *P* = chain pitch (mm)
- *D₁, D₂* = pitch-circle diameters (mm)
- *C* = center distance (mm)
- *L* = chain length in pitches (links)

The pitch-circle diameter of a sprocket is **not** the same as the tip-circle diameter. It is the imaginary circle on which the chain pin centers travel:

$$D = \frac{P}{\sin\left(\dfrac{180°}{N}\right)}$$

The **velocity ratio (VR)** is exactly the ratio of teeth (no slip in a positive drive):

$$\text{VR} = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{D_2}{D_1} = \frac{\omega_1}{\omega_2} = \frac{n_1}{n_2}$$

### C. Chain Length Equation (KTU High-Yield)

The exact chain length in **number of pitches** is given by the well-established empirical formula (Morse / Renold):

$$L = 2\left(\frac{C}{P}\right) + \frac{N_1 + N_2}{2} + \frac{\left(\dfrac{N_2 - N_1}{2\pi}\right)^2 \cdot P}{C}$$

For design use, *L* is always rounded **up to the next even integer** (so the chain has a complete set of inner + outer links).

### D. Power Transmission and Chain Failure

The transmitted power is:

$$P = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{1000} \cdot v \quad \text{(kW)}$$

where *T₁* = tight-side tension (N), *T₂* = slack-side tension (N), *v* = chain linear speed (m/s).

Two governing failure criteria:
1. **Tensile failure of plates/pins** → governed by breaking load and a **factor of safety ≈ 7–10** (shock-loaded motorcycle applications).
2. **Fatigue wear (Elongation):** As pins and bushings wear, the pitch *P* effectively increases. Industry limits useful life to **3% elongation** of original pitch (some standards allow 1.5%).

### E. Why Chains Need Periodic Maintenance

A chain is exposed to dust, water, and high cyclic stress. KTU expects the student to recognize that **chains stretch, wear, and require lubrication** — the opposite of belt or shaft drives.

---

## 2.2 BELT DRIVE — Operational Breakdown

### A. Types Used in Two/Three-Wheelers

- **Flat belt:** Rectangular cross-section. Used in vintage / low-power machines.
- **V-belt (Classical & Narrow):** Trapezoidal cross-section that wedges into the pulley groove. **Standard in CVT scooters (e.g., Honda Activa, TVS Jupiter).**
- **Toothed (Timing) belt:** Positive engagement like a chain, but with a rubber/polyurethane body reinforced with fiberglass. Used in some scooter CVTs and in cam drives of small engines.
- **Multi-rib belt (Serpentine):** Combines flat-belt flexibility with V-belt grip. Used in three-wheelers with auxiliary drives.

### B. V-Belt Geometry and the Capstan (Euler-Eytelwein) Equation

The fundamental governing equation of all friction belt drives is the **capstan equation**:

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta}$$

where:
- *T₁* = tight-side tension (N)
- *T₂* = slack-side tension (N)
- *μ* = coefficient of friction between belt and pulley
- *θ* = angle of wrap/lap (radians)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** For a V-belt, the effective friction is increased because the belt is wedged into the groove. The corrected equation is:

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta / \sin\alpha}$$

where *α* is the half-angle of the V-groove. The factor 1/sin α is the **wedging action multiplier** (≈ 2 to 3 for typical angles).

### C. Belt Length and Angle of Contact

For an open belt drive with diameters *D₁, D₂* and center distance *C*:

$$L = 2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(D_1 + D_2) + \frac{(D_2 - D_1)^2}{4C}$$

Angle of contact on the smaller (driver) pulley (the critical one):

$$\theta_1 = \pi + 2 \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D_2 - D_1}{2C}\right)$$

Angle of contact on the larger pulley:

$$\theta_2 = \pi - 2 \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D_2 - D_1}{2C}\right)$$

Note that **θ₁ + θ₂ = 2π** (360°).

### D. Centrifugal Tension

A belt of mass *m* (kg/m) moving at speed *v* (m/s) experiences a centrifugal tension *T_c* that **reduces the effective transmitting tension**:

$$T_c = m \cdot v^2$$

The net usable tensions become:
- Effective tight side: $T_1 - T_c$
- Effective slack side: $T_2 - T_c$

### E. Power Transmitted

$$P = (T_1 - T_2)\, v = T_c \left(e^{\mu \theta} - 1\right) v$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why CVT Scooters Use V-Belts:** The variable-diameter pulley allows the system to keep the engine near its **maximum-power RPM** while varying vehicle speed — a feat impossible with a fixed chain/sprocket ratio. The belt must withstand high curvature and heat, hence the use of **aramid-fiber-reinforced polyurethane or rubber V-belts**.

---

## 2.3 SHAFT DRIVE — Operational Breakdown

### A. Components

1. **Propeller Shaft:** A hollow or solid steel tube that carries rotation from gearbox output to the final drive.
2. **Universal Joint (Hooke's joint):** Allows angular misalignment between input and output shafts. **Drawback:** Causes non-uniform output velocity unless the input/output angle is zero.
3. **Constant-Velocity (CV) Joint:** A double- or tripod-type joint that **maintains constant output velocity** regardless of angle. Standard in modern shaft-driven motorcycles (BMW, Honda ST-series, Yamaha FJR).
4. **Slip Joint / Spline:** Permits axial length change as the rear suspension moves.
5. **Final Drive Gear Set:** Bevel, hypoid, or worm-and-wheel arrangement that turns the drive 90° to the wheel axis. Hypoid gears (offset) are common in high-capacity shaft-driven motorcycles.

### B. Universal Joint Velocity Relationship

For a single Hooke's joint at angle *φ* between input and output shafts, the output velocity fluctuates sinusoidally:

$$\tan \beta = \frac{\tan \phi \cdot \sin \alpha}{1 + \cos \alpha \cdot \tan^2 \phi \cdot \sin^2 \alpha \cdot \cos^2 \alpha}$$

(where *α* is the input shaft rotation angle and *β* is the output shaft rotation angle). A **double Hooke's joint (two joints at equal and opposite angles)** cancels this fluctuation — the principle used in rear-wheel-drive automobiles.

### C. Why Shaft Drive is Used

- **Low maintenance** (enclosed, lubricated gear set).
- **Long service life** (no wear-parts like chains or belts).
- **Clean operation** (no chain grease, no belt dust).
- **Disadvantages:** Heavier (≈ +10–15 kg), more expensive, slightly less efficient due to gear-mesh losses (≈ 92–95% vs. chain 97–98%).

---

## 2.4 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All equations below are KTU Module 2 examinable. Memorize the meaning of every symbol.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Key Symbols & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sprocket pitch diameter | $D = \dfrac{P}{\sin(180°/N)}$ | *P* = pitch (mm), *N* = teeth |
| 2 | Chain velocity ratio | $\text{VR} = N_2 / N_1 = D_2 / D_1$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | Chain length (pitches) | $L = 2(C/P) + (N_1 + N_2)/2 + [(N_2 - N_1)^2 \cdot P] / (4\pi^2 \cdot C)$ | *L* = number of links |
| 4 | Chain linear speed | $v = P \cdot N_1 \cdot n_1 / 60{,}000$ (m/s) | *n* in rpm, *P* in mm |
| 5 | Power via chain | $P = (T_1 - T_2)\,v / 1000$ (kW) | *T* in N, *v* in m/s |
| 6 | Capstan (flat belt) | $T_1 / T_2 = e^{\mu \theta}$ | *θ* in radians |
| 7 | Capstan (V-belt) | $T_1 / T_2 = e^{\mu \theta / \sin\alpha}$ | *α* = half groove angle |
| 8 | Centrifugal tension | $T_c = m v^2$ | *m* = mass per unit length (kg/m) |
| 9 | Belt length (open) | $L = 2C + (\pi/2)(D_1 + D_2) + (D_2 - D_1)^2 / 4C$ | all lengths in same unit |
| 10 | Wrap angle, small pulley | $\theta_1 = \pi + 2\sin^{-1}\!\left[\,(D_2 - D_1)/(2C)\,\right]$ | radians |
| 11 | Wrap angle, large pulley | $\theta_2 = 2\pi - \theta_1$ | radians |
| 12 | Shaft drive efficiency | $\eta = \eta_{\text{uj}}^2 \cdot \eta_{\text{gear}}$ | typical 0.90–0.95 |
| 13 | Max elongation limit | $\Delta L / L_0 \le 3\%$ (or 1.5% per ISO) | *L₀* = original length |
| 14 | Belt slip check | $v_1 = \pi D_1 n_1$, $v_2 = \pi D_2 n_2$ | linear speeds of pulleys |
| 15 | Slip / creep ratio | $s = (v_1 - v_2)/v_1$ | ≈ 0.01–0.02 for V-belt |

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute-value and division operations in the table above use parentheses, not the vertical pipe symbol, to keep markdown tables safe for KTU PDF rendering.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Chain drives** power the rear wheel of nearly every **geared commuter motorcycle** (Bajaj Pulsar, KTM Duke, Royal Enfield) because they are cheap, light, and can be quickly swapped to change the final-drive ratio.
- **Belt drives** dominate the **scooter segment** (Honda Activa, TVS Jupiter, Suzuki Access) due to the CVT requirement and the clean, maintenance-free appeal.
- **Shaft drives** are found in **premium tourers and three-wheelers** (BMW R 1250 GS, Honda Gold Wing, Yamaha FJR1300, Bajaj RE three-wheeler cargo) where longevity, low maintenance, and high torque matter more than weight and cost.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Chain Length Equation (Pitch Count)

We will derive the chain length *L* in pitches (number of links) for a two-sprocket chain drive with center distance *C*, chain pitch *P*, and sprocket tooth counts *N₁* and *N₂* (assume *N₂ > N₁*).

### Step 1 — Geometric Setup

Imagine wrapping the chain around the two sprockets. The chain consists of:
- Two semicircular arcs hugging sprockets 1 and 2 (each of length *π · D / 2* in absolute terms, but counted in pitches).
- Two straight runs connecting the tangent points.

The arc length on each sprocket in pitches is:

$$\text{Arc}_1 = \frac{\pi D_1 / 2}{P} = \frac{\pi}{2P} \cdot \frac{P}{\sin(180°/N_1)} = \frac{\pi}{2 \sin(180°/N_1)}$$

But for *N* ≥ 17, sin(180°/N) ≈ 180°/N in radians, simplifying to **N/2**. So:

$$\text{Arc}_1 \approx \frac{N_1}{2}, \quad \text{Arc}_2 \approx \frac{N_2}{2}$$

### Step 2 — Straight Portion Length

The two straight tangents have total length *2C* (one above, one below the line of centers), with a small correction factor *y* because the tangents do not start at the centers but at the tangent points.

By similar triangles, the correction is:

$$y = \frac{(D_2 - D_1)^2}{4 \pi^2 C} \cdot P \quad \text{(in pitches)}$$

(This is a small term; for *C* large relative to *D₂ − D₁*, it can be neglected in first-pass design.)

### Step 3 — Total Chain Length in Pitches

Summing the contributions:

$$L = 2 \cdot \frac{C}{P} + \frac{N_1 + N_2}{2} + \frac{P \cdot (N_2 - N_1)^2}{4 \pi^2 C}$$

$$\boxed{\,L = \frac{2C}{P} + \frac{N_1 + N_2}{2} + \frac{P \,(N_2 - N_1)^2}{4 \pi^2 C}\,}$$

**Why this matters in KTU exams:** A student is often given *C/P, N₁, N₂* and asked to compute the chain length — this is a 7-mark application problem in the 14-mark questions.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Capstan Equation for V-Belt

### Step 1 — Free-Body Diagram of a Belt Element

Consider a small element of belt subtending angle *dθ* on the pulley. The element has tight-side tension *T* on one side and *(T + dT)* on the other (since tension increases as we move toward the tight side). The pulley pushes the element inward with normal force *dN*, and friction *μ dN* acts along the tangent opposing motion.

### Step 2 — Equilibrium of the Element

Resolving tangentially:

$$(T + dT) \cos\!\left(\frac{d\theta}{2}\right) - T \cos\!\left(\frac{d\theta}{2}\right) = \mu\, dN$$

For very small *dθ*, cos(dθ/2) ≈ 1, so:

$$dT = \mu\, dN$$

Resolving radially (centripetal balance):

$$dN = T \sin(d\theta) \approx T\, d\theta$$

### Step 3 — Combine and Integrate

$$dT = \mu\, T\, d\theta \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{dT}{T} = \mu\, d\theta$$

Integrating from *T₂* (slack) to *T₁* (tight) over wrap angle *θ*:

$$\int_{T_2}^{T_1} \frac{dT}{T} = \mu \int_{0}^{\theta} d\theta$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{T_1}{T_2}\right) = \mu \theta$$

$$\boxed{\,\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta}\,}$$

### Step 4 — Extension to V-Belt

In a V-belt, the normal force is increased by the **wedging action**. The belt makes contact with both groove walls, so the resultant normal force on the elementary section is *dN / sin α* where *α* is the half-angle of the groove. Substituting:

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta / \sin\alpha}$$

This shows that the V-belt can transmit **2 to 3 times more power** than a flat belt for the same coefficient of friction and wrap angle — the engineering reason why scooters use V-belts.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example: Chain Drive Design

> **Given:** A 150 cc motorcycle engine delivers 8.5 kW at 6500 rpm to a 45-tooth rear sprocket through a 15-tooth engine sprocket using a roller chain of pitch 12.7 mm. Center distance is 280 mm. Compute:
> (a) Sprocket pitch diameters,
> (b) Velocity ratio and rear-wheel rpm,
> (c) Chain linear speed,
> (d) Chain length in pitches and links,
> (e) Tight- and slack-side tensions if the power is fully transmitted (assume T_c is negligible for first iteration).

### Solution

**(a) Pitch diameter of driver sprocket (N₁ = 15):**

$$D_1 = \frac{P}{\sin(180°/N_1)} = \frac{12.7}{\sin(12°)} = \frac{12.7}{0.2079} = 61.08 \text{ mm}$$

**Pitch diameter of driven sprocket (N₂ = 45):**

$$D_2 = \frac{12.7}{\sin(4°)} = \frac{12.7}{0.06976} = 182.05 \text{ mm}$$

**(b) Velocity ratio and rear-wheel rpm:**

$$\text{VR} = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{45}{15} = 3$$

$$n_2 = \frac{n_1}{\text{VR}} = \frac{6500}{3} \approx 2167 \text{ rpm}$$

**(c) Chain linear speed:**

$$v = \frac{\pi D_1 n_1}{60{,}000} = \frac{\pi \times 61.08 \times 6500}{60{,}000} = 20.78 \text{ m/s}$$

(Or equivalently $v = P \cdot N_1 \cdot n_1 / 60{,}000 = 12.7 \times 15 \times 6500 / 60{,}000 = 20.64$ m/s — minor difference from rounding.)

**(d) Chain length in pitches:**

$$L = \frac{2 \times 280}{12.7} + \frac{15 + 45}{2} + \frac{12.7 \times (45 - 15)^2}{4 \pi^2 \times 280}$$

Compute each term:
- First term: $2 \times 280 / 12.7 = 44.094$
- Second term: $(15 + 45)/2 = 30.000$
- Third term: $12.7 \times 900 / (4 \times 9.8696 \times 280) = 11{,}430 / 11{,}054 = 1.034$

$$L = 44.094 + 30.000 + 1.034 = 75.128 \text{ pitches}$$

Round up to the next even integer → **L = 76 pitches (76 links)**.

**(e) Tight- and slack-side tensions:**

With $P = 8.5 \text{ kW}$, $v = 20.64$ m/s:

$$T_1 - T_2 = \frac{1000 \times 8.5}{20.64} = 411.8 \text{ N}$$

For a chain, slack-side tension is typically 10–15% of tight-side tension in a properly tensioned drive. So:

$$T_2 = 0.10 \times T_1 \quad \text{or} \quad T_1 = T_2 / 0.10$$

Substituting:

$$T_1 - 0.10\,T_1 = 411.8 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0.90\,T_1 = 411.8$$

$$T_1 = 457.6 \text{ N}, \quad T_2 = 45.8 \text{ N}$$

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example: V-Belt Drive

> **Given:** A 110 cc scooter CVT uses a V-belt on driver pulley diameter 80 mm and driven pulley diameter 200 mm. Center distance = 350 mm. Coefficient of friction μ = 0.30, half groove angle α = 18°. Belt mass per unit length m = 0.12 kg/m. Engine speed = 7000 rpm. Compute:
> (a) Belt length (open drive),
> (b) Angle of contact on the smaller pulley,
> (c) Centrifugal tension,
> (d) Maximum power transmittable if T₂ is assumed to be 50 N.

### Solution

**(a) Belt length:**

$$L = 2 \times 350 + \frac{\pi}{2}(80 + 200) + \frac{(200 - 80)^2}{4 \times 350}$$

$$L = 700 + 439.82 + 10.286 = 1150.1 \text{ mm} \approx 1150 \text{ mm}$$

**(b) Angle of contact on smaller pulley:**

$$\theta_1 = \pi + 2 \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{200 - 80}{2 \times 350}\right) = \pi + 2 \sin^{-1}(0.1714)$$

$$\sin^{-1}(0.1714) = 0.1722 \text{ rad}$$

$$\theta_1 = \pi + 0.3443 = 3.486 \text{ rad} \approx 199.7°$$

**(c) Centrifugal tension:**

Belt linear speed (using driver):

$$v = \frac{\pi D_1 n_1}{60{,}000} = \frac{\pi \times 80 \times 7000}{60{,}000} = 29.32 \text{ m/s}$$

$$T_c = m v^2 = 0.12 \times (29.32)^2 = 0.12 \times 859.66 = 103.16 \text{ N}$$

**(d) Maximum power transmittable:**

With T₂ = 50 N and *T₁* computed from capstan:

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta_1 / \sin\alpha} = e^{0.30 \times 3.486 / \sin(18°)}$$

$$\sin(18°) = 0.3090$$

$$\text{Exponent} = \frac{0.30 \times 3.486}{0.3090} = 3.385$$

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{3.385} = 29.51$$

$$T_1 = 29.51 \times 50 = 1475.5 \text{ N}$$

Net transmitting tension:

$$(T_1 - T_c) - (T_2 - T_c) = T_1 - T_2 = 1475.5 - 50 = 1425.5 \text{ N}$$

Power:

$$P = (T_1 - T_2)\, v / 1000 = 1425.5 \times 29.32 / 1000 = 41.79 \text{ kW}$$

> This is theoretical maximum; in practice scooter CVTs transmit 5–8 kW.

---

## 3.5 Comparative Analysis Matrix

| Parameter | Chain Drive | Belt Drive (V) | Shaft Drive |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Positive (mesh) | Friction (wedge) | Positive (gear mesh) |
| **Efficiency** | 97–98% | 95–97% | 92–95% |
| **Slip / Creep** | None | Yes (≈ 1–2%) | None |
| **Maintenance** | High (lubrication, tensioning) | Medium (tensioning only) | Very low (sealed) |
| **Service Life** | 20,000–40,000 km | 30,000–60,000 km | > 100,000 km |
| **Noise** | High (chain slap) | Medium (whine) | Low (gear hum) |
| **Weight (typical)** | 2.5–4 kg | 1.0–2.0 kg | 12–18 kg |
| **Cost** | Low | Low | High |
| **Can ratio change at runtime?** | No (fixed sprockets) | Yes (CVT pulleys) | No (fixed gears) |
| **Typical application** | Geared motorcycles | Scooters (CVT) | Tourers, three-wheelers |
| **Failure mode** | Elongation, link fracture | Stretch, slip, heat | Gear wear, seal leak |
| **KTU 2024 Module 2 focus** | Pitch, length, tension | Capstan, centrifugal tension | U-joint, CV joint, hypoid |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Two-Wheeler Drivetrain

The following Mermaid block shows how the engine's output flows through the primary reduction (gearbox inside engine) and then into the secondary drive (Module 2 topic: chain, belt, or shaft) to reach the rear wheel.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Engine Crankshaft] --> B[Primary Reduction Gearbox]
    B --> C{Clutch Engagement}
    C --> D[Transmission Gears 1 to N]
    D --> E[Output Shaft of Gearbox]
    E --> F{Secondary Drive Type}
    F -->|Chain Path| G[Engine Sprocket N1]
    G --> H[Roller Chain]
    H --> I[Rear Sprocket N2]
    I --> J[Rear Wheel Hub]
    F -->|Belt Path CVT| K[Driver Pulley Variable]
    K --> L[Aramid Reinforced V Belt]
    L --> M[Driven Pulley Variable]
    M --> J
    F -->|Shaft Path| N[Propeller Shaft]
    N --> O[Universal Joint or CV Joint]
    O --> P[Bevel or Hypoid Final Drive]
    P --> J
    J --> Q[Road Surface Contact]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The engine → gearbox → clutch path is common to all three secondary-drive types.
- The **decision diamond F** branches into the three Module 2 systems.
- All three paths converge at the rear wheel hub (J) and the road (Q).

---

## 4.2 Roller Chain Anatomy (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Engine Sprocket Teeth] -->|mesh| R1[Roller]
    R1 --> B1[Bushing]
    B1 --> PI1[Pin]
    PI1 --> IP1[Inner Plate x2]
    PI1 --> OP1[Outer Plate x2]
    OP1 --> PI2[Next Pin]
    PI2 --> B2[Bushing]
    B2 --> R2[Roller]
    R2 --> P2[Driven Sprocket Teeth]
```

> **Note on naming:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `P1`, `R1`, `B1`) and avoid reserved Mermaid keywords like `end`, `subgraph`, and `graph`. Labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric text — no bold markers or HTML inside quoted labels.

---

## 4.3 V-Belt CVT Functional Schematic (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ENG[Engine Centrifugal Clutch] --> DRV[Driver Pulley Front Half Slides Inward as RPM Rises]
    DRV --> BELT[Aramid Reinforced V Belt Wedged in Groove]
    BELT --> MOV[Driven Pulley Rear Half Slides Outward Against Spring]
    MOV --> WHL[Rear Wheel Pulley Fixed to Hub]
    WHL --> AX[Rear Axle]
    AX --> TYR[Road Tyre Contact Patch]
```

**How CVT works in this architecture:**
- At low RPM, the driver pulley is small (low gear, high torque).
- As engine RPM rises, the centrifugal weights in the driver pulley force the front half inward, **reducing the working diameter**.
- The belt then climbs higher on the driven pulley, **increasing its effective diameter** → overall ratio drops (high gear).
- This continuous change keeps the engine near its peak-power RPM band.

---

## 4.4 Shaft-Drive Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    GB[Gearbox Output Shaft] --> SJ[Slip Joint Spline Compensates Suspension Travel]
    SJ --> SHF[Propeller Shaft Hollow Steel Tube]
    SHF --> UJ[Universal Joint OR CV Joint at Pinion End]
    UJ --> PIN[Pinion Gear]
    PIN --> RING[Ring Gear Bevel or Hypoid]
    RING --> AXLE[Rear Axle Solid or Half Shaft]
    AXLE --> HUB[Rear Wheel Hub]
    HUB --> TIRE[Road Tyre]
```

> **Reading the architecture:** A shaft-driven drivetrain is essentially a *miniature rear-wheel-drive car* rear axle. The slip joint (SJ) is what allows the rear wheel to move up/down with the suspension without breaking the shaft.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
