# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]**
*List the three principal secondary drive systems used in two and three-wheeled vehicles. State one typical vehicle that uses each system and one key advantage of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Chain Drive** — Used in geared motorcycles (e.g., Bajaj Pulsar 150). **Advantage:** Light, cheap, easy ratio change by swapping sprockets. (1 mark)
2. **Belt Drive (V-belt)** — Used in scooters with CVT (e.g., Honda Activa). **Advantage:** Smooth, no metal-to-metal contact, allows continuously variable ratio. (1 mark)
3. **Shaft Drive** — Used in touring motorcycles and three-wheelers (e.g., BMW R 1250, Bajaj RE auto). **Advantage:** Enclosed, low maintenance, long service life. (1 mark)

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Set A]**
*Define the "capstan equation" for a flat belt drive and write the modified form for a V-belt. Why is the V-belt preferred for scooter CVTs?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Capstan equation (flat belt):** $\dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta}$, where *T₁, T₂* are tight and slack tensions, *μ* is coefficient of friction, and *θ* is wrap angle. (1 mark)
- **Modified form for V-belt:** $\dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta / \sin\alpha}$, where *α* is the half-angle of the V-groove. The denominator sin α (≈ 0.3) increases the exponent, hence the tension ratio. (1 mark)
- **Why preferred for scooter CVTs:** V-belts grip firmly without slipping at high curvature; the V-shape naturally accommodates variable-diameter pulleys; quieter than chains; require no lubrication. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Module 2 Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each 14-mark question in ESE (End Semester Exam) for a 100-mark paper carries two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Module 2 (out of 5 modules) is typically tested by **one full 14-mark question + one or two Part A 3-mark questions**.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, Set A]**

**Question:** *(a)* A motorcycle uses a roller chain of pitch 15.875 mm to connect a 18-tooth engine sprocket to a 48-tooth rear sprocket. The center distance is 320 mm. Calculate: (i) the velocity ratio, (ii) the chain length in number of pitches, (iii) the pitch-circle diameter of each sprocket. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* The same motorcycle travels at 90 km/h at an engine speed of 6000 rpm. Compute the chain linear speed in m/s and the effective wheel rolling radius. State any assumptions made. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Velocity ratio (2 marks):**

$$\text{VR} = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{48}{18} = 2.667$$

(0.5 mark for formula, 1.0 mark for correct substitution, 0.5 mark for final answer.)

**(ii) Chain length in pitches (3 marks):**

$$L = \frac{2C}{P} + \frac{N_1 + N_2}{2} + \frac{P\,(N_2 - N_1)^2}{4 \pi^2 C}$$

- First term: $2 \times 320 / 15.875 = 40.31$ (0.75 mark)
- Second term: $(18 + 48)/2 = 33.00$ (0.75 mark)
- Third term: $\dfrac{15.875 \times (48 - 18)^2}{4 \pi^2 \times 320} = \dfrac{15.875 \times 900}{12{,}633} = 1.131$ (1.0 mark)
- Sum: $L = 40.31 + 33.00 + 1.131 = 74.44$ pitches (0.5 mark)

Round up to next even integer → **L = 76 pitches (76 links)** (additional 0.5 mark for stating the design rule).

**(iii) Pitch-circle diameters (2 marks):**

$$D_1 = \frac{15.875}{\sin(180°/18)} = \frac{15.875}{\sin(10°)} = \frac{15.875}{0.1736} = 91.44 \text{ mm}$$

$$D_2 = \frac{15.875}{\sin(180°/48)} = \frac{15.875}{\sin(3.75°)} = \frac{15.875}{0.06540} = 242.74 \text{ mm}$$

(1 mark each for *D₁* and *D₂*.)

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Chain linear speed (3 marks):**

$$v = \frac{\pi D_1 n_1}{60{,}000} = \frac{\pi \times 91.44 \times 6000}{60{,}000} = 28.73 \text{ m/s}$$

(1 mark for formula, 1 mark for substitution using *D₁* and *n₁*, 1 mark for final answer.)

**Rear sprocket speed (1 mark):**

$$n_2 = \frac{n_1}{\text{VR}} = \frac{6000}{2.667} = 2250 \text{ rpm}$$

**Wheel rolling speed and radius (3 marks):**

Vehicle speed = 90 km/h = 25 m/s.

Assuming no chain slip and no tyre slip, the wheel's outer circumference moves at 25 m/s:

$$v_{\text{wheel}} = \frac{2 \pi R_{\text{eff}} \cdot n_2}{60}$$

$$25 = \frac{2 \pi R_{\text{eff}} \times 2250}{60}$$

$$R_{\text{eff}} = \frac{25 \times 60}{2 \pi \times 2250} = \frac{1500}{14{,}137} = 0.1061 \text{ m} = 106.1 \text{ mm}$$

(1 mark for setting up equation, 1 mark for correct numeric substitution, 1 mark for final answer.)

**Assumptions stated (included in the 3 marks):**
- No chain slip.
- No tyre slip.
- No wind/grade resistance affecting speed.
- Sprocket pitch diameter is the effective rolling diameter (in reality, slightly less than the actual tyre outer radius due to tyre compression).

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, Set B]**

**Question:** *(a)* A scooter CVT uses a V-belt drive. The driver pulley diameter is 75 mm, driven pulley diameter is 180 mm, and center distance is 320 mm. The V-groove half-angle is 18° and the coefficient of friction between belt and pulley is 0.28. Compute: (i) the belt length, (ii) the angle of wrap on the smaller pulley. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* The belt mass is 0.10 kg/m. The driver pulley rotates at 6500 rpm. Calculate the centrifugal tension, and determine the maximum power the belt can transmit if the slack-side tension is 40 N. Use the V-belt capstan equation. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Belt length (4 marks):**

$$L = 2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(D_1 + D_2) + \frac{(D_2 - D_1)^2}{4C}$$

- $2C = 2 \times 320 = 640$ mm (0.5 mark)
- $\dfrac{\pi}{2}(75 + 180) = \dfrac{\pi}{2} \times 255 = 400.55$ mm (1.5 marks)
- $\dfrac{(180 - 75)^2}{4 \times 320} = \dfrac{11{,}025}{1280} = 8.61$ mm (1.5 marks)

$$L = 640 + 400.55 + 8.61 = 1049.16 \text{ mm} \approx 1049 \text{ mm}$$

(0.5 mark for final answer.)

**(ii) Angle of wrap on smaller pulley (3 marks):**

$$\theta_1 = \pi + 2 \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D_2 - D_1}{2C}\right) = \pi + 2 \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{180 - 75}{2 \times 320}\right)$$

$$\theta_1 = \pi + 2 \sin^{-1}(0.1641) = \pi + 2 \times 0.1648 = \pi + 0.3295$$

$$\theta_1 = 3.4711 \text{ rad} = 198.86°$$

(1 mark for formula, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for final answer with correct unit.)

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Centrifugal tension (3 marks):**

Belt linear speed:

$$v = \frac{\pi D_1 n_1}{60{,}000} = \frac{\pi \times 75 \times 6500}{60{,}000} = 25.53 \text{ m/s}$$

(1 mark)

$$T_c = m v^2 = 0.10 \times (25.53)^2 = 0.10 \times 651.78 = 65.18 \text{ N}$$

(1.5 marks for substitution and 0.5 mark for answer.)

**Maximum power (4 marks):**

From V-belt capstan with *α* = 18°:

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta_1 / \sin\alpha} = e^{0.28 \times 3.4711 / \sin(18°)}$$

$$\sin(18°) = 0.3090$$

$$\text{Exponent} = \frac{0.28 \times 3.4711}{0.3090} = \frac{0.9719}{0.3090} = 3.145$$

(1 mark for exponent calculation.)

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{3.145} = 23.21$$

(0.5 mark)

With $T_2 = 40$ N:

$$T_1 = 23.21 \times 40 = 928.4 \text{ N}$$

(0.5 mark)

Net transmitting tension (centrifugal tension cancels out in the difference):

$$(T_1 - T_c) - (T_2 - T_c) = T_1 - T_2 = 928.4 - 40 = 888.4 \text{ N}$$

(0.5 mark)

Power:

$$P = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v / 1000 = 888.4 \times 25.53 / 1000 = 22.68 \text{ kW}$$

(1 mark for the formula and substitution, 0.5 mark for the final answer.)

> **Practical note (credit-eligible):** Real scooter CVTs transmit only 5–8 kW because (i) slip occurs, (ii) belt heat limits grip, (iii) the wrap angle reduces during ratio changes.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks in Drive System Questions:**

1. **Confusing *pitch diameter* with *tip diameter* of a sprocket.** Always use the pitch diameter in chain-length and speed calculations. The tip diameter is only for clearance checks. (Lost: 1–2 marks)

2. **Forgetting to round up the chain length to the next even integer.** A computed *L* = 74.4 must be written as **76 links**, not 75. Odd numbers of links produce an incomplete chain with a mismatched link, which the manufacturer will not supply. (Lost: 1 mark)

3. **Using degrees in the capstan equation instead of radians.** A student writing $T_1/T_2 = e^{\mu \theta}$ with *θ* in degrees will be **wrong by a factor of 57.3**, producing absurd tension ratios. (Lost: 2–3 marks)

4. **Ignoring centrifugal tension in belt problems.** If the belt moves faster than ≈ 20 m/s, *T_c* is significant and must be reported. KTU examiners award a separate 1 mark for identifying this. (Lost: 1 mark)

5. **Computing power with (T₁ − T₂) × v but forgetting to divide by 1000 to get kW.** The unit error is the most common reason for losing the final 0.5 mark. (Lost: 0.5 mark)

6. **Mistaking a single Hooke's joint for a CV joint.** A single Hooke's joint produces a *velocity fluctuation* unless paired with a second joint at equal and opposite angle. This is a 2-mark conceptual error in shaft-drive questions.

7. **In V-belt capstan, forgetting the 1/sin α factor.** Students write the flat-belt equation and lose 1 mark immediately.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Drive Systems:**

- ☐ A chain drive is a **positive (no-slip)** drive; a V-belt drive is a **friction (slip-prone)** drive; a shaft drive is a **positive enclosed** drive.
- ☐ Sprocket pitch diameter: $D = P / \sin(180°/N)$ — never use tip diameter.
- ☐ Chain length in pitches: $L = 2C/P + (N_1+N_2)/2 + P(N_2-N_1)^2 / (4\pi^2 C)$. Always round **up to the next even integer**.
- ☐ Velocity ratio for chain: $N_2/N_1$ (exact, no slip).
- ☐ Capstan (flat belt): $T_1/T_2 = e^{\mu \theta}$ with **θ in radians**.
- ☐ Capstan (V-belt): $T_1/T_2 = e^{\mu \theta / \sin\alpha}$ — the **1/sin α** term is the wedge action.
- ☐ Centrifugal tension in belt: $T_c = m v^2$. It cancels in $(T_1 - T_c) - (T_2 - T_c)$ but matters for belt strength.
- ☐ Belt length (open): $L = 2C + (\pi/2)(D_1 + D_2) + (D_2 - D_1)^2 / 4C$.
- ☐ Angle of contact: small pulley $\theta_1 = \pi + 2\sin^{-1}[(D_2-D_1)/(2C)]$; large pulley $\theta_2 = 2\pi - \theta_1$.
- ☐ Power (chain or belt): $P = (T_1 - T_2) v / 1000$ kW, with *T* in N and *v* in m/s.
- ☐ Chain safe life ends at **3% elongation** of original pitch (ISO/Renold standard).
- ☐ Shaft-drive uses a **propeller shaft + U-joint or CV joint + bevel/hypoid final drive + slip joint**.
- ☐ A **single Hooke's joint** causes velocity fluctuation; **two Hooke's joints** at equal & opposite angles cancel it; **CV joints** (Rzeppa, tripod) eliminate it completely.
- ☐ CVT scooters (Honda Activa) use V-belts because the belt can climb variable-diameter pulleys; chains cannot do this efficiently.
- ☐ Chain = geared motorcycles. Belt = scooters. Shaft = tourers & three-wheelers.
- ☐ Remember to state **assumptions** in numerical problems (no slip, no wear, etc.) — KTU awards marks for this.
- ☐ In SI, use **radians** for angles, **m/s** for velocity, **N** for tension, **kW** for power.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Braking Systems: Drum and Disc Brakes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT631 - TWO AND THREE WHEELED VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Braking Systems: Drum and Disc Brakes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Braking System?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A *braking system* is a controlled energy-conversion mechanism in a vehicle that dissipates the kinetic energy (and potential energy on grades) of a moving mass into **heat energy through friction**, thereby producing a retarding torque that decelerates or brings the vehicle to rest within a stipulated stopping distance, while maintaining directional stability and tyre-road adhesion.

In the context of **PEAUT631 (Two and Three Wheeled Vehicles)**, braking is one of the most safety-critical subsystems. Two-wheelers and three-wheelers have a **unique dynamic problem**: unlike a four-wheeler, a tilting two-wheeler cannot tolerate wheel lock-up on a single wheel (it would low-side the rider). This is why modern two-wheelers use a **disc brake on the front** (≈ 70% of braking effort) and a **drum or disc brake on the rear**, often supplemented by a **Combined Braking System (CBS)** or **Anti-lock Braking System (ABS)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 2):** The student must be able to *sketch, label, and explain* both drum and disc brake assemblies, distinguish between **leading/trailing shoes**, derive **brake torque**, and perform a **comparative analysis** of the two systems.

---

## 1.2 The Two Families of Friction Brakes

| Brake Type | Energy Conversion Mechanism | Primary Use in 2W / 3W |
|---|---|---|
| **Drum Brake** | Internal expanding shoe presses against inner cylindrical surface | Rear wheel of most commuter 2W, all wheels of 3W (auto-rickshaws) |
| **Disc Brake** | Caliper-mounted pads clamp a rotating flat disc | Front wheel of almost all modern 2W, rear of premium / sports 2W |

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are **walking with a friend holding a long rolling log**:

* **Drum brake analogy:** Take a **rope and wrap it around the log**, then pull the ends apart so the rope squeezes the log. The harder you pull, the more friction you create. A drum brake does exactly this — brake shoes *expand outward* against the inside of a drum.

* **Disc brake analogy:** Now imagine a **flat rotating plate** (a roti tawa on a turntable). You stop it by **pinching it between two flat wooden blocks on either side**. The harder you pinch, the faster it stops. A disc brake does exactly this — pads *clamp a rotor* from both sides.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** *Drum → D = Doughnut (shoes push outward); Disc → D = Double-sided (pads push inward from both faces).*

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Recall)

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| $\mu$ | Coefficient of friction (pad-lining) | **0.3 – 0.5** |
| $\rho$ | Density of grey cast-iron drum | **7200 kg/m³** |
| $c_p$ | Specific heat of cast iron | **460 J/kg·K** |
| $k$ | Thermal conductivity of rotor | **46 W/m·K** |
| $\nu$ | Poisson's ratio of rotor | **0.26** |

These constants appear in **thermal/fade analysis** of disc brakes and in **stress calculation** of drum walls.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Drum Brake — Construction and Working

### 2.1.1 Constructional Anatomy

A drum brake consists of the following parts:

* **Brake Drum** — A cylindrical shell, usually of **grey cast iron**, bolted to the wheel hub and rotating with it. Inner diameter is the effective friction surface.
* **Brake Shoes** — Two arcuated steel shoes, lined with friction material (asbestos-free, organic, sintered, or ceramic). They are pivoted at an anchor pin.
* **Wheel Cylinder** — A hydraulic actuator (twin-piston) that converts hydraulic pressure into linear mechanical force.
* **Return Springs** — Pull the shoes back when hydraulic pressure is released.
* **Backing Plate** — A stationary plate to which the wheel cylinder, anchor pin, and adjuster are mounted.
* **Adjuster (Mechanical / Automatic)** — Compensates for lining wear.

### 2.1.2 Working Principle

1. Rider squeezes brake lever → **master cylinder** builds hydraulic pressure.
2. Pressure travels through the brake hose to the **wheel cylinder**.
3. Twin opposed pistons of the wheel cylinder push the two shoes **outward**.
4. The friction lining contacts the rotating drum → **kinetic energy → heat**.
5. When lever is released, return springs retract the shoes → friction ceases.

### 2.1.3 Leading vs. Trailing Shoe — The "Self-Energising" Concept

* **Leading Shoe (Primary):** The shoe whose friction force tends to *rotate it into the drum* (drag direction assists actuation). It produces a **higher torque** for the same actuation force — this is called **self-energising** action.
* **Trailing Shoe (Secondary):** The shoe whose friction force tends to *rotate it away* from the drum. It produces a **lower torque** — **self-de-energising** action.

> [!NOTE]
> **Self-energising factor (K):** For a leading shoe, $K = \dfrac{\mu \, (\rho \cos\theta - a)}{\rho + a \cos\theta}$ where $\rho = R/a$ is the drum-to-pivot radius ratio. When $K > 0$, the shoe is self-energising; when $K < 0$, self-de-energising.

---

## 2.2 Disc Brake — Construction and Working

### 2.2.1 Constructional Anatomy

* **Rotor / Disc** — A flat circular plate of cast iron or carbon-ceramic, attached to the wheel hub.
* **Caliper** — A clamp-like housing that straddles the rotor. Holds pistons and pads.
* **Brake Pads** — Friction material bonded to a steel backing plate.
* **Piston(s)** — Hydraulic pistons that push the pads against the rotor.
* **Dust Seal & Bellows Boot** — Prevents contamination of the sliding surfaces.
* **Guide Pins (Floating Caliper only)** — Allow the caliper to slide laterally.

### 2.2.2 Types of Caliper

1. **Fixed Caliper (Opposed-Piston):** Rigidly bolted to the fork/axle. Pistons on both sides of the rotor. Equal clamping force → no parasitic drag.
2. **Floating (Sliding) Caliper:** Mounted on guide pins. Single (or inboard) piston. When piston pushes the inboard pad, the caliper slides inward, dragging the outboard pad into contact. Used in **80% of motorcycles**.
3. **Moving Caliper:** Pivots about a fulcrum (older design, rare in modern 2W).

### 2.2.3 Working Principle

1. Hydraulic pressure acts on the piston (or pistons).
2. Piston pushes the **inboard pad** against the rotor.
3. In a floating caliper, the reaction force slides the caliper body on guide pins → **outboard pad** also clamps the rotor.
4. Equal-and-opposite friction forces produce a pure **clamping torque** with zero net side-thrust on the rotor.
5. When pressure is released, the **dust seal** (slightly conical) retracts the piston a few microns — this gives the **automatic pad-wear adjustment**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This table consolidates every formula KTU has asked (or is highly likely to ask) for this topic.** Memorise it — it will solve 80% of the numerical sub-parts.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = P \cdot A$ | Hydraulic clamping force from pressure $P$ and piston area $A$ |
| 2 | $T = \mu \cdot F \cdot r_{eff}$ | **General brake torque** (valid for both drum & disc) |
| 3 | $r_{eff} = \dfrac{R + r}{2}$ | Effective radius — **Uniform Pressure Theory** |
| 4 | $r_{eff} = \dfrac{2(R^2 + Rr + r^2)}{3(R + r)}$ | Effective radius — **Uniform Wear Theory** |
| 5 | $T_{drum} = \mu \cdot F \cdot \dfrac{(R+r)}{2}$ | Drum brake torque (uniform pressure, single shoe) |
| 6 | $T_{drum} = \mu \cdot F \cdot \dfrac{2(R^2 + Rr + r^2)}{3(R+r)}$ | Drum brake torque (uniform wear, single shoe) |
| 7 | $T_{disc} = 2 \cdot \mu \cdot F \cdot r_{eff}$ | Disc brake torque (floating caliper — 2 friction surfaces) |
| 8 | $T_{disc} = \mu \cdot F \cdot (R + r)$ | Disc brake torque (using $r_{eff}=(R+r)/2$, ×2 for 2 sides) |
| 9 | $K = \dfrac{\mu(\rho \cos\theta - a)}{\rho + a \cos\theta}$ | Self-energising factor (leading shoe) |
| 10 | $F_{eq} = F(1 + K)$ | Equivalent actuation force including self-energising |
| 11 | $Q = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | Heat energy generated per stop (kinetic energy) |
| 12 | $\dot{Q} = \mu \cdot F \cdot v$ | Instantaneous rate of heat generation |
| 13 | $\Delta T = \dfrac{Q}{m_{drum} \cdot c_p}$ | Temperature rise of drum in a single stop |
| 14 | $\eta_{brake} = \dfrac{T_{out}}{T_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Brake efficiency (used in self-energising analysis) |
| 15 | $\sigma_{hoop} = \dfrac{p \cdot R}{t}$ | Hoop stress in a thin-walled drum (pressure $p$, thickness $t$) |

> **Notation Glossary:** $R$ = outer (drum / disc) radius; $r$ = inner lining / pad contact radius; $F$ = actuation force (hydraulic clamping); $\mu$ = coefficient of friction; $\theta$ = half-contact-angle of shoe; $a$ = pivot-to-drum-centre distance; $\rho = R/a$.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Motorcycle Front Disc:** Higher torque capacity, better **fade resistance** (heat dissipation is open to air → no water fade).
* **Rear Drum on Mopeds / Commuters:** Cheaper, longer pad life, integrated **parking brake** mechanism — a single drum can act as a service brake and a parking brake simultaneously.
* **Three-Wheeler (Auto) Drums:** Mechanical linkage (rod/cable) or hydraulic actuation, simple and rugged for Indian road conditions.
* **CBS (Combined Braking System):** Front disc + rear drum with proportioning valve — a KTU-favourite comparison topic.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Brake Torque of a Drum Brake (Uniform Pressure Theory)

### Assumptions
1. The pressure $p$ acting on the lining is **constant** over the entire contact surface (uniform pressure).
2. The friction coefficient $\mu$ is **constant** over the surface.
3. The shoe pivots about an anchor pin located at the **geometric centre** of the shoe.
4. The actuation force $F$ acts at the centre of the shoe web (the wheel cylinder applies it).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Consider a drum of inner radius $R$ with a single shoe of width $b$ contacting over a half-angle $\theta$. An infinitesimal element of the shoe at angular position $\phi$ subtends an arc $R \, d\phi$ and has area $dA = b \cdot R \, d\phi$.

The elemental normal force on this element is:

$$dF_N = p \cdot dA = p \cdot b \cdot R \, d\phi$$

The elemental tangential (friction) force is:

$$dF_f = \mu \cdot dF_N = \mu \cdot p \cdot b \cdot R \, d\phi$$

The elemental torque about the drum centre is:

$$dT = dF_f \cdot R = \mu \cdot p \cdot b \cdot R^2 \, d\phi$$

Integrate over the full shoe contact angle $2\theta$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \int_{-\theta}^{+\theta} \mu \, p \, b \, R^2 \, d\phi \\[4pt]
  &= \mu \, p \, b \, R^2 \, \big[\phi\big]_{-\theta}^{+\theta} \\[4pt]
  &= \mu \, p \, b \, R^2 \, (2\theta)
\end{aligned}
$$

Now, the total normal force on the shoe equals the actuation force $F$. Equating vertical force balance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= \int_{-\theta}^{+\theta} dF_N = \int_{-\theta}^{+\theta} p \, b \, R \, d\phi \\[4pt]
  &= p \, b \, R \, (2\theta)
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, $p \cdot b \cdot R \cdot (2\theta) = F$, which gives us $p \cdot b \cdot R^2 \cdot (2\theta) = F \cdot R$. Substituting back into the torque equation:

$$
\boxed{T_{drum,UP} = \mu \cdot F \cdot R}
$$

But more rigorously, the **effective radius** for uniform pressure is the geometric mean, which for a shoe that contacts from radius $r$ (lining inner) to $R$ (lining outer) yields:

$$
\boxed{T_{drum,UP} = \mu \cdot F \cdot \dfrac{(R + r)}{2}}
$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** With uniform pressure, the brake torque is *directly proportional* to the actuation force $F$ and to the *average of the inner and outer radii of the lining*.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Brake Torque of a Drum Brake (Uniform Wear Theory)

### Physical Basis
In reality, after prolonged use, the friction material wears more at the centre of the arc than at the ends. Hence, the wear rate $w \propto p \cdot v$ is constant. Since $v \propto R_{local}$, the pressure distribution becomes $p \cdot R_{local} = \text{constant}$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

For uniform wear, $p \cdot R = c$ (a constant). The elemental normal force on a small element $b \, dR$ at radius $R$ is:

$$dF_N = p \cdot b \cdot dR = \dfrac{c}{R} \cdot b \, dR$$

The total actuation force:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= \int_{r}^{R} \dfrac{c \, b}{R} \, dR = c \, b \, \big[\ln R\big]_{r}^{R} \\[4pt]
  &= c \, b \, \ln\left(\dfrac{R}{r}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

The total brake torque (integrating tangential friction force $\times$ moment arm $R$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \int_{r}^{R} \mu \cdot p \cdot b \cdot R \, dR = \int_{r}^{R} \mu \cdot \dfrac{c}{R} \cdot b \cdot R \, dR \\[4pt]
  &= \mu \cdot c \cdot b \cdot \int_{r}^{R} dR = \mu \cdot c \cdot b \, (R - r)
\end{aligned}
$$

Eliminate $c$ from the two equations: $c \cdot b = \dfrac{F}{\ln(R/r)}$. Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \mu \cdot \dfrac{F}{\ln(R/r)} \cdot (R - r) \\[4pt]
  &= \mu \cdot F \cdot \dfrac{(R - r)}{\ln(R/r)}
\end{aligned}
$$

We can rewrite this as $T = \mu \cdot F \cdot r_{eff,UW}$ where:

$$
\boxed{r_{eff,UW} = \dfrac{R - r}{\ln(R/r)} \approx \dfrac{2(R^2 + Rr + r^2)}{3(R + r)}}
$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** Uniform wear is a *more realistic* model for long-term design. It gives a *smaller* effective radius than the uniform-pressure case, meaning the design torque (and hence the design actuation force) is slightly lower — useful for KTU numerical safety-margin questions.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Self-Energising Factor for a Leading Shoe

### Geometric Setup
* Drum radius = $R$
* Distance from shoe pivot to drum centre = $a$
* Shoe contact half-angle = $\theta$
* Actuation force = $F$ (assumed to act at the geometric centre of the friction surface)
* Define dimensionless ratio $\rho = R/a$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

The actuation force $F$ produces a moment about the pivot equal to $F$ times its moment arm. The component of $F$ perpendicular to the drum radius is $F \sin\theta$ (averaged); the effective moment arm is $\dfrac{R + r}{2}$ approximated as $\dfrac{R(1+\cos\theta)}{2}$ when $\rho$ is considered.

The frictional tangential force $\mu F$ produces an additional moment about the pivot. Taking moments about the shoe pivot for a leading shoe (where friction *adds* to the normal force):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total moment about pivot} &= F \cdot \dfrac{(R + r \cos\theta)}{2} + \mu \cdot F \cdot \dfrac{(R \cos\theta - r)}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

For equilibrium, this total moment equals the normal reaction $N$ times its moment arm $(R + a\cos\theta)/2$:

$$
N \cdot \dfrac{(R + a\cos\theta)}{2} = F \cdot \dfrac{(R + r\cos\theta)}{2} + \mu F \cdot \dfrac{(R\cos\theta - r)}{2}
$$

Solving for $N/F$ (the force amplification ratio):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{N}{F} &= \dfrac{R + r\cos\theta + \mu(R\cos\theta - r)}{R + a\cos\theta} \\[4pt]
             &= \dfrac{R + r\cos\theta}{R + a\cos\theta} + \mu \cdot \dfrac{R\cos\theta - r}{R + a\cos\theta}
\end{aligned}
$$

Defining the **self-energising factor** $K$:

$$
\boxed{K = \dfrac{\mu \, (\rho \cos\theta - 1)}{\rho + \cos\theta}}
$$

and the **equivalent actuation force**:

$$
\boxed{F_{eq} = F \, (1 + K)}
$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** For a leading shoe, when $\rho \cos\theta > 1$, $K$ is **positive** → the shoe is **self-energising** (it amplifies its own clamping). When $\rho \cos\theta < 1$ (small drums, wide shoes), the shoe becomes **neutral** or **self-de-energising**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Brake Torque of a Disc Brake

### Step-by-Step Derivation

For a disc brake with floating caliper and a single piston:

* Piston area = $A_p$
* Hydraulic pressure = $P$
* Clamping force from piston = $F_c = P \cdot A_p$

In a floating caliper, the *same* clamping force acts on **both sides** of the rotor (action–reaction, with caliper sliding on pins). Therefore, the **total tangential friction force** on the rotor is:

$$
F_f = 2 \, \mu \, F_c = 2 \, \mu \, P \, A_p
$$

Considering a small annular element of the pad at radius $r$ with radial width $dr$:

$$dF_f = 2 \, \mu \cdot \dfrac{dF_c}{dr} \, dr$$

Assuming uniform pressure distribution on the pad face, $dF_c/dr$ is uniform. The elemental torque:

$$dT = r \cdot dF_f = 2 \, \mu \cdot r \cdot \left(\dfrac{dF_c}{dr}\right) \, dr$$

Integrating from inner pad radius $r_i$ to outer pad radius $r_o$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{disc} &= 2 \, \mu \int_{r_i}^{r_o} r \, \left(\dfrac{F_c}{r_o - r_i}\right) dr \\[4pt]
         &= 2 \, \mu \cdot \dfrac{F_c}{r_o - r_i} \cdot \left[\dfrac{r^2}{2}\right]_{r_i}^{r_o} \\[4pt]
         &= \mu \cdot F_c \cdot (r_i + r_o)
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $r_{eff} = (r_i + r_o)/2$:

$$
\boxed{T_{disc} = 2 \, \mu \, F_c \, r_{eff} = \mu \, F_c \, (r_i + r_o)}
$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Drum vs Disc)

| Parameter | Drum Brake | Disc Brake |
|---|---|---|
| **Torque capacity per unit actuation force** | Moderate (boosted by self-energising) | High |
| **Self-energising possible?** | Yes (leading shoe) | **No** (always neutral) |
| **Fade resistance** | Poor (heat trapped inside drum) | Excellent (open to air) |
| **Water fade** | Significant (drum traps water) | Negligible (rotor is wiped clean by pads) |
| **Pad / lining life** | Long (large area, low pressure) | Shorter (small area, high pressure) |
| **Weight** | Heavier | Lighter (rotor vs drum) |
| **Cooling** | Convective only | Convective **+ radiative** |
| **Cost** | Lower | Higher |
| **Parking brake integration** | Easy (mechanical lever on shoe) | Difficult (needs separate drum-in-disc) |
| **Use in 2W / 3W** | Rear (most), all wheels of 3W | Front (universal), rear (premium) |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Drum Brake — Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid block represents the energy-flow and mechanical-coupling sequence inside a drum brake. It mirrors what examiners expect in a *neat sketch with labelling* answer.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Rider Brake Lever Input"] --> nodeB["Master Cylinder Hydraulic Pressure"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Brake Hose Fluid Transmission"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Wheel Cylinder Twin Pistons"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Primary Shoe Leading Expansion"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["Secondary Shoe Trailing Expansion"]
    nodeE --> nodeG["Friction Contact Inner Drum Surface"]
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH["Friction Torque Generation"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Wheel Deceleration"]
    nodeG --> nodeJ["Heat Dissipation to Atmosphere"]
    nodeK["Return Springs"] -.retract.-> nodeE
    nodeK -.retract.-> nodeF
    nodeL["Anchor Pin Pivot Point"] -.anchors.-> nodeE
    nodeL -.anchors.-> nodeF
```

**Reading the diagram:**
* **Solid arrows** = force / actuation flow.
* **Dotted arrows** = restoring / mechanical-reaction flow.
* `nodeG` is the *single point of energy conversion* — all kinetic energy of the vehicle is converted into heat here.

---

## 4.2 Disc Brake — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP1["Brake Lever Master Cylinder"] --> nodeP2["Hydraulic Line to Caliper"]
    nodeP2 --> nodeP3["Caliper Piston Hydraulic Pressure"]
    nodeP3 --> nodeP4["Inboard Pad Pushes Rotor"]
    nodeP4 --> nodeP5["Reaction Force Slides Caliper on Guide Pins"]
    nodeP5 --> nodeP6["Outboard Pad Clamps Rotor"]
    nodeP4 --> nodeP7["Friction Force Generation"]
    nodeP6 --> nodeP7
    nodeP7 --> nodeP8["Brake Torque on Rotor"]
    nodeP8 --> nodeP9["Wheel Slowdown"]
    nodeP7 --> nodeP10["Heat Dissipation from Rotor Surface"]
    nodeP11["Dust Seal Retraction Spring"] -.returns piston.-> nodeP3
```

---

## 4.3 Comparative Functional Block Diagram (Drum vs Disc)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph sys1["Drum Brake Sub-System"]
        d1["Wheel Cylinder Hydraulic Force"] --> d2["Shoe Expands Outward"]
        d2 --> d3["Internal Friction on Drum Wall"]

    end
    subgraph sys2["Disc Brake Sub-System"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Style Note:** Part A questions are usually asked in the **Continuous Internal Evaluation (CIE)** quiz format or as **short-answer sub-parts in ESE**. Answer each in 3–5 crisp lines with a labelled sketch wherever asked.

---

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define self-energising action in a drum brake. With a neat sketch, explain leading and trailing shoe configurations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

* **Self-energising action (1 mark):** The phenomenon in which the frictional drag force on a brake shoe *adds to the actuation force*, thereby amplifying the normal reaction and the brake torque.
* **Leading shoe (1 mark):** The shoe whose pivot is on the side from which the drum rotates *toward* the shoe's contact surface. Friction rotates it *into* the drum → self-energising.
* **Trailing shoe (1 mark):** The shoe whose pivot is on the side toward which the drum rotates *away*. Friction rotates it *off* the drum → self-de-energising, producing less torque.

*Sketch must show: drum rotation arrow, leading shoe pivot at one side, trailing shoe pivot at the other, friction force arrows.*

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State any four advantages of disc brakes over drum brakes for two-wheeled vehicles.**

**Model Answer (4 × 0.75 = 3 marks):**

1. **Better heat dissipation** — Rotor is open to air; avoids brake fade on long descents.
2. **Self-cleaning action** — Pads wipe the rotor surface, eliminating water fade after rain or wading.
3. **Higher torque-to-weight ratio** — Lighter than an equivalent drum; better for unsprung mass.
4. **No self-de-energising loss** — Disc brake is always neutral, so the brake feel is linear and predictable.
5. *Bonus point:* Easier visual inspection of pad wear.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> * Students frequently **lose 2–3 marks** because they *forget to draw a labelled diagram*. Even if your theory is perfect, KTU examiners deduct marks for missing figures.
> * In numericals, **forgetting the factor of 2** for the disc-brake two-sided friction is a classic 2-mark loss.
> * Always **state the assumption** (uniform pressure vs uniform wear) at the start of a drum-brake numerical.
> * **Unit consistency:** convert mm → m before computing torque in N·m. Mixing units is the #1 silent marks-killer.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of a **single-leading-shoe internal expanding drum brake**. Derive the expression for the **brake torque assuming uniform pressure**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A drum brake fitted to the rear wheel of a motorcycle has a drum inner diameter of **200 mm**, lining thickness of **4 mm**, lining width of **40 mm**, and shoe contact angle of **100°**. The hydraulic actuation force is **1500 N** and the coefficient of friction is **0.35**. Calculate:
    (i) Brake torque assuming **uniform pressure theory**.
    (ii) Brake torque assuming **uniform wear theory**.
    (iii) The hoop stress induced in the drum wall if wall thickness is 6 mm and internal pressure is 0.4 MPa. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — A(a)**

**Construction (3 marks):** Draw and label drum, two brake shoes, wheel cylinder (with twin opposed pistons), anchor pin, return springs, adjuster, backing plate. Mention that the friction lining is riveted/bonded to the shoe web.

**Working (2 marks):** Describe the actuation flow: rider squeezes lever → master cylinder → hydraulic pressure → wheel cylinder pistons push shoes outward → friction with rotating drum → kinetic energy converted to heat → wheel decelerates. When lever is released, return springs retract shoes.

**Derivation (2 marks):** As derived in Section 3.1:

$$T_{drum,UP} = \mu \cdot F \cdot \dfrac{(R + r)}{2}$$

State all assumptions explicitly.

---

#### **Model Solution — A(b)**

**Given:**
* $D = 200$ mm → $R = 100$ mm = 0.100 m
* Lining thickness = 4 mm → $r = 96$ mm = 0.096 m
* Width $b = 40$ mm (not needed for basic formula)
* Contact angle $2\theta = 100°$ (not needed)
* $F = 1500$ N
* $\mu = 0.35$
* Wall thickness $t = 6$ mm, internal pressure $p = 0.4$ MPa

**(i) Uniform Pressure:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{UP} &= \mu \cdot F \cdot \dfrac{(R + r)}{2} \\[4pt]
       &= 0.35 \times 1500 \times \dfrac{(0.100 + 0.096)}{2} \\[4pt]
       &= 0.35 \times 1500 \times 0.098 \\[4pt]
       &= 51.45 \; \text{N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Uniform Wear:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_{eff,UW} &= \dfrac{R - r}{\ln(R/r)} = \dfrac{0.100 - 0.096}{\ln(0.100/0.096)} = \dfrac{0.004}{\ln(1.04167)} = \dfrac{0.004}{0.04082} = 0.0980 \; \text{m} \\[4pt]
T_{UW} &= \mu \cdot F \cdot r_{eff,UW} \\[4pt]
       &= 0.35 \times 1500 \times 0.0980 \\[4pt]
       &= 51.45 \; \text{N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Note: For small thickness-to-radius ratios the two theories give nearly identical results. If the lining were thicker, the difference would be visible.)*

**(iii) Hoop Stress in Drum Wall:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{hoop} &= \dfrac{p \cdot R}{t} = \dfrac{0.4 \times 10^6 \times 0.100}{0.006} \\[4pt]
             &= \dfrac{40000}{0.006} = 6.667 \; \text{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of a **single-piston floating caliper disc brake** used in motorcycles. State its advantages over a fixed-caliper design. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A motorcycle front disc brake has an **outer pad contact radius of 140 mm** and an **inner pad contact radius of 70 mm**. The hydraulic pressure on the caliper piston is **5 MPa** and the piston diameter is **32 mm**. The coefficient of friction between pad and rotor is **0.4**. The vehicle mass is **180 kg** and it decelerates from **60 km/h to rest** in **3 s**. Calculate:
    (i) Clamping force on the rotor.
    (ii) Maximum brake torque generated.
    (iii) Total heat energy dissipated per stop.
    (iv) Average rate of heat generation during the stop. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — B(a)**

**Construction (3 marks):** Draw and label — rotor, caliper body, single piston (inboard side), inboard pad, outboard pad, guide pins, dust seal, bleed nipple. Mark the caliper sliding direction with a double-headed arrow.

**Working (3 marks):** Describe the floating-caliper mechanism step by step:
1. Hydraulic pressure → piston moves → inboard pad clamps rotor.
2. Reaction force slides the entire caliper on guide pins.
3. Outboard pad is dragged into contact on the opposite face.
4. Equal-and-opposite clamping force on both sides.
5. Dust seal flexes back when pressure is released, retracting piston (automatic pad-wear compensation).

**Advantages over Fixed Caliper (1 mark):** Fewer pistons, lighter, cheaper, simpler hydraulic routing — but small disadvantage of slightly higher parasitic drag on guide pins.

---

#### **Model Solution — B(b)**

**Given:**
* $r_o = 140$ mm = 0.140 m, $r_i = 70$ mm = 0.070 m
* $P = 5$ MPa = $5 \times 10^6$ Pa
* Piston diameter $d = 32$ mm → $A_p = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.032)^2 = 8.0425 \times 10^{-4}$ m²
* $\mu = 0.4$
* $m = 180$ kg, $v_i = 60$ km/h = 16.667 m/s, $v_f = 0$, $t = 3$ s

**(i) Clamping Force:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_c &= P \cdot A_p = 5 \times 10^6 \times 8.0425 \times 10^{-4} \\[4pt]
    &= 4021.27 \; \text{N} \approx 4.02 \; \text{kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Brake Torque:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_{eff} &= \dfrac{r_o + r_i}{2} = \dfrac{0.140 + 0.070}{2} = 0.105 \; \text{m} \\[4pt]
T_{disc} &= 2 \cdot \mu \cdot F_c \cdot r_{eff} \\[4pt]
         &= 2 \times 0.4 \times 4021.27 \times 0.105 \\[4pt]
         &= 337.79 \; \text{N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Heat Dissipated per Stop:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \dfrac{1}{2} m v^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 180 \times (16.667)^2 \\[4pt]
  &= \dfrac{1}{2} \times 180 \times 277.78 \\[4pt]
  &= 25000 \; \text{J} = 25 \; \text{kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Average Rate of Heat Generation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{avg} &= \dfrac{Q}{t} = \dfrac{25000}{3} \\[4pt]
              &= 8333.33 \; \text{W} \approx 8.33 \; \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Read this list twice on the morning of your exam. If you can tick every box, you are exam-ready.**

* [ ] A **braking system** converts *kinetic energy* → *heat energy* via friction.
* [ ] **Drum brake** = internal expanding shoes; **Disc brake** = external clamping pads.
* [ ] Drum brake components: *drum, two shoes, wheel cylinder, return springs, anchor pin, backing plate, adjuster*.
* [ ] Disc brake components: *rotor, caliper, piston, pads, dust seal, guide pins*.
* [ ] **Leading shoe** = self-energising (more torque). **Trailing shoe** = self-de-energising (less torque).
* [ ] Disc brake is **never self-energising** — the rotor is squeezed equally on both sides.
* [ ] **Floating caliper** slides on guide pins; **fixed caliper** has opposed pistons.
* [ ] **Uniform Pressure Theory:** $T = \mu \cdot F \cdot (R+r)/2$.
* [ ] **Uniform Wear Theory:** $T = \mu \cdot F \cdot (R-r)/\ln(R/r)$.
* [ ] **Disc brake torque:** $T = 2 \cdot \mu \cdot F_c \cdot r_{eff}$ (factor of 2 for two friction surfaces).
* [ ] **Self-energising factor:** $K = \dfrac{\mu(\rho\cos\theta - 1)}{\rho + \cos\theta}$.
* [ ] **Equivalent actuation force:** $F_{eq} = F(1 + K)$.
* [ ] **Heat per stop:** $Q = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$. **Rate:** $\dot{Q} = \mu F v$.
* [ ] **Hoop stress in drum:** $\sigma = pR/t$.
* [ ] **Disc brake advantages:** better cooling, no water fade, lighter, linear brake feel.
* [ ] **Drum brake advantages:** cheaper, integrated parking brake, longer lining life.
* [ ] **2W configuration:** Front disc + Rear drum (typical commuter); both discs on sports.
* [ ] **3W configuration:** All three wheels drum (most common auto-rickshaws).
* [ ] Always draw a **labelled sketch** in long answers — never describe alone.
* [ ] Always state **assumptions** before numerical — uniform pressure vs uniform wear.
* [ ] Always check **unit consistency** — mm → m before computing N·m.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Battery Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAUT631 - TWO AND THREE WHEELED VEHICLES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Battery Types # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Definition of an Automotive Battery (KTU 2024 Terminology) An **automotive traction battery** is an electrochemical energy storage device that converts stored chemical energy into electrical energy through reversible redox reactions, specifically engineered to deliver high power pulses and sustained energy for the propulsion of **two-wheeled (2W)** and **three-wheeled (3W)** electric vehicles. In the KTU 2024 scheme context, the term encompasses both **auxiliary batteries** (low-voltage, SLI – Starting, Lighting, Ignition) and **traction batteries** (high-voltage, propulsion-grade energy packs). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 – Battery Types):** > A battery is *NOT* an energy source — it is an **energy buffer / storage medium**. It only stores electrical energy that has been previously supplied by an external source (grid, regenerative braking, or alternator). This distinction is critical in 2W/3W powertrain design. ### Formal Classification Anchor Batteries used in 2W/3W vehicles are classified under two broad umbrellas: | Umbrella | Conversion Direction | Rechargeability | Typical Application in 2W/3W | |---|---|---|---| | **Primary Cells** | Chemical → Electrical (one-way) | Non-rechargeable | Remote keys, brake-light sensors | | **Secondary Cells** | Chemical ⇌ Electrical (reversible) | Rechargeable | EV powertrain, SLI, lighting loads | For Module 3 of PEAUT631, the focus is overwhelmingly on **secondary (rechargeable) cells**, as these form the heart of modern electric two- and three-wheelers. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Water Tank Model" Imagine a building's **overhead water tank**: - The **tank's capacity** (litres) = the battery's **capacity (Ah – Ampere-hour)**. - The **water pressure** at the tap = the battery's **voltage (V)**. - The **rate at which water flows out** = the **discharge current (A)** or **C-rate**. - The **time to empty the tank** = the **runtime / range** of the vehicle. > [!NOTE] > **Why this analogy matters in KTU valuation:** > When an examiner asks *"Why does a higher C-rate reduce usable capacity?"* — think of the tank model. If you pull water out *very fast*, friction losses and turbulence at the outlet cause some water to be *unrecoverable*. Similarly, high discharge currents cause **voltage sag** and **heat losses** (I²R), reducing usable energy. This is exactly what **Peukert's Law** formalises mathematically (covered in Section 2). --- ## 1.3 Battery Types — The Master Taxonomy The major rechargeable chemistries relevant to 2W/3W electric vehicles are: 1. **Lead-Acid Batteries (LAB)** - Flooded Lead-Acid (FLA) / Wet-cell - Valve Regulated Lead-Acid (VRLA) - Absorbent Glass Mat (AGM) - Gel Cell 2. **Nickel-based Batteries** - Nickel-Cadmium (NiCd) - Nickel-Metal Hydride (NiMH) 3. **Lithium-Ion (Li-ion) Family** *(dominant in modern 2W/3W EVs)* - Lithium Iron Phosphate (LFP) - Lithium Nickel Manganese Cobalt Oxide (NMC) - Lithium Nickel Cobalt Aluminum Oxide (NCA) - Lithium Manganese Oxide (LMO) - Lithium Titanate (LTO) 4. **Emerging Chemistries** - Sodium-Ion (Na-ion) - Solid-State Lithium (research stage) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Industry Reality Check (KTU Board expects this):** > As of 2024–2026, **LFP** dominates the Indian 2W/3W EV market (e.g., Ola S1, Hero Vida, Ather, TVS iQube, Bajaj Chetak premium variants, Mahindra Treo, Euler HiLoad). **NMC** is preferred for premium / performance 2W EVs needing higher energy density. Lead-acid still survives in low-speed 3W cargo loaders and entry-level e-rickshaws (cost-driven). --- ## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Standard Vocabulary for KTU Answers) Every battery answer must use these terms precisely: - **Nominal Voltage (V):** The reference voltage of the chemistry (e.g., LFP = **3.2 V**, NMC ≈ **3.7 V**, Lead-Acid = **2.0 V/cell**). - **Capacity (Ah):** Total charge stored, measured at a defined discharge rate. - **Energy (Wh):** $E = V_{nom} \times Q_{Ah}$ - **Specific Energy (Wh/kg):** Mass-based energy density. - **Energy Density (Wh/L):** Volume-based energy density. - **Specific Power (W/kg):** Mass-based power capability. - **C-rate:** Discharge/charge current normalised to capacity. - **State of Charge (SoC):** Instantaneous available capacity as a % of full. - **State of Health (SoH):** Aged capacity as a % of original. - **Depth of Discharge (DoD):** Complement of SoC — % of capacity already used. - **Cycle Life:** Number of full charge-discharge cycles before SoH drops to 80 %. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Lead-Acid Batteries — The Veteran Workhorse ### Operating Principle A lead-acid cell consists of: - **Positive electrode:** Lead dioxide (PbO₂) - **Negative electrode:** Spongy lead (Pb) - **Electrolyte:** Sulphuric acid (H₂SO₄, ~37 % w/w, density ≈ 1.28 g/cm³) ### Electrochemistry (Discharge Reaction) $$\text{Pb}_{(s)} + \text{PbO}_{2(s)} + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{SO}_{4(aq)} \longrightarrow 2\,\text{PbSO}_{4(s)} + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)}$$ ### Electrochemistry (Charge Reaction — Reverse) $$\text{PbSO}_{4(s)} + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)} \longrightarrow \text{Pb}_{(s)} + \text{PbO}_{2(s)} + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{SO}_{4(aq)}$$ ### Why Lead-Acid Still Survives in 3W EVs - **Lowest cost per Wh** (≈ ₹8–12/Wh at pack level in India, 2024) - Robust, recyclable (≈ 97 % recycled globally) - Tolerates overcharge poorly but survives abuse - Heavy — low specific energy (**30–50 Wh/kg**) ### Variants | Variant | Electrolyte State | Sealed? | Typical 2W/3W Use | |---|---|---|---| | **Flooded (FLA)** | Liquid, free | No (vented) | E-rickshaw (L5M cargo) | | **AGM** | Absorbed in glass mat | Yes (VRLA) | Premium scooter start-stop | | **Gel** | Thixotropic SiO₂ gel | Yes (VRLA) | Electric auto (L5 passenger) | --- ## 2.2 Nickel-Metal Hydride (NiMH) Although largely displaced by Li-ion, NiMH appears in older hybrid 2W prototypes and certain industrial 3W applications. - **Positive:** Nickel oxyhydroxide (NiOOH) - **Negative:** Hydrogen-absorbing alloy (MH) - **Electrolyte:** KOH (alkaline) $$\text{NiOOH} + \text{MH} \longrightarrow \text{Ni(OH)}_2 + \text{M}$$ ### Pros & Cons (for 2W/3W) - ✅ Safer than early Li-ion, robust at high SoC - ❌ **Memory effect** (partial), self-discharge high (~30 %/month) - ❌ Specific energy only **60–120 Wh/kg** > [!NOTE] > **KTU Mnemonic:** *NiMH = "Ni**M**emory **H**igh" — high memory effect, high self-discharge.* --- ## 2.3 The Lithium-Ion Family — Dominant Chemistry in Modern 2W/3W EVs All Li-ion cells share a common architecture: - **Positive electrode (cathode):** Lithium-transition-metal oxide - **Negative electrode (anode):** Graphite (or Si-graphite, or LTO) - **Electrolyte:** LiPF₆ in organic carbonate solvent - **Separator:** Polyethylene / polypropylene microporous film ### Universal Discharge Reaction $$\text{Li}_x\text{C}_6 + \text{Li}_{1-x}\text{MO}_2 \longrightarrow 6\text{C} + \text{LiMO}_2$$ Where **M** is the transition metal (Fe, Mn, Ni, Co, etc., depending on chemistry). ### 2.3.1 Lithium Iron Phosphate (LFP — LiFePO₄) | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | Nominal Voltage | **3.2 V** | | Specific Energy | **90–160 Wh/kg** | | Cycle Life | **2,000–5,000+** | | Thermal Runaway Onset | **≈ 270 °C** (very safe) | | Cost (2024 India) | Lowest among Li-ion | - **Advantages:** Long cycle life, excellent thermal safety, cobalt-free (ethical + supply-chain advantage), low cost. - **Disadvantages:** Lower voltage → more cells in series for same pack voltage; lower energy density. ### 2.3.2 Lithium Nickel Manganese Cobalt (NMC) | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | Nominal Voltage | **3.6–3.7 V** | | Specific Energy | **150–220 Wh/kg** | | Cycle Life | **1,000–2,500** | | Thermal Runaway Onset | **≈ 210 °C** | - Common ratios: NMC 111 (1:1:1), NMC 532, NMC 622, NMC 811 (increasing Ni → higher energy, lower stability). ### 2.3.3 Lithium Titanate (LTO — Li₄Ti₅O₁₂) - Anode is LTO instead of graphite. - Nominal cell voltage: **2.3 V** (lowest among Li-ion). - Cycle life: **>10,000 cycles**. - Charging at **10C** possible. - Used in heavy-duty 3W commercial fleets requiring fast opportunity charging. ### 2.3.4 Solid-State Batteries (Emerging) Replace liquid electrolyte with a solid ceramic/polymer electrolyte. Promises **>400 Wh/kg** and elimination of flammable solvent. Pilot deployments in 2W segment expected post-2026. --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** The vertical bar `|` symbol is rendered using `\vert` to prevent markdown table parser breakage. | # | Formula | LaTeX Form | Meaning / Where Used | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Stored Energy | $E = V_{nom} \times Q_{Ah}$ | Pack-level energy in Wh | | 2 | Specific Energy | $E_m = \dfrac{E}{m_{pack}}$ | Wh/kg — range proxy | | 3 | C-rate definition | $I = C_{rate} \times Q_{Ah}$ | Convert C-rate to current | | 4 | Pack voltage | $V_{pack} = N_s \times V_{cell}$ | $N_s$ cells in series | | 5 | Pack capacity | $Q_{pack} = N_p \times Q_{cell}$ | $N_p$ cells in parallel | | 6 | Pack energy | $E_{pack} = V_{pack} \times Q_{pack}$ | Total Wh available | | 7 | Peukert's Law | $t = \dfrac{H}{I^{n}}$ | Capacity loss at high current | | 8 | Round-trip Efficiency | $\eta_{RT} = \dfrac{E_{out}}{E_{in}} \times 100\,\%$ | Charge-discharge losses | | 9 | SoC (Coulomb Counting) | $SoC(t) = SoC_0 + \dfrac{1}{Q}\!\int_{0}^{t} I(\tau)\,d\tau$ | Battery Management System (BMS) | | 10 | SoH | $SoH = \dfrac{Q_{aged}}{Q_{new}} \times 100\,\%$ | Degradation indicator | | 11 | Internal heat gen. | $Q_{heat} = I^{2} \cdot R_{int}$ | Thermal management design | | 12 | Open-circuit voltage | $V_{oc} = V_0 + \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\big(\text{[Ox]}\big/\text{[Red]}\big)$ | Nernst equation basis | --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Range Estimation:** Specific energy × pack mass = available energy → divided by Wh/km consumption gives range. - **Fast-Charge Feasibility:** C-rate × capacity = max charge current → determines charging socket spec (e.g., LFP at 1C, LTO at 5C+). - **Thermal Design:** $I^{2}R_{int}$ heat must be removed; determines coolant sizing for LFP/NMC packs. - **BMS Algorithm:** SoC and SoH drive dashboard SoC %, range prediction, and warranty claims. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Energy Stored in a Battery Pack ### Problem Setup A typical 2W EV (e.g., entry-level electric scooter) uses a pack of **N_s = 16 cells in series**, with **N_p = 1** parallel string. Each cell is LFP with: - Nominal voltage: $V_{cell} = 3.2 \text{ V}$ - Capacity: $Q_{cell} = 50 \text{ Ah}$ Find the pack nominal energy in kWh. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Pack voltage (cells in series add voltages):** $$V_{pack} = N_s \times V_{cell}$$ $$V_{pack} = 16 \times 3.2 \text{ V}$$ $$V_{pack} = 51.2 \text{ V}$$ **Step 2 — Pack capacity (series string does not increase capacity):** $$Q_{pack} = N_p \times Q_{cell}$$ $$Q_{pack} = 1 \times 50 \text{ Ah} = 50 \text{ Ah}$$ **Step 3 — Pack energy (Wh):** $$E_{pack} = V_{pack} \times Q_{pack}$$ $$E_{pack} = 51.2 \text{ V} \times 50 \text{ Ah}$$ $$E_{pack} = 2560 \text{ Wh}$$ **Step 4 — Convert to kWh:** $$E_{pack} = \dfrac{2560}{1000} = 2.56 \text{ kWh}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Validation:** Entry-level e-scooters in India (2024) typically advertise **2.5–3 kWh** packs — our calculation matches the market reality. This is a **likely 7-mark sub-part in KTU ESE**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — C-rate to Actual Current Conversion ### Problem A 3W EV (L5 passenger auto-rickshaw) uses an LFP pack rated **7.2 kWh, 96 V nominal, 75 Ah**. The manufacturer specifies a **maximum discharge C-rate of 3C**. Find the peak discharge current and the corresponding peak power. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Maximum discharge current:** $$I_{max} = C_{rate} \times Q_{pack}$$ $$I_{max} = 3 \times 75 \text{ Ah}$$ $$I_{max} = 225 \text{ A}$$ **Step 2 — Peak discharge power:** $$P_{peak} = V_{pack} \times I_{max}$$ $$P_{peak} = 96 \text{ V} \times 225 \text{ A}$$ $$P_{peak} = 21{,}600 \text{ W}$$ $$\boxed{P_{peak} \approx 21.6 \text{ kW}}$$ **Step 3 — Cross-check against vehicle class:** A typical L5 auto-rickshaw uses a **7–15 kW peak** motor. Our pack can deliver 21.6 kW peak → healthy margin for hill starts and overtaking. ✅ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Applying Peukert's Law ### Problem A 48 V, 30 Ah lead-acid battery (3W e-rickshaw) is rated using the **20-hour (C/20) rate**. Peukert exponent is **n = 1.25**. Find the actual usable capacity when discharged at the **2-hour (C/2) rate**. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Rated discharge current (20-hour rate):** $$I_{rated} = \dfrac{Q_{rated}}{20} = \dfrac{30}{20} = 1.5 \text{ A}$$ **Step 2 — Actual discharge current (2-hour rate):** $$I_{actual} = \dfrac{Q_{rated}}{2} = \dfrac{30}{2} = 15 \text{ A}$$ **Step 3 — Apply Peukert's Law:** $$t_{actual} = \dfrac{H}{I_{actual}^{\,n}}$$ where **H = rated capacity in Peukert form** = $I_{rated}^{\,n} \times 20$. $$H = (1.5)^{1.25} \times 20$$ $$H \approx 1.6325 \times 20 \approx 32.65$$ $$t_{actual} = \dfrac{32.65}{(15)^{1.25}}$$ $$(15)^{1.25} = 15^{1} \times 15^{0.25} = 15 \times 1.9679 \approx 29.52$$ $$t_{actual} = \dfrac{32.65}{29.52} \approx 1.106 \text{ hours}$$ **Step 4 — Actual capacity delivered:** $$Q_{actual} = I_{actual} \times t_{actual} = 15 \times 1.106 \approx 16.6 \text{ Ah}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** The driver expected 30 Ah, but at 2C discharge they only got **~16.6 Ah — a 45 % loss!** This is why lead-acid is unsuitable for high-power 2W/3W EVs despite low cost. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — State of Charge (Coulomb Counting) in BMS ### Problem A 3.5 kWh NMC pack (nominal 72 V, 48.6 Ah) starts a journey at **SoC₀ = 80 %**. Over **45 minutes**, the BMS integrates a constant current draw of **30 A**. Find the final SoC, ignoring losses. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Charge removed (Ah):** $$\Delta Q = I \times t = 30 \text{ A} \times 0.75 \text{ h} = 22.5 \text{ Ah}$$ **Step 2 — SoC change:** $$\Delta SoC = \dfrac{\Delta Q}{Q_{pack}} \times 100\,\%$$ $$\Delta SoC = \dfrac{22.5}{48.6} \times 100\,\% \approx 46.30\,\%$$ **Step 3 — Final SoC:** $$SoC_{final} = SoC_0 - \Delta SoC$$ $$SoC_{final} = 80\,\% - 46.30\,\% = 33.70\,\%$$ $$\boxed{SoC_{final} \approx 33.7\,\%}$$ **Step 4 — Energy consumed (cross-check):** $$E_{consumed} = V_{nom} \times \Delta Q = 72 \times 22.5 = 1620 \text{ Wh} = 1.62 \text{ kWh}$$ Energy check: 46.3 % × 3.5 kWh ≈ 1.62 kWh ✅ --- ## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Battery Selection for 2W/3W > [!NOTE] > **This matrix is THE most frequently asked comparative table in KTU ESE Module 3.** Memorise the parameter ranges; use them as a "cheat sheet" for any "compare and contrast" question. | Parameter | Lead-Acid (FLA) | NiMH | LFP | NMC | LTO | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Nominal V / cell | 2.0 V | 1.2 V | **3.2 V** | 3.7 V | 2.3 V | | Specific Energy (Wh/kg) | 30–50 | 60–120 | 90–160 | 150–220 | 60–80 | | Energy Density (Wh/L) | 60–110 | 140–300 | 250–400 | 400–650 | 130–180 | | Cycle Life (to 80 % SoH) | 300–500 | 500–1,000 | **2,000–5,000** | 1,000–2,500 | >10,000 | | Peak C-rate (discharge) | 3–5 C | 5 C | 3–5 C | 3–5 C | **10–20 C** | | Fast-Charge Capable | No | Limited | Yes (1–2 C) | Yes (1–2 C) | Yes (**5–10 C**) | | Thermal Runaway (°C) | ~150 | ~200 | **~270** | ~210 | ~250 | | Cost (₹/kWh, India 2024) | 8,000–12,000 | 18,000–25,000 | 12,000–16,000 | 16,000–22,000 | 35,000–50,000 | | Memory Effect | None | Yes | None | None | None | | Self-Discharge (%/month) | 3–5 | **20–30** | 1–3 | 1–3 | 1–3 | | Recycling Infrastructure | Excellent | Limited | Developing | Developing | Developing | | Best 2W/3W Application | Low-speed e-rickshaw, SLI | Legacy hybrid 2W | **Mainstream e-2W/e-3W** | Performance e-2W | Heavy-duty e-3W fleet | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Diagram Note:** Physical battery drawings (cell cross-sections, tab welding details) are complex and not ideal for Mermaid rendering. Therefore, the diagrams below are **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** mappings showing the *system-level topology and classification logic*, which is what the KTU Board expects in 14-mark descriptive answers. ## 4.1 Battery-Type Classification Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD ROOT["AUTOMOTIVE BATTERY FOR 2W AND 3W VEHICLES"] PRIM["PRIMARY CELLS"] SEC["SECONDARY CELLS RECHARGEABLE"] ROOT --> PRIM ROOT --> SEC PRIM --> PRIM1["Alkaline AA Remote Keys"] PRIM --> PRIM2["Lithium Coin CR2032 Sensors"] SEC --> LEAD["LEAD ACID FAMILY"] SEC --> NICK["NICKEL FAMILY"] SEC --> LI["LITHIUM ION FAMILY"] SEC --> EMER["EMERGING FAMILY"] LEAD --> LFLA["Flooded FLA E Rickshaw Cargo"] LEAD --> LAGM["AGM Premium 2W Start Stop"] LEAD --> LGEL["Gel Cell E Auto Passenger"] NICK --> NICD["NiCd Industrial Legacy"] NICK --> NIMH["NiMH Early Hybrid 2W"] LI --> LFP["LFP LiFePO4 Mainstream 2W 3W"] LI --> NMC["NMC Performance 2W"] LI --> NCA["NCA Premium Segment"] LI --> LMO["LMO Power Tool Legacy"] LI --> LTO["LTO Heavy Duty 3W Fleet"] EMER --> NAION["Sodium Ion Pilot Projects"] EMER --> SSB["Solid State Pilot Projects"] ``` ## 4.2 Battery-Pack Architecture — Cell → Module → Pack (BMS Topology) ```mermaid flowchart LR CELL["CELL
3.2 V LFP
50 Ah

1P x 50 Ah"] MOD["MODULE
16S1P

Cell Balancing

Thermal Cutoff"] VCU["VEHICLE CONTROL UNIT
Motor Controller
Charger Interface"] CELL --> PAR1 PAR1 --> MOD MOD --> PACK BMS -.monitors.-> CELL BMS -.monitors.-> MOD BMS -.monitors.-> PACK PACK --> VCU ``` ## 4.3 Battery Selection Decision Flow for 2W/3W Application ```mermaid flowchart TD START["SELECT BATTERY FOR 2W OR 3W VEHICLE"] Q1{"Daily Range
Required?"} Q2{"Budget per kWh
Constraint?"} Q3{"Cycle Life
Required?"} Q4{"Fast Charge
Needed?"} LOWR["Low Range Less than 40 km per day"] HIGHR["High Range More than 80 km per day"] LOWB["Tight Budget below 15000 INR per kWh"] MIDB["Mid Budget 15000 to 22000"] HIGHB["Performance Budget above 22000"] LCYC["Under 1000 cycles"] HCYC["More than 2500 cycles"] LFC["No Fast Charge Slow AC Only"] HFC["Yes Fast Charge DC Needed"] FLA["CHOOSE FLA OR AGM LEAD ACID"] LFP1["CHOOSE LFP LiFePO4"] NMC1["CHOOSE NMC FOR ENERGY DENSITY"] LTO1["CHOOSE LTO FOR FLEET FAST CHARGE"] START --> Q1 Q1 --> LOWR Q1 --> HIGHR LOWR --> Q2 Q2 --> LOWB Q2 --> MIDB LOWB --> FLA MIDB --> Q3 Q3 --> LCYC Q3 --> HCYC LCYC --> NMC1 HCYC --> LFP1 HIGHR --> Q4 Q4 --> LFC Q4 --> HFC LFC --> LFP1 HFC --> LTO1 ``` > [!NOTE] > **How to use this in a KTU answer:** When the question is *"Which battery is best for a delivery-fleet 3W cargo auto in Kerala's hilly terrain?"* — trace through the flowchart: High range (hill climb + delivery loops) → Mid budget → High cycle life → Fast charge (depot turnaround) → concludes **LFP** is the sweet spot. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) ### Question A1: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]** **"Distinguish between primary and secondary cells with one example each used in two-wheeled vehicles." (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Primary Cell | Secondary Cell | |---|---|---| | Reaction direction | Irreversible (Chemical → Electrical only) | Reversible (Chemical ⇌ Electrical) | | Rechargeable | No | Yes | | Internal resistance | Generally higher | Lower (designed for cycling) | | Example in 2W | Lithium coin cell (CR2032) in remote key / alarm | LFP / NMC pack in electric scooter | | Typical use | Backup memory, immobiliser | Traction (propulsion) | *(3 marks — full marks requires both difference and one correct example each.)* --- ### Question A2: **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]** **"Why is Lithium Iron Phosphate (LFP) preferred over NMC for Indian entry-level electric two-wheelers despite its lower specific energy?" (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** 1. **Thermal safety:** LFP thermal runaway onset ≈ 270 °C vs. NMC ≈ 210 °C — critical in India's hot climate and for low-cost passive-cooling packs. *(1 mark)* 2. **Cycle life:** 2,000–5,000 cycles vs. NMC's 1,000–2,500 — better for high-utilisation daily commute / commercial fleets. *(1 mark)* 3. **Cost & ethics:** Cobalt-free, cheaper raw materials, simpler BMS; offsets the lower specific energy through bigger pack volume (which is acceptable in a 2W frame). *(1 mark)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) ### 📌 Question A (Choice 1): **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **"With neat sketches and necessary equations, explain the construction, working principle, and charging–discharging reactions of a Valve Regulated Lead-Acid (VRLA) battery used in three-wheeled electric auto-rickshaws. Compare its performance with Lithium Iron Phosphate (LFP) for the same application." (14 Marks)** #### Part (a) — VRLA Construction & Working (7 Marks) **Construction:** - **Positive plate:** Lead dioxide (PbO₂) pasted on a lead-antimony grid. - **Negative plate:** Spongy lead (Pb) on lead-calcium grid. - **Separator:** AGM (glass mat) or gel (SiO₂-thickened H₂SO₄). - **Electrolyte:** Immobilised — cannot spill, valve regulates gas escape. - **Container:** Sealed polypropylene, flame-arrestor vent. **Working Principle (Discharge):** $$\text{Pb} + \text{PbO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow 2\,\text{PbSO}_4 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ **Working Principle (Charge — reverse reaction):** $$2\,\text{PbSO}_4 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Pb} + \text{PbO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$$ **Oxygen Recombination Cycle (key VRLA feature):** During overcharge, oxygen evolved at the positive plate diffuses through the separator to the negative plate and recombines with hydrogen to form water again — making the battery "sealed" in practice. *(3 marks for equations + 4 marks for construction narrative.)* #### Part (b) — VRLA vs. LFP Comparison (7 Marks) | Parameter | VRLA (Gel/AGM) | LFP (LiFePO₄) | |---|---|---| | Specific Energy | 30–40 Wh/kg | 100–160 Wh/kg | | Cycle Life | 400–700 | 2,000–5,000 | | Operating Temperature Range | -20 to 50 °C (derated) | -10 to 55 °C (with BMS) | | Maintenance | Almost zero (sealed) | Zero (BMS-managed) | | Cost per kWh (2024) | ₹8,000–12,000 | ₹12,000–16,000 | | Weight for 5 kWh pack | ~140 kg | ~40 kg | | Range penalty on a 3W auto | Significant | Marginal | | Safety (thermal runaway) | Risk of thermal runaway at high SoC | Much safer | **Conclusion:** For an L5 passenger auto-rickshaw doing **>80 km/day**, LFP wins on cycle life, weight, and range. VRLA wins only on **upfront capex** for budget-constrained operators. *(7 marks — full marks requires table + concluding engineering judgement.)* --- ### 📌 Question B (Choice 2): **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]** **"A 3W electric cargo loader uses a battery pack with the following specifications: Chemistry = LFP, Pack voltage = 96 V, Pack capacity = 100 Ah, Peukert exponent is not applicable (assume ideal). The vehicle consumes 80 Wh/km. The driver starts the day at SoC = 100 % and drives until SoC = 20 %."** **(a) Calculate the available energy in kWh, total range in km, and the energy consumed in kWh.** **(b) If the battery is replaced with an NMC pack of the same 96 V nominal but only 70 Ah, recalculate the range and comment on the volume/weight trade-off.** (14 Marks) #### Part (a) — LFP Pack Calculations (7 Marks) **Step 1 — Available Energy in the pack:** $$E_{pack} = V_{pack} \times Q_{pack}$$ $$E_{pack} = 96 \text{ V} \times 100 \text{ Ah}$$ $$E_{pack} = 9600 \text{ Wh} = 9.6 \text{ kWh}$$ **Step 2 — Usable energy (between SoC 100 % and 20 %):** $$E_{usable} = E_{pack} \times (SoC_{start} - SoC_{end})$$ $$E_{usable} = 9.6 \times (1.00 - 0.20) = 9.6 \times 0.80 = 7.68 \text{ kWh}$$ **Step 3 — Total range:** $$R = \dfrac{E_{usable}}{\text{Consumption}} = \dfrac{7680 \text{ Wh}}{80 \text{ Wh/km}}$$ $$\boxed{R = 96 \text{ km}}$$ **Step 4 — Energy actually consumed:** $$E_{consumed} = E_{usable} = 7.68 \text{ kWh}$$ *(Full 7 marks requires all four sub-results.)* #### Part (b) — NMC Replacement Analysis (7 Marks) **Step 1 — New pack energy with NMC, 96 V, 70 Ah:** $$E_{pack,NMC} = 96 \times 70 = 6720 \text{ Wh} = 6.72 \text{ kWh}$$ **Step 2 — Usable energy (80 % DoD window):** $$E_{usable,NMC} = 6.72 \times 0.80 = 5.376 \text{ kWh}$$ **Step 3 — New range:** $$R_{NMC} = \dfrac{5376}{80} \approx 67.2 \text{ km}$$ **Step 4 — Range drop:** ΔR = 96 − 67.2 = **28.8 km (~30 % reduction)**. **Step 5 — Volume/Weight trade-off comment (engineering reasoning):** - NMC specific energy ≈ 180 Wh/kg vs. LFP ≈ 130 Wh/kg → **NMC pack mass ~30 % lower** for same energy → cargo payload increases. - But NMC capacity (Ah) is lower → fewer cells in parallel → if cell capacity matches, total *cell count* may stay similar; the gain is mainly in **gravimetric density**, not volumetric. - For a **cargo 3W** where every kg of payload matters, NMC is justified; for **passenger 3W** where range matters more, LFP is preferred. $$\boxed{\text{Engineering verdict: } \text{NMC trades 30 \% range for 30 \% weight savings — chosen when payload > range priority.}}$$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Battery-Type Questions:** > 1. **Confusing *energy* (Wh) with *capacity* (Ah).** Always write units. "100 Ah battery" without voltage = 0 marks for energy questions. > 2. **Stating "Li-ion is the best" without justification.** Examiners want *which Li-ion chemistry* (LFP / NMC / LTO) and *why*. > 3. **Writing Peukert's Law without defining the rated current basis.** Specify "rated at C/20" or "rated at 1C" explicitly. > 4. **Forgetting the Nernst / open-circuit voltage dependence on SoC.** Students often treat V as constant — it isn't. SoC-vs-OCV curve is part of LFP/NMC characterisation. > 5. **Ignoring safety.** Every battery answer MUST include at least one safety/thermal-runaway statement for full marks. > 6. **Mixing up cell, module, and pack.** "96 V, 50 Ah" describes the **pack**, not a cell. Misnaming levels costs 1–2 marks. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - 🔋 **Two families to know cold:** **Lead-Acid** (legacy, cheap, heavy) and **Lithium-Ion** (modern, dominant). Everything else (NiMH, LTO, Na-ion) is "also-ran" for 2W/3W in 2024–2026. - 🔋 **Master the "BIG 3" Li-ion chemistries:** LFP (safe + cheap + long-cycle), NMC (energy-dense), LTO (fast-charge + ultra-long-cycle). - 🔋 **Mnemonic for cell voltages:** **"3-2-7-3"** → LFP ≈ 3.2 V, LTO ≈ 2.3 V, NMC ≈ 3.7 V, LMO ≈ 4.0 V. - 🔋 **Always quote specific energy (Wh/kg) and cycle life together** — they trade off against each other. - 🔋 **Pack = Series × Voltage, Parallel × Capacity.** Series raises V, parallel raises Ah. Energy = V × Ah. - 🔋 **SoC + SoH + DoD** are the three pillars of any BMS answer. - 🔋 **Peukert's Law is the secret weapon** for impressing the examiner in any 14-mark numerical — show the full derivation, don't skip steps. - 🔋 **Thermal runaway temperatures:** LFP (~270 °C) > LTO (~250 °C) > NMC (~210 °C) > Lead-acid (~150 °C). - 🔋 **India-specific fact (2024):** LFP dominates Indian 2W/3W market; NMC used in performance/premium 2W; FLA still in budget e-rickshaws. - 🔋 **Three mandatory safety statements:** thermal runaway onset, BMS necessity, recycling pathway. - 🔋 **For 14-mark questions:** always include (i) a comparison table, (ii) the key equations, (iii) an engineering verdict with at least one trade-off statement.

topics.title: Battery Chargers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT631 - TWO AND THREE WHEELED VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Battery Chargers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **battery charger** is an electrical/electronic power conversion device that supplies controlled electrical energy in the form of DC current to a rechargeable battery pack, forcing a reversal of the electrochemical reactions that occur during discharge. In the specific context of two- and three-wheeled electric vehicles (EVs), a battery charger is the critical power-electronic interface between the AC mains supply (or a DC fast-charging station) and the on-board traction battery, ensuring safe, efficient, and longevity-preserving energy transfer.

The charger must perform three simultaneous functions: (1) **AC-to-DC rectification** (or DC-to-DC buck/boost conversion for fast chargers), (2) **charging profile regulation** following manufacturer-defined current-voltage-time envelopes, and (3) **battery management system (BMS) communication** to terminate or modulate charging based on State-of-Charge (SoC), cell voltage, and temperature feedback.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 emphasizes the topology, control philosophy, and charging algorithms relevant to lead-acid (for low-speed e-rickshaws and e-scooters with portable packs) and lithium-ion chemistries (for high-performance electric two-wheelers like Ola S1, Ather 450X, TVS iQube, Bajaj Chetak).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **water tank (battery)** that is half-empty. You want to fill it. If you just shove a high-pressure fire hose into the tank, the water sloshes violently, the tank may crack, and a lot of water splashes out as waste (heat/electrolyte loss). If you pour too slowly, you will never finish. What you actually need is a **smart filling station** that:
- Starts with a high flow rate when the tank is nearly empty (Bulk / Constant Current stage),
- Reduces the flow as the tank approaches full and switches to gentle topping (Absorption / Constant Voltage stage),
- Keeps a tiny trickle of flow running to compensate for natural evaporation (Float / Trickle stage),
- And constantly monitors the water level, temperature, and pressure so it can stop the moment the tank is full.

A **battery charger is exactly this "smart filling station"** for electrons in an electrochemical cell. The "smartness" comes from power-electronic switches (SCRs, MOSFETs, IGBTs) and a control loop (analog IC like UC3906, or digital microcontroller / DSP).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

Key numerical benchmarks for two- and three-wheeler chargers in the Indian market:

- **On-board charger power range:** 250 W – 3.3 kW (typically 600 W – 1.5 kW for premium e-2W)
- **Off-board fast charger power:** 3.3 kW – 22 kW
- **Nominal pack voltage (e-2W):** 48 V, 60 V, 72 V (Li-ion); 48 V, 60 V (Lead-acid)
- **Charging efficiency target:** $\eta \geq 90\%$ for SMPS-based chargers
- **Power Factor (PF) target:** $\geq 0.95$ (mandated by IEC 61000-3-2)
- **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD):** $< 5\%$ for current drawn from mains

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bharat EV Standards (India):** All public charging stations in India for light EVs (2W/3W) follow **Bharat DC-001** (15 V/200 A connector) for low-voltage DC fast charging and **AC001** for slow AC charging. This is regulated by the Ministry of Heavy Industries, Government of India.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Battery Chargers for 2W / 3W

#### 2.1.1 By Topology
1. **Conventional Linear / Ferroresonant (Tap) Charger** – Uses a multi-tap transformer and diode bridge. Heavy, bulky, but reliable. Still used in low-cost lead-acid e-rickshaw chargers.
2. **SCR (Thyristor) Phase-Controlled Charger** – Uses Silicon Controlled Rectifiers to vary the conduction angle $\alpha$ of the AC mains, thereby controlling DC output. Classic topology.
3. **Switch-Mode Power Supply (SMPS) Charger** – Uses high-frequency (50 kHz – 200 kHz) PWM switching through MOSFETs/IGBTs. Compact, light, high efficiency. **Industry standard today.**
4. **Resonant / LLC Converter Charger** – A subclass of SMPS using zero-voltage switching (ZVS) for very high efficiency (>95%).
5. **Microcontroller / DSP-Controlled Charger** – Digital implementation with closed-loop control of CC/CV profile.

#### 2.1.2 By Location
- **On-Board Charger (OBC):** Mounted inside the vehicle. Limited by size, weight, and heat dissipation. Typical rating 600 W – 3.3 kW.
- **Off-Board Charger:** External standalone unit (like a laptop charger). Can be much higher power.

### 2.2 Charging Profiles (Charging Algorithms)

The charging profile is a pre-defined trajectory of **current (I) vs voltage (V) vs time (t)** that the charger must follow to safely replenish a battery. KTU examiners frequently test the following profiles:

#### 2.2.1 Constant Current (CC) Charging
The charger delivers a fixed, regulated current regardless of battery voltage. The voltage rises linearly as the battery accepts charge.

**Used for:** Initial bulk charging of lead-acid; equalization of Ni-Cd.

**Drawback:** If not terminated, leads to overcharging, gassing, and thermal runaway.

#### 2.2.2 Constant Voltage (CV) Charging
The charger holds a fixed terminal voltage. As the battery charges, its EMF rises, so the **current naturally tapers** (decays exponentially) following an RC-like curve.

**Used for:** Standby float charging; finishing stage of VRLA and Li-ion.

#### 2.2.3 IU (CC-CV) Profile – The Industry Workhorse
A two-stage algorithm:
- **Stage 1 (Bulk / CC):** Deliver rated current $I_{max}$ until terminal voltage reaches absorption voltage $V_{abs}$.
- **Stage 2 (Absorption / CV):** Hold $V_{abs}$ constant; current decays from $I_{max}$ to cutoff current $I_{cutoff}$ (typically 0.05 C).
- **Termination:** Charger shuts off OR transitions to float.

#### 2.2.4 IUI Profile
- I (Bulk CC) → U (Absorption CV) → I (Equalization CC at lower rate)
- Used for flooded lead-acid traction batteries in e-rickshaws.

#### 2.2.5 Pulse Charging
Pulses of high current interspersed with rest periods. Allows for chemical equalization of cells, reduces polarization, and minimizes gassing. Especially useful for Li-ion longevity.

#### 2.2.6 Trickle / Float Charging
A very low current ($I_{trickle} \approx 0.01 C$ to $0.05 C$) maintained indefinitely to compensate for self-discharge. Used when the vehicle is parked for long durations.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity / Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Unit** | **Remarks** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| C-rate | $C$ | $C = \dfrac{I_{charge}}{Q_{rated}}$ | hour$^{-1}$ | 1C = full charge in 1 h |
| Charging Time (theoretical) | $t_{ch}$ | $t_{ch} = \dfrac{Q_{batt} \cdot 100}{I_{ch} \cdot \eta_{ch}}$ | hours | Accounts for inefficiency |
| Energy Stored | $E$ | $E = V_{nom} \cdot Q_{batt}$ | Wh | $Q$ in Ah |
| State of Charge | $SoC$ | $SoC = SoC_{0} + \dfrac{1}{Q_{rated}} \int_{0}^{t} I(\tau) \, d\tau$ | % | Coulomb counting |
| Constant Current Cutoff | $V_{abs}$ | $V_{abs} = n_{cells} \cdot V_{cell,abs}$ | V | $n$ = number of cells |
| Power Loss in Charger | $P_{loss}$ | $P_{loss} = P_{out}\left(\dfrac{1}{\eta} - 1\right)$ | W | $\eta$ = efficiency |
| RMS Charging Current (SCR) | $I_{rms}$ | $I_{rms} = \dfrac{I_{m}}{2}\sqrt{1 - \dfrac{\alpha}{\pi} + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2\pi}}$ | A | $\alpha$ = firing angle |
| Average DC Output (SCR) | $V_{dc}$ | $V_{dc} = \dfrac{V_{m}}{\pi}\left(1 + \cos\alpha\right)$ | V | Full-wave controlled bridge |
| Power Factor (SCR) | $PF$ | $PF = \dfrac{V_{rms} \cdot I_{1,rms} \cos\phi_{1}}{V_{rms} \cdot I_{rms}}$ | – | Distorted current |
| Total Harmonic Distortion | $THD$ | $THD = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} I_{n,rms}^{2}}}{I_{1,rms}} \times 100$ | % | For SMPS chargers |
| Heat Generated | $Q_{heat}$ | $Q_{heat} = I^{2} R_{int} \cdot t$ | J | Internal resistance $R_{int}$ |
| Lithium-ion per-cell absorption | $V_{abs,cell}$ | $4.20 \pm 0.05$ V | V | For NMC/NCA chemistry |
| Lithium-ion per-cell float | $V_{float,cell}$ | $3.60 - 3.70$ V | V | Storage voltage |
| Lead-acid per-cell absorption | $V_{abs,cell}$ | $2.40 - 2.45$ V | V | VRLA |
| Lead-acid per-cell float | $V_{float,cell}$ | $2.25 - 2.30$ V | V | Standby |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Pipes:** The notation $V_{cell,abs}$ uses no vertical bars. If you must write $\vert V_{cell,abs} \vert$ in an exam, prefer $\lvert V_{cell,abs} \rvert$ or $\mid V_{cell,abs} \mid$ to avoid LaTeX rendering issues in markdown tables.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Battery chargers are not just "plug and forget" boxes. In modern two- and three-wheeled EVs, the charger interacts with:

- **Vehicle Control Unit (VCU)** – For SoC display, range estimation, thermal management.
- **Battery Management System (BMS)** – For cell balancing, temperature cut-off, and CAN bus telemetry.
- **Grid (V2G future scope)** – Vehicle-to-Grid technology will require bidirectional chargers using the same hardware as the on-board unit.

A poorly designed charger can permanently damage a battery in **one single charging session** (over-voltage lithium plating) or degrade it over **6 months** (chronic overcharge of lead-acid causing sulfation).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Average DC Output of a Single-Phase Full-Wave Controlled Bridge Rectifier

This derivation is **extremely high-yield for KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions** because it is the mathematical heart of an SCR-based charger.

**Statement:** A single-phase full-bridge rectifier is fed from an AC source $v_{s}(t) = V_{m} \sin(\omega t)$ and feeds a battery load (modeled as an EMF $E$ in series with resistance $R$). The SCRs are fired at angle $\alpha$. Derive the average DC output voltage.

**Step 1: Write the source voltage equation.**

$$v_{s}(t) = V_{m} \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 2: Identify the conduction window.**
