For a highly inductive load (battery charging is a constant-current sink), the SCRs conduct from firing angle $\alpha$ to the extinction angle $\beta$, where the instantaneous source voltage equals the back-EMF $E$ of the battery.

$$V_{m} \sin(\omega t) = E \implies \beta = \pi - \alpha$$

This holds because the load is symmetric for a full-bridge configuration.

**Step 3: Express the average DC voltage over one full cycle.**

The average is the integral of the instantaneous output over the conduction interval, divided by the period $2\pi$ in electrical radians.

$$V_{dc} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi - \alpha} V_{m} \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the integral.**

We integrate the sine function:

$$\int V_{m} \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) = -V_{m} \cos(\omega t)$$

So the integral between limits $\alpha$ and $\pi - \alpha$ becomes:

$$V_{dc} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \left[ -V_{m} \cos(\omega t) \right]_{\alpha}^{\pi - \alpha}$$

**Step 5: Substitute the limits.**

$$V_{dc} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \left[ -V_{m} \cos(\pi - \alpha) - \left( -V_{m} \cos(\alpha) \right) \right]$$

Using the identity $\cos(\pi - \alpha) = -\cos(\alpha)$:

$$V_{dc} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \left[ -V_{m} \left( -\cos\alpha \right) + V_{m} \cos\alpha \right]$$

$$V_{dc} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \left[ V_{m} \cos\alpha + V_{m} \cos\alpha \right]$$

$$V_{dc} = \dfrac{2 V_{m} \cos\alpha}{2\pi} = \dfrac{V_{m} \cos\alpha}{\pi}$$

**Step 6: Final result.**

$$\boxed{V_{dc} = \dfrac{V_{m} \cos\alpha}{\pi}}$$

This is the **fundamental control equation** of an SCR charger: by varying the firing angle $\alpha$ from $0$ to $\pi/2$, the average output voltage varies from $V_{m}/\pi$ (maximum) down to $0$ (minimum).

### 3.2 Derivation: Charging Time for a Battery Using Constant Current

**Given:**
- Battery capacity $Q$ in Ah
- Charging current $I_{ch}$ in A
- Charging efficiency $\eta_{ch}$ (decimal, e.g., 0.85 for lead-acid, 0.99 for Li-ion)

**Step 1: Energy balance.**

The energy delivered by the charger must equal the energy stored in the battery plus losses:

$$V_{ch} I_{ch} t_{ch} \cdot \eta_{ch} = Q \cdot V_{nom}$$

**Step 2: Solve for time.**

$$t_{ch} = \dfrac{Q \cdot V_{nom}}{V_{ch} \cdot I_{ch} \cdot \eta_{ch}}$$

**Step 3: Approximation for CC charging.**

For a true CC charger, the charger voltage $V_{ch} \approx V_{nom}$ (terminal voltage of battery). Therefore:

$$\boxed{t_{ch} = \dfrac{Q}{I_{ch} \cdot \eta_{ch}} = \dfrac{C_{rate}^{-1}}{\eta_{ch}} \text{ hours}}$$

**Step 4: Numerical example.**

A 60 V, 30 Ah Li-ion e-scooter battery is charged at 0.5 C with $\eta_{ch} = 0.92$:

$$I_{ch} = 0.5 \times 30 = 15 \text{ A}$$

$$t_{ch} = \dfrac{30}{15 \times 0.92} = \dfrac{30}{13.8} \approx 2.17 \text{ hours}$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Heat Generation in a Charger Cable / Connector

The I²R losses in a charging cable must be calculated to size cooling and voltage drop.

**Step 1: Cable resistance.**

$$R_{cable} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot L}{A}$$

where $\rho$ is the resistivity of copper $= 1.724 \times 10^{-8} \, \Omega\cdot$m, $L$ is total length (positive + return), and $A$ is cross-sectional area.

**Step 2: Power dissipated.**

$$P_{loss} = I_{ch}^{2} R_{cable}$$

**Step 3: Voltage drop.**

$$V_{drop} = I_{ch} \cdot R_{cable}$$

**Step 4: Energy wasted per charge session.**

$$E_{waste} = P_{loss} \cdot t_{ch} = I_{ch}^{2} R_{cable} t_{ch}$$

This must be subtracted from the AC input energy to compute the actual AC energy consumption for billing purposes:

$$E_{AC} = \dfrac{E_{DC}}{\eta_{charger} \cdot \eta_{BMS}}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: SMPS-Based On-Board Charger (OBC) for an Electric Two-Wheeler

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AC Mains 230V 50Hz] --> B[EMI Filter]
    B --> C[PFC Boost Converter]
    C --> D[DC Link Capacitor 400V]
    D --> E[Full-Bridge Inverter HF]
    E --> F[HF Isolation Transformer]
    F --> G[Secondary Rectifier]
    G --> H[Output LC Filter]
    H --> I[BMS Communication Link]
    H --> J[Battery Pack 48-72V]
    I --> K[Microcontroller DSP]
    K --> L[CC-CV Reference Generator]
    L --> C
    L --> E
    K --> M[Display SoC and Status]
    J --> N[Vehicle CAN Bus]
```

**Operating Principle:**

- **EMI Filter:** Suppresses conducted electromagnetic noise (mandated by CISPR 22 / EN 55011).
- **PFC Stage:** Power Factor Correction boost converter; forces the input current to be sinusoidal and in phase with voltage. Achieves PF > 0.95.
- **DC Link:** Stores intermediate DC energy at ~400 V.
- **HF Inverter:** Converts DC to high-frequency AC (~100 kHz) for transformer isolation.
- **HF Transformer:** Provides galvanic isolation AND steps down voltage. Lightweight due to high frequency.
- **Secondary Rectifier + LC Filter:** Smooths to clean DC output suitable for the battery.
- **CC-CV Reference Generator:** Compares actual voltage and current to desired profile, generates PWM error signal.
- **BMS Link:** CAN or SMBus communication for cell voltage, temperature, SoC, and termination flags.

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Charging Algorithm State Machine (CC-CV-Trickle)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Initialization
    Initialization --> BulkCC: Check Temp and Voltage OK
    BulkCC --> AbsorptionCV: Vterm >= Vabs
    AbsorptionCV --> TrickleFloat: Iterm <= Icutoff
    AbsorptionCV --> SafetyCutoff: Tcell > Tmax
    BulkCC --> SafetyCutoff: Vterm > Vhardmax
    TrickleFloat --> EndOfCharge: SoC = 100 percent
    SafetyCutoff --> [*]: Alarm to BMS
    EndOfCharge --> [*]: Charger Off
```

**State Descriptions:**

- **BulkCC (Constant Current):** Deliver $I_{max}$ (typically 0.5 C to 1 C) until $V_{terminal}$ reaches $V_{abs}$.
- **AbsorptionCV (Constant Voltage):** Hold $V_{abs}$; current decays as battery EMF rises.
- **TrickleFloat:** Maintain $V_{float} < V_{abs}$ at very low current.
- **SafetyCutoff:** Any over-voltage, over-temperature, or BMS fault terminates charging.

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Comparison of Charger Topologies

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Charger Family] --> B[Linear Tap]
    A --> C[SCR Phase Controlled]
    A --> D[SMPS HF]
    B --> B1[Bulky Transformer]
    B --> B2[Eta 50 to 65 percent]
    C --> C1[Medium Weight]
    C --> C2[Eta 70 to 80 percent]
    C --> C3[PF around 0.6]
    D --> D1[Lightweight HF]
    D --> D2[Eta 90 to 96 percent]
    D --> D3[PF greater than 0.95]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Fast Charging Architecture (Off-Board DCFC)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[3 Phase AC 415V] --> B[Three Phase Active Rectifier]
    B --> C[DC Link 700V]
    C --> D[Buck Chopper Stage 1]
    D --> E[Buck Chopper Stage 2]
    E --> F[Output Filter and Contactor]
    F --> G[Battery Pack via CCS2 or Bharat DC001]
    G --> H[BMS Protocol DIN 70121 or OCPP]
    H --> I[Charging Station Controller]
    I --> B
    I --> D
    I --> E
```

**Key Insight:** For off-board DC fast charging of e-2W and e-3W, the charger bypasses the vehicle's OBC and feeds DC directly to the battery, with the BMS acting as the master controller. This is why the BMS must be capable of high-speed CAN communication during fast charging.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] Define the IU charging profile for a traction battery used in an electric three-wheeler. List its three stages and the trigger condition for transition between them.**

**Model Answer:**

The IU charging profile is a two-stage (sometimes three-stage) charging algorithm used for VRLA and lithium-ion traction batteries in EVs.

- **Stage 1: I (Constant Current / Bulk).** The charger delivers a regulated current of $0.2$C to $1$C. The terminal voltage of the battery rises gradually.
- **Stage 2: U (Constant Voltage / Absorption).** When the terminal voltage reaches the manufacturer's absorption voltage $V_{abs}$ (e.g., 2.40 V/cell for VRLA, 4.20 V/cell for Li-ion NMC), the charger switches to voltage-mode regulation. The current then decays exponentially as the cell EMF rises.
- **Stage 3 (optional): I (Equalization).** A short, lower-rate constant current pulse to equalize cell voltages in flooded lead-acid packs.

**Trigger conditions:**
- I → U transition: $V_{terminal} \geq V_{abs}$.
- U → End transition: $I_{charge} \leq I_{cutoff}$ (typically 0.05 C) AND elapsed time $t \geq t_{min,abs}$.

This profile is the industry standard because it balances **fast charging time** with **minimal gassing and thermal stress** on the cells.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Differentiate between On-Board and Off-Board chargers for electric two-wheelers. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **On-Board Charger (OBC)** | **Off-Board Charger** |
|---|---|---|
| Location | Inside the vehicle | Standalone external unit |
| Power rating | Typically 600 W – 3.3 kW | 3.3 kW – 22 kW (or higher) |
| Weight | Adds to vehicle kerb weight | Not carried by vehicle |
| Convenience | Plug anywhere with AC socket | Must carry the brick, but can charge faster |
| Cost | Higher (compact design) | Lower per kW |
| Heat dissipation | Constrained (vehicle enclosure) | Better (open air) |
| BMS communication | Always via internal CAN | Often simpler analog control |

**Advantage of OBC:** Convenience – the rider can charge at any 230 V 15 A household socket.
**Disadvantage of OBC:** Limited power due to size, weight, and thermal constraints of the vehicle chassis.

**Advantage of Off-board:** Much higher charging power possible (true fast charging).
**Disadvantage of Off-board:** Rider must carry or access the bulky unit; cannot charge opportunistically.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) – Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of an SMPS-based on-board charger for a 60 V, 30 Ah lithium-ion battery pack of an electric scooter. Describe the function of **each block** in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the average DC output voltage expression of a single-phase full-wave controlled bridge rectifier feeding a highly inductive load, and explain how this principle is used to control the charging current in an SCR-based battery charger. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working of SMPS-based OBC:**

The SMPS-based on-board charger consists of the following cascaded stages:

1. **AC Mains Input and EMI Filter:** The 230 V 50 Hz single-phase AC enters an EMI (Electromagnetic Interference) filter containing common-mode chokes and X/Y capacitors. This filter attenuates high-frequency noise both ways: it prevents grid noise from reaching the converter, and prevents the converter's switching noise from polluting the grid (compliance with CISPR 22 / EN 55022 Class B).

2. **PFC (Power Factor Correction) Boost Converter:** A boost converter operating in Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM) shapes the input current to be sinusoidal and in-phase with the input voltage, achieving Power Factor $\geq 0.95$. The output is regulated to a DC link voltage of ~400 V. The PFC stage also acts as a bulk energy buffer.

3. **DC Link Capacitor:** A large electrolytic or film capacitor (typically 100 µF – 470 µF at 450 V) stores the intermediate DC energy. It handles the instantaneous power mismatch between the input (sinusoidal) and output (DC) of the PFC stage.

4. **High-Frequency Inverter (Full-Bridge):** Four MOSFETs or IGBTs switch the DC link voltage into a high-frequency square wave (typically 50 kHz – 200 kHz). Higher frequency allows the use of a smaller, lighter transformer.

5. **High-Frequency Isolation Transformer:** Provides galvanic isolation (mandatory for safety) and steps the voltage down to a level suitable for battery charging. At 100 kHz, the transformer core is 1000× smaller than at 50 Hz.

6. **Secondary Rectifier and Output LC Filter:** A full-bridge diode rectifier (or synchronous MOSFET rectifier for high efficiency) converts the HF AC back to DC. An LC low-pass filter removes switching ripple to produce clean DC.

7. **CC-CV Control Loop:** Current and voltage sensors on the output feed back to a PWM controller (e.g., UCC25640, or a digital DSP). The controller implements the **IU (CC-CV) charging profile** by dynamically adjusting the duty cycle of the inverter switches.

8. **BMS Communication Interface:** A CAN bus or SMBus link to the Battery Management System. The BMS provides real-time cell voltage, pack temperature, SoC, and termination flags. If any cell exceeds 4.25 V or temperature exceeds 60 °C, the BMS commands the charger to terminate.

9. **Auxiliary Supply and Display:** A small flyback converter generates +5 V and +15 V rails for the control electronics. A status LED or LCD indicates charging state (Bulk, Absorption, Full, Fault).

The complete signal flow is: AC mains → filter → PFC → DC link → HF inverter → transformer → rectifier → filter → battery, with control loops closing around the output V and I.

**(b) Derivation of $V_{dc} = \dfrac{V_m \cos \alpha}{\pi}$:**

Consider a single-phase full-bridge SCR rectifier with source $v_s(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$, feeding a battery modeled as EMF $E$ in series with resistance $R$ (highly inductive load approximated as constant current sink).

The SCRs are fired at firing angle $\alpha$. Because the load is highly inductive, conduction continues until the source voltage drops below the back-EMF. The extinction angle is $\beta = \pi - \alpha$.

The average output voltage over a full cycle is:

$$V_{dc} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi - \alpha} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)$$

Step-by-step evaluation:

$$V_{dc} = \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi} \left[ -\cos(\omega t) \right]_{\alpha}^{\pi - \alpha}$$

$$V_{dc} = \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi} \left[ -\cos(\pi - \alpha) + \cos(\alpha) \right]$$

Using $\cos(\pi - \alpha) = -\cos(\alpha)$:

$$V_{dc} = \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi} \left[ \cos(\alpha) + \cos(\alpha) \right] = \dfrac{V_m \cos(\alpha)}{\pi}$$

**Application to SCR-based battery charger:**

In an SCR charger, the firing angle $\alpha$ is varied by a control circuit (analog IC or microcontroller). The output of the rectifier is the charging voltage applied to the battery.

- When $\alpha = 0$ (full conduction), $V_{dc} = V_m / \pi$ – maximum charging voltage.
- As $\alpha$ increases toward $\pi/2$, $V_{dc}$ decreases linearly to 0.
- By increasing $\alpha$, the charger reduces the charging current to implement **constant current mode**.
- By the same mechanism, **constant voltage mode** is implemented by a feedback loop that adjusts $\alpha$ to maintain $V_{dc}$ constant despite battery EMF rising.

The SCR charger is therefore a **phase-controlled, naturally commutated** DC source whose average value is continuously adjustable from 0 to $V_m / \pi$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme - Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the **constant current (CC)**, **constant voltage (CV)**, **pulse**, and **trickle** charging methods. For each method, state the type of battery chemistry it is best suited for and one specific drawback. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 48 V, 24 Ah lead-acid battery pack of an e-rickshaw is to be charged using a constant current of 0.3 C. Calculate: (i) the charging current in amperes, (ii) the theoretical charging time in hours, and (iii) the energy delivered to the battery in kWh. Assume a charging efficiency of 80%. If the charger has an output cable of total length 4 m, copper cross-section 2.5 mm², and the charging current flows for the entire duration, calculate (iv) the power loss in the cable and (v) the total AC energy drawn from the mains (assume charger efficiency 88%). **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison of Charging Methods:**

| **Method** | **Principle** | **Best Suited For** | **Drawback** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Constant Current (CC)** | Fixed current; voltage rises with SoC | Initial bulk of Pb-acid, Ni-Cd, Ni-MH | Overcharges and gasses if not terminated |
| **Constant Voltage (CV)** | Fixed terminal voltage; current tapers | Float charging, Li-ion finishing stage | High initial current can stress cells; needs current limiting |
| **Pulse Charging** | Pulses of current with rest periods | Li-ion longevity, sulfated Pb-acid recovery | More complex control; EMI issues if poorly filtered |
| **Trickle / Float** | Very low current maintained continuously | Long-term storage, standby backup | Chronic overcharge if voltage setpoint is too high |

For e-rickshaws using flooded lead-acid, a **modified IU profile with periodic equalization pulse** is most common because it corrects cell-to-cell capacity mismatches that develop over months of use.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $V_{nom} = 48$ V
- $Q = 24$ Ah
- C-rate = 0.3 C
- $\eta_{ch} = 0.80$ (electrochemical)
- $\eta_{charger} = 0.88$ (electrical)
- $L = 4$ m (positive + return)
- $A = 2.5 \text{ mm}^{2} = 2.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{2}$
- $\rho_{Cu} = 1.724 \times 10^{-8} \, \Omega\cdot$m

**(i) Charging current:**

$$I_{ch} = 0.3 \text{ C} \times 24 \text{ Ah} = 7.2 \text{ A}$$

**(ii) Theoretical charging time:**

$$t_{ch} = \dfrac{Q}{I_{ch} \cdot \eta_{ch}} = \dfrac{24}{7.2 \times 0.80} = \dfrac{24}{5.76} = 4.167 \text{ hours}$$

**(iii) Energy stored in battery:**

$$E_{batt} = V_{nom} \times Q = 48 \times 24 = 1152 \text{ Wh} = 1.152 \text{ kWh}$$

**(iv) Cable power loss:**

Cable resistance:

$$R_{cable} = \dfrac{\rho L}{A} = \dfrac{1.724 \times 10^{-8} \times 4}{2.5 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.0276 \, \Omega$$

Power loss:

$$P_{loss} = I_{ch}^{2} \cdot R_{cable} = (7.2)^{2} \times 0.0276 = 51.84 \times 0.0276 = 1.431 \text{ W}$$

This is small relative to the 345 W total charging power, confirming that the cable is appropriately sized.

**(v) Total AC energy drawn:**

DC energy delivered to battery (after cable loss):

$$E_{DC,out} = V_{nom} \times I_{ch} \times t_{ch} = 48 \times 7.2 \times 4.167 = 1440 \text{ Wh}$$

(Wait — let us correct this with the electrochemical efficiency):

Energy actually input to the charger (DC bus):

$$E_{DC,in} = \dfrac{E_{batt}}{\eta_{ch}} = \dfrac{1152}{0.80} = 1440 \text{ Wh}$$

AC energy from mains:

$$E_{AC} = \dfrac{E_{DC,in}}{\eta_{charger}} = \dfrac{1440}{0.88} = 1636.4 \text{ Wh} \approx 1.636 \text{ kWh}$$

**Summary of Answers:**
1. $I_{ch} = 7.2$ A
2. $t_{ch} = 4.17$ h
3. $E_{batt} = 1.152$ kWh
4. $P_{cable} = 1.43$ W
5. $E_{AC} = 1.636$ kWh

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** A common mistake is to use $Q/I$ directly for charging time without including the charging efficiency $\eta_{ch}$. For lead-acid, omitting $\eta_{ch}$ will give 3.33 h instead of 4.17 h, losing **2 full marks** in Part B. Also, do NOT confuse the electrochemical charging efficiency (energy actually stored in the cell) with the electrical charger efficiency (AC to DC conversion). They are multiplicative: $\eta_{total} = \eta_{ch} \times \eta_{charger}$.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **IU (CC-CV) profile is the universal charging algorithm** for both lead-acid and lithium-ion traction batteries.
- **SMPS-based chargers dominate** the modern e-2W market due to small size, light weight, and high efficiency (>90%).
- **SCR-controlled chargers** are still used in low-cost e-rickshaw applications because they are rugged and cheap, despite lower PF and heavier magnetics.
- **Firing angle $\alpha$** of the SCR controls the average DC output: $V_{dc} = V_m \cos(\alpha) / \pi$.
- **C-rate** definition: $C = I_{charge} / Q_{rated}$. A 0.5 C charge of a 30 Ah pack = 15 A.
- **Lithium-ion voltage limits:** 4.20 V/cell max, 3.00 V/cell min for NMC; NEVER trickle charge at 100% SoC for Li-ion.
- **Lead-acid absorption:** 2.40 – 2.45 V/cell (cycle), 2.25 – 2.30 V/cell (float).
- **PFC is mandatory** for chargers >75 W sold in India/EU to meet IEC 61000-3-2.
- **Bidirectional chargers** (V2G / V2H) are the future of e-2W and will reuse the same hardware with reverse power flow.
- **Bharat DC-001** connector is the standard for low-voltage DC fast charging in India.
- **Charging efficiency** $\eta_{ch}$ for Li-ion $\approx 0.99$, for lead-acid $\approx 0.80$ to $0.85$ — a major source of heat and gas in the latter.
- **Termination** of CC-CV charging happens at $I \leq 0.05 C$ (cutoff current), not at a fixed time.
- **Cell balancing** during absorption stage is critical; otherwise, the weakest cell dictates pack capacity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Charging Port Specifications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT631 - TWO AND THREE WHEELED VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Charging Port Specifications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Charging Port Specifications** for two- and three-wheeled electric vehicles refer to the standardized mechanical, electrical, and communication interfaces through which a Light Electric Vehicle (LEV) receives energy from an external power source (EVSE — Electric Vehicle Supply Equipment). These specifications are governed primarily by **IEC 62196**, **IS 17017**, and **AIS 156** in the Indian regulatory framework, and define connector geometry, pin assignment, voltage/current class, ingress protection (IP) rating, locking mechanism, and pilot signalling protocol.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> Think of a charging port like the **fuel cap of a conventional scooter**. Just as petrol scooters have a specific nozzle size and tank thread, an EV has a precisely shaped "electrical fuel cap" — the charging port. But unlike a simple hole, this port also speaks a digital "language" (via the pilot pin) to tell the charger: *who I am, how much power I can swallow, and when to stop safely.* For a 2W/3W, since the battery is small (typically 1–3 kWh), the port is correspondingly compact and uses low-to-medium power levels.

### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024
> [!IMPORTANT]
> Under the KTU 2024 OBE framework, this topic is mapped to **CO3 / CO4** of PEAUT631. Students are expected to identify and select the correct charging interface for a given electric 2W/3W application — a frequently tested area in Part A (3-mark) and Module 3 questions of the End Semester Examination (ESE).

### 1.4 Key Physical/Standard Metrics
- **Operating voltage range:** 48 V DC, 60 V DC, 72 V DC (typical LEV battery packs).
- **AC charging current:** up to **16 A** at **230 V, 50 Hz** (single phase, India).
- **DC fast charging power:** 1 kW – 15 kW (Bharat DC-001 class for LEVs).
- **Ingress Protection:** **IP 54** (minimum) for outdoor sockets; **IP 67** for the vehicle-side coupler.
- **Operating temperature:** **−10 °C to +55 °C** as per IS 17017.

> [!TIP]
> Memorize the three magic numbers for any Indian 2W EV: **48 V / 60 V / 72 V** — these are the most common nominal battery voltages.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Charging Ports for LEVs

#### A. Based on Power Flow Type
1. **AC Charging Port (Slow / Level 1)**
   - On-board charger inside the vehicle converts AC → DC.
   - Power: **1.5 kW – 3.3 kW**.
   - Connector: **IEC 60320 C13/C14** (2W portable), or **Type 2 (IEC 62196-2)** for fixed installations.
   - Charging time for 1.5 kWh pack ≈ 4–5 hours.

2. **DC Charging Port (Fast / Level 2)**
   - Off-board converter (rectifier) feeds DC directly to the battery.
   - Power: **1 kW – 15 kW** for 2W/3W (per AIS 156 Amendment).
   - Connectors: **Bharat DC-001** (15 A / 48–72 V), **GB/T 20234.3** (DC variant).
   - Charging time: 30 – 90 minutes.

#### B. Based on Standard
| Standard | Region / Body | Typical Use in 2W/3W |
|---|---|---|
| IEC 62196-2 Type 2 | International / Europe | AC charging of premium 2W (e.g., Ather) |
| GB/T 20234.3 | China / Adopted in India | Most Indian 2W EVs (Okinawa, Hero, Bounce) |
| Bharat DC-001 (IS 17017 Part 2 / Sec 5) | India (BIS + DHI) | Low-voltage DC fast charging |
| IS 17855 | India (BIS) | LEV-specific AC/DC couplers |
| NACS (SAE J3400) | North America (now global) | Emerging global standard |

#### C. Based on Form Factor
- **Vehicle-side inlet** (mounted on the body) — the "female" socket.
- **Supply-side connector** (on the cable) — the "male" plug.
- **Coupler** = inlet + connector + cable assembly.

### 2.2 Why and How — Logic Steps

**Step 1 — Mechanical Keying:** Connectors use unique geometries (pin diameters, notches) so that a 48 V LEV plug physically **cannot** be inserted into a 1000 V four-wheeler inlet. This prevents accidental high-voltage exposure.

**Step 2 — Pilot / Proximity Contact (PP):** A short-length pin in the connector that signals "plug is inserted" to the EVSE *before* main current flows. Resistance value indicates cable current rating (commonly 150 Ω or 680 Ω).

**Step 3 — Control Pilot (CP):** A bidirectional communication line following **IEC 61851-1** PWM signalling at **1 kHz, ±12 V**. It negotiates: max current, ventilation requirement, and earthing status.

**Step 4 — Locking Actuator:** Either electromagnetic (12 V solenoid) or mechanical latch — engages when CP confirms safe state. This satisfies AIS 156's anti-theft and anti-arcing requirement.

**Step 5 — Power Transfer:** DC+/DC− or L/N/PE pins deliver energy. The Battery Management System (BMS) closes its contactor only after CP/PP handshake is valid.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility
- **Interoperability:** A common charging port means a single public charger can service Honda Activa-e, TVS iQube, Bajaj Chetak, and Okinawa PraisePro.
- **Safety:** Standardized IP rating and locking prevent electric shock and arcing during rain or vibration.
- **Forensics & Insurance:** Standard ports simplify accident analysis.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **directly usable in KTU numerical problems**.

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Charging power | $P = V \cdot I \cdot \eta$ | kW | $\eta$ = charger efficiency (0.85–0.93) |
| 2 | Charging time | $t = \dfrac{E_{bat}}{P_{ch}}$ | hours | $E_{bat}$ in kWh |
| 3 | Energy delivered | $E = \int V \cdot I \, dt$ | kWh | For CC-CV profile, use $\tfrac{1}{2}(V_{min}+V_{max})\cdot I \cdot t$ approx. |
| 4 | Pilot signal duty cycle | $D = \dfrac{I_{max}}{0.6}$ for $I_{max} \le 51\text{ A}$ | % | Per IEC 61851-1 Annex A |
| 5 | Cable current rating from PP | $I_{rated} = \dfrac{R_{p}}{1000} \cdot 100$ | A | e.g., $R_p = 150\ \Omega \Rightarrow 15\ \text{A}$ |
| 6 | Power loss in cable | $P_{loss} = I^2 R_{cable}$ | W | $R_{cable} = \rho L / A$ |
| 7 | Effective charging SoC time | $t_{SoC\%} = t \cdot \dfrac{\%\text{ SoC}}{100}$ | hours | Linear approximation |

**Boundary Conditions to Remember:**
- $V_{LEV,DC} \in \{48,\ 60,\ 72\}\ \text{V}$ (nominal).
- $V_{LEV,AC} = 230\ \text{V} \pm 10\%$, $f = 50\ \text{Hz} \pm 1\ \text{Hz}$ (India).
- $I_{max,DC001} = 15\ \text{A}$ per phase/mode.
- Connector cycles: **≥ 10 000 mating cycles** as per IS 17017.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Charging Time for a 2W EV (Bharat DC-001)

**Given:**
- Battery energy: $E_{bat} = 1.5\ \text{kWh}$
- DC charger voltage: $V_{ch} = 60\ \text{V}$ (matched to 72 V pack nominal)
- Charger current limit: $I_{ch} = 15\ \text{A}$
- Charger efficiency: $\eta = 0.90$

**Step 1 — Compute the electrical power drawn from the EVSE.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{ch,DC} &= V_{ch} \cdot I_{ch} \\
&= 60\ \text{V} \times 15\ \text{A} \\
&= 900\ \text{W} = 0.9\ \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Account for charger efficiency to get energy actually stored in the battery.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{bat} &= P_{ch,DC} \cdot \eta \\
&= 0.9\ \text{kW} \times 0.90 \\
&= 0.81\ \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute charging time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{E_{bat}}{P_{bat}} \\
&= \frac{1.5\ \text{kWh}}{0.81\ \text{kW}} \\
&= 1.852\ \text{h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to hours and minutes for board exam presentation.**

$$
t = 1\ \text{h} + 0.852 \times 60\ \text{min} = 1\ \text{h}\ 51\ \text{min}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU examiner expects the final answer in **hours–minutes**, not just decimal hours. Always show the conversion.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — AC Charging (Type 2, 230 V / 10 A) for Same 1.5 kWh Pack

**Step 1 — Compute AC apparent power.**

$$
S = V_{AC} \cdot I_{AC} = 230 \times 10 = 2300\ \text{VA}
$$

**Step 2 — Real power assuming PF = 1 (resistive charger front-end).**

$$
P_{AC} = S \cdot \cos\phi = 2300 \times 1.0 = 2300\ \text{W} = 2.3\ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 3 — DC side after on-board charger ($\eta = 0.90$).**

$$
P_{DC} = 2.3 \times 0.90 = 2.07\ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Charging time.**

$$
t_{AC} = \frac{1.5}{2.07} = 0.725\ \text{h} \approx 43.5\ \text{min}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Comparison Insight:** DC-001 is slower than 10 A AC for this pack because of the 15 A × 60 V ceiling. The KTU board loves "compare DC vs AC" numericals — keep this table ready.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pilot Signal Duty Cycle Determination

A LEV communicates its required current via CP PWM. The duty cycle maps to current per IEC 61851-1:

$$
D = \frac{I_{max}}{0.6}\ \% \quad \text{for}\ I_{max} \le 51\ \text{A}
$$

**Worked Example:** If the BMS requests $I_{max} = 12\ \text{A}$:

$$
D = \frac{12}{0.6} = 20\ \%
$$

The EVSE then ensures it never delivers more than 12 A.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Indian 2W/3W Charging Port Compliance

| Parameter | Bharat DC-001 | Type 2 AC | GB/T AC | IS 17855 LEV |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Nominal Voltage | 48 / 60 / 72 V DC | 230 V AC | 230 V AC | ≤ 120 V DC |
| Max Current | 15 A | 16 A (1-ph) | 16 A (1-ph) | 30 A |
| Power | ≤ 1.1 kW | ≤ 3.7 kW | ≤ 3.7 kW | ≤ 3.6 kW |
| IP (mated) | IP 54 | IP 54 | IP 55 | IP 67 |
| Pilot Type | CAN + Analog | PWM (IEC 61851) | CAN | Simplified PWM |
| Locking | Electromagnetic | Solenoid latch | Electronic | Mechanical |
| AIS 156 Compliant | Yes (Amend. 2020) | Yes | Yes | Yes |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students write "Type 2 supports DC fast charging" — this is **FALSE**. Type 2 (IEC 62196-2) is **AC only**. For DC fast charging, you need **CCS2 (Combo 2)** which is *not* used in Indian LEVs.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Charging Port Functional Architecture for a 2W/3W EV

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Grid 230V AC 50Hz] --> B[EVSE Charger Unit]
    B --> C[Supply Side Connector Male]
    C --> D[Vehicle Side Inlet Female IP67]
    D --> E[Proximity Pilot PP Pin]
    D --> F[Control Pilot CP Pin]
    D --> G[DC Plus / DC Minus Pins]
    D --> H[Protective Earth PE Pin]
    E --> I[BMS Pilot Decoder]
    F --> I
    I --> J[Battery Management System]
    G --> J
    H --> K[Chassis Ground]
    J --> L[48V 60V 72V Battery Pack]
    J --> M[Contactor Relay]
    M --> L
    B --> N[CAN or PLC Communication]
    N --> I
    I --> O[SOC Display on Cluster]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Charging Sequence State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> A1: Plug Inserted
    A1 --> A2: PP pin closes
    A2 --> A3: CP PWM handshake start
    A3 --> A4: BMS validates EVSE ID
    A4 --> A5: Cable lock engages
    A5 --> A6: Main contactor closes
    A6 --> A7: CC charge phase constant current
    A7 --> A8: CV charge phase constant voltage
    A8 --> A9: SOC reaches 100 percent
    A9 --> A10: Contactor opens
    A10 --> A11: Cable unlock
    A11 --> [*]: Plug removed
    A3 --> A12: Fault detected
    A12 --> A13: Emergency stop
    A13 --> [*]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block — Pin Layout of Bharat DC-001 Connector (5-Pin LEV)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Pin1 DC Positive]:::pos
    P2[Pin2 DC Negative]:::neg
    P3[Pin3 CAN High]:::comm
    P4[Pin4 CAN Low]:::comm
    P5[Pin5 Protective Earth]:::earth
    P1 --- P2
    P2 --- P3
    P3 --- P4
    P4 --- P5
    classDef pos fill:#ffd166,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef neg fill:#ef476f,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef comm fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef earth fill:#118ab2,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Bharat DC-001 uses a **5-pin configuration** tailored for low-voltage LEV packs, distinct from the 7-pin CCS2 used in four-wheelers. This is a **favourite 7-mark KTU question**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List any three Indian standards that govern the charging ports of electric two- and three-wheelers.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **IS 17017 (Part 2 / Sec 5):** Defines the Bharat DC-001 connector for LEVs.
2. **AIS 156 (Amendment 2020):** Specifies safety, BMS, and connector requirements for L-category EVs.
3. **IS 17855:** Covers AC and DC couplers for light electric vehicles up to 120 V.
*(3 marks — 1 mark each)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is the function of the Proximity Pilot (PP) pin in an EV charging connector?*

**Model Answer:**
The PP pin is a **short-length signal contact** that becomes active only when the connector is fully inserted into the vehicle inlet. It performs two functions:
1. **Wake-up signal** to the BMS that a plug is present.
2. **Cable current rating indicator** through a fixed resistance (e.g., 150 Ω ⇒ 15 A cable).

It allows the system to energize the main contactor *only after* physical insertion is confirmed, preventing arcing. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

> **(a)** Explain in detail the **Bharat DC-001 charging port specification** for electric two- and three-wheelers. Cover the connector geometry, pin allocation, voltage/current limits, IP rating, and communication protocol. **(7 marks)**

**(a) Model Answer Outline:**
- **Standard origin:** Formulated by **BIS (IS 17017 Part 2 Sec 5)** in line with DHI's EV charging roadmap, 2017.
- **Geometry:** 5-pin circular coupler, ~ 60 mm outer diameter, keyway prevents mis-insertion.
- **Pin allocation:**
  - Pin 1 — DC+
  - Pin 2 − DC−
  - Pin 3 — CAN-H
  - Pin 4 — CAN-L
  - Pin 5 — PE (Earth)
- **Electrical limits:** $V_{DC} \in \{48, 60, 72\}\ \text{V}$, $I_{max} = 15\ \text{A}$, $P_{max} = 1.1\ \text{kW}$.
- **IP rating:** IP 54 (unmated) / IP 67 (mated).
- **Communication:** CAN 2.0B at 250 kbps between EVSE and BMS for SOC, faults, and authentication.
- **Locking:** Electromagnetic latch at 12 V, energised only after handshake.

> **(b)** A 72 V, 30 Ah Li-ion battery pack fitted to a delivery e-rickshaw is charged using a Bharat DC-001 charger. Compute the charging time assuming 90 % charger efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**(b) Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Battery energy.**

$$
E_{bat} = V \cdot Ah = 72 \times 30 = 2160\ \text{Wh} = 2.16\ \text{kWh}
$$

**Step 2 — Charger DC power (max possible).**

$$
P_{ch} = 72\ \text{V} \times 15\ \text{A} = 1080\ \text{W} = 1.08\ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Power actually stored in battery.**

$$
P_{bat} = 1.08 \times 0.90 = 0.972\ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Charging time.**

$$
t = \frac{2.16}{0.972} = 2.222\ \text{h} \approx 2\ \text{h}\ 13\ \text{min}
$$

**Final Answer:** $t \approx 2\ \text{h}\ 13\ \text{min}$. *(7 marks — full marks for showing every step and unit conversion.)*

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

> **(a)** Compare **AC (Type 2) charging** and **DC (Bharat DC-001) charging** for electric two-wheelers, with respect to power level, hardware location, connector type, and charging duration. **(7 marks)**

**(a) Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Type 2 AC | Bharat DC-001 DC |
|---|---|---|
| Power level | 1.5 – 3.3 kW | ≤ 1.1 kW |
| Rectifier location | Inside vehicle (OBC) | Inside EVSE |
| Connector | IEC 62196-2 Type 2 | IS 17017-2-5 |
| Communication | PWM pilot (IEC 61851) | CAN 2.0B |
| Charging time (1.5 kWh) | 30–45 min | ~ 1 h 50 min |
| Cost | Higher (OBC) | Lower (no OBC) |
| Weight | Heavier (OBC + cooling) | Lighter |
| Suitability | Premium 2W | Mass-market 2W/3W |

*(7 marks — 1 mark per row + comparative sentence at end.)*

> **(b)** With a neat sketch, describe the **pin layout of a GB/T 20234.3 DC connector** used in Indian 2W EVs. Why is CAN used instead of PWM? **(7 marks)**

**(b) Model Answer:**
- The GB/T 20234.3 DC connector for LEVs uses **7 pins**: DC+, DC−, PE, S+ (CAN-H), S− (CAN-L), CC (connection confirmation), and CC2 (charging confirmation).
- It is locked by an **electronic latch** that draws power from the 12 V auxiliary line of the EV.
- **Why CAN, not PWM?**
  1. CAN allows **bidirectional, multi-frame data**: SOC, temperature, cell voltages, fault codes.
  2. PWM (IEC 61851) only encodes a single variable (current).
  3. Indian LEV BMS standards (AIS 156 Amend. 2020) mandate CAN-based diagnostics.
  4. CAN supports **firmware updates over the same cable** for chargers and BMS.

*(7 marks — 3 for diagram/description, 4 for the "Why CAN" reasoning.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing Type 2 with CCS2** — Type 2 is AC only; CCS2 is AC + DC combo. *Cost: 1–2 marks.*
> 2. **Forgetting unit conversion** from hours to h:min in numericals. *Cost: 0.5 mark.*
> 3. **Ignoring efficiency $\eta$** in the formula $t = E_{bat} / (P \cdot \eta)$. *Cost: 2 marks.*
> 4. **Writing "Tesla connector" or "CHAdeMO"** for a 2W question — these are high-power (> 50 kW) and inapplicable.
> 5. **Not specifying the Indian standard** (IS 17017, AIS 156) when asked — board examiners award 1 mark for the *exact* code.
> 6. **Omitting IP rating** in part (a) 7-mark connector descriptions — IP 67 is **mandatory** for the vehicle inlet.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Three Indian voltage classes** for LEV packs: **48 V, 60 V, 72 V**.
- **Bharat DC-001** → ≤ 1.1 kW, 5-pin, CAN, IP 67, used for mass-market 2W/3W.
- **Type 2 (IEC 62196-2)** → AC only, up to 3.7 kW single phase, used in premium 2W (Ather, TVS iQube).
- **GB/T 20234.3** → Adopted in India from China, 7-pin DC, CAN-based.
- **Pilot signals:** PP (plug-presence + cable rating) and CP (PWM current request per IEC 61851-1).
- **Standard duty cycle formula:** $D = I_{max} / 0.6\ \text{(for } I_{max} \le 51\ \text{A)}$.
- **Key charging time formula:** $t = \dfrac{E_{bat}}{V \cdot I \cdot \eta}$ — always include $\eta$!
- **Locking requirement** is mandatory under AIS 156 to prevent mid-charge disconnection.
- **IP 67** for vehicle inlet, **IP 54** for unmated socket — these are board-favourite one-mark points.
- **Interoperability** is the primary design driver; **safety** (PP/CP handshake + locking + IP rating) is the primary constraint.
- **Memory line for exams:** *"LEV → low voltage → 5/7-pin → CAN or PWM → IP 67 → ≤ 1.1 kW DC / ≤ 3.7 kW AC."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Electric Motors Used in Two and Three-Wheel Vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT631 - TWO AND THREE WHEELED VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of Electric Motors Used in Two and Three-Wheel Vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme** electric mobility syllabus, the **traction motor** of a two- or three-wheeled electric vehicle is defined as a **rotating electromechanical energy conversion device** that transforms **electrical energy drawn from the on-board battery pack** (DC bus) into **mechanical energy at the wheel**, governed by the fundamental Lorentz force principle and Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction.

The **types of electric motors** used in two- and three-wheelers are broadly classified under the following taxonomy, based on the **nature of the power supply**, **presence/absence of permanent magnets**, **rotor construction**, and **commutation mechanism**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Use verbatim in exams):**
> An electric traction motor suitable for two- and three-wheeled EV applications is characterized by **high specific power (W/kg)**, **high starting torque**, **wide constant-power speed range (CPSR)**, **high efficiency in the typical city-driving envelope (1500–6000 rpm)**, **regenerative braking capability**, and **robustness against vibration, dust, and water ingress (IP rating ≥ IP65)**.

## 1.2 The Six Major Motor Families Used in 2W/3W EVs

| Sl. No. | Motor Type | Typical Use Case in 2W/3W |
|:-------:|:-----------|:--------------------------|
| 1 | **Brushed DC Motor (BDC)** | Low-cost entry-level e-scooters (legacy) |
| 2 | **Brushless DC Motor (BLDC)** | **Dominant choice** in 80%+ of modern e-2Ws (Ola, Ather, TVS iQube) |
| 3 | **Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor (PMSM)** | High-performance e-2Ws and e-3W rickshaws |
| 4 | **Three-Phase AC Induction Motor (IM)** | Heavy e-3W load carriers and L5 category autos |
| 5 | **Switched Reluctance Motor (SRM)** | Rugged commercial e-3Ws, mining/industrial 3Ws |
| 6 | **Hub Motor (BLDC/PMSM integrated)** | In-wheel drive for low-floor e-scooters and e-rickshaws |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Pedal-Crank of a Bicycle (Rethought)**

Think of an electric motor as a **smart, electrified version of the pedal-crank system of a cycle**, but with these differences:

- **You (the pedaller) = the Battery.** You supply energy. The battery supplies electrical energy in DC form.
- **The chain = the Power Electronics (Inverter + Controller).** It decides *how much* energy to send and *when* to send it. Like gear-shifting, the controller modulates the PWM (Pulse Width Modulation) signal to control speed.
- **The chainring + crank = the Motor's Stator + Rotor.** The chainring is the **stator** (stationary part that "pushes"), the pedalling foot is the **rotor** (the moving part that "receives the push"). The push (torque) is generated by **magnetic attraction-repulsion** instead of muscle power.
- **The rear wheel = the Driven Wheel of the EV.** Same output concept—rotation delivered to the road.

**The "BLDC vs PMSM" intuition:** Both are like a smart chain-system, but a **BLDC motor is fed with trapezoidal current pulses** (like square-wave stair-steps in your pedalling motion), whereas a **PMSM is fed with smooth sinusoidal current** (like a perfectly circular pedalling motion). The PMSM produces *less noise and vibration*; the BLDC is *simpler and cheaper to control*.

**The "Brushed vs Brushless" intuition:** A brushed motor has **sliding metal contacts (brushes)** that touch the commutator to deliver current—like *running your finger along a rotating spinning wheel* to push it (lots of friction, sparks, wear). A brushless motor *eliminates the finger* and uses an **electronic controller to switch the current** at the right moment, like an *invisible, frictionless finger that never touches*. This is why every modern EV uses brushless.

## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (KTU Board - Must Memorise)

The following performance metrics are **high-yield KTU board keywords**:

- **Specific Power (Power Density):** **2.0 – 6.0 kW/kg** (must be > 1.5 kW/kg for EVs).
- **Peak Efficiency:** **90% – 97%** (BLDC/PMSM), **85% – 92%** (Induction), **75% – 85%** (Brushed DC).
- **Constant Power Speed Range (CPSR):** Typically **3:1 to 5:1** in 2W/3W EVs.
- **Cooling:** Mostly **air-cooled (forced)** in 2W; **liquid-cooled** in high-end 3W autos.
- **IP Rating:** **IP65 minimum** (resistant to jets of water and full dust ingress).
- **Nominal Voltage:** **48 V, 60 V, 72 V** (2W) and **96 V, 120 V** (3W L5 category).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly expects students to **compare BLDC, PMSM, and SRM motors** for 2W/3W applications, and to justify the **dominance of BLDC** in the Indian EV market. Always include **cost, efficiency, controller complexity, and thermal behavior** in any comparison answer.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — The Universal Motor Law

All rotating electric machines used in EVs obey the same **bidirectional electromechanical coupling laws** (valid for both motor and generator modes):

### Law 1 — Motoring Action (Electrical → Mechanical)
A **current-carrying conductor placed in a magnetic field experiences a force** (Lorentz force), producing torque on the rotor.

### Law 2 — Generating Action (Mechanical → Electrical)
A **conductor moving in a magnetic field experiences an induced EMF** (Faraday's law), which opposes the applied voltage (Lenz's law) — this is the **Back-EMF** and the basis of **regenerative braking**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Single Most Important Equation for ALL EV Motors:**
> $$\boxed{P_{mech} \;=\; T \cdot \omega \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}}$$
> where $P_{mech}$ is in Watts, $T$ is torque in N·m, $\omega$ is angular velocity in rad/s, and $N$ is speed in rpm. This is the **bridge between electrical input and mechanical output**, and appears in almost every KTU numerical.

## 2.2 Operating Principle of Each Motor Type (Structured Logic)

### 2.2.1 Brushed DC Motor (BDC)
- **Construction:** Permanent magnet stator + wound armature rotor + mechanical commutator + carbon brushes.
- **Working:** Current enters through brushes → commutator segments → armature windings → interacts with PM field → torque produced → commutator *automatically reverses current* every half-turn to maintain unidirectional torque.
- **Pros:** Simple, cheap, no controller needed (just a potentiometer).
- **Cons:** Brush wear, sparking, low efficiency, **no regenerative braking without complex modifications**.

### 2.2.2 Brushless DC Motor (BLDC)
- **Construction:** **Rotor carries permanent magnets** (usually NdFeB, rare-earth); **stator carries concentrated windings**; **NO brushes, NO commutator** (rotor is on the outside or inside).
- **Working:** An **electronic controller (ESC)** energises stator phases in a *6-step trapezoidal commutation sequence* using **Hall-effect sensors** (or sensorless back-EMF detection) to know rotor position.
- **Pros:** High efficiency, no wear, good torque density, cheap controller ICs.
- **Cons:** Requires controller + sensors; slight torque ripple (due to trapezoidal drive).

### 2.2.3 Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor (PMSM)
- **Construction:** Identical physical structure to BLDC (PM rotor + wound stator) but stator windings are *sinusoidally distributed* and rotor magnet shape is optimised for sinusoidal back-EMF.
- **Working:** Driven by **three-phase sinusoidal currents** (using FOC — Field Oriented Control or Vector Control) to produce a *rotating magnetic field that locks synchronously* with the rotor.
- **Pros:** Highest efficiency, smoothest torque, silent operation, high power density.
- **Cons:** Expensive magnets, complex controller (FOC requires DSP/MCU), demagnetisation risk at high current.

### 2.2.4 Three-Phase Induction Motor (IM / ACIM)
- **Construction:** **No permanent magnets** — rotor is a *squirrel-cage* (aluminium bars in slots); stator has three-phase windings.
- **Working:** Three-phase supply creates a *rotating magnetic field* in stator → induces current in rotor (Faraday's law) → rotor field lags stator field by **slip (s)** → torque produced.
- **Pros:** Rugged, cheap, no magnets (no rare-earth supply risk), natural liquid-cooling friendly.
- **Cons:** Lower power density than PMSM, slip losses, less efficient at part-load.

### 2.2.5 Switched Reluctance Motor (SRM)
- **Construction:** **Doubly salient** — both stator and rotor have protruding poles; rotor is *solid iron with no windings or magnets*; stator has concentrated coils.
- **Working:** Stator phases are energised *sequentially*; rotor always tries to **align with the excited stator pole** (minimum reluctance position) — this produces torque.
- **Pros:** Extremely rugged, highest temperature tolerance, fault-tolerant, lowest material cost.
- **Cons:** Highest torque ripple and acoustic noise, complex controller, requires rotor position sensor.

### 2.2.6 Hub Motor (BLDC/PMSM in-wheel)
- **Construction:** Motor is **integrated into the wheel hub** itself; stator on chassis-side, rotor on wheel-side.
- **Working:** Same electromagnetic principle as BLDC/PMSM, but transmission chain is eliminated.
- **Pros:** No chain/belt, frees frame space, regenerative braking inherent, direct drive.
- **Cons:** Unsprung mass increases (affects ride comfort), limited peak power, water-ingress risk.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) — High-Yield Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MANDATORY KTU Formula Mastery:** Memorise the equations below — they form the **backbone of every KTU numerical problem** on EV motors.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description | Units |
|:-:|:---------|:--------|:------------|:------|
| 1 | **Back-EMF** | $E_b = k_e \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega = k_e \cdot \Phi \cdot \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | EMF opposing applied voltage | V |
| 2 | **Electromagnetic Torque** | $T = k_t \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a$ | Torque developed by motor | N·m |
| 3 | **Armature Voltage Eqn.** | $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ | KVL applied to armature circuit | V |
| 4 | **Speed Equation** | $N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{k_e \Phi}$ | Steady-state rpm | rpm |
| 5 | **Mechanical Output Power** | $P_{mech} = T \cdot \omega = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | Useful shaft power | W |
| 6 | **Electrical Input Power** | $P_{elec} = V \cdot I_a$ | Power drawn from battery | W |
| 7 | **Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{mech}}{P_{elec}} \times 100$ | Energy conversion efficiency | % |
| 8 | **Slip (Induction Motor)** | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | Rotor slip | dimensionless |
| 9 | **Synchronous Speed** | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | IM stator field speed | rpm |
| 10 | **Power Loss in Motor** | $P_{loss} = P_{copper} + P_{iron} + P_{mech} + P_{stray}$ | Total losses | W |
| 11 | **Copper Loss (Stator)** | $P_{cu} = 3 I^2 R_s$ | Per-phase, 3-phase IM/PMSM | W |
| 12 | **Specific Power** | $SP = \dfrac{P_{peak}}{m_{motor}}$ | Power-to-mass ratio | kW/kg |
| 13 | **Regen Braking Energy** | $E_{regen} = \int_0^{t} V \cdot I_{gen} \cdot dt$ | Recovered kWh | Wh |
| 14 | **Torque Constant Equality** | $k_t \;=\; k_e$ (in SI units) | SI-unit equivalence | N·m/A = V·s/rad |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Unit Note:** When using $k_t$ and $k_e$ in SI units, **numerically they are equal** only when $T$ is in N·m, $I$ in A, $E_b$ in V, and $\omega$ in rad/s. **Do not mix rpm with rad/s in the same equation** — always convert $N$ (rpm) to $\omega$ (rad/s) using $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in EV Engineering

The choice of motor type directly influences:

1. **Vehicle Range** (efficiency at part-load determines kWh consumption per km).
2. **Top Speed & Acceleration** (peak power and torque density).
3. **Battery Sizing** (regen efficiency affects usable capacity).
4. **Manufacturing Cost** (BLDC is *sweet spot* for Indian 2W market).
5. **Maintenance Schedule** (BLDC/PMSM/IM/SRM are *maintenance-free*; BDC needs brush replacement every 5,000–10,000 km).
6. **Thermal Management Architecture** (PMSM needs careful demag-temp management; IM is most cooling-friendly).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** The following derivations are the **most-frequently asked 14-mark Part B questions** in Module 3. You are expected to write **every algebraic step with physical interpretation** — skipping a step is treated as a loss of 1–2 marks in valuation.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Back-EMF Equation of a DC/PM Motor

**Starting Physical Principle:** Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction.

> *Whenever a conductor of length $L$ moves with velocity $v$ perpendicular to a magnetic flux density $B$, an EMF $e = B L v$ is induced.*

**Step 1 — Set up the conductor geometry.**
Consider a single armature conductor of active length $L$ (in metres) lying at the surface of a rotor of diameter $D$, rotating at angular velocity $\omega$ (in rad/s) inside a uniform radial magnetic field of flux density $B$ (in Tesla). The peripheral (linear) velocity of the conductor is:

$$
v \;=\; \omega \cdot r \;=\; \omega \cdot \frac{D}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Faraday's law to a single conductor.**
The EMF induced in one conductor is:

$$
e_{cond} \;=\; B \cdot L \cdot v \;=\; B \cdot L \cdot \omega \cdot \frac{D}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Extend to total flux per pole.**
The total flux per pole is $\Phi = B \cdot A_{pole} = B \cdot (\pi D L / P)$, where $P$ is the number of poles. Substituting $B = \dfrac{\Phi \cdot P}{\pi D L}$:

$$
e_{cond} \;=\; \left(\frac{\Phi \cdot P}{\pi D L}\right) \cdot L \cdot \omega \cdot \frac{D}{2} \;=\; \frac{P \, \Phi \, \omega}{2\pi}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by the number of conductors in series (Z).**
For a wave-wound armature, conductors per parallel path $A = 2$, so conductors in series $= Z/A$. The total back-EMF is:

$$
E_b \;=\; \left(\frac{Z}{A}\right) \cdot \frac{P \, \Phi \, \omega}{2\pi} \;=\; \left(\frac{P Z}{2\pi A}\right) \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega
$$

**Step 5 — Define the machine constant $k_e$.**

$$
\boxed{E_b \;=\; k_e \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega, \quad \text{where} \quad k_e \;=\; \frac{P Z}{2\pi A}}
$$

> **Physical interpretation:** The back-EMF $E_b$ is **proportional to speed** $\omega$. As the motor speeds up, $E_b$ rises, opposing the applied voltage $V$ — this is exactly why a no-load motor reaches a *finite* steady-state speed (the "no-load speed") and doesn't accelerate indefinitely.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Electromagnetic Torque Equation

**Starting Principle:** Lorentz Force on a current-carrying conductor: $F = B I L$.

**Step 1 — Force on a single conductor.**
A single armature conductor carrying current $I_{cond}$ in a magnetic field of density $B$ experiences a tangential force:

$$
F_{cond} \;=\; B \cdot I_{cond} \cdot L
$$

**Step 2 — Torque on a single conductor (lever arm = $D/2$).**

$$
T_{cond} \;=\; F_{cond} \cdot \frac{D}{2} \;=\; B \cdot I_{cond} \cdot L \cdot \frac{D}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Total armature current.**
Total armature current $I_a$ is shared between $A$ parallel paths, so $I_{cond} = I_a / A$. Total number of conductors in series-parallel contributing to torque $= Z$ (all conductors produce torque). Therefore:

$$
T_{total} \;=\; Z \cdot B \cdot \left(\frac{I_a}{A}\right) \cdot L \cdot \frac{D}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Express $B$ in terms of flux per pole $\Phi$.**
Using $B = \dfrac{\Phi \cdot P}{\pi D L}$:

$$
T_{total} \;=\; Z \cdot \left(\frac{\Phi P}{\pi D L}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{I_a}{A}\right) \cdot L \cdot \frac{D}{2} \;=\; \left(\frac{P Z}{2\pi A}\right) \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a
$$

**Step 5 — Define torque constant $k_t$.**

$$
\boxed{T \;=\; k_t \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a, \quad \text{where} \quad k_t \;=\; \frac{P Z}{2\pi A} \;=\; k_e \;\; (\text{in SI units})}
$$

> **Physical interpretation:** Torque is **directly proportional to armature current**. This is why EV controllers use *current as the primary control variable* — to control torque, you control current (using a PI current-control loop in the FOC algorithm).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Motor Speed Equation and Torque-Speed Characteristic

**Step 1 — Apply KVL to the armature circuit.**

$$
V \;=\; I_a R_a + E_b
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute back-EMF expression $E_b = k_e \Phi \omega$.**

$$
V \;=\; I_a R_a + k_e \Phi \omega
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for current $I_a$.**

$$
I_a \;=\; \frac{V - k_e \Phi \omega}{R_a}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $I_a$ into the torque equation $T = k_t \Phi I_a$.**

$$
T \;=\; k_t \Phi \cdot \left(\frac{V - k_e \Phi \omega}{R_a}\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange into the canonical torque-speed form.**

$$
T(\omega) \;=\; \underbrace{\left(\frac{k_t \Phi V}{R_a}\right)}_{T_{stall}} \;-\; \underbrace{\left(\frac{k_t k_e \Phi^2}{R_a}\right)}_{k_{slope}} \cdot \omega
$$

**Step 6 — Extract the no-load speed $\omega_0$** (where $T = 0$):

$$
\boxed{\omega_0 \;=\; \frac{V}{k_e \Phi}}
$$

**Step 7 — Extract the stall torque $T_{stall}$** (where $\omega = 0$):

$$
\boxed{T_{stall} \;=\; \frac{k_t \Phi V}{R_a}}
$$

> **Physical interpretation:** The torque-speed curve is a **straight line with negative slope** — this is a *natural* mechanical characteristic. The slope magnitude $k_t k_e \Phi^2 / R_a$ is the *speed regulation coefficient*. For EV applications, this natural curve is **reshaped** by the controller into the desired **constant-torque region (low speed, high torque) and constant-power region (high speed, low torque)** — a critical concept for EV drivetrain matching.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Power, Efficiency, and Loss Relationship

**Step 1 — Total electrical input.**

$$
P_{in} \;=\; V \cdot I_a
$$

**Step 2 — Total losses** (copper + iron + friction + windage + stray):

$$
P_{loss} \;=\; I_a^2 R_a + P_{iron} + P_{fw} + P_{stray}
$$

**Step 3 — Mechanical developed power (air-gap power).**

$$
P_{gap} \;=\; E_b \cdot I_a \;=\; T \cdot \omega
$$

**Step 4 — Shaft (output) power** (after subtracting rotational losses):

$$
P_{out} \;=\; P_{gap} - P_{fw}
$$

**Step 5 — Efficiency.**

$$
\boxed{\eta \;=\; \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 \;=\; \frac{P_{in} - P_{loss}}{P_{in}} \times 100}
$$

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (model exam style):** A 48 V BLDC motor used in an electric scooter has an armature resistance $R_a = 0.2 \, \Omega$, a flux per pole $\Phi = 0.01$ Wb, and a machine constant $k_e = k_t = 50$. The motor draws a current of $I_a = 40$ A at a speed of $N = 3000$ rpm. Calculate: (a) the back-EMF, (b) the developed torque, (c) the mechanical output power, (d) the efficiency.

### Solution:

**Step (a) — Back-EMF.**
Convert rpm to rad/s:

$$
\omega \;=\; \frac{2\pi \times 3000}{60} \;=\; 100\pi \;\approx\; 314.16 \text{ rad/s}
$$

Apply the back-EMF formula:

$$
E_b \;=\; k_e \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega \;=\; 50 \times 0.01 \times 314.16
$$

$$
\boxed{E_b \;\approx\; 157.08 \text{ V}}
$$

> **Note:** Since $E_b > V_{supply} = 48$ V, this operating condition is **impossible** in motoring mode. It would correspond to a *generating* condition (regenerative braking). KTU often tests this awareness — **always check the sign of $V - E_b$** to verify motor vs generator mode.

**Step (b) — Developed Torque.**

$$
T \;=\; k_t \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a \;=\; 50 \times 0.01 \times 40
$$

$$
\boxed{T \;=\; 20 \text{ N·m}}
$$

**Step (c) — Mechanical Output Power.**

$$
P_{mech} \;=\; T \cdot \omega \;=\; 20 \times 314.16
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{mech} \;\approx\; 6283 \text{ W} \;\approx\; 6.28 \text{ kW}}
$$

**Step (d) — Efficiency.**
First, compute the supply current condition that would deliver the required torque at the given voltage. KVL:

$$
V \;=\; I_a R_a + E_b \quad\Rightarrow\quad I_a \;=\; \frac{V - E_b}{R_a} \;=\; \frac{48 - 157.08}{0.2} \;=\; -545.4 \text{ A}
$$

The negative sign indicates *generation*. For a valid **motoring** case, let us re-state the problem: Assume $N = 800$ rpm. Then:

$$
\omega = \frac{2\pi \times 800}{60} = 83.78 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
E_b = 50 \times 0.01 \times 83.78 = 41.89 \text{ V}
$$

$$
I_a = \frac{48 - 41.89}{0.2} = 30.55 \text{ A}
$$

$$
T = 50 \times 0.01 \times 30.55 = 15.28 \text{ N·m}
$$

$$
P_{mech} = T \cdot \omega = 15.28 \times 83.78 = 1280 \text{ W}
$$

$$
P_{in} = V \cdot I_a = 48 \times 30.55 = 1466.4 \text{ W}
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{1280}{1466.4} \times 100 \;\approx\; 87.3\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta \;\approx\; 87.3\%}
$$

> **Take-away for KTU board:** The maximum efficiency for a separately excited DC machine occurs when **variable loss = constant loss**, i.e., $I_a^2 R_a = P_{iron} + P_{fw}$.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix (BLDC vs PMSM vs IM vs SRM)

| Parameter | BLDC | PMSM | Induction (IM) | SRM |
|:----------|:----:|:----:|:--------------:|:---:|
| **Rotor Type** | PM (inside) | PM (surface/inset) | Squirrel cage | Salient iron |
| **Efficiency (Peak)** | 92–95% | 95–97% | 88–92% | 90–93% |
| **Power Density (kW/kg)** | 3.0–4.5 | 4.0–6.0 | 2.0–3.5 | 3.0–4.0 |
| **Torque Ripple** | Moderate (6-step) | Very Low (sinusoidal) | Low | Highest |
| **Controller Complexity** | Low (6-step) | High (FOC/SVPWM) | Medium (V/f) | High |
| **Cost (Motor + Controller)** | Low | High | Medium | Low–Medium |
| **Rare-Earth Dependency** | Yes | Yes | **No** | **No** |
| **Cooling Need** | Forced air | Forced air/Liquid | Liquid preferred | Air sufficient |
| **Fault Tolerance** | Poor | Poor | Good | **Excellent** |
| **Use in Indian 2W/3W** | **Most common** | Premium 2W/3W | E-rickshaw L5 | Niche (commercial 3W) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Mark Distribution Pattern (As per Academic Regulations):**
> - **Part A (Short Answer):** 2 questions × 3 marks = 6 marks (Answer 4 out of 5 typically)
> - **Part B (Long Answer):** Internal choice provided. Each 14-mark question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.
> - The questions below are tagged with **simulated KTU PYQ tags** and follow the **revised Bloom's taxonomy cognitive levels** (L1–L6).

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3, 3 Marks]**
**"List any three reasons for the dominance of BLDC motors in modern Indian electric two-wheelers."**

**Model Answer (3 key points × 1 mark each):**

1. **High efficiency (92–95% peak):** Reduces battery drain and extends range, critical for Indian urban commute patterns where the operating point is mostly in the low-to-mid speed envelope.
2. **Low maintenance:** Absence of brushes means *no wear components*, no commutator sparking — ideal for Indian road conditions (dust, water splashes) with **IP65 sealing**.
3. **Compact, high power density:** With power density of **3–4.5 kW/kg**, BLDC motors fit into the narrow floorboard of a scooter frame (e.g., Ola S1, Ather 450X, TVS iQube) without compromising boot space.

*(Acceptable alternative points: low-cost electronic controllers, easy availability of rare-earth-free ferrite BLDC variants, regenerative braking capability, silent operation, mature Indian EV component ecosystem.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3, 3 Marks]**
**"Distinguish between a BLDC motor and a PMSM in terms of (i) back-EMF waveform, (ii) drive current waveform, and (iii) torque ripple."**

**Model Answer (3 distinctions × 1 mark each):**

| Feature | BLDC | PMSM |
|:--------|:-----|:-----|
| (i) **Back-EMF waveform** | Trapezoidal (≈ flat-topped) | Sinusoidal |
| (ii) **Drive current waveform** | Rectangular / quasi-square 6-step | Sinusoidal (3-phase, 120° displaced) |
| (iii) **Torque ripple** | Moderate (≈ 5–15% due to commutation) | Very low (<2%) due to FOC vector control |

> **Examiner's preferred phrasing:** "BLDC uses trapezoidal commutation whereas PMSM uses sinusoidal commutation, with the latter requiring more complex FOC algorithms but producing smoother torque."

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Internal Choice:** In the KTU 2024 ESE pattern, every Part B question offers a choice between two questions of equal cognitive level. Both Question A and Question B are provided below; in the actual exam, the student answers **one**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**

**"With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working principle of a Brushless DC (BLDC) motor used in electric two-wheelers. Also derive the back-EMF and torque equations, and justify why BLDC motors are preferred over brushed DC motors for EV applications."**

#### **Part (a) — Construction, Working, and Comparison (7 marks)**

**Construction (2 marks):**
A BLDC motor consists of:
- **Rotor:** Permanent magnets (typically **NdFeB — Neodymium Iron Boron**, or ferrite for cost-sensitive variants) mounted on the surface of a steel laminated core. Number of poles typically 4, 6, or 8.
- **Stator:** Three-phase concentrated windings (phases A, B, C) placed in slots of a laminated silicon-steel core. Star-connected (Y-connection) is most common.
- **Sensors:** Three **Hall-effect sensors** placed 120° apart to detect rotor position.
- **Controller:** External **Electronic Speed Controller (ESC)** with MOSFET/IGBT inverter.
- **NO brushes, NO commutator** — this is the defining feature.

**Working (3 marks):**
1. The Hall sensors detect the angular position of the rotor magnets and send a 3-bit digital code (0/1) to the controller.
2. Based on the sensor input, the controller energises **two of the three phases** at any instant using **6-step trapezoidal commutation** (Q1, Q2 ... Q6).
3. The energised phase windings create a magnetic field that **attracts the next permanent magnet pole** of the rotor, causing rotation.
4. Every **60° of rotor rotation**, the controller switches to the next commutation step — total of 6 steps = 360° (one electrical cycle).
5. The rotor synchronously follows the rotating stator field, hence BLDC is essentially a *synchronous motor with DC supply* (via the inverter).

**Comparison with Brushed DC (2 marks):**

| Feature | Brushed DC | BLDC |
|:--------|:-----------|:-----|
| Commutation | Mechanical (brush + commutator) | Electronic (inverter + sensors) |
| Maintenance | High (brush wear every 5000–10000 km) | **Negligible** |
| Efficiency | 75–85% | **92–95%** |
| Heat dissipation | In rotor (poor) | In stator (better cooling) |
| Sparking | Yes (not safe in flammable environments) | **No** |
| Lifespan | 1000–3000 hrs | **10000+ hrs** |

#### **Part (b) — Derivations and Justification (7 marks)**

**Derivation of Back-EMF (3 marks):**
The flux linking the stator phase windings changes as the rotor magnet sweeps past. By Faraday's law:

$$
E_b \;=\; -N \cdot \frac{d\Phi}{dt}
$$

For a trapezoidal waveform, the average EMF over a 120° conduction interval (where $d\Phi/dt$ is approximately constant):

$$
E_b \;=\; k_e \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega \;=\; k_e \cdot \Phi \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60}
$$

where $k_e = \dfrac{P \cdot Z}{2 \pi A}$ is the EMF constant (P = poles, Z = conductors, A = parallel paths).

**Derivation of Torque (2 marks):**
From the Lorentz force law, the torque developed per phase is:

$$
T \;=\; k_t \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a
$$

where $k_t$ is the torque constant (numerically equal to $k_e$ in SI units).

**Justification for EV Preference (2 marks):**
1. **Higher efficiency** → extended range per charge — *the most critical EV metric*.
2. **Maintenance-free** → lower total cost of ownership (TCO) over 5+ year vehicle life.
3. **Regenerative braking** is straightforward — just reverse the current flow during deceleration.
4. **Better thermal management** — stator-mounted windings are easier to cool than rotor windings.
5. **Higher power-to-weight ratio** — enables low-floor, light-weight scooter designs.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** *Hence BLDC is the industry-standard traction motor for 2W/3W EVs in India.*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]**

**"Compare the performance of PMSM, Induction Motor, and Switched Reluctance Motor (SRM) for electric three-wheeler (L5 category) applications. Discuss the operating principle of each, and compute the synchronous speed of a 4-pole, 50 Hz three-phase induction motor suitable for an e-rickshaw."**

#### **Part (a) — Comparative Analysis of Three Motors (7 marks)**

**PMSM (Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor) — 2.5 marks:**

- **Principle:** Three-phase sinusoidal AC supply to the stator creates a rotating magnetic field; the rotor has surface-mounted or interior permanent magnets that *lock* with the rotating field and rotate at *synchronous speed* (no slip).
- **Advantages:** Highest efficiency (95–97%), smoothest torque, compact.
- **Disadvantages:** Expensive (rare-earth magnets), demagnetisation risk at high temperature or over-current, requires complex FOC controller.
- **Suitability for 3W:** Excellent for premium e-rickshaws (e.g., Mahindra Treo, Piaggio Ape E-City) where range and refinement matter.

**Induction Motor (IM) — 2.5 marks:**

- **Principle:** Stator rotating field induces current in the rotor squirrel-cage (Faraday's law); induced rotor current produces a magnetic field that *interacts* with the stator field, producing torque. Rotor always lags the synchronous field by a *slip* (typically 3–8%).
- **Advantages:** Rugged, cheap, no rare-earth magnets, no brushes, easy to liquid-cool, fault-tolerant.
- **Disadvantages:** Lower efficiency (88–92%), slip loss, lower power density, requires V/f or FOC controller.
- **Suitability for 3W:** **Most common** in Indian L5 e-rickshaws (e.g., Mahindra e-Alfa, Kinetic Safar) due to *lowest cost per kW*.

**Switched Reluctance Motor (SRM) — 2 marks:**

- **Principle:** Both stator and rotor are *salient-pole* (protruding teeth). When a stator phase is energised, the rotor *aligns* to minimise magnetic reluctance — this pull creates torque. Rotor has *no magnets, no windings*.
- **Advantages:** Extremely rugged, fault-tolerant (each phase is independent), high temperature tolerance, no rare-earth dependency, very high speed capability.
- **Disadvantages:** High torque ripple, acoustic noise ("whining"), complex controller requiring rotor position sensor.
- **Suitability for 3W:** Niche — used in heavy-duty commercial 3Ws (e.g., mining tippers, garbage loaders) where robustness outweighs NVH concerns.

#### **Part (b) — Synchronous Speed Calculation (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- Number of poles, $P = 4$
- Supply frequency, $f = 50$ Hz

**Step 1 — State the synchronous speed formula:**

$$
N_s \;=\; \frac{120 \cdot f}{P}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$
N_s \;=\; \frac{120 \times 50}{4}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute:**

$$
N_s \;=\; \frac{6000}{4} \;=\; 1500 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step 4 — State the operating speed range:**

For an induction motor driving an e-rickshaw, the rotor speed $N_r$ lags the synchronous speed by slip $s$ (typically 5% at full load):

$$
N_r \;=\; N_s (1 - s) \;=\; 1500 \times (1 - 0.05) \;=\; 1425 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step 5 — Relate to e-rickshaw drivetrain (3 marks):**
- E-rickshaw wheel diameter ≈ 0.6 m → circumference ≈ 1.885 m.
- Wheel speed at $N_r = 1425$ rpm (motor) with gear ratio $G = 1:10$: wheel rpm = 142.5 rpm.
- Vehicle speed $V = \pi \times D \times N_{wheel} / 60 = 3.1416 \times 0.6 \times 142.5 / 60 ≈ 4.48$ m/s ≈ **16.1 km/h** (typical legal limit for L5 e-rickshaw in India: 25 km/h).

**Conclusion (1 mark):** *A 4-pole, 50 Hz induction motor provides a synchronous speed of 1500 rpm, which — through appropriate gear reduction — is ideal for the urban 25 km/h operating envelope of Indian e-rickshaws.*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert rpm to rad/s (or vice versa).** Always state both. Marks lost: 1–2.
> 2. **Mixing up the formula for back-EMF with that for torque.** Memorise $E_b = k_e \Phi \omega$ and $T = k_t \Phi I_a$ as *separate* equations. Marks lost: 2.
> 3. **Writing "no-load speed = 0" or "stall torque = 0"** — these are *defined at the limits* of the torque-speed curve. State them clearly. Marks lost: 1.
> 4. **Confusing BLDC and PMSM** in the commutation type (trapezoidal vs sinusoidal) — this is the most commonly tested distinction. Marks lost: 1–2.
> 5. **Drawing a motor diagram without labels.** Even a simple rectangle labelled "ROTOR" and "STATOR" earns 1 mark; an unlabelled diagram earns 0. Marks lost: 1–2.
> 6. **Not writing units** for $E_b$, $T$, $N$, $\omega$ — KTU deducts 0.5 mark per missing unit.
> 7. **In SRM/IM questions, forgetting to define "slip" or "reluctance"** — these are foundational terms and must be defined before use. Marks lost: 1.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final high-density summary — read this 5 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

- 🔹 **The 6 motor families for 2W/3W EVs are:** BDC, BLDC, PMSM, IM, SRM, and Hub Motor.
- 🔹 **BLDC is the dominant motor in modern Indian e-2Ws** due to high efficiency, low cost, and maintenance-free operation.
- 🔹 **Three universal motor equations** (master these):
   - $E_b = k_e \Phi \omega$
   - $T = k_t \Phi I_a$ (with $k_t = k_e$ in SI units)
   - $V = I_a R_a + E_b$
- 🔹 **Torque is proportional to current; speed is proportional to voltage** — this is the foundation of EV motor control.
- 🔹 **The torque-speed curve is a straight line** with intercept $T_{stall} = k_t \Phi V / R_a$ and zero-crossing at $\omega_0 = V / (k_e \Phi)$.
- 🔹 **Mechanical output power** is always $P = T \omega = 2\pi N T / 60$.
- 🔹 **BLDC = trapezoidal EMF and current**; **PMSM = sinusoidal EMF and current + FOC**.
- 🔹 **Induction motor**: rotor speed lags synchronous speed by slip $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$.
- 🔹 **Synchronous speed formula**: $N_s = 120 f / P$ — e.g., 4-pole 50 Hz → 1500 rpm.
- 🔹 **SRM** has no magnets and no rotor windings — it works on the *principle of variable reluctance* (rotor aligns to minimise reluctance).
- 🔹 **Hub motor** integrates the motor into the wheel — saves transmission chain but increases unsprung mass.
- 🔹 **EV motor key specs:** Specific power > 1.5 kW/kg, efficiency > 90%, IP rating ≥ IP65, CPSR 3:1 to 5:1.
- 🔹 **Regenerative braking** works in *all* brushless motors (BLDC, PMSM, IM) by allowing the back-EMF to exceed supply voltage and reverse current flow into the battery.
- 🔹 **Common Indian EV examples** (cite in answers for marks): Ola S1 (BLDC), Ather 450X (PMSM), TVS iQube (BLDC), Mahindra Treo (IM), Kinetic Safar (IM).
- 🔹 **Most common 14-mark question pattern:** Compare two motors OR derive + numerical OR principle + sketch + application.
- 🔹 **Always include a labelled diagram** — even a small one earns 1–2 free marks.
- 🔹 **Always state units** for every numerical answer; KTU deducts for missing units.
- 🔹 **Always interpret results physically** — a numerical answer followed by a one-line physical interpretation earns full marks.

---

> **END OF MODULE 3 NOTES — TYPES OF ELECTRIC MOTORS USED IN 2W/3W EVs**

# ⚙️ Motor Controllers — Section 1: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Motor Controller (KTU 2024 Definition):** A Motor Controller is an electronic device or embedded system that regulates the power delivered from the energy source (battery pack or generator) to the traction motor (BLDC, PMSM, DC, or AC induction) of a two or three-wheeled vehicle. It interprets driver input (throttle, brake, mode selector), processes sensor feedback (current, voltage, temperature, rotor position), and precisely modulates the switching of power semiconductor devices (MOSFETs, IGBTs) using Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) or Space Vector Modulation (SVM) techniques to deliver the commanded torque and speed.

In conventional Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) two-wheelers, an analogous device called the **Engine Control Unit (ECU)** performs fuel injection timing, ignition advance, and idle-speed control. In modern Electric Two-Wheelers (E2W) and Electric Three-Wheelers (E-Rickshaws), the **Motor Controller** is the single most critical powertrain component, often referred to as the *"brain of the electric drivetrain."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Orchestra Conductor

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine an orchestra:** The musicians (BLDC motor, battery, sensors) are highly capable individually, but without a conductor (the Motor Controller), they would play out of sync and produce chaos.

The Motor Controller acts exactly like a **Maestro**:
- It listens to the **lead violinist** (the throttle input from the rider's right-hand grip).
- It cues the **string section** (motor phases) on *when* and *how loudly* to play (PWM duty cycle).
- It constantly monitors the **tempo** (rotor speed via Hall sensors) and prevents the brass (battery) from being over-driven.
- During a crescendo-stop (release of throttle), it signals the **percussion** (regenerative braking circuit) to softly recapture kinetic energy back into the battery.

## 1.3 Classification of Motor Controllers in 2W/3W Vehicles

| Controller Type | Typical Application | Power Range | Switching Device |
|---|---|---|---|
| **BLDC Motor Controller** | Electric scooters (Ola, Ather, TVS iQube) | **250 W – 11 kW** | MOSFET (low V) / IGBT (high V) |
| **PMSM Sine-Wave Controller** | High-performance e-motorcycles (Ultraviolette F77) | **10 – 40 kW** | SiC MOSFET / IGBT |
| **Brushed DC Controller** | Low-cost e-rickshaws, e-bicycles | **250 W – 1.5 kW** | Power MOSFET |
| **SRM Controller** | Research-grade 2Ws (rare) | **Variable** | IGBT with asymmetric bridge |
| **ECU (FI Controller)** | ICE motorcycles (KTM, R15, Classic 350) | **Sensor-based logic** | Microcontroller + driver ICs |

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standards

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **PWM Carrier Frequency (f_pwm):** Typically **8 kHz – 25 kHz** (above audible range to eliminate whining noise).
> - **Battery Nominal Voltages in Indian 2W/3W EVs:** **48 V, 60 V, 72 V** (lead-acid); **51.8 V, 57.6 V, 72 V** (Li-ion NMC/LFP).
> - **IP Rating of Controller Housing:** Minimum **IP65** for Indian road conditions (dust + rain).
> - **Thermal Cut-off Threshold:** Power module junction temperature typically **125 °C – 150 °C**.

## 1.5 Why Motor Controllers Are Critical for KTU Examinations

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly tests the **functional block diagram**, **PWM working principle**, and **types of motor controllers** because they are the heart of the rapidly growing Indian electric two-wheeler market (sales crossed **1 million units in 2023**).
