# 🔬 Motor Controllers — Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Architecture of a Modern BLDC Motor Controller

A typical BLDC controller for an electric two-wheeler is composed of the following hierarchical blocks:

### 2.1.1 Input Signal Conditioning Layer
- **Throttle Input:** A Hall-effect throttle grip outputs **0.8 V – 4.2 V** (corresponding to 0% – 100% throttle). The controller's ADC samples this at **≥ 1 kHz**.
- **Hall Sensor Inputs (H1, H2, H3):** Rotor position feedback from 3 Hall sensors placed 120° apart inside the motor.
- **Brake Levers:** Two-channel brake cut-off switches (left and right) for safety interlocks.
- **Mode Selector Switch:** Eco / Normal / Sport modes change the throttle map.

### 2.1.2 Microcontroller / DSP Layer
- Runs a **Field-Oriented Control (FOC)** or **Trapezoidal commutation** algorithm.
- Executes at a loop frequency of typically **10 kHz – 20 kHz**.
- Computes the required duty cycle $D(t)$ for each PWM channel in real time.

### 2.1.3 Gate Driver Layer
- Boosts the **3.3 V** logic signal from the MCU to **10–15 V** required to drive MOSFET gates.
- Provides galvanic isolation via **bootstrap circuits** or **optocouplers**.

### 2.1.4 Power Stage (3-Phase Inverter)
- Six switching devices arranged in a **three-phase bridge** (3 high-side + 3 low-side).
- For **48 V systems**, N-channel Power MOSFETs (e.g., IRF4110, IPT007N06N) are common.
- For **> 96 V systems**, IGBTs (e.g., STGB10NC60HD) are preferred.

### 2.1.5 Protection & Feedback Layer
- **Current sensing** via shunt resistor or Hall-effect current sensor (e.g., ACS712).
- **Voltage sensing** across DC bus.
- **Temperature sensing** via NTC thermistor mounted on the heatsink.

## 2.2 Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) — The Heart of Motor Control

### 2.2.1 Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PWM** is a modulation technique in which the width of rectangular pulses is varied in proportion to the amplitude of the desired average output signal, while keeping the switching frequency constant.

### 2.2.2 Mathematical Principle

The **average output voltage** delivered to a motor phase is governed by the duty cycle:

$$V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{DC}$$

Where:
- $V_{DC}$ = Battery/DC bus voltage (in Volts)
- $D$ = Duty cycle = $\dfrac{t_{on}}{T_s}$
- $t_{on}$ = ON-time of the switching device per cycle
- $T_s$ = Switching period = $\dfrac{1}{f_{PWM}}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** By varying $D$ from 0 to 1, the controller can smoothly command any average motor voltage from 0 V up to $V_{DC}$ without using bulky rheostats, achieving efficiency often **> 92%**.

## 2.3 Commutation Logic for a 3-Phase BLDC Motor

A BLDC motor has 6 electrical states per mechanical revolution (for a single pole-pair rotor). The controller switches the phases in a sequence driven by Hall sensor feedback:

| Step | Hall (H1, H2, H3) | Active High-Side | Active Low-Side |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 101 | Phase A | Phase B |
| 2 | 100 | Phase A | Phase C |
| 3 | 110 | Phase B | Phase C |
| 4 | 010 | Phase B | Phase A |
| 5 | 011 | Phase C | Phase A |
| 6 | 001 | Phase C | Phase B |

Each step corresponds to a **60° electrical sector**. Commutation frequency at motor speed $N$ (in RPM) and pole pairs $P$ is:

$$f_{comm} = \dfrac{N \cdot P}{60}$$

## 2.4 The ECU in ICE Two-Wheelers (Contrast with EV Controller)

In a fuel-injected ICE motorcycle (e.g., KTM Duke 390, Yamaha R15 V4), the **Engine Control Unit** is the analog of the motor controller. It performs:

1. **Fuel Injection Control:** Computes injector pulse width using the speed-density method.
2. **Ignition Timing:** Advances/retards spark based on RPM, MAP, and knock sensor.
3. **Idle Air Control:** Maintains stable idle RPM via a stepper motor bypass valve.
4. **OBD-II Diagnostics:** Reports fault codes (DTCs) for emissions compliance.

### Key Difference Summary

| Feature | ICE ECU | EV Motor Controller |
|---|---|---|
| Output Actuator | Injector + Ignition Coil | 3-Phase Inverter (MOSFET/IGBT) |
| Throttle Mapping | Air-fuel ratio lookup table | Torque lookup table |
| Efficiency Range | 25–38% (engine) | 85–95% (motor + drive) |
| Emissions Concern | Catalytic converter needed | Zero tailpipe emissions |
| Response Time | ~ 50 ms | < 1 ms (electrical) |

## 2.5 ⚡ KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This formula sheet contains every equation you must memorize for ESE and module tests.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{DC}$ | Average DC voltage from PWM | Volts |
| 2 | $D = \dfrac{t_{on}}{T_s}$ | Definition of duty cycle | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $T_s = \dfrac{1}{f_{PWM}}$ | Switching period | Seconds |
| 4 | $f_{comm} = \dfrac{N \cdot P}{60}$ | BLDC commutation frequency | Hz |
| 5 | $P_{out} = T_{motor} \cdot \omega_{m}$ | Mechanical output power | Watts |
| 6 | $\omega_{m} = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | Angular velocity from RPM | rad/s |
| 7 | $E_b = K_e \cdot \omega_m$ | Back-EMF constant relation | Volts |
| 8 | $V_{DC} = E_b + I_a \cdot R_a$ | Armature voltage equation | Volts |
| 9 | $T_{motor} = K_t \cdot I_a$ | Motor torque from armature current | N·m |
| 10 | $V_{L-L,rms} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot M \cdot V_{DC}$ | Line-to-line RMS voltage (Sine PWM) | Volts |
| 11 | $\eta_{drive} = \dfrac{P_{out,mech}}{P_{electrical,in}}$ | Drive system efficiency | % |
| 12 | $E_{regen} = \dfrac{1}{2} J \cdot (\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2)$ | Energy recovered in regen braking | Joules |
| 13 | $V_{LL} = \dfrac{V_{DC}}{\sqrt{3}}$ | DC bus utilization in SVM (max) | Volts |
| 14 | $f_{PWM,opt} = \dfrac{V_{DC}}{8 \cdot L_s \cdot \Delta I}$ | Optimal PWM frequency for current ripple | Hz |

## 2.6 Why This Matters in Real Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> The choice of motor controller directly determines an electric two-wheeler's **range**, **gradeability**, **top speed**, and **battery longevity**. Companies like **Ather Energy**, **Ola Electric**, and **TVS Motor** design their own proprietary FOC-based controllers in-house to gain competitive advantage in the Indian market.

# 📐 Motor Controllers — Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Average Output Voltage from a PWM Signal

We will rigorously derive the average voltage seen by the motor phase from a PWM waveform applied at the gate of the high-side switch.

### Given:
- DC bus voltage = $V_{DC}$ (Volts)
- Switching period = $T_s$ (seconds)
- ON-time of switch = $t_{on}$ (seconds)

### Step 1 — Define the Duty Cycle
By definition, the duty cycle is the fraction of the period during which the switch is ON:

$$D = \dfrac{t_{on}}{T_s}$$

### Step 2 — Express the OFF-time
Since the switch is OFF for the remaining fraction of the period:

$$t_{off} = T_s - t_{on} = T_s (1 - D)$$

### Step 3 — Write the Instantaneous Output Voltage
During $t_{on}$, the output equals $V_{DC}$. During $t_{off}$, the output is 0 V. Therefore:

$$v_o(t) = \begin{cases} V_{DC}, & 0 \leq t < t_{on} \\ 0, & t_{on} \leq t < T_s \end{cases}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Average Over One Cycle
The average value of a periodic waveform is the integral over one period, divided by the period:

$$V_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{T_s} \int_{0}^{T_s} v_o(t) \, dt$$

### Step 5 — Substitute the Piecewise Function
Split the integral into the ON portion and the OFF portion:

$$V_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{T_s} \left[ \int_{0}^{t_{on}} V_{DC} \, dt + \int_{t_{on}}^{T_s} 0 \, dt \right]$$

### Step 6 — Evaluate the Integrals
The first integral evaluates to $V_{DC} \cdot t_{on}$; the second is zero:

$$V_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{T_s} \cdot V_{DC} \cdot t_{on}$$

### Step 7 — Substitute the Definition of D

$$\boxed{V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{DC} = \dfrac{t_{on}}{T_s} \cdot V_{DC}}$$

This confirms the central PWM equation used throughout the motor controller.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Motor Output Power and Torque Relationship

### Given:
- Motor speed = $N$ (RPM)
- Motor torque = $T_{motor}$ (N·m)

### Step 1 — Convert RPM to Angular Velocity
One revolution corresponds to $2\pi$ radians, and one minute has 60 seconds:

$$\omega_m = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} \quad \left[\dfrac{\text{rad}}{\text{s}}\right]$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Definition of Mechanical Power
Mechanical power is torque multiplied by angular velocity:

$$P_{out} = T_{motor} \cdot \omega_m$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Angular Velocity in RPM Form

$$P_{out} = T_{motor} \cdot \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Example
> A typical electric scooter (e.g., Ola S1 Pro) has a peak motor torque of **58 N·m** at a peak RPM of **5400 RPM**.

$$P_{out} = 58 \times \dfrac{2\pi \times 5400}{60}$$

$$P_{out} = 58 \times \dfrac{2 \times 3.14159 \times 5400}{60}$$

$$P_{out} = 58 \times \dfrac{33,929.2}{60} = 58 \times 565.49$$

$$\boxed{P_{out} \approx 32{,}798 \text{ W} \approx 32.8 \text{ kW}}$$

This matches the manufacturer's claimed peak power of ~ 33 kW, validating the formula.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Commutation Frequency vs. Pole Pairs

### Given:
- Motor speed = $N$ RPM
- Number of pole pairs = $P$

### Step 1 — Mechanical Revolutions per Second
Convert RPM to revolutions per second:

$$n = \dfrac{N}{60} \quad \text{[rev/s]}$$

### Step 2 — Electrical Cycles per Mechanical Revolution
Each pole pair produces one electrical cycle per mechanical revolution:

$$f_{electrical} = n \cdot P$$

### Step 3 — Final Formula

$$\boxed{f_{comm} = \dfrac{N \cdot P}{60}}$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Example
> A hub motor of an e-rickshaw has $N = 600$ RPM and $P = 23$ pole pairs.

$$f_{comm} = \dfrac{600 \times 23}{60} = \dfrac{13{,}800}{60} = 230 \text{ Hz}$$

This 230 Hz is well within the bandwidth of typical Si-MOSFET controllers (up to several kHz).

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Optimal PWM Frequency for Current Ripple Control

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Current ripple** causes motor heating and torque pulsations. The choice of $f_{PWM}$ is a design trade-off.

### Given:
- DC bus voltage = $V_{DC}$
- Phase inductance = $L_s$ (Henries)
- Allowable peak-to-peak current ripple = $\Delta I$ (Amperes)

### Step 1 — Voltage Across Inductance During ON-time
By the inductor equation $v_L = L_s \cdot \dfrac{di}{dt}$:

$$\dfrac{\Delta I}{\Delta t} = \dfrac{V_{DC}}{L_s}$$

### Step 2 — Solve for the ON-time
Since the ripple must build up during half the switching cycle (in unipolar switching):

$$t_{on,max} = \dfrac{L_s \cdot \Delta I}{V_{DC}}$$

### Step 3 — Set This Equal to T_s/2
For symmetric ripple, the minimum period is twice this time:

$$T_s = \dfrac{2 \cdot L_s \cdot \Delta I}{V_{DC}}$$

### Step 4 — Invert to Get Frequency

$$\boxed{f_{PWM,opt} = \dfrac{V_{DC}}{2 \cdot L_s \cdot \Delta I}}$$

> Note: A more conservative version uses an 8 instead of a 2 in the denominator (for 2-level unipolar with both halves considered), giving the textbook form $f_{PWM,opt} = \dfrac{V_{DC}}{8 L_s \Delta I}$.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common Numerical Patterns for KTU Problems

| Problem Pattern | Input Data Needed | Formula to Apply | Expected Answer Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Average motor voltage from PWM | $V_{DC}, D$ | $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{DC}$ | 0 – $V_{DC}$ V |
| Peak motor power | $T_{motor}, N$ | $P_{out} = T \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60}$ | Hundreds of W to tens of kW |
| Commutation freq. | $N, P$ | $f_{comm} = \frac{NP}{60}$ | 50 – 1000 Hz typical |
| Optimal PWM freq. | $V_{DC}, L_s, \Delta I$ | $f_{PWM,opt} = \frac{V_{DC}}{8 L_s \Delta I}$ | 8 – 25 kHz typical |
| DC bus utilization | Modulation index $M$ | $V_{L-L,rms} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{2}} M V_{DC}$ | up to 0.907 $V_{DC}$ |
| Energy recovered | Inertia $J$, $\omega_1, \omega_2$ | $E = \frac{1}{2} J(\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2)$ | Hundreds of kJ |

## 4.3 PWM Generation Logic Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TB
    A[READ THROTTLE ADC VALUE] --> B[MAP TO TORQUE REQUEST]
    B --> C[READ HALL SENSOR POSITION]
    C --> D[COMPUTE TARGET PWM DUTY CYCLE]
    D --> E[APPLY CURRENT LIMIT FROM LOOKUP TABLE]
    E --> F[UPDATE TIMER COMPARE REGISTER]
    F --> G[TIMER HARDWARE GENERATES PWM ON 3 PHASES]
    G --> H[READ ACTUAL PHASE CURRENT VIA SHUNT]
    H --> I{CURRENT LESS THAN LIMIT}
    I -->|YES| J[CONTINUE NORMAL OPERATION]
    I -->|NO| K[REDUCE DUTY CYCLE TO LIMIT CURRENT]
    K --> F
    J --> L[CHECK TEMPERATURE SENSOR]
    L --> M{TEMP LESS THAN 100 DEG C}

    M -->|NO| N[DERATE OUTPUT OR SHUTDOWN WITH FAULT CODE]
```

```mermaid

    S4 --> S5[STATE 5 PHASE C HIGH PHASE A LOW]
    S5 --> S6[STATE 6 PHASE C HIGH PHASE B LOW]
    S6 --> S1
    H1[HALL SENSOR CHANGE DETECTED] --> ROT[60 DEGREE ELECTRICAL STEP]
    ROT --> S1
```

# 📝 Motor Controllers — Section 5: KTU 2024 Examination Question Bank & Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Define a motor controller and list any **four** essential functions it performs in an electric two-wheeler.

**Model Answer:**
> A **motor controller** is an electronic circuit that regulates the power flow from the battery to the traction motor of an electric two-wheeler by controlling the switching of power semiconductor devices.
>
> The four essential functions are:
> 1. **Throttle interpretation** — converting the rider's throttle input into a torque request.
> 2. **Commutation** — sequencing the 3-phase inverter to produce rotating magnetic field.
> 3. **Current limiting** — protecting the motor and battery from over-current damage.
> 4. **Regenerative braking** — converting motor action to generator action during deceleration to feed energy back into the battery.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024]** What is **PWM** and why is a PWM frequency of at least **8 kHz** preferred in BLDC motor controllers for two-wheelers?

**Model Answer:**
> **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)** is a switching technique where the width of a rectangular pulse is varied to control the average voltage delivered to the motor.
>
> A PWM frequency of **≥ 8 kHz** is preferred because:
> 1. It is **above the audible range** for humans (≤ 20 kHz), but specifically above the most sensitive band (2–5 kHz) — eliminating the high-pitched *whining noise* that is annoying to riders and pedestrians.
> 2. It provides **finer resolution** of the duty cycle, leading to smoother torque output.
> 3. The current ripple in the motor windings becomes **negligibly small**, reducing copper losses and acoustic noise.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Module question of 14 marks has **internal choice**. You must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B. Each question has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q]** With the help of a functional block diagram, explain the operation of a **BLDC motor controller** used in modern electric two-wheelers. Also, derive the expression for the average output voltage.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Block Diagram and Working

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Introduction:**
The BLDC motor controller is the central electronic module in an electric two-wheeler. It acts as an interface between the **battery**, the **rider inputs**, and the **BLDC hub motor**.

**Step 2 — List the Functional Blocks:**
1. **Throttle Input** — Hall-effect twist grip producing 0.8 V to 4.2 V.
2. **Signal Conditioning and ADC** — Filters and converts analog signals to digital.
3. **Microcontroller / DSP** — Runs the FOC or trapezoidal algorithm.
4. **Gate Driver Stage** — Boosts 3.3 V logic to 10–15 V MOSFET drive.
5. **Three-Phase Inverter** — Six MOSFETs/IGBTs arranged as 3 half-bridges.
6. **Current, Voltage, and Temperature Sensors** — Provide closed-loop feedback.
7. **Protection Circuitry** — Over-current, over-voltage, over-temperature cut-off.

**Step 3 — Working Sequence:**
- When the rider twists the throttle, the ADC samples the throttle voltage.
- The MCU maps the throttle position to a torque demand using a lookup table.
- Based on Hall sensor feedback (H1, H2, H3), the MCU determines the **rotor electrical angle**.
- The MCU computes the appropriate **PWM duty cycle** for each of the 3 high-side and 3 low-side switches.
- The 3-phase inverter applies the PWM voltage to the appropriate motor phases, producing a rotating magnetic field that drives the rotor.
- Current and temperature sensors continuously monitor the system; if any parameter exceeds its safe limit, the controller reduces duty cycle or shuts down.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Average Voltage Derivation

**Model Solution:**

Given:
- Switching period = $T_s$
- ON-time = $t_{on}$
- DC bus voltage = $V_{DC}$

**Derivation:**

$$\text{Step 1: } D = \dfrac{t_{on}}{T_s}$$

$$\text{Step 2: } t_{off} = T_s - t_{on} = T_s(1 - D)$$

$$\text{Step 3: } V_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{T_s} \int_{0}^{T_s} v_o(t) dt$$

$$\text{Step 4: } V_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{T_s} \left[ V_{DC} \cdot t_{on} + 0 \cdot t_{off} \right]$$

$$\text{Step 5: } V_{avg} = \dfrac{V_{DC} \cdot t_{on}}{T_s}$$

$$\boxed{\therefore V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{DC}}$$

**Interpretation:** When $D = 0$, the motor receives 0 V (idle). When $D = 1$, it receives full $V_{DC}$. By continuously varying $D$, the controller delivers any desired average voltage smoothly and efficiently.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Q]** Compare the **motor controller of an electric two-wheeler (BLDC type)** with the **ECU of an ICE motorcycle**. Discuss the role of PWM in both systems.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Comparison

**Model Solution (in tabular form):**

| Parameter | BLDC Motor Controller (EV) | ECU (ICE Motorcycle) |
|---|---|---|
| **Function** | Controls motor speed and torque via PWM | Controls fuel injection and ignition timing |
| **Output Stage** | 3-phase MOSFET/IGBT inverter | Injector + ignition coil driver |
| **Efficiency** | 85–95% | Inherent engine efficiency 25–38% |
| **Response Time** | < 1 ms | ~ 50 ms |
| **Throttle Interface** | Direct electronic (ride-by-wire) | Often direct cable + TPS sensor |
| **Emissions** | Zero tailpipe emissions | Tailpipe CO, HC, NOx (needs catalytic converter) |
| **Major Inputs** | Throttle, Hall sensors, current, voltage | MAP, CKP, IAT, O2 sensor |
| **Cooling Required** | Heatsink + sometimes liquid | Engine self-cooling |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Role of PWM

**Model Solution:**

**In the EV BLDC Controller:**
PWM is used to **vary the average DC bus voltage** applied to the motor phases via the 3-phase inverter. By controlling the duty cycle $D$, the controller regulates the motor's **torque** smoothly. Higher duty cycle → higher torque.

**In the ICE ECU:**
PWM is used at the **injector driver** stage. The injector solenoid is pulsed open for a specific pulse width (in milliseconds) per engine cycle. The **longer the pulse width, the more fuel** is injected. Also, the **idle air control valve** uses PWM to meter bypass air.

**Common Advantages of PWM in Both:**
1. **Low power dissipation** in the switching device (ON-state or OFF-state, never linear).
2. **Precise digital control** of analog quantities.
3. **High efficiency** (> 90%) compared to linear regulation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall:**
> Students often confuse the role of PWM in EV controllers (motor voltage control) with the role of PWM in ICE ECUs (injector pulse width). Mentioning this distinction explicitly will earn you **2 bonus marks** and demonstrate conceptual clarity. Also, students frequently forget to include the **regenerative braking function** when listing motor controller functions — this is a guaranteed **1-mark deduction** in most valuations.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this on the morning of your exam.**

- ✅ **Motor Controller** = *Brain* of EV powertrain; regulates battery-to-motor power flow.
- ✅ **ECU** = analog for ICE motorcycles; controls fuel + ignition.
- ✅ **PWM Average Voltage Formula:** $\;V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{DC}\;$ — must be memorized verbatim.
- ✅ **PWM Frequency in 2W EVs:** 8–25 kHz (above audible range).
- ✅ **Six-step Trapezoidal Commutation:** Driven by 3 Hall sensors 120° apart.
- ✅ **Commutation Frequency Formula:** $\;f_{comm} = \dfrac{N \cdot P}{60}$.
- ✅ **Power Formula:** $\;P_{out} = T_{motor} \cdot \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$.
- ✅ **Regenerative braking** turns the motor into a generator during deceleration.
- ✅ **Three-phase inverter** uses 6 switches (3 high + 3 low side) — this is a favorite KTU diagram question.
- ✅ **IP65** is the minimum ingress protection for controller housings in Indian conditions.
- ✅ **FOC (Field-Oriented Control)** = advanced algorithm used in premium e-scooters (Ather, Ola).
- ✅ **DC bus utilization** in Sine PWM = max **90.7%** of $V_{DC}$; in Space Vector Modulation = up to **100%**.
- ✅ Always draw a **labeled block diagram** for any 7-mark question — it alone fetches **3–4 marks**.
- ✅ Memorize the difference between **average voltage, RMS voltage, and peak voltage** in PWM.
- ✅ **Thermal protection** at **125 °C** junction is the industry standard for power MOSFETs.
- ✅ **Current sensing** is done via shunt resistor (cheap) or Hall-effect sensor (isolated).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Routine Maintenance and Servicing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT631 - TWO AND THREE WHEELED VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Routine Maintenance and Servicing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Routine Maintenance and Servicing** of two and three-wheeled vehicles refers to the systematic, scheduled, and preventive set of mechanical, electrical, and lubrication operations performed at predetermined intervals — measured in **kilometers (km)**, **operating hours**, or **calendar time (days/months)** — to ensure the vehicle remains in safe, reliable, emission-compliant, and operationally efficient condition throughout its design service life.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this encompasses:
- **Preventive Maintenance (PM):** Scheduled interventions before failure occurs.
- **Predictive Maintenance:** Condition-based servicing using wear indicators and diagnostic readings.
- **Breakdown Maintenance:** Corrective action after a fault manifests.
- **Running Maintenance:** Daily/weekly rider-level checks (oil level, tyre pressure, chain slack).
- **Periodic Maintenance:** Manufacturer-stipulated service intervals (e.g., every **3000 km**, **6000 km**, **12000 km**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For two and three-wheeled vehicles, routine maintenance is not a single action — it is a **multi-tiered hierarchical system** ranging from rider-performed daily checks to authorized service-center overhauls.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of routine maintenance like **human health check-ups**:
- **Daily brushing & bathing** = Daily checks (oil level, tyre pressure, light operation).
- **Visiting a dentist every 6 months** = Periodic service (oil change, filter cleaning).
- **Annual full-body check-up** = Major service (valve clearance, brake shoe replacement).
- **Emergency room visit** = Breakdown maintenance.

Just as neglecting health check-ups leads to costly diseases, skipping scheduled services in a motorcycle or auto-rickshaw accelerates wear of the **engine, transmission, brakes**, and **electrical systems**, drastically shortening vehicle life and increasing emissions.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

- **Service Interval:** Typically **3000 km – 5000 km** for two-wheelers and **5000 km – 10000 km** for three-wheelers.
- **Engine Oil Grade:** **SAE 10W-30 / 20W-40 / 20W-50** (JASO MA / JASO MB specification).
- **Tyre Pressure (Solo):** Front **25 psi (1.75 kg/cm²)**, Rear **28 psi (2.0 kg/cm²)** for typical commuter motorcycles.
- **Battery Electrolyte Specific Gravity:** **1.26 – 1.28 g/cm³** at 27 °C (fully charged lead-acid).
- **Spark Plug Gap:** **0.6 – 0.7 mm** for standard 4-stroke motorcycles.
- **Valve Clearance (Cold):** Intake **0.05 – 0.10 mm**, Exhaust **0.10 – 0.15 mm**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — JASO MA / JASO MB:** JASO (Japanese Automotive Standards Organization) ratings classify motorcycle oils. **JASO MA** has higher friction modifiers (suited for wet clutches); **JASO MB** is fuel-economy optimized (slippery, used in scooters with centrifugal clutches).

### 1.4 Why Routine Maintenance Matters in KTU Context

The KTU 2024 scheme expects graduates entering the automotive industry to understand that **70% of vehicle failures** in the two-wheeler segment are attributable to **neglected preventive maintenance**. This module directly supports Course Outcomes (COs) related to vehicle subsystems, reliability engineering, and workshop practice.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Hierarchical Maintenance Structure (KTU Standard)

Routine maintenance follows a strict **3-tier hierarchy**:

#### Tier 1 — Running Maintenance (Rider-Level)
Performed before each ride or weekly:
- Engine oil level check (dipstick / sight glass).
- Tyre pressure and tread depth inspection.
- Brake lever free-play and brake fluid level.
- Chain slack and lubrication (chain-drive motorcycles).
- Headlight, tail-light, indicator, and horn operation.
- Fuel cock / petcock operation and fuel level.
- Mirror, horn, and control cable free-play.

#### Tier 2 — Periodic / Schedule Maintenance (Service Center)
Performed at manufacturer intervals (commonly **Free Service, Paid Service 1, 2, 3, …**):
- Engine oil and oil filter replacement.
- Air filter cleaning / replacement.
- Spark plug inspection and gap reset.
- Valve clearance (tappet) adjustment.
- Carburetor / EFI throttle body cleaning.
- Brake shoe / pad wear measurement and replacement.
- Clutch cable free-play adjustment.
- Throttle cable free-play adjustment.
- Drive chain tensioning, alignment, and lubrication.
- Battery electrolyte top-up and terminal cleaning.
- General lubrication of pivots, hinges, and cables.

#### Tier 3 — Major Overhaul Maintenance
Performed at **20000 – 40000 km** or every **2 – 4 years**:
- Engine decarbonization (carbon deposit removal from combustion chamber, piston crown, exhaust port).
- Cylinder honing or rebore (if wear exceeds limit).
- Piston ring replacement.
- Bearing and bushing replacement.
- Suspension link bush replacement.
- Wiring harness inspection.
- Complete brake system overhaul (master cylinder, caliper, wheel cylinder).

### 2.2 Engine Servicing Subsystem (Deep Dive)

#### 2.2.1 Lubrication System
The **splash + pressurized wet-sump system** is dominant in two-wheelers.

**Oil Change Interval Formula:**
$$
\text{Oil Life (km)} = \frac{\text{Sump Capacity (L)} \times \text{Oil Degradation Factor}}{\text{Burn Rate (L/1000 km)}}
$$

Where Oil Degradation Factor accounts for:
- Thermal breakdown above **120 °C** oil temperature.
- Fuel dilution (> **2%** by volume degrades viscosity).
- Particulate contamination (silicon, carbon, iron from wear).

**Recommended Drain Interval:**
$$
\text{Drain Interval (km)} = \frac{\text{Oil Capacity (mL)}}{\text{Consumption Rate (mL/1000 km)}}
$$

#### 2.2.2 Air Intake System
- **Foam-type** air filter (most scooters): Clean with **non-flammable solvent**, oil with **SAE 30** filter oil.
- **Paper pleated** air filter (most motorcycles): Replace every **6000 – 10000 km**, never wash with water/oil.

**Clogged Air Filter Effect:**
$$
\text{Air-Fuel Ratio (λ)} \downarrow \Rightarrow \text{Mixture Richens} \Rightarrow \text{CO Emissions} \uparrow, \text{BSFC} \uparrow
$$

#### 2.2.3 Fuel System
- **Carburetor:** Every **4000 – 6000 km** — disassemble, clean jets with **compressed air (max 2 bar)**, inspect float, needle, and throttle valve.
- **EFI (Electronic Fuel Injection):** No scheduled cleaning, but **injector ultrasonic cleaning** every **30000 km**.

#### 2.2.4 Ignition System
- **Spark Plug Reading (Color Code):**
  - **Light tan / grey** = Ideal combustion.
  - **Black / sooty** = Rich mixture, clogged air filter, weak ignition.
  - **White / blistered** = Lean mixture, overheating, possible burnt valve.
  - **Oily / wet** = Oil fouling, worn piston rings, valve guide failure.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Students must remember the spark plug color diagnostic chart — it appears in nearly every KTU university exam under Module 4.

### 2.3 Transmission & Drivetrain Maintenance

| Subsystem | Inspection Item | Tolerance / Spec | Frequency |
|-----------|----------------|------------------|-----------|
| Clutch cable | Free play at lever end | **10 – 20 mm** | Every 3000 km |
| Drive chain | Slack (mid-span, unloaded) | **20 – 30 mm** | Every 1000 km |
| Drive chain | Lubrication | Chain lube spray/wax | Every 500 km |
| Sprocket | Tooth wear (hook shape) | Replace if hooked | Every 10000 km |
| CVT belt (scooter) | Crack, width reduction | Replace if < **22 mm** | Every 20000 km |
| Gear shift linkage | Smooth engagement | No notch feel | Every 6000 km |

### 2.4 Brake System Maintenance

**Disc Brake Pad Wear Limit:**
$$
\text{Pad Remaining Thickness} \geq 1.5 \text{ mm} \quad (\text{Minimum safety})
$$

**Drum Brake Shoe Wear Limit:**
$$
\text{Lining Thickness} \geq 1.5 \text{ mm} \quad \text{above rivet head}
$$

**Brake Fluid Specification:**
- **DOT 3 / DOT 4** (Glycol-based) — change every **2 years** (hygroscopic — absorbs moisture).
- **Boiling Point (DOT 4, dry):** **> 230 °C**, (wet): **> 155 °C**.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Spec |
|---|-------------------|-----------|--------------|
| 1 | Oil drain interval | $D = \dfrac{C}{r}$ where $C$ = capacity, $r$ = burn rate | km |
| 2 | Tyre wear rate | $W = \dfrac{T_t - T_m}{D}$ | mm / 1000 km |
| 3 | Brake pad service life | $L_p = \dfrac{T_p - T_{\min}}{W_p}$ | km |
| 4 | Chain slack tolerance | $S = \dfrac{L}{50} \approx 20{-}30 \text{ mm}$ | mm |
| 5 | Battery state of charge | $\text{SOC} = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{measured}} - 1.10}{1.28 - 1.10} \times 100\%$ | % |
| 6 | Valve clearance (cold) | IN: 0.05–0.10 mm; EX: 0.10–0.15 mm | mm |
| 7 | Spark plug gap | $g = 0.6{-}0.7 \text{ mm}$ | mm |
| 8 | Specific fuel consumption | $\text{BSFC} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{P_b}$ | g/kWh |
| 9 | Air-fuel ratio (stoichiometric) | $\lambda = 1.0 \Rightarrow \text{AFR} = 14.7{:}1$ | ratio |
| 10 | Service cost per km | $C_{km} = \dfrac{C_{\text{parts}} + C_{\text{labor}}}{D_{\text{interval}}}$ | ₹/km |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the **Indian automotive aftermarket** (estimated at **₹1.5+ lakh crore industry**), routine maintenance forms the **economic backbone** of:
- **Authorized service centers** (Hero, Honda, Bajaj, TVS, Royal Enfield).
- **Independent garages (roadside mechanics)**.
- **OEM warranty claims processing** (failure to follow schedule voids warranty).

Understanding this topic enables graduates to work as **service engineers, workshop supervisors, OEM technical trainers, and dealership managers**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Engine Oil Drain Interval

**Problem Statement:** A commuter motorcycle has an engine oil sump capacity of **1000 mL**. Field data shows an average oil consumption of **50 mL per 1000 km**. Determine the recommended oil drain interval.

**Given:**
- $C = 1000 \text{ mL}$ (sump capacity)
- $r = 50 \text{ mL/1000 km}$ (consumption rate)
- Recommended oil level minimum: **30% of capacity** before topping up is mandatory.

**Step 1 — Identify limiting constraint.**

The oil must not drop below 30% of capacity. Hence **usable oil volume**:
$$
V_{\text{usable}} = 0.70 \times C = 0.70 \times 1000 = 700 \text{ mL}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply drain interval formula.**
$$
D = \dfrac{V_{\text{usable}}}{r}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute.**
$$
D = \dfrac{700 \text{ mL}}{50 \text{ mL/1000 km}}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute.**
$$
D = 14 \times 1000 \text{ km} = 14000 \text{ km}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply safety factor (SF = 0.7) for oxidation degradation.**
$$
D_{\text{recommended}} = D \times \text{SF} = 14000 \times 0.7 = 9800 \text{ km}
$$

**Conclusion:** Manufacturer typically rounds to **10000 km**, but conservative fleets use **7000 km** for severe Indian operating conditions (dust, heat, stop-go traffic).

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Battery State of Charge

**Problem:** A 12 V motorcycle lead-acid battery shows electrolyte specific gravity of **1.21** at 27 °C. Determine state of charge.

**Step 1 — Reference values.**
- Fully charged: $\rho_{\text{full}} = 1.28 \text{ g/cm}^3$
- Fully discharged: $\rho_{\text{empty}} = 1.10 \text{ g/cm}^3$

**Step 2 — Apply SOC formula.**
$$
\text{SOC} = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{measured}} - \rho_{\text{empty}}}{\rho_{\text{full}} - \rho_{\text{empty}}} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values.**
$$
\text{SOC} = \dfrac{1.21 - 1.10}{1.28 - 1.10} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 4 — Compute numerator and denominator.**
$$
\text{Numerator} = 0.11, \quad \text{Denominator} = 0.18
$$

**Step 5 — Final calculation.**
$$
\text{SOC} = \dfrac{0.11}{0.18} \times 100\% = 61.11\%
$$

**Step 6 — Temperature correction (if electrolyte temperature differs from 27 °C):**
$$
\rho_{27} = \rho_T + 0.0007 \times (T - 27)
$$

**Conclusion:** Battery is at **~61% state of charge**, indicating it requires **immediate charging** before further use.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Brake Pad Service Life Estimation

**Problem:** A front disc brake pad has initial thickness **8.0 mm**, minimum safe thickness **1.5 mm**, and wears at **0.6 mm per 5000 km**. Find service life.

**Step 1 — Wearable thickness.**
$$
\Delta T = T_{\text{initial}} - T_{\text{min}} = 8.0 - 1.5 = 6.5 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply wear rate formula.**
$$
L_p = \dfrac{\Delta T}{W_p}
$$

where $W_p = 0.6 \text{ mm / 5000 km} = 0.12 \text{ mm / 1000 km}$.

**Step 3 — Substitute.**
$$
L_p = \dfrac{6.5 \text{ mm}}{0.12 \text{ mm / 1000 km}}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute.**
$$
L_p = 54.17 \times 1000 \text{ km} = 54167 \text{ km}
$$

**Conclusion:** Brake pads will reach wear-out limit at approximately **54000 km** under standard conditions.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Maintenance Schedule for Typical 150cc Motorcycle

| Service | Interval (km) | Engine Oil | Oil Filter | Air Filter | Spark Plug | Valve Clearance | Chain | Brake | Battery |
|---------|--------------|-----------|------------|------------|------------|----------------|-------|-------|---------|
| Free Service 1 | 500 – 750 | Check | — | — | Inspect | — | Lube | Check | Check |
| Paid Service 1 | 3000 – 4000 | Replace | Replace | Clean | Inspect | Inspect | Adjust | Check | Top-up |
| Paid Service 2 | 6000 – 8000 | Replace | Replace | Replace | Replace | Adjust | Adjust | Inspect | Inspect |
| Paid Service 3 | 12000 | Replace | Replace | Replace | Replace | Adjust | Replace if worn | Replace pads if < 1.5 mm | Inspect |
| Major Service | 24000 – 30000 | Replace | Replace | Replace | Replace | Inspect | Replace sprockets | Overhaul | Test |

### 3.5 Troubleshooting Flow Matrix (KTU Frequently Tested)

| Symptom | Probable Cause | Maintenance Action |
|---------|---------------|--------------------|
| Hard starting | Fouled spark plug, weak ignition, clogged fuel line | Clean/replace plug, check ignition coil, flush carburetor |
| Excessive smoke (white) | Coolant leak / water entry | Check head gasket |
| Excessive smoke (blue) | Oil burning — worn rings/valve seals | Top-end overhaul |
| Excessive smoke (black) | Rich mixture — clogged air filter | Replace air filter |
| Poor mileage | Incorrect valve clearance, dragging brake, clogged filter | Adjust valves, free brake, replace filter |
| Brake fade | Overheated fluid, worn pads | Bleed brakes, replace fluid, replace pads |
| Chain noise | Insufficient lubrication, wrong tension | Lube, adjust to **20–30 mm** slack |
| Battery overcharge | Faulty voltage regulator / rectifier | Test regulator (output **13.5–14.5 V**) |
| Steering wobble | Loose headstock bearings, worn tyre, unbalanced wheel | Tighten stem, replace bearings, balance wheel |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Maintenance Tier Hierarchy (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Routine Maintenance System] --> B[Tier 1 - Running Maintenance]
    A --> C[Tier 2 - Periodic Maintenance]
    A --> D[Tier 3 - Major Overhaul Maintenance]

    B --> B1[Daily Oil Level Check]
    B --> B2[Tyre Pressure Check]
    B --> B3[Light and Horn Test]
    B --> B4[Brake Lever Test]
    B --> B5[Chain Visual Check]

    C --> C1[Engine Oil Change]
    C --> C2[Air Filter Cleaning]
    C --> C3[Spark Plug Inspection]
    C --> C4[Valve Clearance Adjust]
    C --> C5[Brake Pad Measurement]
    C --> C6[Battery Electrolyte Check]
    C --> C7[Carburetor Cleaning]

    D --> D1[Engine Decarbonization]
    D --> D2[Cylinder Rebore]
    D --> D3[Piston Ring Replacement]
    D --> D4[Brake System Overhaul]
    D --> D5[Suspension Bushing Replace]
    D --> D6[Wiring Harness Audit]
```

### 4.2 Engine Servicing Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Vehicle Arrives at Service] --> S2{Reading Odometer}
    S2 -->|0 to 3000 km| S3[Running Checks Only]
    S2 -->|3000 to 6000 km| S4[Standard Periodic Service]
    S2 -->|6000 to 12000 km| S5[Extended Periodic Service]
    S2 -->|Above 12000 km| S6[Major Service Required]

    S3 --> R1[Oil Top-up, Visual Inspection]
    S4 --> R2[Oil and Filter Change, Plug Check]
    S5 --> R3[Oil, Filter, Air Filter, Plug Replace]
    S6 --> R6[Full Inspection, Decarbonization, Wear Part Replace]

    R1 --> END1[Release Vehicle]
    R2 --> END2[Release Vehicle]
    R3 --> END3[Release Vehicle]
    R6 --> END4[Release after Test Ride]
```

### 4.3 Spark Plug Color Diagnostic Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Spark Plug Condition] --> A1[Light Tan - Normal]
    SP --> A2[Black Sooty - Rich Mix]
    SP --> A3[White Blistered - Lean Mix]
    SP --> A4[Oily Wet - Oil Fouling]

    A2 --> F1[Replace Air Filter]
    A2 --> F2[Check Carburetor Float]
    A2 --> F3[Inspect Choke Operation]

    A3 --> F4[Clean Injector Jets]
    A3 --> F5[Check for Vacuum Leak]
    A3 --> F6[Inspect Intake Manifold Gasket]

    A4 --> F7[Compression Test]
    A4 --> F8[Check Valve Stem Seals]
    A4 --> F9[Inspect Piston Rings]
```

### 4.4 Brake Maintenance Subsystem Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BM[Brake System Service] --> T1{Disc Brake or Drum Brake}
    T1 -->|Disc| DB[Inspect Pad Thickness]
    T1 -->|Drum| DM[Inspect Shoe Lining]

    DB --> DB1{Pad Thickness Greater than 1.5 mm}
    DB1 -->|Yes| DB2[Reuse Pad - Clean Caliper]
    DB1 -->|No| DB3[Replace Pad Pair]
    DB2 --> DB4[Check Disc Runout - Max 0.10 mm]
    DB4 --> DB5[Bleed Brake Lines]
    DB3 --> DB5
    DB5 --> DB6[Refill DOT 4 Fluid - Test Braking]

    DM --> DM1{Lining Above Rivet Head by 1.5 mm}
    DM1 -->|Yes| DM2[Adjust Star Adjuster]
    DM1 -->|No| DM3[Replace Shoe Pair]
    DM2 --> DM4[Test at Low Speed]
    DM3 --> DM4
```

### 4.5 Block Architecture — Workshop Service Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    J1[Job Card Open - Record Odometer] --> J2[Customer Complaint Note]
    J2 --> J3[Visual Inspection by Mechanic]
    J3 --> J4{Any Fault Identified}
    J4 -->|No| J5[Proceed with Routine Schedule]
    J4 -->|Yes| J6[Estimate Repair Cost]
    J6 --> J7{Customer Approval}
    J7 -->|No| J8[Close Job Card]
    J7 -->|Yes| J9[Perform Repair and Routine Service]
    J5 --> J9
    J9 --> J10[Quality Inspection by Supervisor]
    J10 --> J11[Test Ride]
    J11 --> J12[Vehicle Wash and Delivery]
    J12 --> J13[Update Service Record and Close Job Card]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**List any six daily/weekly running maintenance checks to be performed by a two-wheeler rider before starting a journey.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Engine oil level** — check via dipstick or sight glass; top-up if below lower mark.
2. **Tyre pressure** — front typically **25 psi**, rear **28 psi** (solo, check owner's manual).
3. **Brake operation** — front and rear lever/pedal feel; ensure no sponginess or excessive travel.
4. **Chain slack and lubrication** — **20–30 mm** mid-span vertical play; lubricate if dry.
5. **Lights and horn** — headlamp high/low beam, tail lamp, indicators, brake light, horn function.
6. **Fuel level** — ensure adequate fuel for journey; check for leaks around petcock.
7. **Mirror and control cable free-play** — mirrors adjusted; clutch and throttle cables within **10–20 mm** free-play.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Explain the meaning of JASO MA and JASO MB oil specifications. Why is JASO MA preferred for motorcycles with wet multi-plate clutches?**

**Model Answer:**

- **JASO MA (Japanese Automotive Standards Organization — MA):** Higher friction modifier content; maintains **higher static and dynamic friction** in clutch plates to prevent slippage during power transmission. Suitable for motorcycles with **wet multi-plate clutches** where engine oil also lubricates the clutch.
- **JASO MB:** Lower friction; optimized for **fuel economy**, used in scooters with **centrifugal automatic clutches** where clutch slip is not a concern.

**Reason JASO MA is preferred for motorcycles:** Wet clutch plates rely on oil friction to engage and transmit torque. JASO MB's friction-reducing additives cause **clutch slippage**, leading to **power loss, overheating, and premature clutch plate wear**. Hence, manufacturer-recommended grade for most Indian motorcycles is **JASO MA / API SL or higher**.

---

### PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat flowchart, explain the **three-tier hierarchy of routine maintenance** of a two-wheeler. List any **five checks** done under running maintenance.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Describe the **complete periodic service procedure** at **6000 km** for a 150cc commuter motorcycle, including engine oil change, air filter replacement, spark plug service, valve clearance adjustment, and chain maintenance. Mention specifications and tolerances.

---

#### MODEL ANSWER FOR QUESTION A

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

Routine maintenance is classified into three tiers based on frequency, complexity, and who performs it:

**Tier 1 — Running Maintenance (Rider-Level):**
Performed before every ride or weekly. No tools required typically. Quick visual and functional checks.

**Five key checks:**
1. Engine oil level via dipstick/sight glass.
2. Tyre pressure (gauge check) and visual tread inspection.
3. Brake lever/pedal travel and feel.
4. Chain tension and lubrication.
5. Light, horn, and indicator operation.

**Tier 2 — Periodic Maintenance (Service Center):**
Performed at manufacturer-stipulated intervals (**3000 km, 6000 km, 12000 km**). Includes oil/filter change, plug check, valve clearance, brake inspection, battery service.

**Tier 3 — Major Overhaul Maintenance:**
Performed at **20000–40000 km** or after major component failure. Includes decarbonization, piston-ring replacement, suspension overhaul, complete brake system service.

*Refer to Mermaid diagram in SECTION 4.1 for visualization.*

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

At the **6000 km paid service**, the following operations are performed:

**1. Engine Oil and Oil Filter Replacement:**
- Drain oil while warm.
- Replace oil filter (if cartridge-type) or clean (if mesh-type).
- Refill with manufacturer-grade oil, e.g., **SAE 10W-30 JASO MA**.
- Capacity typically **1000 mL**.

**2. Air Filter Replacement:**
- Remove air filter cover.
- Inspect paper element; replace (do not wash).
- Reinstall cover ensuring airtight seal.

**3. Spark Plug Service:**
- Remove plug using **plug spanner**.
- Inspect electrode color (refer diagnostic chart in SECTION 4.3).
- Clean with wire brush; set gap to **0.6–0.7 mm** using feeler gauge.
- Apply anti-seize on threads; torque to **12–15 Nm**.

**4. Valve Clearance Adjustment:**
- Remove valve cover; rotate crankshaft to TDC on compression stroke.
- Measure clearance using feeler gauge.
- Specification (cold): Intake **0.05–0.10 mm**, Exhaust **0.10–0.15 mm**.
- Adjust via locknut and screw on rocker arm.

**5. Drive Chain Maintenance:**
- Check slack at mid-span; adjust to **20–30 mm** vertical play.
- Clean with kerosene/diesel; dry; apply chain lube evenly.
- Check sprocket teeth for hooking; replace if hooked.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with examples the **troubleshooting procedure** for an engine that shows **(i) excessive black smoke, (ii) blue smoke, and (iii) white smoke**. Also mention the spark plug color interpretation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **complete brake system maintenance procedure** for a motorcycle with **front disc brake and rear drum brake**, including pad/shoe inspection, fluid bleed, and specifications.

---

#### MODEL ANSWER FOR QUESTION B

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

| Symptom | Probable Cause | Maintenance Action |
|---------|---------------|--------------------|
| Black smoke | Rich air-fuel mixture | Replace clogged air filter, check choke operation, inspect carburetor float level, verify fuel pump pressure in EFI |
| Blue smoke | Engine oil burning | Check piston ring wear (compression test), inspect valve stem seals, verify breather system |
| White smoke | Coolant leaking into combustion chamber | Pressure-test cooling system, replace head gasket, inspect cylinder head for cracks |

**Spark Plug Color Interpretation:**
- **Light tan/grey** — Normal combustion.
- **Black/sooty** — Rich mixture, weak ignition.
- **White/blistered** — Lean mixture, overheating.
- **Oily/wet** — Oil fouling, worn rings, valve guide failure.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Front Disc Brake Service:**
1. Inspect brake pad thickness through caliper window. Replace if **< 1.5 mm**.
2. Check disc runout with DTI gauge; max **0.10 mm**. Resurface or replace if exceeded.
3. Inspect caliper piston seals for leakage; clean with brake cleaner.
4. Bleed brake lines starting from farthest caliper to master cylinder using DOT 4 fluid.
5. Refill master cylinder reservoir to MAX line.
6. Test brake lever for firm feel — should not be spongy.

**Rear Drum Brake Service:**
1. Remove rear wheel; take off brake panel.
2. Measure shoe lining thickness; replace if **< 1.5 mm** above rivet head.
3. Clean brake drum with sandpaper (not solvent — avoid asbestos dust inhalation in older vehicles).
4. Inspect return springs; replace if stretched or fatigued.
5. Reassemble, adjust star-wheel nut until slight drag, then back off 1–2 clicks.
6. Test brake at low speed.

**Specifications Summary:**
- DOT 4 brake fluid — boiling point (dry) **> 230 °C**.
- Brake fluid replacement interval — every **2 years**.
- Disc thickness minimum — embossed on disc (commonly **3.0 mm** for 150cc class).
- Drum inner diameter max — embossed on drum (commonly **130 mm**).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing JASO MA and JASO MB** — students often claim JASO MB is for motorcycles; it is actually for scooters/automatic CVT vehicles.
> 2. **Wrong brake pad minimum thickness** — many answer **2.0 mm**; correct is **1.5 mm**.
> 3. **Tyre pressure values** — students state **30/35 psi**; correct for most Indian commuter motorcycles is **25/28 psi** (refer owner's manual).
> 4. **Spark plug gap** — frequently written as **1.0 mm**; correct range is **0.6–0.7 mm**.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention JASO/SAE/API grade** when listing oil specifications — costs 1 mark.
> 6. **Skipping safety factor in oil drain interval calculation** — examiner expects a final answer in a **round figure** with the safety factor considered.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Routine Maintenance and Servicing**

- ✅ Three-tier hierarchy: **Running → Periodic → Overhaul**.
- ✅ Service intervals: **3000 km / 6000 km / 12000 km** for motorcycles.
- ✅ Engine oil spec: **SAE 10W-30 / 20W-40, JASO MA**, capacity **~1000 mL** for 150cc class.
- ✅ Tyre pressure: Front **25 psi**, Rear **28 psi** (solo) — **never exceed** manufacturer's max.
- ✅ Valve clearance (cold): Intake **0.05–0.10 mm**, Exhaust **0.10–0.15 mm**.
- ✅ Spark plug gap: **0.6–0.7 mm**; tan color = healthy.
- ✅ Chain slack: **20–30 mm** mid-span; lubricate every **500 km**.
- ✅ Brake pad minimum: **1.5 mm**; fluid **DOT 4**; change every **2 years**.
- ✅ Battery SOC: $\text{SOC} = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{measured}} - 1.10}{0.18} \times 100\%$.
- ✅ Smoke diagnosis: **Black = rich, Blue = oil, White = coolant**.
- ✅ Air filter — **paper type = replace only**, **foam type = clean and oil**.
- ✅ Carburetor cleaning interval: **4000–6000 km**; do not overtighten jets.
- ✅ Major service interval: **20000–40000 km** — decarbonization, rebore if needed.
- ✅ Always **refer to manufacturer's service manual** for exact specs (KTU values).
- ✅ Neglect of schedule **voids OEM warranty** — important industry-relevant fact.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Common Troubleshooting and Repair Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT631 - TWO AND THREE WHEELED VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Common Troubleshooting and Repair Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Troubleshooting** in the context of two and three-wheeled vehicles refers to the **systematic diagnostic process** of identifying, isolating, and rectifying faults in mechanical, electrical, fuel, and chassis subsystems using logical reasoning, sensory observations, measured parameters, and standardized service procedures.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, troubleshooting encompasses:
- **Symptom Recognition** – identifying abnormal vehicle behaviour
- **Fault Isolation** – narrowing down to the root cause
- **Root Cause Analysis (RCA)** – confirming the actual defective component
- **Rectification** – applying appropriate repair or replacement procedures
- **Verification** – confirming restoration to specified performance

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Vehicle as a Human Body**
>
> Think of a motorcycle or scooter exactly like a human body:
> - The **engine** is the **heart** (pumps energy)
> - The **fuel system** is the **digestive system** (converts raw input into usable energy)
> - The **electrical system** is the **nervous system** (carries signals and powers functions)
> - The **brakes** are the **muscles** (provide controlled motion)
> - The **suspension** is the **skeletal frame** (absorbs shocks)
>
> When a doctor treats a patient, they don't randomly prescribe medicine. They ask *"Where does it hurt?"* (symptom), then perform tests (diagnosis), identify the disease (fault), and finally treat it (repair). **Vehicle troubleshooting follows the exact same clinical methodology.**

## 1.3 Classification of Vehicle Faults

| Category | Examples | Typical Frequency |
|---|---|---|
| **No-start conditions** | Dead battery, fuel starvation, ignition failure | **High** |
| **Performance issues** | Poor acceleration, rough idle, backfiring | **High** |
| **Abnormal noises** | Knocking, grinding, squealing | **Medium** |
| **Fluid-related** | Oil leaks, coolant loss, brake fluid seepage | **Medium** |
| **Electrical anomalies** | Dim lights, blown fuses, sensor faults | **High** |
| **Handling defects** | Pulling, wobble, hard steering | **Low–Medium** |
| **Braking defects** | Spongy lever, poor stopping, dragging | **Critical** |

## 1.4 Standardized Troubleshooting Approach

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The universally accepted **6-Step KTU Troubleshooting Methodology**:
>
> 1. **Verify the Complaint** – Reproduce the fault under safe conditions
> 2. **Collect Information** – Gather history, mileage, recent service records
> 3. **Visual & Sensory Inspection** – Look, listen, smell, touch
> 4. **Measure & Test** – Use multimeters, compression gauges, vacuum gauges
> 5. **Isolate the Fault** – Eliminate working subsystems one by one
> 6. **Repair, Verify, Document** – Fix, test-ride, log the repair
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Systematic Fault Diagnosis Framework

The diagnostic logic in two and three-wheeled vehicles follows a **hierarchical reduction** principle — start broad (system level) and narrow down (component level).

### 2.1.1 Engine Troubleshooting — Subsystem Logic

**Starting Problems (Cranking Issues)**

```
Symptom: Engine does not crank
   ↓
Check 1: Battery Voltage > 12.4 V?
   ↓ (No) → Recharge / Replace battery
   ↓ (Yes)
Check 2: Headlight brightens during cranking?
   ↓ (Dims heavily) → Weak battery or bad earth
   ↓ (Stays bright) → Starter motor / solenoid fault
```

**Rough Idling Analysis**

```
Rough / Unstable Idle
   ↓
Inspect: Throttle Body / Carburetor cleanliness
   ↓
Check: Idle Speed Screw setting
   ↓
Verify: Spark plug condition, gap, and colour
   ↓
Measure: Intake manifold vacuum (should be 400-500 mmHg)
   ↓
Test: Compression pressure across all cylinders
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip**: Examiners love the **"spark-plug reading"** technique. A properly running engine produces a **light tan / coffee-with-milk coloured** electrode. **Black sooty** = rich mixture; **White blistered** = lean / overheating; **Oily black** = worn piston rings or valve guides.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Standard Value | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Battery State of Charge | $SOC = \dfrac{V_{measured} - 10.5}{12.6 - 10.5} \times 100$ | **75-100 %** | % | Determines battery health |
| Compression Ratio | $r_c = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2}$ | **8.5 – 12 : 1** | ratio | Engine design verification |
| Compression Pressure Test | $P_{comp} \geq 0.75 \times P_{rated}$ | **≥ 10 kg/cm²** | kg/cm² | Wear diagnosis |
| Charging System Voltage | $V_{charging} = V_{alternator\;output}$ | **13.5 – 14.5 V** | V | Voltage regulator check |
| Ohm's Law (Circuit test) | $V = I \cdot R$ | — | V, A, $\Omega$ | Electrical diagnostics |
| Brake Fluid Boiling Point | $T_{DOT4} \geq 230°\text{C}$ | **≥ 230 °C** | °C | Brake safety |
| Tire Pressure (Solo) | $P_{front} \approx 25$; $P_{rear} \approx 28$ | **25 / 28 psi** | psi | Handling and safety |
| Spark Plug Gap | $g_{plug} = 0.6 - 0.7\;\text{mm}$ | **0.6 – 0.7** | mm | Ignition efficiency |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on Tables**: Per KTU-PREMIER formatting rules, all absolute-value expressions use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of the pipe character to prevent markdown table corruption.

## 2.3 Electrical System Troubleshooting Logic

### 2.3.1 The "KCL & KVL" Diagnostic Application

When a circuit branch shows **zero current** despite a confirmed voltage source, the technician applies the open-circuit hypothesis:

$$
I = \dfrac{V}{R_{total}} = \dfrac{V}{R_1 + R_2 + R_3}
$$

If $I = 0$, then either:
- **Open circuit** at any node (broken wire, blown fuse, loose connector)
- **Infinite resistance** (corroded contact, failed component)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Real-World Utility**: This same principle governs EV diagnostic procedures for electric two-wheelers (e.g., Ola, Ather, TVS iQube), where fault codes map to high-resistance open-circuits in the CAN bus harness.

## 2.4 Fuel System Troubleshooting — Carburetor vs. EFI

| Parameter | Carburetor System | Electronic Fuel Injection (EFI) |
|---|---|---|
| Fault detection | Visual / smell | **OBD-II scanner codes** |
| Common fault | Float stuck, jet blocked | Injector clogged, sensor drift |
| Idle control | Idle air screw | **ISC valve / stepper motor** |
| Adjustment | Manual sync | **ECU-mapped** |
| Diagnostic tool | Tachometer + visual | **Scan tool + multimeter** |

## 2.5 Brake System Troubleshooting

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Spongy Brake Lever Diagnosis**
>
> A spongy brake lever on a motorcycle usually indicates **air in the hydraulic line**. The fix follows Pascal's law:
>
> $$F_{output} = F_{input} \times \dfrac{A_{caliper}}{A_{master}}$$
>
> Air bubbles compress, reducing the effective force transmission efficiency.

**Bleeding Sequence** (Master Cylinder → Caliper → Bleeder Valve):

$$
\text{Bleed Pressure} \approx 1 - 2 \;\text{bar} \quad ; \quad \text{Fluid DOT 3 / DOT 4}
$$

## 2.6 Transmission & Clutch Troubleshooting

| Symptom | Probable Cause | Repair Action |
|---|---|---|
| Clutch slips | Worn friction plates, weak spring | Replace plates; adjust cable |
| Hard shifting | Bent shift fork, worn dogs | Disassemble gear cluster |
| Chain noise | Improper tension, worn sprocket | Adjust to **20-30 mm slack** |
| Chain derailment | Worn chain, misaligned sprocket | Replace chain & sprocket set |

**Chain Slack Formula** (industry standard):

$$
S_{chain} = \dfrac{L_{loaded} - L_{taut}}{L_{taut}} \times 100 \;\vert\; \approx 1.5 - 2.0 \;\% \text{ of span}
$$

## 2.7 Suspension & Steering Troubleshooting

**Front Fork Oil Capacity Standard Reference**

$$
V_{fork\,oil} = 250 - 350 \;\text{ml per leg} \quad (\text{viscosity } 10\text{W}/30)
$$

Worn fork seals exhibit the **classic oil-residue ring** on the stanchion. A leaking fork seal reduces damping efficiency by approximately **30-50 %** per KTU workshop manuals.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> These diagnostic methodologies are not theoretical — they are the **daily operating procedure** in:
> - **Royal Enfield Service Centres** (India's largest two-wheeler service network)
> - **Honda Authorised Service Centres**
> - **Ather Energy Service Network** (electric 2-wheelers)
> - **Bajaj Three-Wheeler Workshops** (auto-rickshaw fleet maintenance)
> - **Fleet Operators** (Ola, Uber, Rapido, Swiggy delivery fleets)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Battery State-of-Charge (SOC) Derivation — Exhaustive

The State of Charge (SOC) is mathematically defined as the **ratio of available capacity to rated capacity**, expressed as a percentage. Derivation follows:

**Step 1 — Define the variables**
- Let $V_{rated}$ = **rated full-charge voltage** = **12.6 V**
- Let $V_{min}$ = **deep-discharge voltage** = **10.5 V**
- Let $V_{measured}$ = the voltmeter reading at the battery terminals

**Step 2 — Establish the linear mapping**
The voltage-capacity relationship for a 12 V lead-acid battery is approximately linear between full charge and deep discharge.

$$
SOC\;\vert_{\%} \propto (V_{measured} - V_{min})
$$

**Step 3 — Apply boundary conditions**
At $V_{measured} = V_{min} = 10.5\;\text{V}$, SOC = 0 %.  
At $V_{measured} = V_{rated} = 12.6\;\text{V}$, SOC = 100 %.

**Step 4 — Normalise to percentage**
We require a value between 0 and 100. Divide by the range and multiply by 100.

$$
\begin{aligned}
SOC\;\vert_{\%} &= \dfrac{V_{measured} - V_{min}}{V_{rated} - V_{min}} \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{V_{measured} - 10.5}{12.6 - 10.5} \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{V_{measured} - 10.5}{2.1} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example**
Suppose a 12 V motorcycle battery measures **12.1 V** at rest (engine off, surface charge removed).

$$
\begin{aligned}
SOC\;\vert_{\%} &= \dfrac{12.1 - 10.5}{2.1} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{1.6}{2.1} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= 76.19\;\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation**: The battery is approximately **76 % charged** — usable but should be recharged before extended use.

## 3.2 Charging System Voltage Diagnostic Derivation

The alternator (or magneto / stator) output is regulated to **13.5 – 14.5 V DC** to charge the 12 V battery without overcharging.

**Diagnostic Equation**:
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{regulator\;output} &= V_{alternator\;AC\;RMS} \times \sqrt{2} \times \eta_{rectifier} \\[4pt]
\text{where } \eta_{rectifier} &\approx 0.85 - 0.92
\end{aligned}
$$

**Acceptance Criterion (KTU Spec)**:

$$
13.5 \;\text{V} \;\leq\; V_{charging} \;\leq\; 14.5 \;\text{V}
$$

**Step 1 — Measure** battery voltage with engine **OFF** → record $V_{off}$  
**Step 2 — Start engine**, idle at **2000 rpm**, measure $V_{charging}$  
**Step 3 — Compare**:

$$
\Delta V = V_{charging} - V_{off}
$$

**Step 4 — Diagnostic Logic**:

| $\Delta V$ Reading | Diagnosis |
|---|---|
| $\Delta V \leq 0.3\;\text{V}$ | **Charger not working** — check stator / regulator |
| $0.3 \;\leq\; \Delta V \;\leq\; 2.0\;\text{V}$ | **Healthy charging system** |
| $\Delta V > 2.5\;\text{V}$ | **Overcharging** — regulator replacement needed |

## 3.3 Compression Pressure Loss Derivation

For a healthy single-cylinder engine, the compression pressure must equal a known fraction of the rated pressure.

**Industry Standard Acceptance Limit**:

$$
P_{acceptable} \geq 0.75 \times P_{rated}
$$

**Step 1 — Identify rated pressure** (from workshop manual, e.g., for a 150 cc motorcycle): $P_{rated} = 12\;\text{kg/cm}^2$

**Step 2 — Apply the criterion**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{min\;acceptable} &= 0.75 \times 12 \\[4pt]
&= 9.0\;\text{kg/cm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Measure** with a compression gauge held at WOT (Wide Open Throttle), cranking for 5 engine revolutions.

**Step 4 — Compare and conclude**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } P_{measured} &\geq 9.0 \;\rightarrow\; \text{Engine healthy} \\
\text{If } P_{measured} &< 9.0 \;\rightarrow\; \text{Worn rings, valves, or head gasket leak}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Differential diagnosis between rings and valves** using the **wet test**:

$$
P_{wet} - P_{dry} \;\begin{cases} > 1.0 \;\text{kg/cm}^2 \rightarrow \text{Ring wear} \\ \leq 0.5 \;\text{kg/cm}^2 \rightarrow \text{Valve / head gasket} \end{cases}
$$

Adding **3-5 ml of oil** through the spark-plug hole and re-testing — if pressure rises significantly, **piston rings are worn** (oil temporarily seals the gap).

## 3.4 Brake Hydraulic Pressure Derivation (Pascal's Law Applied)

**Step 1 — State Pascal's Law**: Pressure applied to a confined fluid is transmitted equally in all directions.

$$
P = \dfrac{F_{input}}{A_{master}} = \dfrac{F_{output}}{A_{caliper}}
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange** to find $F_{output}$:

$$
F_{output} = F_{input} \times \dfrac{A_{caliper}}{A_{master}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute realistic values** (e.g., Honda Activa):
- $A_{master} = 0.785 \;\text{cm}^2$ (master cylinder piston)
- $A_{caliper} = 2.54 \;\text{cm}^2$ (caliper piston, 18 mm dia)
- $F_{input}$ at lever = **20 N** (typical hand grip force)

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{output} &= 20 \times \dfrac{2.54}{0.785} \\[4pt]
&= 20 \times 3.236 \\[4pt]
&= 64.7\;\text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Mechanical Advantage Ratio**:

$$
MA = \dfrac{A_{caliper}}{A_{master}} = 3.236 \;\text{:}\; 1
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This shows how a modest **20 N hand force** at the brake lever becomes **~65 N clamping force** at the caliper — but **only if the system is free of air**. Air bubbles compress, and the system loses hydraulic integrity.

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Engine Won't Start

| Test Conducted | Expected (Healthy) | Measured | Conclusion |
|---|---|---|---|
| Battery voltage at rest | **12.4 – 12.7 V** | 11.2 V | Battery weak → charge/test |
| Spark at plug (held to ground) | **Strong blue spark** | No spark | Ignition / CDI / coil fault |
| Fuel at carburettor inlet | **Fuel flows** | No fuel | Fuel pump / vacuum petcock fault |
| Compression (cranking) | **≥ 9 kg/cm²** | 6 kg/cm² | Mechanical wear |
| Fuse integrity | **0 Ω** | $\infty$ | Blown fuse |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examination Pattern**: Always present diagnostic tables in this **observation → measurement → conclusion** format. This is the examiner's preferred valuation key structure.

## 3.6 Three-Wheeler (Auto-Rickshaw) Specific Fault Matrix

| Symptom | Likely Cause | Diagnostic Action | Repair |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rear axle knocking | Worn differential bearings | Jack rear, rotate wheel, feel play | Replace bearing set |
| CNG leakage | Loose manifold / regulator | Soap solution test | Tighten / replace gasket |
| Meter flickering | Loose earth, regulator issue | Voltage drop test | Clean earth, check VR |
| Excessive steering play | Worn kingpin / bush | Move wheel side-to-side | Replace bush |
| Vibration at 40 km/h | Wheel balancing / bent rim | Spin wheel on stand | Balance / replace rim |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Six-Step KTU Troubleshooting Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1 Verify the Complaint] --> B[Step 2 Collect Vehicle History]
    B --> C[Step 3 Visual and Sensory Inspection]
    C --> D[Step 4 Measure and Test with Instruments]
    D --> E[Step 5 Isolate the Faulty Subsystem]
    E --> F[Step 6 Repair Replace and Verify]
    F --> G{Test Ride Successful?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Document and Return Vehicle]
    G -- No --> C

    style A fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F6FEB,color:#000
    style B fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F6FEB,color:#000
    style C fill:#FFF4E5,stroke:#F59E0B,color:#000
    style D fill:#FFF4E5,stroke:#F59E0B,color:#000
    style E fill:#FEE8E7,stroke:#EF4444,color:#000
    style F fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#10B981,color:#000
    style G fill:#F3E8FF,stroke:#8B5CF6,color:#000
    style H fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#10B981,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Engine No-Start Diagnostic Tree (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Engine Does Not Start] --> S1{Engine Cranks}
    S1 -- No --> S2[Check Battery Voltage]
    S2 --> S3{Battery Above 12V}
    S3 -- No --> S4[Recharge or Replace Battery]
    S3 -- Yes --> S5[Check Starter Motor and Solenoid]
    S5 --> S6[Inspect Wiring and Earth Strap]
    S1 -- Yes --> S7{Check for Spark}
    S7 -- No --> S8[Inspect Spark Plug Coil CDI Unit]
    S8 --> S9[Test Ignition Switch and Kill Switch]
    S7 -- Yes --> S10{Check Fuel Delivery}
    S10 -- No --> S11[Inspect Fuel Pump Petcock Filter]
    S11 --> S12[Clean Carburetor or Injectors]
    S10 -- Yes --> S13{Compression Test}
    S13 -- Low --> S14[Disassemble Top End Check Rings Valves]

    style S0 fill:#FEE8E7,stroke:#EF4444,color:#000
    style S4 fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#10B981,color:#000
    style S9 fill:#FFF4E5,stroke:#F59E0B,color:#000
    style S12 fill:#FFF4E5,stroke:#F59E0B,color:#000
    style S14 fill:#FEE8E7,stroke:#EF4444,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture of Vehicle Subsystems

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph POWERTRAIN
        ENG[Engine Block]
        CLU[Clutch Assembly]
        GEAR[Gearbox]
        CHN[Drive Chain or Shaft]
    end

    subgraph FUELSYS
        TANK[Fuel Tank]
        PUMP[Fuel Pump]
        CARB[Carburetor or EFI]
        INJ[Injector Rail]
    end

    subgraph ELECSYS
        BAT[Battery 12V]
        ALT[Alternator Stator]
        REG[Voltage Regulator]
        CDI[CDI Unit]
        COIL[Ignition Coil]
        PLUG[Spark Plug]
    end

    subgraph CHASSIS
        FRAME[Frame]
        SUSP[Suspension Forks and Shocks]
        BRK[Brake System Disc and Drum]
        WHL[Wheels and Tyres]
    end

    BAT --> CDI
    ALT --> REG --> BAT
    CDI --> COIL --> PLUG --> ENG
    TANK --> PUMP --> CARB --> ENG
    TANK --> PUMP --> INJ --> ENG
    ENG --> CLU --> GEAR --> CHN --> WHL
    SUSP --> WHL
    BRK --> WHL
    FRAME --> SUSP
    FRAME --> ENG

    style ENG fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F6FEB,color:#000
    style BAT fill:#FFF4E5,stroke:#F59E0B,color:#000
    style BRK fill:#FEE8E7,stroke:#EF4444,color:#000
    style FRAME fill:#F3E8FF,stroke:#8B5CF6,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Brake Bleeding Sequence (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Step 1 Top Up Master Cylinder] --> B[Step 2 Open Bleeder Valve]
    B --> C[Step 3 Pump Brake Lever Slowly]
    C --> D[Step 4 Close Valve Before Lever Bottom]
    D --> E[Step 5 Check Fluid Level in Master]
    E --> F{All Air Removed}
    F -- No --> B
    F -- Yes --> G[Step 6 Tighten Bleeder and Refit Dust Cap]

    style A fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F6FEB,color:#000
    style G fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#10B981,color:#000
```

## 4.5 Charging System Diagnosis Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Measure Battery Voltage Engine OFF] --> N[Record V off]
    N --> O[Start Engine Idle 2000 RPM]
    O --> P[Measure Battery Voltage Engine ON]
    P --> Q[Calculate Delta V]
    Q --> R{Delta V Range}
    R -- Less than 0.3V --> X1[Charging System Faulty]
    R -- 0.3V to 2.0V --> X2[System Healthy]
    R -- Greater than 2.5V --> X3[Overcharging Replace Regulator]

    style X1 fill:#FEE8E7,stroke:#EF4444,color:#000
    style X2 fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#10B981,color:#000
    style X3 fill:#FEE8E7,stroke:#EF4444,color:#000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]
**Q: List any three common causes of an engine not starting in a two-wheeler, and the immediate checks a mechanic should perform.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — each cause + check = 1 mark):**

1. **Dead or weak battery** → Check resting voltage with a multimeter. Should read **≥ 12.4 V**.
2. **No fuel reaching the carburetor** → Disconnect fuel line at carburetor inlet; fuel should flow by gravity (or via pump).
3. **No spark at spark plug** → Remove plug, hold against engine block, crank — observe for a strong **blue spark**.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]
**Q: What is the significance of the spark plug colour in diagnosing engine health? Mention three colour conditions and their meaning.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — each colour = 1 mark):**

The spark plug electrode colour reflects the **air–fuel mixture quality** and combustion efficiency:

| Colour | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Light tan / coffee-with-milk** | Ideal combustion, healthy engine |
| **Black sooty** | Rich mixture — check carburetor jet / float level |
| **White blistered** | Lean mixture or overheating — check for vacuum leaks |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE Module Pattern**, students must answer **ONE full question** of 14 marks from the Module 4 choice. Each question has **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**.

---

### 📘 QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU 2024 Pattern]

**Q: A motorcycle brought to your workshop exhibits the following symptoms: (i) engine cranks but does not start, (ii) headlight dims significantly during cranking, (iii) horn is weak. As a service technician, systematically diagnose the fault using a logical troubleshooting framework, and explain each measurement with the relevant instrument used.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Diagnose the most probable cause and justify with the systematic test sequence.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Symptom Verification (1 mark)**
The customer reports crank-but-no-start. Confirm by attempting to start in a safe environment.

**Step 2 — Probable Cause Identification (2 marks)**
Since the engine cranks (starter motor functions) but the headlight dims heavily and the horn is weak, the **battery is the prime suspect**. The dimming indicates voltage drop under load.

**Step 3 — Battery Voltage Measurement (2 marks)**
Use a **digital multimeter** across the battery terminals (engine OFF, surface charge removed by waiting 10 minutes after running):

$$
V_{measured} \approx 11.0\;\text{V} \quad (\text{below the healthy } 12.4\;\text{V threshold})
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate State of Charge (2 marks)**
Applying the SOC formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
SOC &= \dfrac{11.0 - 10.5}{12.6 - 10.5} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{0.5}{2.1} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= 23.8\;\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark)**: Battery is severely discharged (~24 %). Recommended action: **recharge with a 1.5 A trickle charger for 8-10 hours**, then load-test. If battery fails to hold charge, **replace**.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**If battery is found healthy, describe the next three diagnostic checks in sequence, with the instruments used and acceptance criteria.**

**Model Solution:**

**Check 2 — Spark Test (2 marks)**
- **Instrument**: Spark tester or grounded plug method
- **Procedure**: Remove spark plug, reconnect HT lead, hold plug electrode against engine metal, crank
- **Acceptance criterion**: Strong **blue spark** should jump a **6-8 mm air gap**

**Check 3 — Fuel Delivery Test (2 marks)**
- **Instrument**: Visual inspection + fuel line disconnection
- **Procedure**: Disconnect fuel line at carburettor inlet; turn ignition ON; verify fuel flow
- **Acceptance criterion**: Steady fuel flow without bubbles

**Check 4 — Compression Test (2 marks)**
- **Instrument**: Compression gauge threaded into spark plug hole
- **Procedure**: Hold throttle at WOT, crank 5 revolutions
- **Acceptance criterion**: Compression **≥ 0.75 × rated** (e.g., ≥ 9 kg/cm² for a 12 kg/cm² rated engine)

**Final Conclusion (1 mark)**: These three checks (Spark → Fuel → Compression) cover the **classic engine-starting triad**. Whichever measurement fails to meet the acceptance criterion pinpoints the faulty subsystem.

---

### 📗 QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU 2024 Pattern]

**Q: A three-wheeler (passenger auto-rickshaw) is reported to have spongy front brakes, reduced stopping power, and uneven tyre wear on the front-left wheel. Diagnose each defect, recommend appropriate repair procedures with relevant technical specifications, and justify with underlying engineering principles.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the diagnosis and repair of the spongy brakes and reduced stopping power.**

**Model Solution:**

**Diagnosis of Spongy Lever (2 marks)**
Spongy brakes indicate **air in the hydraulic system** or **low brake fluid level**. Applying Pascal's law:

$$
P = \dfrac{F}{A} \quad \text{and} \quad F_{output} = F_{input} \times \dfrac{A_{caliper}}{A_{master}}
$$

Air bubbles compress under lever force, reducing transmitted hydraulic pressure — the lever feels "mushy".

**Step 1 — Check master cylinder fluid level (1 mark)**
Top up with **DOT 3 or DOT 4** brake fluid to the MAX line.

**Step 2 — Bleed the brake system (2 marks)**
Sequence: Master Cylinder → Wheel Cylinder/Caliper → Bleeder Valve. Pump lever slowly, close bleeder before lever reaches bottom of stroke to prevent air re-entry.

**Step 3 — Inspect brake lines (1 mark)**
Look for leaks, cracks, or loose banjo-bolt fittings.

**Step 4 — Inspect brake pads/shoes (1 mark)**
Minimum pad thickness: **≥ 2 mm** of friction material remaining.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Diagnose the uneven front-left tyre wear and recommend repair with specifications.**

**Model Solution:**

**Diagnosis of Uneven Tyre Wear (3 marks)**
Uneven wear on a single wheel typically indicates:
- **Wheel alignment defect** — incorrect camber or toe setting
- **Worn suspension component** — bent stub axle, worn kingpin bush
- **Improper tyre pressure** — check cold pressure

**Step 1 — Measure Tyre Pressure (1 mark)**
- Front: **25 psi (solo)**, **28 psi (with load)** — three-wheeler loaded condition applies
- Use a calibrated **tyre-pressure gauge**

**Step 2 — Inspect Suspension Components (2 marks)**
- **Kingpin / steering bush play**: grip the wheel at 9 and 3 o'clock positions, attempt rocking — any perceptible play indicates **worn bushes** (acceptable ≤ 1-2 mm)
- **Stub axle straightness**: rotate wheel, observe runout using a dial indicator (limit: **≤ 0.5 mm**)

**Step 3 — Wheel Alignment Correction (1 mark)**
- Adjust **camber angle** to manufacturer spec (typically **0° to ±0.5°**)
- Adjust **toe-in** to spec (typically **0 to +2 mm toe-in**)

**Final Conclusion**: Replace worn bushes, correct alignment, rotate tyres, and verify with a road test. Document findings for warranty records.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes in Module 4 Questions:**
>
> 1. **Skipping the "Verify the Complaint" step** — Examiners award 1-2 marks for confirming the symptom. Starting directly with disassembly loses this.
> 2. **Not stating the instrument name** — "Measure voltage" without specifying "multimeter" loses the technical vocabulary mark.
> 3. **Omitting acceptance criteria** — Saying "compression is low" without quoting a numerical threshold (e.g., **≥ 9 kg/cm²**) loses application marks.
> 4. **Confusing "cranking" vs. "starting"** — The engine cranks but fails to fire. Students often interchange these and confuse examiners.
> 5. **Forgetting the safety disclaimer** — Always state "isolate the battery before working on electrical systems" in repair procedures.
> 6. **No cause-effect linkage** — "Brake is spongy because of air" is better than just "brake is spongy". Examiners reward **causal reasoning**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 4**
>
> ✅ **Troubleshooting methodology** = 6 systematic steps (Verify → History → Inspect → Measure → Isolate → Repair)
>
> ✅ **Engine will not start triad** = Spark + Fuel + Compression — all three must be present
>
> ✅ **Battery healthy voltage** = **12.4 – 12.7 V** at rest
>
> ✅ **Charging system healthy** = **13.5 – 14.5 V** with engine running
>
> ✅ **Spark plug healthy colour** = light tan / coffee-with-milk
>
> ✅ **Compression acceptance** = ≥ **75 % of rated pressure**
>
> ✅ **Brake fluid bleeding sequence** = Master → Caliper → Bleeder valve (closed before lever bottom)
>
> ✅ **Brake fluid standard** = DOT 3 / DOT 4 (boiling point ≥ 230 °C)
>
> ✅ **Pascal's law** governs hydraulic brakes — air compressibility breaks the system
>
> ✅ **Front tyre pressure** ≈ **25 psi solo / 28 psi loaded**
>
> ✅ **Spark plug gap** = **0.6 – 0.7 mm**
>
> ✅ **Chain slack** = **20-30 mm** total vertical play
>
> ✅ **Front fork oil** = **250-350 ml** per leg (10W/30 viscosity)
>
> ✅ **Always isolate the battery** before electrical repair
>
> ✅ **Document every repair** — KTU 2024 emphasises service-record maintenance
>
> ✅ **Three-wheeler specific** — kingpin bush play ≤ 1-2 mm, stub-axle runout ≤ 0.5 mm
>
> ✅ **EFI vs. Carburettor** — EFI requires scan-tool diagnostics; carburettor relies on visual/manual adjustment
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety Systems and Regulations: Retrofitting of IC Engines to EV in Two/Three Wheelers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT631 - TWO AND THREE WHEELED VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Safety Systems and Regulations: Retrofitting of IC Engines to EV in Two/Three Wheelers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Retrofitting** in the context of two and three-wheeled vehicles refers to the systematic process of replacing the Internal Combustion (IC) engine powertrain (petrol/diesel) with an Electric Vehicle (EV) powertrain—comprising an electric motor, motor controller, Battery Management System (BMS), and Lithium-ion battery pack—while retaining the existing chassis, suspension, brakes, and body structure of the vehicle.
