## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Equivalent Mass Saved by Material Substitution

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU Numerical)**: A motorcycle swingarm is currently manufactured from steel ($\rho_{st} = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\sigma_{y,st} = 350 \text{ MPa}$). It is proposed to replace it with an aluminium alloy ($\rho_{Al} = 2700 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\sigma_{y,Al} = 276 \text{ MPa}$). If the design is **strength-driven** (i.e., the cross-section is sized to carry a peak bending load), calculate the percentage mass reduction achieved.

#### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Identify the governing design constraint.**

In a strength-driven design, the maximum bending stress in the beam must not exceed the yield strength:

$$\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M \cdot c}{I} \leq \sigma_y$$

where $M$ is the peak bending moment, $c$ is the distance from the neutral axis to the outer fibre, and $I$ is the second moment of area.

**Step 2: Recognize that $I$ is proportional to $A \cdot k^2$, where $k$ is the radius of gyration.**

For a beam of a given geometric shape (e.g., rectangular, circular, I-section), the ratio $c/\sqrt{I}$ depends only on the **shape**, not the size. If we maintain the **same shape** (just scale the cross-section), then:

$$\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$$

where $Z = I/c$ is the section modulus.

**Step 3: Express mass in terms of design parameters.**

Let the beam have length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$, and density $\rho$. The mass is:

$$m = \rho \cdot A \cdot L$$

For a given shape, the section modulus is $Z = \alpha \cdot A^{3/2}$, where $\alpha$ is a shape-dependent constant (e.g., for a rectangle of width $b$ and height $h$, $Z = bh^2/6$ and $A = bh$, so $Z = A^{3/2}/(\sqrt{6}\sqrt{b})$ — illustrating the shape dependence).

**Step 4: Equate the required section modulus for both materials.**

For the same applied moment $M$:

$$Z_{required} = \dfrac{M}{\sigma_y}$$

Therefore:
$$A_{required} = \left(\dfrac{M}{\alpha \cdot \sigma_y}\right)^{2/3}$$

**Step 5: Compute the ratio of masses.**

$$\dfrac{m_{Al}}{m_{st}} = \dfrac{\rho_{Al} \cdot A_{Al,req} \cdot L}{\rho_{st} \cdot A_{st,req} \cdot L} = \dfrac{\rho_{Al}}{\rho_{st}} \cdot \left(\dfrac{\sigma_{y,st}}{\sigma_{y,Al}}\right)^{2/3}$$

**Step 6: Substitute numerical values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{m_{Al}}{m_{st}} &= \dfrac{2700}{7850} \cdot \left(\dfrac{350}{276}\right)^{2/3} \\
&= 0.3439 \cdot (1.2681)^{2/3} \\
&= 0.3439 \cdot (1.2681)^{0.6667}
\end{aligned}
$$

Now compute $(1.2681)^{2/3}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln(1.2681) &= 0.2376 \\
\dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \ln(1.2681) &= \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot 0.2376 = 0.1584 \\
\exp(0.1584) &= 1.1716
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$\dfrac{m_{Al}}{m_{st}} = 0.3439 \cdot 1.1716 = 0.4029$$

**Step 7: Calculate the percentage mass reduction.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%MR &= \dfrac{m_{st} - m_{Al}}{m_{st}} \times 100 \\
&= (1 - 0.4029) \times 100 \\
&= 59.71\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion**: Replacing a strength-driven steel swingarm with an aluminium 6061-T6 swingarm yields approximately **60% mass reduction**. This is the theoretical upper bound; practical values are typically **40–50%** due to design conservatism, joining requirements, and minimum gauge constraints.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip**: Many students incorrectly use $m_{Al}/m_{st} = \rho_{Al}/\rho_{st} = 0.344$ and report a 65.6% mass reduction. This is WRONG because it ignores the strength penalty. The correct approach (above) accounts for the fact that aluminium needs a *larger* cross-section to carry the same load, consuming more volume.

### 3.2 Derivation: Fuel Economy Improvement from Mass Reduction

**Problem Statement**: A three-wheeler (autorickshaw) has a curb mass of **400 kg** and a fuel efficiency of **25 km/L** under a standard urban driving cycle. Using the sensitivity coefficient of **0.5 L/100km per 100 kg** of mass reduction, estimate the new fuel efficiency if **30 kg** is removed by replacing conventional steel panels with GFRP panels.

#### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Convert the current fuel efficiency to L/100km.**

$$
FE_{current} = \dfrac{100 \text{ km}}{25 \text{ km/L}} = 4.0 \text{ L}/100\text{km}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the change in fuel consumption.**

The sensitivity coefficient is $0.5 \text{ L}/100\text{km}$ per 100 kg, which equals $0.005 \text{ L}/100\text{km}$ per kg.

$$
\Delta FE = 0.005 \cdot 30 = 0.15 \text{ L}/100\text{km}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the new fuel consumption.**

$$
FE_{new} = FE_{current} - \Delta FE = 4.0 - 0.15 = 3.85 \text{ L}/100\text{km}
$$

**Step 4: Convert back to km/L.**

$$
FE_{new} = \dfrac{100}{3.85} = 25.97 \text{ km/L}
$$

**Step 5: Compute the percentage improvement.**

$$
\% \text{ Improvement} = \dfrac{25.97 - 25.00}{25.00} \times 100 = 3.88\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note**: The 30 kg mass reduction (7.5% of curb mass) results in a fuel economy improvement of approximately **3.88%**. This is consistent with industry rule-of-thumb: **a 10% mass reduction yields ~4–6% fuel economy improvement** for 2W/3W in urban cycles.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Material Selection Decision Table

This matrix is a high-yield study tool for KTU exams. Use it to compare materials across multiple criteria.

| Selection Criterion | Steel (AHSS) | Aluminium 6061-T6 | Magnesium AZ91D | Titanium Ti-6Al-4V | CFRP |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Density ($\text{kg/m}^3$) | **7850** | **2700** | **1740** | **4500** | **1600** |
| Young's Modulus ($E$, GPa) | 200 | 69 | 45 | 114 | 70–150 (anisotropic) |
| Yield Strength ($\sigma_y$, MPa) | 350–1500 | 276 | 160 | 880 | 500–1500 (anisotropic) |
| Specific Modulus ($E/\rho$, $\text{GN}\cdot\text{m/kg}$) | 25.5 | 25.6 | 25.9 | 25.3 | 44–94 |
| Specific Strength ($\sigma_y/\rho$, $\text{kN}\cdot\text{m/kg}$) | 45–191 | 102 | 92 | 196 | 313–938 |
| Relative Cost (per kg, indexed) | 1.0× | 3–5× | 4–6× | 15–30× | 20–50× |
| Corrosion Resistance | Poor (needs coating) | Good (oxide layer) | Poor (needs coating) | Excellent | Excellent |
| Recyclability | Excellent | Excellent | Good | Good | Poor |
| Manufacturability | Excellent (weld, form) | Good (weld, form) | Fair (limited formability) | Poor (machining difficult) | Fair (layup, RTM) |
| Typical 2W/3W Application | Frame, engine mounts | Swingarm, subframe, wheels | Engine cases, brackets | Exhaust, fasteners, racing | Fairings, subframes, racing |

### 3.4 Engineering Reasoning: Why Specific Modulus Matters More Than Specific Strength for Chassis

**Step 1**: A motorcycle chassis is typically designed for **stiffness**, not strength. Why? Because excessive deflection causes:
- Wobble at high speeds
- Inaccurate steering feedback
- Fatigue failure from dynamic loads
- Poor handling and rider confidence

**Step 2**: Stiffness in bending is governed by $EI$ (or $EA$ for axial). To maintain equal stiffness with minimum mass:

$$\text{Minimize } \rho \cdot A \text{ subject to } E \cdot I = \text{constant}$$

**Step 3**: This is a constrained optimization problem. For a given shape family, $I \propto A^2$, so:

$$E \cdot A^2 = \text{constant} \implies A \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{E}}$$

**Step 4**: The optimal mass becomes:

$$m \propto \rho \cdot A \cdot L \propto \dfrac{\rho}{\sqrt{E}}$$

**Step 5**: To minimize $m$, we need to **maximize** $\dfrac{\sqrt{E}}{\rho}$, which is mathematically equivalent to maximizing the **specific modulus** $E/\rho$.

**Step 6**: From the table above, **CFRP** has the highest $E/\rho$ (up to **94 GN·m/kg**), nearly **4× that of any metal**. This is why CFRP is the preferred choice for high-end motorcycle chassis despite its cost.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error**: Students often argue "CFRP is best because it has the highest specific strength." For *chassis applications*, this is misleading. The correct criterion is **specific modulus** because chassis design is **stiffness-driven**, not strength-driven. Reserve specific strength for components like brake levers, fasteners, and suspension links.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Lightweight Material Selection for 2W/3W

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the decision logic an engineer follows when selecting a lightweight material for a given 2W/3W component. It captures the interactions between *functional requirement*, *material property*, *manufacturing constraint*, and *cost target*.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Component e.g. Swingarm]:::start
    B{Is the component a chassis or structural part?}:::decision
    C{Is the design stiffness-driven or strength-driven?}:::decision
    D[Chassis: stiffness-driven]:::note
    E[Linkage: strength-driven]:::note
    F[Compute required E/rho]:::process
    G[Compute required sigma_y/rho]:::process
    H{Is cost primary constraint?}:::decision
    I[Select CFRP if cost is secondary]:::process
    J[Select Al 6061-T6 or AHSS]:::process
    K[Select CFRP or Ti-6Al-4V]:::process
    L[Select AHSS or Al 7075-T6]:::process
    M[Verify with FEA and physical testing]:::process
    N[Final Material Selection]:::endgoal

    A --> B
    B -- Yes --> C
    B -- No --> G
    C -- Stiffness --> D
    C -- Strength --> E
    D --> F
    E --> G
    F --> H
    H -- Yes --> J
    H -- No --> I
    G --> H
    I --> M
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N

  +-------------------------------+     <- Bottom face sheet (CFRP, 0.5 mm)
```

**Key Insight**: The two thin, stiff face sheets carry the bending loads, while the lightweight core resists shear and prevents the faces from buckling. The result is a structure with **the stiffness of a solid panel ~10× thicker**, at a fraction of the mass.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: Define "Specific Strength" and "Specific Modulus." Why are these properties more relevant than absolute strength and stiffness in 2W/3W lightweight design? [3 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**Model Answer**:

**Specific Strength** is the ratio of a material's ultimate tensile strength ($\sigma_u$) to its density ($\rho$), expressed as $SS = \sigma_u / \rho$ in units of $\text{kN}\cdot\text{m/kg}$.

**Specific Modulus** is the ratio of Young's modulus ($E$) to density ($\rho$), expressed as $SM = E / \rho$ in units of $\text{GN}\cdot\text{m/kg}$.

In 2W/3W lightweight design, the **primary design objective is mass minimization subject to functional constraints** (stiffness, strength, fatigue life). Since every additional kilogram reduces power-to-weight ratio, fuel economy, and acceleration, designers need metrics that normalize material performance against weight. Specific strength and specific modulus do exactly this — they rank materials by **performance per unit mass**, enabling direct comparison across widely different material families (steel, aluminium, CFRP, etc.). A high specific strength allows thinner, lighter sections to carry the same load, while a high specific modulus allows lighter, equally stiff structures.

*(Word count: ~140 | Target: 3-mark concise answer)*

---

#### Question 2: List any FOUR advanced materials used in modern 2W/3W vehicles with one typical application each. [3 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**Model Answer**:

| # | Advanced Material | Typical 2W/3W Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Aluminium Alloy 6061-T6 / 7075-T6** | Swingarm, subframe, forged wheels, handlebar |
| 2 | **Carbon Fibre Reinforced Polymer (CFRP)** | Front fairing, subframe, racing monocoque chassis |
| 3 | **Magnesium Alloy AZ91D** | Engine cases, gearbox housings, instrument clusters |
| 4 | **Titanium Alloy Ti-6Al-4V** | Exhaust header pipes, fasteners, racing connecting rods |

*(Optional 5th: Glass Fibre Reinforced Polymer — body panels for 3W autos.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]

**(a)** Explain the concept of **"Right Material in the Right Place" (RMRP)** in the context of 2W/3W lightweight design. Discuss how this principle is applied in modern multi-material motorcycle architecture with at least **three specific examples** from real vehicles. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A motorcycle's **rear subframe** is currently manufactured from mild steel ($\rho_{st} = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\sigma_{y,st} = 250 \text{ MPa}$). It is proposed to replace it with **Aluminium 6061-T6** ($\rho_{Al} = 2700 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\sigma_{y,Al} = 276 \text{ MPa}$). The subframe has a tubular cross-section and the design is **strength-driven** under a peak bending load. Calculate the **percentage mass reduction** achieved. State any **two practical challenges** that might reduce the actual mass savings below this theoretical value. **[7 Marks]**

---

##### Model Solution to Question A(a):

**Definition of RMRP (2 marks)**: The "Right Material in the Right Place" principle states that in a multi-material vehicle, **each component or sub-region should be made from the material that best satisfies its local functional requirements (load, stiffness, temperature, cost) at minimum mass**. It rejects the idea of using a single material for the entire vehicle in favour of a *hybrid* design philosophy.

**Three Real-Vehicle Examples (5 marks)**:

1. **Ducati Panigale V4 / Ducati Superleggera**: The **front subframe** is a magnesium casting (≈ 1.2 kg saved vs. aluminium), the **swingarm** is single-sided aluminium 7075-T6 (≈ 30% lighter than the steel equivalent), and the **fairings** are CFRP. The engine cases are aluminium, while connecting rods and valves are titanium. Each material is matched to its specific function.

2. **BMW S1000RR / HP4 Race**: The **main frame** is an aluminium perimeter structure (stiffness-driven, high $E/\rho$), the **rear subframe** is CFRP (mass-driven, no crash energy absorption needed), the **wheels** are forged aluminium (mass-driven at the unsprung end), and the **brake discs** are optional carbon-ceramic (thermal capacity + mass).

3. **Honda CB300F / KTM 390 Duke (Commuter Segment)**: The **main frame** is a steel trellis (cost-driven, high formability for mass production), the **swingarm** is rectangular-section steel for cost reasons, but the **wheels** are cast aluminium and the **engine cases** are die-cast aluminium. The RMRP principle is applied in a *cost-constrained* manner — only where the mass savings justify the cost premium is a lighter material chosen.

---

##### Model Solution to Question A(b):

**Step 1**: Establish the design constraint. For a strength-driven tubular cross-section:

$$\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M}{Z} \leq \sigma_y \implies Z_{req} = \dfrac{M}{\sigma_y}$$

**Step 2**: For a circular tube of mean diameter $D$ and wall thickness $t$ (with $t \ll D$), the section modulus is $Z \approx \pi D^2 t / 4$ and the cross-sectional area is $A \approx \pi D t$. Eliminating $D$ gives $Z \propto A^{3/2}$ (for a fixed shape ratio $t/D$). Therefore, the required area scales as:

$$A_{req} \propto \sigma_y^{-2/3}$$

**Step 3**: The mass ratio is:

$$\dfrac{m_{Al}}{m_{st}} = \dfrac{\rho_{Al} \cdot A_{Al,req}}{\rho_{st} \cdot A_{st,req}} = \dfrac{\rho_{Al}}{\rho_{st}} \cdot \left(\dfrac{\sigma_{y,st}}{\sigma_{y,Al}}\right)^{2/3}$$

**Step 4**: Substitute values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{m_{Al}}{m_{st}} &= \dfrac{2700}{7850} \cdot \left(\dfrac{250}{276}\right)^{2/3} \\
&= 0.3439 \cdot (0.9058)^{2/3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5**: Compute $(0.9058)^{2/3}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln(0.9058) &= -0.0989 \\
\dfrac{2}{3} \cdot (-0.0989) &= -0.0659 \\
\exp(-0.0659) &= 0.9362
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6**: Final ratio:

$$\dfrac{m_{Al}}{m_{st}} = 0.3439 \cdot 0.9362 = 0.3220$$

**Step 7**: Mass reduction percentage:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%MR &= (1 - 0.3220) \times 100 \\
&= 67.80\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Theoretical mass reduction ≈ 67.8%**.

**Two practical challenges (2 marks)**:

1. **Minimum gauge / manufacturing constraints**: Aluminium tubes have a minimum practical wall thickness (~1.2 mm) due to manufacturing limits (hydroforming, bending). Below this, the part becomes hard to handle, weld, and inspect. Steel can be drawn to thinner walls economically.

2. **Joining and fatigue**: Aluminium requires different joining techniques (TIG welding, friction stir welding, rivets) compared to steel (MIG, spot welding). The weld zone in aluminium has reduced fatigue strength, often necessitating design oversizing at the joint, which erodes the theoretical mass savings.

*(Other valid challenges: corrosion dissimilarity, cost premium, crash energy mismatch, NVH tuning differences.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Internal Choice Alternative]

**(a)** With the help of a **suitable schematic and a comparative table**, discuss the role of **aluminium alloys, magnesium alloys, and CFRP** in the lightweight construction of two and three-wheeled vehicles. Your answer must address **density, specific modulus, cost, manufacturability, and at least one real-world application** for each material. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A three-wheeled auto-rickshaw has a curb mass of **450 kg** and delivers a fuel efficiency of **22 km/L** under a mixed urban-suburban drive cycle. The manufacturer proposes to replace the conventional steel roof and side panels (total mass **35 kg**) with **GFRP composite panels** of equivalent strength, achieving a mass saving of **60%** on these panels. Assuming a fuel-economy sensitivity coefficient of **0.5 L/100km per 100 kg** of mass reduction, compute the **new fuel efficiency in km/L** and the **percentage improvement** over the baseline. **[7 Marks]**

---

##### Model Solution to Question B(a):

**Schematic Description (2 marks)**: A typical multi-material 2W/3W architecture with annotated materials. *(Refer to Section 4.2 Mermaid diagram for reference.)* The schematic should show a motorcycle or auto-rickshaw with the following callouts: aluminium swingarm, magnesium engine case, CFRP fairing/subframe, GFRP body panels.

**Comparative Table (3 marks)**:

| Property | Aluminium 6061-T6 | Magnesium AZ91D | CFRP (UD, 60%Vf) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Density ($\text{kg/m}^3$) | **2700** | **1740** | **1600** |
| Young's Modulus ($E$, GPa) | 69 | 45 | 130 (longitudinal) |
| Specific Modulus ($E/\rho$, $\text{GN}\cdot\text{m/kg}$) | 25.6 | 25.9 | 81.3 |
| Yield/Tensile Strength (MPa) | 276 | 160 | 1500 (longitudinal) |
| Cost Index (vs. steel = 1) | 3–5× | 4–6× | 20–50× |
| Manufacturability | Excellent (cast, forge, extrude) | Good (cast, limited forming) | Fair (RTM, autoclave) |
| Real-World 2W/3W Application | Yamaha R15 swingarm, Bajaj Pulsar wheels | BMW R1200GS engine case, Ducati gearbox | Ducati Panigale fairing, BMW HP4 Race monocoque |

**Discussion (2 marks)**:
- **Aluminium** is the **workhorse of lightweighting** for 2W/3W. It offers a balanced combination of low density, good specific modulus, excellent manufacturability (cast, forge, extrude, weld), and reasonable cost. It is the material of choice for swingarms, wheels, subframes, and engine cases in mid-to-high-end commuter and sport motorcycles.
- **Magnesium** is the **lightest structural metal** and offers a small improvement in specific modulus over aluminium. However, its poor corrosion resistance, limited formability, and high-temperature strength loss restrict it to **enclosed, low-temperature components** like engine cases, gearbox housings, and brackets. Used selectively in premium BMW and Ducati models.
- **CFRP** offers **the highest specific strength and specific modulus** of any commonly used structural material, but at a steep cost premium. Its **anisotropic** nature allows the engineer to "tune" stiffness and strength in specific directions. Used in racing fairings, subframes, and even full monocoque chassis in high-end sportbikes (Ducati Superleggera, Honda RC213V-S).

---

##### Model Solution to Question B(b):

**Step 1**: Calculate the actual mass saved.

$$
m_{saved} = 35 \text{ kg} \times 0.60 = 21 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 2**: Convert baseline fuel efficiency to L/100km.

$$
FE_{baseline} = \dfrac{100}{22} = 4.545 \text{ L}/100\text{km}
$$

**Step 3**: Calculate the fuel consumption reduction using the sensitivity coefficient.

Sensitivity $= 0.5 \text{ L}/100\text{km}$ per 100 kg $= 0.005 \text{ L}/100\text{km}$ per kg.

$$
\Delta FE = 0.005 \times 21 = 0.105 \text{ L}/100\text{km}
$$

**Step 4**: Calculate the new fuel consumption.

$$
FE_{new} = 4.545 - 0.105 = 4.440 \text{ L}/100\text{km}
$$

**Step 5**: Convert back to km/L.

$$
FE_{new} = \dfrac{100}{4.440} = 22.52 \text{ km/L}
$$

**Step 6**: Percentage improvement.

$$
\% \text{ Improvement} = \dfrac{22.52 - 22.00}{22.00} \times 100 = 2.36\%
$$

**Final Answer**: New fuel efficiency = **22.52 km/L**, improvement = **2.36%** over baseline.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — How Students Lose Marks**:
> 1. **Forgetting to convert km/L to L/100km first** before applying the sensitivity coefficient. The sensitivity is given in L/100km, so you MUST work in that unit.
> 2. **Using the wrong mass** — some students use the full 35 kg panel mass instead of the *actual saved* mass (21 kg). Read the problem carefully.
> 3. **Rounding errors**: Keep at least 3–4 significant figures throughout; do not round at intermediate steps.
> 4. **In Part A(a) for Question A**: Students often list *applications* without explaining *why* the material is suited. Always justify with property linkage.
> 5. **Missing the "design is strength-driven" assumption**: The formula $m_{Al}/m_{st} = (\rho_{Al}/\rho_{st})(\sigma_{y,st}/\sigma_{y,Al})^{2/3}$ is only valid for strength-driven designs. For *stiffness-driven* designs, replace $\sigma_y$ with $\sqrt{E}$ in the exponent (because $A \propto 1/E$ for equal stiffness, so $A \propto 1/\sqrt{E}$ gives $A^{2/3}$ — actually, for stiffness-driven design with $EI$ constant and $I \propto A^2$, the mass ratio becomes $(\rho_{Al}/\rho_{st}) \cdot (E_{st}/E_{Al})^{1/2}$). **Do not mix these up!**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Advanced Materials and Lightweight Construction**

#### A. Core Definitions (Must Memorize)
- **Specific Strength** = $\sigma_u / \rho$ — relevant for *strength-driven* components (fasteners, levers, linkages).
- **Specific Modulus** = $E / \rho$ — relevant for *stiffness-driven* components (chassis, subframes, swingarms).
- **Multi-Material Design (MMD)**: The "Right Material in the Right Place" philosophy.
- **Unsprung Mass**: Mass not supported by the suspension (wheels, brake discs, lower fork legs, part of swingarm). Reducing unsprung mass has a **2–3× greater impact on ride quality** than equal reductions in sprung mass.
- **Sprung Mass**: Mass supported by the suspension (frame, engine, fuel, rider). Reducing sprung mass improves handling and braking.

#### B. Material Densities (Memorize)
- Steel = **7850 kg/m³** | Aluminium = **2700 kg/m³** | Magnesium = **1740 kg/m³** | Titanium = **4500 kg/m³** | CFRP = **1600 kg/m³** | GFRP = **1900 kg/m³**

#### C. Key Numerical Formulas
- **% Mass Reduction** (strength-driven) = $\left[1 - \dfrac{\rho_{new}}{\rho_{old}} \cdot \left(\dfrac{\sigma_{y,old}}{\sigma_{y,new}}\right)^{2/3}\right] \times 100$
- **% Mass Reduction** (stiffness-driven) = $\left[1 - \dfrac{\rho_{new}}{\rho_{old}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{E_{old}}{E_{new}}}\right] \times 100$
- **Fuel Economy Sensitivity** ≈ 0.4–0.6 L/100km per 100 kg mass reduction
- **Rule of 10%**: A 10% mass reduction yields ≈ 4–6% fuel economy improvement and ≈ 6–8% acceleration improvement (urban cycle)

#### D. Application Memory Map
- **Swingarm**: Al 7075-T6, CFRP, or Mg (unsprung mass reduction)
- **Frame**: Steel trellis (cost), Al twin-spar (mid-premium), CFRP monocoque (racing)
- **Subframe**: CFRP (premium) or Al (mid)
- **Engine Case**: Mg (premium) or Al (most cases)
- **Wheels**: Forged Al (most), Mg (rare), Carbon (racing)
- **Fairings**: CFRP (sport), GFRP (3W autos), ABS plastic (commuter)
- **Exhaust**: Ti (premium racing), Stainless steel (most), Al (limited high-T use)

#### E. Common Exam Traps
1. Confusing *strength-driven* vs. *stiffness-driven* mass reduction formulas.
2. Forgetting the **2/3 power** exponent in strength-driven derivation.
3. Reporting density ratio as mass reduction % without accounting for required cross-section change.
4. Claiming "CFRP is best" without qualifying *for what* application (chassis vs. fastener).
5. Not converting km/L ↔ L/100km before applying sensitivity coefficients.
6. Ignoring **manufacturability and joining** constraints when discussing real-world mass savings.
7. Missing the **unsprung mass** distinction — a 5 kg reduction at the wheel is worth more than 5 kg at the engine.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Smart and Connected Vehicle Technologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT631 - TWO AND THREE WHEELED VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Smart and Connected Vehicle Technologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Smart Vehicle Technology — Formal KTU Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned):**
> **Smart Vehicle Technology** refers to the integration of advanced electronic systems, sensors, actuators, and embedded controllers into two- and three-wheeled vehicles to enable semi-autonomous or fully-autonomous decision-making, real-time performance optimization, enhanced safety, and improved rider/driver experience without continuous human intervention.

> **Connected Vehicle Technology** is the complementary discipline that uses **Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X)** communication — encompassing **V2V (Vehicle-to-Vehicle)**, **V2I (Vehicle-to-Infrastructure)**, **V2N (Vehicle-to-Network)**, and **V2P (Vehicle-to-Pedestrian)** channels — to exchange operational, navigational, and safety data between the vehicle and its external environment using wireless protocols such as **CAN, LIN, Bluetooth, Wi-Fi, 4G LTE, 5G, and DSRC.**

Together, these form the **Intelligent Transportation System (ITS)** backbone of modern two/three-wheelers.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Smartphone-ification" of a Scooter**
>
> Imagine your grandfather's old kinetic Honda scooter. It had a carburetor, points-based ignition, and a mechanical key. It had no idea where it was, how fast it was going electronically, or whether the rider had fallen. That is a **conventional vehicle**.
>
> Now imagine a 2025 **Ather 450X** or **Ola S1 Pro**. It has:
> - A **GPS chip** (it knows where it is — like Google Maps on a phone),
> - A **6-axis IMU** (it knows if it has fallen — like a phone detecting rotation),
> - A **4G SIM** (it can call for help — like a phone making a call),
> - A **mobile app** (the owner can lock/unlock it remotely — like a smart lock),
> - An **AI chip** (it predicts range, optimizes battery — like a phone's predictive text).
>
> This transformation — from a **dumb mechanical machine** to a **networked, sensor-rich, AI-aware cyber-physical system** — is exactly what **Smart and Connected Vehicle Technology** means.
>
> The vehicle is no longer just a *means of transport*; it is a **mobile IoT node** on wheels, generating terabytes of data per year and participating actively in the urban digital ecosystem.

---

## 1.3 Why Smart & Connected Tech Matters for 2/3 Wheelers

Two- and three-wheelers dominate urban mobility in countries like India, accounting for **over 80%** of registered vehicles. They are also the **most vulnerable** road users — riders in India face a fatality rate that is **~3 times higher** per km than car occupants. Smart technologies address this disproportionate risk.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Highlight Box:**
> - **India's two-wheeler market:** ~20 million units sold annually (2024 data).
> - **Connected 2W penetration:** Projected **> 60%** by 2030.
> - **Key drivers:** Mandated **AIS-140 GPS** for commercial fleets, **ABS** for >125 cc bikes, growing EV adoption, and FAME-II subsidy compliance requiring telematics.

---

## 1.4 Taxonomy of Smart & Connected Features in 2/3 Wheelers

| Layer | Technology | 2W/3W Application Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Perception (Sensing)** | IMU, GPS, Wheel-speed, Current/Voltage sensors | Fall detection, geo-fencing |
| **Processing (ECU)** | 32-bit MCUs, SoCs (e.g., NXP S32K) | BMS, motor controller logic |
| **Actuation** | BLDC controllers, ABS modulators, smart locks | Regenerative braking, anti-theft |
| **Connectivity** | CAN, BLE, 4G/5G, DSRC | V2X, OTA updates, fleet tracking |
| **Cloud/Edge** | MQTT, AWS IoT, Kafka telemetry pipelines | Predictive maintenance, ride analytics |
| **HMI (Human-Machine Interface)** | TFT displays, haptic handlebars, HUD helmets | Rider alerts, navigation |
| **Security** | AES-256, TPM modules, secure boot | Anti-theft, data privacy |

---

## 1.5 Core Metrics & Standards

The following constants and standards are **bolded for emphasis** as they frequently appear in KTU questions:

- **V2X Communication Latency Target:** **< 10 ms** (3GPP Release 16)
- **DSRC Band:** **5.85 – 5.925 GHz** (IEEE 802.11p)
- **CAN Bus Speed (Classical CAN):** **1 Mbps** max
- **CAN FD Speed:** **5 Mbps**
- **Standard EV Charger Connector (India):** **IS 17017 / IEC 62196** — Type 6 (Bharat DC-001) and Type 2 (AC)
- **AIS-140 Mandate:** GPS + emergency button for all public service vehicles
- **Bluetooth LE Range (Class 2):** **~10 m**; power: **0 dBm**
- **Regenerative Braking Energy Recovery:** **5 – 15%** of total energy

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a Smart-Connected 2W/3W System

A modern connected two-wheeler (e.g., Ather, TVS iQube, Hero Vida) follows a **layered cyber-physical architecture**:

### Layer 1 — Sensor & Actuator Layer
- **Wheel speed sensors** (Hall effect): measure wheel angular velocity $\omega_w$ in **rad/s**.
- **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)**: 3-axis accelerometer + 3-axis gyroscope, often with magnetometer (9-DoF).
- **Current & voltage transducers** (e.g., LEM LA-series): measure battery SoC and motor input.
- **Throttle Position Sensor (TPS)**: rotary potentiometer giving 0–5 V.
- **Brake pressure / lever travel sensor**.

### Layer 2 — Embedded ECU Layer
- **Vehicle Control Unit (VCU):** Master 32-bit MCU (e.g., Infineon Aurix TC2xx, NXP S32K3xx).
- **Battery Management System (BMS):** Master + slave architecture for cell monitoring.
- **Motor Controller:** BLDC/PMSM inverter driver (e.g., TI C2000 series).
- **Telematics Control Unit (TCU):** Hosts GNSS, 4G modem, SIM, BLE.

### Layer 3 — Communication Layer
- **In-vehicle bus:** CAN 2.0B / CAN FD.
- **Short-range wireless:** BLE 5.0 for phone pairing & smart helmet link.
- **Long-range wireless:** 4G LTE Cat-1/Cat-M1 for cloud; 5G NR sidelink for V2X.
- **Wired diagnostic:** UART, K-Line (legacy), OBD-II (limited on 2W).

### Layer 4 — Cloud & Application Layer
- **IoT broker:** MQTT over TLS.
- **Backend services:** REST APIs for ride data, OTA firmware.
- **Mobile/Web apps:** Android Auto / Apple CarPlay extensions, custom OEM apps.

---

## 2.2 Key Subsystems — Analytical Breakdown

### A. Battery Management System (BMS) — The Brain of EV 2W

A BMS performs:
1. **Cell voltage monitoring** (sampling every **10–100 ms**).
2. **State of Charge (SoC) estimation** — Coulomb counting + Kalman filter.
3. **State of Health (SoH) tracking** — Internal resistance $R_{int}$ growth over cycles.
4. **Thermal management** — triggers fan / liquid cooling above **45 °C**.
5. **Cell balancing** — Active or passive.

#### Coulomb Counting (Fundamental Equation)

$$ \text{SoC}(t) = \text{SoC}(t_0) - \frac{1}{C_{rated}} \int_{t_0}^{t} \eta \cdot I_{batt}(\tau) \, d\tau $$

Where:
- $C_{rated}$ = rated battery capacity in **Ah**
- $I_{batt}$ = instantaneous current (positive = discharge, negative = charge)
- $\eta$ = Coulombic efficiency ($\approx$ **0.98 – 0.99** for Li-ion)
- $\text{SoC}(t_0)$ = initial SoC

### B. Anti-lock Braking System (ABS) — Imitation of Smart Logic

ABS prevents wheel lockup using a **wheel-slip control loop**:

$$ \lambda = \frac{v_{wheel}}{v_{vehicle}} = \frac{R_{eff} \cdot \omega_w}{v_{vehicle}} $$

The control objective is to keep $\lambda$ in the **15 – 25% slip range**, where the tire-road friction coefficient $\mu$ is maximum.

Controller types: **PID** (legacy), **sliding-mode**, **fuzzy-logic** (modern Bosch/Continental units).

### C. Traction Control System (TCS)

Uses IMU pitch/yaw rate + wheel-speed differential:

$$ \Delta\omega = \omega_{rear} - \omega_{front} \cdot r_{ratio} $$

If $\Delta\omega >$ threshold, ECU reduces throttle / advances ignition retard.

### D. Regenerative Braking — Energy Recovery

$$ E_{regen} = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \tau_{gen}(t) \cdot \omega_{motor}(t) \, dt $$

In practice, 2W EVs recover **5–15%** of traction energy. Limit is the **Li-ion charge acceptance** (max ~0.5C charging rate typically, to avoid plating).

---

## 2.3 V2X Communication — Channel Models

### Free-Space Path Loss (Friis Equation) for V2X

$$ PL_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi}{c}\right) $$

Where:
- $d$ = distance in **m**
- $f$ = frequency in **Hz**
- $c = 3 \times 10^8$ **m/s**

For **DSRC @ 5.9 GHz** at 100 m, $PL \approx 92$ dB.

### Link Budget

$$ P_{rx} = P_{tx} + G_{tx} + G_{rx} - PL_{dB} - L_{misc} $$

Received signal must exceed receiver sensitivity (typically **−97 dBm** for DSRC).

### 5G V2X Latency Budget

$$ T_{end-to-end} = T_{sense} + T_{compute} + T_{tx} + T_{prop} + T_{process} $$

Target: **< 10 ms** for collision-warning use cases.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation | Key Symbols / Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Coulomb SoC | $\text{SoC} = \text{SoC}_0 - \frac{\eta}{C}\int I\,dt$ | $C$ in Ah, $I$ in A | Foundation of BMS |
| 2 | Wheel slip ratio | $\lambda = (v_w - v_v)/v_v$ | dimensionless | Target: 0.15 – 0.25 |
| 3 | Friis Path Loss | $PL = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) - 147.55$ | dB | d in m, f in Hz |
| 4 | Link budget | $P_{rx} = P_{tx} + G_t + G_r - PL$ | dBm | Receiver sens. > $P_{rx}$ |
| 5 | Battery Energy | $E = V_{nom} \cdot C \cdot 3600 / 1000$ | Wh | $V_{nom}$ in V, $C$ in Ah |
| 6 | Regen energy | $E_{reg} = \int \tau \omega\,dt$ | J | 5–15% of traction energy |
| 7 | EV Range | $R = E_{batt} \cdot \eta_{sys} / (C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2)$ | km | Simplified aerodynamic model |
| 8 | Motor power | $P = \tau \cdot \omega$ | W | 2W EVs: 1 – 7 kW peak |
| 9 | Coulombic efficiency | $\eta = Q_{out}/Q_{in}$ | dimensionless | Li-ion ≈ 0.99 |
| 10 | SoH | $\text{SoH} = (R_{int,now} - R_{int,new}) / (R_{int,EOL} - R_{int,new})$ | % | EOL = 100% impedance rise |
| 11 | CAN Bus length | $L_{max} \approx 5 \times 10^6 / (n_r \cdot f_{baud})$ | m | $n_r$ = prop. delay factor |
| 12 | OTA file size time | $t_{OTA} = S_{firmware} / (BW_{channel} \cdot \eta_{MAC})$ | s | For 50 MB @ 4G, ≈ 30 – 90 s |
| 13 | GNSS accuracy | $\sigma_{GNSS} \approx 2.5$ m (single-freq) | m | RTK improves to cm-level |
| 14 | Energy density | $E_d = E_{batt} / m_{batt}$ | Wh/kg | Li-ion: 150 – 250 Wh/kg |
| 15 | Charging power | $P_{ch} = V_{ch} \cdot I_{ch}$ | W | Limit by BMS: ~0.5C typically |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are not just academic — they are the exact equations used by **OEM calibration engineers** to tune BMS, ABS, and TCU firmware at companies like **Ather, Ola Electric, Bajaj, TVS, and Hero MotoCorp**.

---

## 2.5 IoT Protocol Stack for 2W/3W

| Layer | Protocol | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Application | MQTT / HTTP REST | Lightweight, pub-sub for telemetry |
| Transport | TCP (TLS 1.2/1.3) | Reliability for OTA |
| Network | IPv4/IPv6 over LTE/5G | Wide coverage |
| Datalink | LTE MAC / 5G NR | Cellular standards |
| Physical | 700 MHz – 5.9 GHz RF | Licensed & unlicensed bands |

For in-vehicle: **CAN 2.0B (11-bit ID)** or **CAN FD (64-byte payload)**.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: EV Range from Battery & Vehicle Parameters

**Problem Setup:** A 2W EV has a battery pack of nominal voltage $V_{nom} = 51.8$ V, capacity $C = 30$ Ah, system efficiency $\eta_{sys} = 0.85$, aerodynamic drag coefficient $C_d = 0.65$, frontal area $A = 0.6$ m², rolling resistance coefficient $C_{rr} = 0.015$, vehicle mass $m = 130$ kg, and cruises at $v = 45$ km/h. Air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

### Step 1 — Battery Usable Energy

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{batt} &= V_{nom} \cdot C \cdot \eta_{DoD} \cdot 3600 / 1000 \\
&= 51.8 \times 30 \times 0.85 \times 3.6 \\
&= 4754.76 \text{ Wh} \approx 4.75 \text{ kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $\eta_{DoD}$ = depth-of-discharge efficiency = 0.85 (battery not fully drained to protect cells).

### Step 2 — Convert Velocity

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= 45 \text{ km/h} = 45 / 3.6 = 12.5 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Traction Force Required at Constant Speed

At steady speed, traction force = sum of resistive forces:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{resist} &= F_{rolling} + F_{aero} + F_{grade} \\
&= m g C_{rr} + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 + m g \sin\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

For level road, $\sin\theta = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{rolling} &= 130 \times 9.81 \times 0.015 = 19.13 \text{ N} \\
F_{aero} &= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.65 \times 0.6 \times (12.5)^2 \\
&= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.65 \times 0.6 \times 156.25 \\
&= 37.34 \text{ N} \\
F_{resist} &= 19.13 + 37.34 = 56.47 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Power Required at the Wheels

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{wheel} &= F_{resist} \cdot v = 56.47 \times 12.5 = 705.9 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Power Drawn from Battery (after system losses)

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{batt} &= P_{wheel} / \eta_{sys} = 705.9 / 0.85 = 830.5 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Time to Deplete Battery

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= E_{batt} / P_{batt} = 4754.76 / 830.5 = 5.725 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7 — Range

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= v \cdot t = 12.5 \text{ m/s} \times 5.725 \times 3600 \text{ s} \\
&= 257,625 \text{ m} \approx \mathbf{257.6 \text{ km}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** Real-world **Ather 450X** with a 2.9 kWh battery delivers ~80–100 km in Eco mode. Our derivation assumes ideal cruising conditions. Actual range drops 30–40% due to stop-go traffic, acceleration events, payload, and wind — all real-world derating factors a smart BMS can estimate dynamically.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: V2X Link Budget for a 2W Broadcasting Position

**Setup:** DSRC transmitter on a 2W, $f = 5.9$ GHz, $P_{tx} = 20$ dBm, $G_{tx} = G_{rx} = 2$ dBi, distance $d = 200$ m, miscellaneous loss $L_{misc} = 4$ dB. Receiver sensitivity $P_{sens} = -97$ dBm. Is the link viable?

### Step 1 — Free-Space Path Loss

$$
\begin{aligned}
PL_{dB} &= 20 \log_{10}(200) + 20 \log_{10}(5.9 \times 10^9) - 147.55 \\
&= 46.02 + 155.42 - 147.55 \\
&= 53.89 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Received Power

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{rx} &= P_{tx} + G_{tx} + G_{rx} - PL_{dB} - L_{misc} \\
&= 20 + 2 + 2 - 53.89 - 4 \\
&= -33.89 \text{ dBm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Link Margin

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{link} &= P_{rx} - P_{sens} = -33.89 - (-97) = 63.11 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** A massive **63 dB** link margin exists. DSRC is more than sufficient for **200 m** V2V messaging in 2W use cases. This is why DSRC/C-V2X can warn a rider about an unseen approaching vehicle well before visual contact.

---

## 3.4 Mathematical Model: Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) for 3W

A 3W (e-rickshaw) traveling at $v_{ego}$ follows a lead vehicle at $v_{lead}$, with inter-vehicle gap $d$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ddot{x}_{ego} &= -k_p (d - d_{safe}) - k_d \dot{d} - k_i \int (d - d_{safe})\,dt \\
\text{where } d_{safe} &= v_{ego} \cdot t_{gap} + d_0
\end{aligned}
$$

With typical values: $t_{gap} = 1.5$ s, $d_0 = 2$ m. Gains $k_p, k_d, k_i$ tuned via Ziegler-Nichols.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**Define V2X communication. List any four variants with one-line functions.**

**Model Answer:**
V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) is a wireless communication paradigm enabling a vehicle to exchange data with its surrounding entities. Variants:

- **V2V (Vehicle-to-Vehicle):** Peer 2W/3W exchange position & speed for collision avoidance.
- **V2I (Vehicle-to-Infrastructure):** Communication with traffic signals, RSU for green-wave optimization.
- **V2N (Vehicle-to-Network):** Cellular link to cloud for navigation, OTA, infotainment.
- **V2P (Vehicle-to-Pedestrian):** Phone-pedestrian detection via BLE or 5G sidelink to warn rider.
- **V2G (Vehicle-to-Grid):** Bi-directional EV charging — sells stored energy back to grid.

*(Any 4 expected for 3 marks.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**What is a Battery Management System (BMS)? State any two of its functions in an electric two-wheeler.**

**Model Answer:**
A **BMS** is an embedded electronic system that monitors, controls, and protects a rechargeable battery pack in an electric two-wheeler.

**Functions:**
1. **State of Charge (SoC) estimation** using Coulomb counting + Kalman filtering, displayed to the rider.
2. **Cell balancing** (active or passive) to equalize individual Li-ion cell voltages and prevent capacity mismatch.
3. **Thermal protection** — cuts off charging/discharging if cell temperature exceeds **60 °C**.
4. **Over-voltage / under-voltage cutoff** to keep cells within safe **2.5 V – 4.2 V** range.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Module 4 contains **TWO** full 14-mark questions. Students must answer **ONE** (internal choice). Each question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (Choice 1):** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the architecture of a smart and connected electric two-wheeler with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of (i) Telematics Control Unit and (ii) Battery Management System.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a suitable sketch, describe the working of an Anti-lock Braking System (ABS) for a two-wheeler. How does a wheel-slip sensor feed data to the ABS ECU?

---

#### Model Solution — (a) Architecture & Roles

**Architecture (5 layered cyber-physical model):**

1. **Sensing Layer:** Wheel speed (Hall sensors), IMU (6/9 DoF), current shunt, thermistor, throttle position sensor, brake lever switch.
2. **Processing Layer:** VCU (master), BMS (battery slave), Motor Controller (BLDC inverter), TCU (telematics).
3. **Actuation Layer:** Inverter switching, ABS modulator solenoid, smart lock relay, TFT display driver, haptic motors in handlebar.
4. **Connectivity Layer:** CAN 2.0B bus in-vehicle; BLE 5.0 (short range, phone pairing); 4G LTE Cat-1 (cloud); DSRC/C-V2X (V2X).
5. **Cloud & App Layer:** MQTT broker, OTA server, mobile app, AI analytics engine.

**Block Diagram: (See Section 4.1 mermaid diagram in this note).**

**Role of TCU:**
The **Telematics Control Unit (TCU)** is the vehicle's "data modem." It hosts:
- **GNSS receiver** (GPS, GLONASS, NavIC) → position, speed, heading.
- **4G LTE modem** → cloud communication, OTA downloads, eCall.
- **BLE 5.0** → smartphone pairing, smart helmet pairing.
- **SIM slot (e-SIM increasingly)** → always-on connectivity.
- **Accelerometer + gyroscope** → crash detection, fall detection, ride analytics.
- It enables **AIS-140 compliance** for commercial 3W and **anti-theft geofencing**.

**Role of BMS:**
The **Battery Management System** is the "guardian" of the Li-ion pack. It:
- Monitors **cell voltage** (±5 mV accuracy), **pack current** (shunt or Hall), **temperature** (NTC at 6+ points).
- Computes **SoC** in real time for the dashboard range display.
- Performs **cell balancing** (passive: resistor bleeding; active: capacitor/inductor transfer).
- Disconnects the contactor on **over-voltage (>4.2 V/cell)**, **under-voltage (<2.5 V/cell)**, **over-current**, or **over-temperature (>60 °C)**.
- Communicates with VCU over **CAN** at 250 kbps/500 kbps.
- Logs **SoH (State of Health)** for warranty and resale valuation.

---

#### Model Solution — (b) ABS Working & Slip Sensor

**ABS — Purpose:**
Prevents wheel lockup during emergency braking, retaining **steerability** and reducing stopping distance by maintaining tire slip in the optimal 15 – 25% range.

**Components:**
1. **Wheel Speed Sensor (WSS):** Toothed reluctor ring + permanent-magnet pickup coil mounted on fork (front) and swingarm (rear). Generates a **sinusoidal AC voltage** with frequency proportional to wheel speed.

$$
f_{WSS} = \frac{n \cdot \omega_{wheel}}{2\pi} \quad [\text{Hz}]
$$

where $n$ = number of teeth on reluctor.

2. **ABS ECU (Electronic Control Unit):** Runs slip control algorithm at **~100 Hz** loop rate.
3. **Hydraulic Modulator:** Contains **inlet valve, outlet valve, and return pump**.
4. **Brake Lines & Caliper:** Standard hydraulic system.

**Working — 4-Phase Cycle:**

- **Pressure Hold:** Wheel decelerating; ECU detects slip rising toward threshold. Closes inlet valve, holds pressure.
- **Pressure Reduce:** Slip exceeds ~25%. ECU opens outlet valve, pressure released into accumulator. Wheel re-accelerates.
- **Pressure Reapply:** Slip falls below 10%. ECU closes outlet, return pump re-pushes fluid into caliper, restoring braking.
- **Cycle repeats** at 4 – 10 Hz typically; rider feels a pulsing lever.

**Slip Sensor → ECU Data Path:**

> WSS (analog AC) → Active Filter & Comparator → Frequency-to-Voltage Converter → MCU ADC pin → Software computes $\omega_{wheel}$ and $\lambda$ → PID/Sliding-mode controller → PWM signals to modulator valves.

For modern **inertial-based** ABS (Bosch ABS 9i, Continental MK100), an **IMU** supplements WSS to estimate vehicle reference speed $\hat{v}_{veh}$ when slip makes WSS data unreliable.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2):** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the various sensors used in a modern connected two-wheeler. Explain how an IMU enables fall detection.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the working of a regenerative braking system in an electric 2W. Derive an expression for the regenerative energy recovered during a braking event.

---

#### Model Solution — (a) Sensors & IMU Fall Detection

**Sensors in a Modern Connected 2W:**

| Sensor | Type | Output | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wheel speed | Hall effect / variable reluctance | Digital pulse train | Speedometer, ABS, TCS |
| Throttle position | Rotary potentiometer | 0–5 V analog | VCU torque request |
| Brake lever | Hall switch | Digital | Regenerative braking trigger |
| Battery current | Shunt resistor / Hall | Analog mV | SoC computation |
| Battery voltage | ADC | Digital | SoC, health |
| Cell temperature | NTC thermistor | Analog | BMS thermal protection |
| Motor temperature | NTC in winding | Analog | Controller derating |
| IMU (6/9-axis) | MEMS | I²C/SPI digital | Fall detect, lean angle, TCS |
| GNSS | GPS L1/L5 + NavIC | UART NMEA | Position, speed, geofence |
| Accelerometer (TCU) | MEMS 3-axis | Digital | Crash detection, eCall |
| Camera (front/rear) | CMOS + ISP | MIPI CSI | ADAS, dashcam, V2P |
| Radar (24/77 GHz) | FMCW | CAN | Blind spot, rear collision |
| Ultrasonic | Piezoelectric | GPIO | Parking assist (3W) |
| Steering damper angle | Hall | Analog | Lean angle telemetry |

**IMU-based Fall Detection:**

An IMU typically contains a 3-axis accelerometer + 3-axis gyroscope (±2000 °/s) + 3-axis magnetometer.

A fall event signature consists of **three temporal phases**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Phase 1 (Tilt):} &\quad \theta_{roll} > 60° \text{ within } \Delta t_1 \approx 200 \text{ ms} \\
\text{Phase 2 (Impact):} &\quad a_{z} > 4g \text{ (peak spike)} \\
\text{Phase 3 (Post-impact):} &\quad \omega_{roll} \approx 0 \text{ for } t > 3 \text{ s (vehicle stationary on side)}
\end{aligned}
$$

A decision tree or simple **threshold + timer** logic (e.g., if Phase-1 AND Phase-2 AND Phase-3 all true within **2 s** window) triggers:
1. **eCall / bCall** to emergency services with GPS coordinates.
2. **SMS to emergency contact** via TCU.
3. **Hazard lights activation** (via CAN).
4. **Engine/motor cutoff** for safety.

This is the same architecture used in **Apple iPhone Crash Detection** (introduced 2022) and **Google Pixel Car Crash Detection**, scaled down to a vehicle-grade IMU.

---

#### Model Solution — (b) Regenerative Braking — Working & Derivation

**Working Principle:**

In an electric 2W, the BLDC/PMSM motor acts as a **generator** when rotated by the wheel's kinetic energy. The motor controller switches the inverter IGBTs/MOSFETs in a controlled manner so that the back-EMF drives current back into the battery.

**Stages:**
1. **Rider releases accelerator / squeezes brake lever.** Brake lever Hall sensor sends a CAN message (e.g., `0x201` byte 1 = brake_pct).
2. **VCU commands motor controller** to enter regen mode. Torque reference becomes **negative**.
3. **Inverter short-circuits the motor windings** in a controlled sequence, creating a **counter-torque** (regen torque $\tau_{reg}$).
4. **Current flows back into the battery** through the high-side DC-DC link. BMS must accept charge current (typically limited to **0.3C – 0.5C**).
5. **Friction brakes** are blended in for additional deceleration if regen torque alone is insufficient.

**Mathematical Derivation:**

Let the vehicle have mass $m$ moving at initial velocity $v_0$ and final velocity $v_f$. Kinetic energy available:

$$
E_k = \frac{1}{2} m (v_0^2 - v_f^2)
$$

The regenerative torque acts opposite to wheel rotation. The angular deceleration of the wheel is:

$$
\alpha_{wheel} = \frac{\tau_{reg}}{I_{wheel,eq}}
$$

where $I_{wheel,eq}$ is the equivalent moment of inertia of the wheel + motor rotor + gear reduction, referred to the wheel.

Assuming $\tau_{reg}$ is approximately constant over a braking interval $\Delta t$, the energy recovered is:

$$
E_{regen} = \int_0^{\Delta t} \tau_{reg} \cdot \omega_{wheel}(t)\, dt
$$

$$
\Rightarrow E_{regen} = \tau_{reg} \cdot \int_0^{\Delta t} \omega_{wheel}(t)\, dt
$$

But $\omega_{wheel}(t) = \omega_0 - \alpha t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{regen} &= \tau_{reg} \int_0^{\Delta t} (\omega_0 - \alpha t)\, dt \\
&= \tau_{reg} \left[ \omega_0 \Delta t - \tfrac{1}{2} \alpha (\Delta t)^2 \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $\alpha = \tau_{reg}/I_{wheel,eq}$:

$$
E_{regen} = \tau_{reg} \omega_0 \Delta t - \frac{\tau_{reg}^2 (\Delta t)^2}{2 I_{wheel,eq}}
$$

The **regenerative efficiency** is then:

$$
\eta_{regen} = \frac{E_{regen}}{\frac{1}{2}m(v_0^2 - v_f^2)} \times 100\%
$$

For a typical EV 2W (e.g., Ather 450X) with $m = 110$ kg decelerating from 40 km/h to 20 km/h:

$$
E_k = 0.5 \times 110 \times ((11.11)^2 - (5.56)^2) = 5090 \text{ J}
$$

If regen recovers 60% of this, $E_{regen} \approx 3054$ J $\approx$ **0.85 Wh** — enough to extend range by **1–2 km** in stop-go city use.

**Limitations:**
- Battery cannot accept arbitrarily high charge C-rate.
- Below ~10 km/h, regen torque becomes weak (low back-EMF).
- Friction brakes still needed for ABS intervention (regen is too slow).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Lose NO Marks!**
>
> 1. **Missing Units:** A "7-mark derivation" without units on the final answer → lose **1 mark minimum**. Always state $N$, $Nm$, $rad/s$, $J$, $Wh$.
> 2. **Block Diagram Sketches:** In Q.A(a) above, the architecture diagram is worth **at least 3 marks** out of 7. Skipping it costs heavy marks. Always draw neat labelled boxes with arrows.
> 3. **Vague Phrases:** Writing *"Sensors are used"* is worth **0 marks**. State **type of sensor + output + function** (e.g., *"Hall-effect wheel speed sensor outputs a digital pulse train whose frequency is proportional to wheel angular velocity, used by ABS ECU to compute slip ratio."*).
> 4. **Confusing ABS vs. TCS:** ABS prevents **wheel lockup** (too much braking); TCS prevents **excess wheel spin** (too much throttle). Don't mix them up.
> 5. **Confusing SoC vs. SoH:** SoC = current charge level (% remaining, like a fuel gauge). SoH = battery age/health (%, drops over years). Examiners test this distinction.
> 6. **CAN vs. CAN FD:** Classical CAN is 1 Mbps max with 8-byte payload. CAN FD is up to 5 Mbps with 64-byte payload. Mixing these up loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 7. **V2X Standards Confusion:** DSRC = IEEE 802.11p, used in US/EU. C-V2X = 3GPP cellular, used in China & emerging globally. Both target <10 ms latency.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📌 Critical Things to Remember — Smart & Connected 2W/3W**

### ✅ Definitions to Memorize
- **Smart Vehicle** = sensor-rich + decision-making electronics
- **Connected Vehicle** = wireless data exchange with outside world
- **V2X** = V2V + V2I + V2N + V2P + V2G
- **IoT** = sensors + connectivity + cloud + analytics
- **ABS** = Anti-lock Braking System (slip control 15-25%)
- **TCS** = Traction Control System
- **BMS** = Battery Management System (SoC, SoH, balancing, protection)
- **ECU/VCU/TCU** = Electronic / Vehicle / Telematics Control Unit

### ✅ Numerical Values to Memorize
- DSRC band: **5.85 – 5.925 GHz**
- CAN 2.0B: **1 Mbps, 8-byte**; CAN FD: **5 Mbps, 64-byte**
- Li-ion cell voltage: **2.5 V (min) – 4.2 V (max)**, nominal 3.6 – 3.7 V
- V2X latency target: **< 10 ms**
- BLE 5.0 range: **~200 m (long range mode) / ~10 m (normal)**
- 4G LTE for 2W telematics: **Cat-1 or Cat-M1** (low power, always-on)
- Regen energy share: **5 – 15%** of traction energy
- AIS-140 mandate: **GPS + SOS button** for public service vehicles
- ABS mandate India: **>125 cc 2W** (AIS-131)

### ✅ Architecture Layers to Draw
1. Sensing → 2. Processing (VCU/BMS/MCU/TCU) → 3. Actuation → 4. Connectivity (CAN/BLE/LTE) → 5. Cloud/App

### ✅ Key Equations to Memorize
- SoC: $\text{SoC}(t) = \text{SoC}(t_0) - \frac{1}{C}\int \eta I\,dt$
- Slip: $\lambda = (v_w - v_v)/v_v$, target 0.15 – 0.25
- Friis: $PL = 20\log(d) + 20\log(f) - 147.55$
- Regen energy: $E_{reg} = \int \tau \omega\,dt$
- Range: $R = E_{batt} \eta_{sys} / P_{wheel}$
- Battery energy: $E = V_{nom} \cdot C \cdot 3600/1000$ (in Wh)

### ✅ Smart Features Mapped to Subsystems
- **ABS** → WSS + Hydraulic Modulator
- **TCS** → IMU + WSS differential
- **Fall Detection** → IMU 3-phase logic (tilt, impact, post-impact)
- **OTA Updates** → A/B partition + signed firmware + rollback
- **Anti-theft** → GPS geofence + BLE proximity unlock
- **Connected cluster** → 4G TCU + TFT dashboard
- **Smart helmet** → BLE + HUD + rear camera + IMU
- **Smart charging** → OCPP protocol + SoC-aware charge rate

- **Ola Electric** — S1 Pro (MoveOS, hypercharger, app control)
